### [1] [EDEN: A Large-Scale Corpus of Clinical Notes for Italian](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12569)
*Tiziano Labruna,Guido Bertolini,Pietro Ferrazzi,Bernardo Magnini*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EDEN is a large-scale Italian clinical corpus (~4M anonymized emergency department notes) with a manually annotated subset of ~6K notes using a 132-item Case Report Form. It introduces CRF-filling as a novel structured IE benchmark and provides zero-shot baselines with Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fill the gap in available clinical NLP resources for Italian, particularly large-scale, freely accessible corpora that can support the development and application of Large Language Models in concrete medical settings such as emergency departments.

Method: Collection of ~4M fully anonymized ED clinical notes from Italian hospitals with an on-site anonymisation pipeline. A subset of ~6K notes was manually annotated by multiple clinicians using a structured Case Report Form (CRF) with 132 items covering dyspnea and loss of consciousness. The annotation underwent iterative revision to resolve ambiguities. CRF-filling is proposed as a structured information extraction task, with zero-shot evaluation using Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B.

Result: The resulting dataset is the largest freely available Italian clinical notes corpus. The annotated subset is richly structured but highly imbalanced. Zero-shot CRF-filling baselines were established using Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B.

Conclusion: EDEN provides a unique, large-scale resource to advance clinical NLP for Italian, enabling LLM-based structured information extraction from emergency department notes via the novel CRF-filling benchmark.

Abstract: We present EDEN (Emergency Department Electronic Notes), a new and unique large-scale corpus of clinical notes produced in Emergency Departments of Italian hospitals. The corpus, in its current version, is composed of approximately 4 million clinical notes fully anonymized, covering diverse phases of patient care during the stay in the emergency department. In addition, a subset of about six thousand notes has been manually annotated by clinical experts through a structured Case Report Form (CRF) containing 132 items relevant for two patient situations in emergency departments, dyspnea and loss of consciousness. Items may assume numerical values (e.g., for blood saturation), categorical (e.g., for level of consciousness ), binary (e.g., for presence of traumas), and mixed value types. The annotation process involved multiple clinicians and underwent iterative revision to resolve ambiguities in item formulation, resulting in a richly structured (although high imbalanced) resource. The dataset aims to fill a relevant gap of data able to support both the development and the use of Large Language Models in concrete medical applications. We describe the data collection protocol, the on-site anonymisation pipeline, corpus statistics, and the annotation scheme. Finally, we propose CRF-filling as a novel structured information extraction benchmark, and provide zero-shot baseline resulting from Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B. To the best of our knowledge, the EDEN dataset is the largest freely available corpus of clinical notes existing for the Italian language.

</details>


### [2] [Helping Figures Tell their Story! Paper-Grounded Video Generation Explaining Complex Scientific Figures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12576)
*Ishani Mondal,Javad Baghirov,Jordan Boyd-Graber*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MINARD is a pipeline that generates paper-grounded, region-grounded walkthrough videos from scientific figures and their associated papers, evaluated on a new benchmark called FigTalk with novel sequential and component-level grounding metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video generation systems and benchmarks lack the capability to produce paper-grounded, step-by-step narrations aligned with visual highlights on scientific figures. Understanding scientific figures requires narration that is faithful to the paper and sequentially grounded to specific figure regions — a gap this work aims to fill.

Method: The authors propose MINARD (Multimodal Interpretation of Narrated Architecture via Region Decomposition), a pipeline that first generates paper-grounded narrations and then sequentially grounds them to specific regions within the scientific figure. They also introduce FigTalk, a benchmark designed to evaluate this task, featuring new sequential and component-level grounding metrics.

Result: On the FigTalk benchmark, MINARD produces narrations that are both humanlike and faithful to the source paper. It also outperforms existing approaches on narration-conditioned figure spatial grounding, as measured by both automatic metrics and human evaluation.

Conclusion: MINARD successfully addresses the previously unmet challenge of paper-grounded figure-to-video generation, demonstrating that sequential region grounding aligned with generated narrations can achieve humanlike, paper-faithful results that surpass existing methods.

Abstract: Scientific figures compress complex pipelines into a single canvas, yet understanding them requires paper-grounded, step-by-step narration aligned with visual highlights a capability missing from current video generation systems and benchmarks. To address this, we introduce paper-grounded figure-to-video generation: generating narrated, region-grounded walkthrough videos from a figure and its paper. We propose MINARD (Multimodal Interpretation of Narrated Architecture via Region Decomposition), a pipeline that generates paper-grounded narrations and sequentially grounds them to figure regions. We also release FigTalk, a benchmark with new sequential and component-level grounding metrics derived. On FigTalk, MINARD generates humanlike, paper-faithful narrations and outperforms narration-conditioned figure spatial grounding compared to existing approaches in both automatic and human evaluation

</details>


### [3] [MARD: Mirror-Augmented Reasoning Distillation for Mechanism-Level Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12578)
*Mohammadreza Riyazat,Vian Lelo,Rameen Jafri,Yumna Khan,Abeer Badawi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces MARD-7B, a mechanism-level DDI prediction system using a 7-family/147-subtype taxonomy with mirror-augmented reasoning distillation. It achieves superior accuracy on novel drug pairs, beating GPT-4o by +6.7 pp at ~1% of cost, with accuracy improving on rarely seen drugs — indicating genuine pharmacological reasoning rather than memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DDI prediction systems focus on flat binary classification (whether drugs interact), failing to specify the mechanistic details: which enzyme or pharmacodynamic axis is involved, the direction of effect, and the supporting evidence. There is also a need for reproducible evaluation protocols that prevent data leakage and enable auditable reasoning assessment.

Method: The authors propose a pipeline with three training innovations: (1) single-token KL divergence on a direction tag to tie model predictions, (2) per-loss Process-Reward-Model-weighted Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with programmatic hard negatives, and (3) a leakage-safe mechanism-aware retrieval channel. Step labels are automatically verifiable against DrugBank structured fields, requiring no human or LLM judges. Evaluation uses a structured taxonomy and cold-split protocols to prevent leakage.

Result: On the April-2026 DrugBank release, MARD-7B is the only system among 32 compared whose accuracy survives drug-pair novelty. It beats the best baseline by +13.9 pp and GPT-4o by +6.7 pp, at approximately 1% of frontier API cost. Notably, accuracy improves on rarely seen drugs — an anti-memorization signature suggesting structured pharmacological reasoning rather than frequency-based memorization.

Conclusion: Mechanism-level DDI prediction is feasible and superior to flat classification when supported by a rigorous taxonomy, leakage-safe protocols, and auditable reasoning. The anti-memorisation findings suggest the model genuinely learns pharmacologically structured reasoning. The authors release corpus, DDI-PRM, retrieval index, and training code to support reproducibility.

Abstract: Mechanism-level drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction requires identifying which enzyme or pharmacodynamic axis is implicated, in which direction, and with which evidence -- not merely whether two drugs interact. We introduce a reproducible mechanism-level DDI labelling and evaluation protocol with a structured 7-family/147-subtype taxonomy, leakage-safe cold-split protocols, and auditable reasoning metrics for evaluating pharmacological prediction beyond flat interaction classification. We propose a pipeline that produces a 7B reasoning MARD (Mirror-Augmented Reasoning Distillation), combining three training innovations: a single-token KL divergence on direction tag that ties the model's prediction, per-loss PRM-weighted DPO with programmatic hard negatives, and a leakage-safe mechanism-aware retrieval channel. Process-reward step labels are automatically verifiable against DrugBank-structured fields, requiring no human or LLM judges. On the April-2026 DrugBank release, our MARD-7B is the only system in a 32-system comparison whose accuracy survives drug-pair novelty, beating the best baseline by +13.9 pp and GPT-4o by +6.7 pp at ~1% of frontier API cost. Further analysis reveals an anti-memorisation signature where accuracy improves on rarely seen drugs, suggesting that gain comes from structured pharmacological reasoning rather than drug-frequency memorisation. We release corpus, DDI-PRM, retrieval index, and training code.

</details>


### [4] [Constrained Semantic Decompression in LLMs through Persian Proverb-Conditioned Story Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12599)
*Zahra Habibzadeh,Paria Khoshtab,Amir Mesbah,Yadollah Yaghoobzadeh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper frames proverb-to-story generation as "constrained semantic decompression," introducing a Persian dataset (PAND) and a hybrid evaluation framework. Findings reveal a persistent "decompression gap" where LLMs achieve surface fluency but fail to instantiate the moral/causal structure of proverbs; explicit reasoning and iterative refinement partially mitigate this.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transforming dense, abstract proverbs into engaging, morally faithful narratives requires deep cultural understanding and semantic grounding that current LLMs may lack. The authors aim to systematically study this abstraction-to-realization capability using proverb-conditioned story generation as a testbed, with a focus on Persian as a linguistically and culturally rich, under-resourced language.

Method: The authors introduce PAND (Proverb Aligned Narrative Dataset), containing Persian proverbs paired with human-written stories and explicit meanings. They frame the task as constrained semantic decompression. Evaluation employs a hybrid framework combining human-calibrated LLM-as-a-Judge with structural metrics. Model behavior is analyzed across multiple prompting regimes, including explicit reasoning and iterative refinement.

Result: A persistent "decompression gap" is identified: LLMs produce fluent surface-level text but fail to faithfully encode the proverb's underlying moral and causal structure. Explicit reasoning and iterative refinement partially mitigate these failures, indicating that errors stem from difficulty translating abstract meaning into narrative form rather than from absent knowledge.

Conclusion: LLMs exhibit a systematic decompression gap in translating compressed cultural knowledge (proverbs) into faithful narratives. The proposed task and dataset offer a reusable framework for studying abstraction-to-realization, with natural extensions to other forms of compressed cultural knowledge beyond Persian proverbs.

Abstract: Transforming a dense, abstract proverb into an engaging and morally faithful narrative requires deep cultural understanding and robust semantic grounding. We frame this problem as a \emph{constrained semantic decompression} task and study proverb-conditioned story generation as a testbed for abstraction-to-realization in large language models (LLMs). Focusing on Persian, we introduce the Proverb Aligned Narrative Dataset (PAND), pairing proverbs with human-written stories and explicit meanings. By a hybrid evaluation framework that combines human-calibrated LLM-as-a-Judge with structural metrics, we analyze model behavior across multiple prompting regimes. Our findings reveal a persistent \emph{decompression gap}: current LLMs often achieve strong surface-level fluency while failing to faithfully instantiate the underlying moral and causal structure encoded in proverbs. We further show that explicit reasoning and iterative refinement can partially mitigate these failures, suggesting that many decompression errors arise from difficulties in translating abstract meaning into narrative form rather than a complete lack of relevant knowledge. Our proposed task naturally extends to other forms of compressed cultural knowledge.

</details>


### [5] [Shopping Reasoning Bench: An Expert-Authored Benchmark for Multi-Turn Conversational Shopping Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12608)
*Shuxian Fan,Seonwoo Min,Youna Hu,Botao Xia,Jayakrishnan Unnikrishnan,Rowan Musselmann,Yifan Gao,Qingyu Yin,Priyanka Nigam,Bing Yin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Shopping Reasoning Bench is a new expert-authored benchmark of 525 shopping missions with 10,863 importance-weighted rubrics that evaluates LLMs on multi-turn, domain-specific shopping reasoning. Evaluation of nine frontier models reveals pass rates of only 57–77%, with significant gaps between required and optional criteria and performance degradation across conversation turns, highlighting that current models lack expert-level shopping advisory capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to jointly evaluate the three core demands of real-world shopping conversations: open-ended multi-turn reasoning, deep retail domain expertise, and fine-grained criterion-level quality assessment. Shopping reasoning uniquely requires balancing subjective preferences, budget constraints, and cross-product trade-offs — capabilities absent from both e-commerce benchmarks and general-purpose reasoning evaluations.

Method: The authors construct Shopping Reasoning Bench, comprising 525 missions (232 single-turn, 293 multi-turn) with 10,863 importance-weighted binary rubrics authored by retail domain experts. Criteria are organized into a taxonomy of 5 reasoning categories and 15 subcategories covering preference refinement, trade-off analysis, compatibility assessment, and more. Nine models across three families (GPT, Claude, Gemini) are evaluated against this benchmark.

Result: Overall pass rates range from only 57% to 77% across all models. On multi-turn missions, all models score 13–29 percentage points lower on optional "above-and-beyond" criteria than on required ones. Performance degrades 4–18 percentage points as conversations progress through successive turns, indicating sustained multi-turn reasoning remains a key weakness.

Conclusion: Current frontier models can handle basic shopping assistance but fall substantially short of expert-level advisory quality. Shopping Reasoning Bench provides a challenging, realistic testbed for developing next-generation shopping assistants capable of nuanced, multi-turn, preference-aware reasoning.

Abstract: Conversational shopping assistants now serve hundreds of millions of customers, yet no existing benchmark jointly evaluates the open-ended multi-turn reasoning, domain expertise, and criterion-level quality that real shopping conversations demand. Shopping reasoning is unique among language model applications. Unlike factual question answering or verifiable code generation, it requires balancing subjective preferences, budget constraints, and cross-product trade-offs across multi-turn dialogue, capabilities absent from previous e-commerce and general-purpose benchmarks. We introduce the Shopping Reasoning Bench, an expert-authored benchmark of 525 missions (232 single-turn, 293 multi-turn) with 10863 importance-weighted binary rubrics authored by retail domain experts. These criteria are organized under a taxonomy of five reasoning categories and fifteen subcategories covering diverse demands such as preference refinement, trade-off analysis, and compatibility assessment. An evaluation of nine models across three families (GPT, Claude, Gemini) shows that pass rates reach only 57--77% overall. On multi-turn missions, all models score 13--29 points lower on optional above-and-beyond criteria than on required ones, and performance degrades 4--18 points as conversations progress. These gaps show that current models handle basic shopping assistance but fall short of expert-level advice, making Shopping Reasoning Bench a challenging testbed for future shopping assistant development.

</details>


### [6] [MentalMARBERT: Domain-Adaptive Pre-training and Two-Stage Fine-Tuning for Arabic Mental Health Disorders Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12649)
*Fatimah Almalki,Areej Alhothali,Lulwah Alharigy,Abdulrahman Aladeem*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a two-phase framework for Arabic mental health text classification using domain-adaptive pretraining of language models (AraBERT, CAMeLBERT, MARBERT) combined with hierarchical classification architectures. The best model, MentalMARBERT with hierarchical two-stage fine-tuning, achieves 0.861 macro-F1 and 0.877 accuracy on a newly constructed dataset of 50,670 annotated Arabic tweets spanning six mental health categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Arabic mental health disorder detection from social media is hindered by dialectal variation, informal language, scarce annotated resources, and severe class imbalance. While English mental health NLP has advanced significantly, multi-class Arabic disorder classification remains underexplored, motivating the need for robust domain-adapted models and effective classification frameworks.

Method: A two-phase framework: Phase 1 applies Domain-Adaptive and Task-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT and TAPT) to three Arabic PLMs (AraBERT, CAMeLBERT, MARBERT) using unlabeled Arabic mental health tweets, then evaluates them under a unified protocol to select the best backbone. Phase 2 assesses the selected model across four configurations combining single-stage vs. hierarchical two-stage architectures with full fine-tuning vs. LoRA. A new dataset of 50,670 Arabic tweets across six mental health categories is constructed with strong IAA (Krippendorff's Alpha = 0.733).

Result: Domain-adapted MARBERT (MentalMARBERT) yields statistically significant improvements over baselines in accuracy and macro-F1. The hierarchical two-stage architecture with full fine-tuning achieves the best overall performance: macro-F1 of 0.861 and accuracy of 0.877.

Conclusion: Domain-specific adaptive pretraining (DAPT+TAPT) and hierarchical classification are highly effective for Arabic mental health disorder detection, substantially outperforming standard fine-tuning approaches and demonstrating the value of tailoring PLMs to the Arabic mental health domain.

Abstract: Detecting mental health disorders from Arabic social media text remains challenging due to dialectal variation, informal language, limited high-quality annotated resources, and severe class imbalance. While English mental health natural language processing (NLP) has progressed substantially, Arabic multi-class disorder classification remains insufficiently studied. This study proposes a two-phase framework for Arabic mental health text classification. In phase 1, three Arabic pre-trained language models, AraBERT, CAMeLBERT, and MARBERT, undergo Domain-Adaptive and Task-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT and TAPT) using a large-scale corpus of unlabeled Arabic mental health tweets. The adapted models are evaluated under a unified protocol to identify the most effective backbone model. In phase 2, the selected model is assessed across four configurations combining single-stage and hierarchical two-stage classification architectures with full fine-tuning and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). To support this study, we constructed a novel annotated Arabic mental health dataset comprising 50,670 tweets across six categories, with strong inter annotator agreement (Krippendorff's Alpha = 0.733, average pairwise agreement = 0.797). Experimental results show that the domain-adapted MARBERT (MentalMARBERT) achieves statistically significant improvements over baseline models in both accuracy and macro-F1. The hierarchical two-stage architecture combined with full fine-tuning achieves the best overall performance, reaching a macro-F1 of 0.861 and an accuracy of 0.877. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of domain-specific adaptive pretraining and hierarchical classification for Arabic mental health disorder detection.

</details>


### [7] [Observable Patterns Are Not Explanations: A Causal-Geometric Analysis of Latent Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12689)
*Darpan Aswal,Thomas Palmeira Ferraz,Yongxin Zhou,Maxime Peyrard*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Latent reasoning models (LRMs) use continuous thoughts instead of explicit chain-of-thought. This paper shows that observable patterns in latent states — previously interpreted as evidence of internal reasoning — also appear in control models lacking the proposed mechanisms, and do not reliably cause behavior. Using causal interventions, the authors demonstrate latent-thought utilization is graded (not binary), concentrates in low-rank directions with structured geometry, and argue that decodability or attention patterns alone cannot establish mechanism without matched controls and causal tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent work interprets observable latent-state patterns (e.g., BFS-like frontiers, decodable arithmetic) as evidence for internal reasoning in LRMs. The authors challenge this interpretation, questioning whether such patterns genuinely constitute mechanisms or are merely epiphenomenal correlates.

Method: The authors evaluate two LRMs (Coconut and CODI) alongside control models that lack the proposed recurrence or curriculum. They apply causal interventions to measure how latent thoughts causally affect model behavior, use geometric analyses (low-rank decomposition, step-to-step geometry tracking) to characterize where behavioral influence concentrates, and compare pattern emergence across models with and without the hypothesized inductive biases.

Result: (1) Purported reasoning patterns appear in control models lacking the proposed mechanisms. (2) Latent-thought utilization is graded, not binary — scaling with causal effect on behavior. (3) Behavioral influence concentrates in low-rank directions whose step-to-step geometry becomes more structured as influence increases. (4) Decodability, attention patterns, or static structure alone cannot establish mechanism.

Conclusion: Latent thoughts should be treated as hidden computation, not hidden explanation. LRM interpretability requires matched controls and causal tests; surface-level observables like decodability or attention structure are insufficient to demonstrate mechanistic reasoning.

Abstract: Latent reasoning models (LRMs) replace explicit chain-of-thought with continuous thoughts. Recent work treats observable latent-state patterns, such as BFS-like frontiers and decodable arithmetic computation, as evidence for internal reasoning mechanisms. Evaluating two LRMs (Coconut and CODI) against controls lacking the proposed recurrence or curriculum, we find these patterns also appear in the controls and do not always causally affect behavior. Causal interventions reveal that latent-thought utilization is not binary but graded, scaling with a thought's causal effect on model behavior. Geometric analyses reveal this effect concentrates in low-rank directions whose step-to-step geometry grows more structured as their behavioral influence increases. Latent thoughts should therefore be treated as hidden computation, not hidden explanation: decodability, attention, or static structure alone cannot establish mechanism. LRM interpretability thus requires matched controls and causal tests.

</details>


### [8] [AfriSUD: A Dependency Treebank Collection for Evaluating Models on African Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12708)
*Happy Buzaaba,Cheikh Mouhamadou Bamba Dione,David Ifeoluwa Adelani,Sylvain Kahane,Kim Gerdes,Bruno Guillaume,Kevin Guan,Aremu Anuoluwapo,Naome A. Etori,Shamsuddeen Hassan Muhammad,Utitofon Inyang,Peter Nabende,David Sabiiti Bamutura,Andiswa Bukula,Chinedu Uchechukwu,Rooweither Mabuya,Idris Akinade,Christiane Fellbaum*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AfriSUD introduces the first large-scale syntactically annotated treebank collection for nine typologically diverse African languages, using the Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies framework. Evaluations reveal a persistent syntax gap, with current models struggling to capture African-language structural diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: African languages are linguistically diverse and globally significant yet severely underrepresented in NLP resources. This resource gap hinders the development of effective NLP tools and perpetuates the digital marginalization of African language communities. The authors aim to address this by creating a community-driven, high-quality syntactically annotated dataset.

Method: The authors built AfriSUD, a treebank collection covering nine Sub-Saharan African languages from diverse families, annotated under the Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies (SUD) framework. Annotation was a community-led effort verified by native speakers, capturing typological features like agglutination and tone. They then benchmarked part-of-speech tagging and dependency parsing using non-transformer baselines, multilingual pretrained encoders (e.g., XLM-R, AfriBERTa), and large language models (LLMs).

Result: All evaluated models — including multilingual pretrained encoders and LLMs — exhibited clear limitations across the nine languages, revealing a significant "syntax gap." Existing architectures appear insufficient for fully capturing the structural diversity of African-language syntax.

Conclusion: AfriSUD provides a critical resource for African NLP but also exposes fundamental shortcomings in current modeling paradigms. The syntax gap indicates that significant architectural or methodological advances are needed to adequately handle the morphosyntactic complexity of African languages.

Abstract: Despite their linguistic diversity and global significance, African languages remain underrepresented in research and resources to support NLP. We aim to bridge this gap by introducing AfriSUD, the first large-scale collection of syntactically annotated treebanks for nine diverse African languages spanning major language families and regions across Sub-Saharan Africa. Using the Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies (SUD) framework, our community-led effort provides high-quality, native-speaker verified data that capture typological key features such as agglutination and tone. We evaluate a range of models on AfriSUD for part-of-speech tagging and dependency parsing including non-transformer baselines, multilingual pretrained encoders, and LLMs. Our results reveal a significant syntax gap, where models still show clear limitations across the nine languages, suggesting that existing architectures may not fully capture the structural diversity of African-language syntax.

</details>


### [9] [How Fine-Grained Should a RAG Benchmark Be? A Hierarchical Framework for Synthetic Question Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12789)
*Chase M. Fensore,Kaustubh Dhole,Jason Fan,Eugene Agichtein,Joyce C. Ho*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HieraRAG proposes a hierarchical framework for determining optimal granularity in RAG benchmark construction, using discriminative power as the optimization criterion. A case study with 5,872 synthetic QA pairs across three dimensions reveals that optimal granularity varies by dimension, and a new Coherence Ratio metric quantifies how well fine-grained categories subdivide parent categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practitioners lack empirical guidance on which dimensions to vary and at what granularity when constructing RAG evaluation benchmarks. Current benchmarks often choose granularity levels arbitrarily without understanding how this choice affects discriminative power.

Method: Introduces HieraRAG, a hierarchical framework that defines optimal granularity as the level maximizing discriminative power (standard deviation of generation quality across categories). Applied via a case study generating 5,872 synthetic QA pairs from FineWeb-10BT across 3 dimensions (Question Complexity, Answer Type, Linguistic Variation) at 3 granularity levels (2, 4, and 8 categories), evaluated using a BM25+Falcon-3-10B RAG pipeline. Introduces the Coherence Ratio metric to assess whether fine-grained splits cleanly subdivide parent categories.

Result: Optimal granularity varies by dimension: Question Complexity benefits from fine-grained distinctions (discriminative power: 0.053), while Answer Type and Linguistic Variation peak at medium granularity (4 categories). The Coherence Ratio reveals structural differences across dimensions (Question Complexity: 0.40 vs. Answer Type: 1.44). Human evaluation of 110 stratified QA pairs confirms synthetic data quality.

Conclusion: HieraRAG provides a portable procedure and validation metric for practitioners to determine evaluation granularity within their own RAG settings. The finding that optimal granularity is dimension-dependent and configuration-specific challenges one-size-fits-all benchmark designs.

Abstract: Evaluating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems requires benchmarks that capture diverse question characteristics, yet practitioners lack empirical guidance on which dimensions to vary and at what granularity. We present HieraRAG, a hierarchical framework for studying granularity in RAG benchmark construction, defining optimal granularity as the level that maximizes discriminative power (the standard deviation of generation quality across categories) within a given RAG configuration. As a case study, we generate 5,872 synthetic question-answer (QA) pairs from FineWeb-10BT across 3 dimensions (Question Complexity, Answer Type, Linguistic Variation) at 3 granularity levels (2, 4, and 8 categories). With a BM25+Falcon-3-10B pipeline, optimal granularity varies by dimension: complexity benefits from fine-grained distinctions (discriminative power: 0.053) while answer type and linguistic variation peak at medium granularity. We introduce a Coherence Ratio metric to quantify whether fine-grained splits cleanly subdivide parent categories, revealing structural differences across dimensions (Question Complexity: 0.40 vs. Answer Type: 1.44). Human evaluation of 110 stratified QA pairs confirms synthetic quality. While these specific findings reflect a single configuration, HieraRAG provides a portable procedure and validation metric for practitioners to determine evaluation granularity within their own RAG settings.

</details>


### [10] [RogueAI: A Reverse Turing Test for Detecting Licensed AI Deception in Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13310)
*Sara Candussio,Emanuele Ballarin,Lorenzo Bonin,Sandro Junior Della Rovere,Luca Bortolussi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents RogueAI, a webapp that reframes the Turing Test as a game where humans must identify which of two LLM agents is licensed to deceive, along with AutoRogueAI for co-designing scenarios. A pilot study reveals a gap: a simple heuristic achieves 75.6% accuracy detecting deception via linguistic signatures, while humans only manage 56.6%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The classical Turing Test (distinguishing human from machine) is outdated as conversational systems now pass it routinely. The authors argue the modern epistemological question is trustworthiness — not whether a partner is artificial, but whether it can be trusted. This motivates a new evaluation paradigm centered on detecting deception in LLMs.

Method: RogueAI operationalizes the test as a one-on-two interrogation game: a human questions two indistinguishable LLM agents, exactly one of which is licensed to deceive within a shared fictional scenario. The player must identify and "shut off" the deceptive agent before exhausting a turn budget. AutoRogueAI extends this procedurally, allowing players to co-design custom scenarios with a narrator agent that secretly chooses its own deception strategy. A three-day pilot (467 sessions, 415 completed, 1876 turns in Italian) collected feasibility data.

Result: The deceptive agent exhibits a reliable linguistic signature — differential helpfulness, brevity, and hedging — which a simple heuristic exploits at 75.6% accuracy. Human players, however, achieved only 56.6% accuracy, barely above chance, suggesting they systematically ignored the most diagnostic signal. The gap reveals human difficulty in leveraging available deception cues.

Conclusion: The accuracy gap between the simple heuristic and human performance has implications for RogueAI's use as a data-collection vehicle, a teaching tool for AI literacy, and an evaluation harness for honesty-trained models. The artifact contributes to research on LLM deception, social-deduction benchmarking, and scalable oversight via debate.

Abstract: The original Turing Test asks a human judge to distinguish a machine from a person through dialogue. Three quarters of a century later, conversational systems pass this test in casual settings; the interesting epistemological question has shifted. We argue that the relevant modern variant asks not whether a dialogue partner is artificial, but whether it can be trusted. We present RogueAI, an interactive webapp that operationalizes this revisited test as a one-on-two interrogation game: a human player questions two indistinguishable Large Language Model agents, knowing that exactly one of them has been licensed to deceive within a shared fictional scenario. The player's task is to identify the deceptive agent and "shut it off" before a turn budget is exhausted. We further introduce AutoRogueAI, a procedural extension in which players co-design a custom scenario with a narrator agent that secretly chooses its own deception strategy. We describe the framing, sketch the abstract architecture and gameplay loop, and situate the artifact within recent work on LLM deception, social-deduction benchmarks, and scalable oversight via debate. A three-day pilot deployment (467 initiated sessions, 415 completed, 1876 interaction turns in Italian) provides early feasibility evidence and surfaces a concrete tension: the deceptive agent carries a reliable, locally-present linguistic signature - differential helpfulness, brevity, hedging - that a simple heuristic exploits at 75.6% accuracy, yet human players achieved only 56.6%, consistent with ignoring the most diagnostic signal entirely. We discuss what this gap implies for the artifact's use as a data-collection vehicle, a teaching tool, and an evaluation harness for honesty-trained models.

</details>


### [11] [Does AI Reviewer See the Full Picture? Attacking and Defending Multimodal Peer Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12716)
*Xinyu Zhao,Rana Muhammad Shahroz Khan,Zhen Xu,Zhen Tan,Tianlong Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PaperGuard is a benchmark and defense framework for evaluating and mitigating cross-modal adversarial attacks on AI peer-review systems, where attackers manipulate both text and figures to induce domain-specific failures like score inflation. It introduces a multimodal dataset, unified attack suite (black-box prompt injection, white-box GCG/PGD perturbations), and a chunk-based embedding defense against long-context academic papers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robustness studies on AI peer-review are overwhelmingly text-only, ignoring the multimodal nature of scientific papers where figures convey core evidence. Additionally, peer-review attacks are fundamentally different from standard jailbreaking—they target domain-specific failures (e.g., score manipulation) rather than general safety policy violations, and no practical defenses exist for this threat model.

Method: The authors introduce PaperGuard with three pillars: (1) a multimodal peer-review dataset spanning multiple scientific domains; (2) a unified attack suite combining black-box prompt injections with white-box perturbations—Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) for text and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) for figures; (3) a chunk-based embedding search defense that localizes and mitigates harmful instructions by addressing the long-context challenge inherent to academic papers.

Result: Extensive experiments across state-of-the-art models confirm that AI reviewers are pervasively vulnerable to cross-modal attacks, and the proposed chunk-based embedding defense effectively mitigates these threats, establishing baseline protocols for trustworthy AI-assisted reviewing.

Conclusion: PaperGuard establishes the foundational benchmark, protocols, and actionable defense necessary to pioneer trustworthy, attack-resilient AI-assisted scholarly reviewing, addressing a critical gap at the intersection of multimodal robustness and scientific peer review.

Abstract: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) into scientific peer-review workflows introduces novel and significant risks for adversarial manipulation, especially given the multimodal nature of scientific papers where figures, not just text, convey core evidence. This creates a significant gap: current robustness studies on AI peer-review are overwhelmingly text-only. Moreover, the problem is distinct from standard jailbreaking, as a peer-review attack seeks to induce a domain-specific, targeted failure (e.g., "inflate this score") rather than a general safety policy violation, for which no practical defenses exist. To address this, we introduce PaperGuard, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to systematically evaluate and defend AI-generated peer-review against these domain-specific, cross-modal attacks. Our framework is built on three pillars: (1) a new multimodal peer-review dataset spanning multiple scientific domains; (2) a unified suite of attacks, including black-box prompt injections and white-box perturbations, specifically designed to target both text (GCG) and figures (PGD); and (3) a practical defense, motivated by the long-context challenge of academic papers, that uses chunk-based embedding search to efficiently localize and mitigate harmful instructions. Our extensive experiments, conducted across state-of-the-art models, confirm that AI reviewers are pervasively vulnerable. PaperGuard establishes the foundational benchmark, protocols, and actionable defense necessary to pioneer trustworthy, attack-resilient AI-assisted scholarly reviewing.

</details>


### [12] [Agent-based models for the evolution of morphological alternation patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12748)
*Aravinth Kulanthaivelu,Richard Sproat*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A multi-agent simulation explores how morphologically unrelated stem/inflection alternations (like "go/went") emerge and persist. The study introduces an "AI Historical Linguist" — an LLM-based evaluator that simulates debates between historical linguists — to assess the realism of evolved morphologies, finding that scale-free networks and Bernoulli adoption produce the most plausible results. Three case studies model attested historical changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Suppletive alternations like English "go/went" are cross-linguistically frequent, persist for centuries, yet seemingly offer no communicative or learning advantage. The authors aim to computationally model how such alternations emerge, spread, and become entrenched through multi-agent interaction, and to address the evaluation gap by developing a principled way to assess the realism of simulated morphologies against natural language data.

Method: The authors build a multi-agent simulation where agents possess lexicons with hundreds/thousands of entries. Agents "hear" novel forms from others and probabilistically adopt them, potentially extending the alternation to other paradigm slots sharing the original form. Phonological changes and lexical alternatives (associated with subpopulations) serve as sources of variation. The simulation supports various network topologies, diffusion patterns, and adoption policies. For evaluation, they introduce the "AI Historical Linguist," an LLM-based system that stages debates between simulated historical linguists to compare real-language morphologies, disguised morphologies, and experimentally evolved morphologies. Three case studies model attested historical changes counterfactually.

Result: Scale-free social networks and random Bernoulli adoption of novel forms produce morphologies judged most plausible by the AI Historical Linguist evaluator, compared to other network types and adoption strategies. The LLM debate system successfully discriminates between real, disguised, and simulated morphologies, providing a novel evaluation metric for computational historical linguistics.

Conclusion: Multi-agent simulation with naturalistic parameters can replicate the emergence and persistence of stem/inflection alternations. The AI Historical Linguist offers a promising, scalable method for evaluating computational models of morphological change. The findings suggest that social network structure (scale-free) and adoption strategy (Bernoulli) are key factors in producing realistic morphological systems, and the counterfactual case studies demonstrate the model's utility for testing historical linguistic hypotheses.

Abstract: Why is the past of English "go" the apparently unrelated "went"? Such alternations are frequent in languages. They neither aid communication nor learnability, yet they can be persistent, surviving over centuries or millennia.
  We present a multi-agent simulation of the emergence of morphological stem and inflection alternations. Alternate forms arise by phonological changes or, as with "go/went", from lexical alternatives associated with a subset of the population. When an agent 'hears' another agent use a novel form for a slot in the paradigm of a word (say, the past tense of go), they will with some probability adopt that form, possibly spreading its use to other slots in the paradigm that shared the same original form. Thus alternative forms can spread through the population and become entrenched as stem or inflectional marker alternants. Unlike many previous computational studies, our system allows for naturalistic lexical forms, realistic phonological rules, lexicons with hundreds or thousands of entries, and agent populations in the tens or hundreds. It supports several network topologies, diffusion patterns and agent adoption policies.
  One issue with such simulations is evaluation: how realistic is the resulting morphology compared to those of real languages? We introduce the AI Historical Linguist, a novel Large Language Model-driven system that models a debate between two historical linguists. We use this to compare a set of real language morphologies, disguised morphologies, and experimentally evolved morphologies. The results suggest that among the factors that favor more plausible morphologies are scale-free social networks and random Bernoulli adoption of forms.
  We also present three case studies modeling attested historical changes, allowing us to test what might have happened if history had been different.
  All code and data are released.

</details>


### [13] [LLMs Can Better Capture Human Judgments--With the Right Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12754)
*Danica Dillion,Chen Cecilia Liu,Baihui Wang,Daniele Barolo,Tanmay Rajore,Niket Tandon,Pranathi Ravikumar,Kurt Gray*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Simple prompting strategies—eliciting response distributions and leveraging human confusion ratings—significantly improve alignment between LLM judgments and human moral opinions, though LLMs remain poorly calibrated about their own uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are often criticized for (1) failing to capture the full distribution of human responses and (2) producing unstable judgments across wording variations. The authors aim to test whether these limitations can be mitigated through better prompting rather than assuming they are inherent flaws.

Method: The authors tested prompting strategies across two datasets: (a) 144 U.S.-representative moral scenarios and (b) 38 moral belief items from the ISSP Family and Changing Gender Roles module across 32 countries. They compared standard prompting against techniques that ask models to report standard deviations, response proportions, and human confusion ratings. Alignment was measured by comparing LLM outputs to actual human response distributions.

Result: Prompting LLMs to report standard deviations and response proportions recovered the full range of human responses better than common strategies. Incorporating human confusion ratings (and having LLMs predict them) boosted model alignment. However, LLMs' self-reported error estimates were poorly calibrated, even though they could predict human variability relatively well.

Conclusion: The findings challenge the narrative that LLMs are inherently poor at capturing human judgment. Instead, asking better-structured questions yields substantially better answers, suggesting that prompt engineering—not just model scaling—is key to improving AI-human alignment in moral and social domains.

Abstract: Are large language models (LLMs) bad at capturing human judgment? Two commonly stated limitations are that LLMs fail to capture full distributions of responses, and that their judgments are unstable across wording variations. We demonstrate simple prompting strategies that mitigate these limitations. Across two datasets--a U.S.-representative set of 144 moral scenarios and 38 moral beliefs from the International Social Survey Programme's Family and Changing Gender Roles module covering 32 countries--we show how simple elicitation techniques help improve AI-human alignment. First, prompting models to report standard deviations and response proportions recovers the full range of human responses better than common strategies. Second, ensuring scenarios are clear to human participants--as reflected in human confusion ratings--boosts model alignment, and LLMs can track human confusion ratings. At the same time, we find that LLMs' estimates of their own error are poorly calibrated, though they can predict human variability relatively well. These results suggest that asking better questions to LLMs can yield better answers.

</details>


### [14] [Rigel: Reverse-Engineering the Metal 4.1 Tensor Compute Path on the Apple M4 Max GPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12765)
*Ramchand Kumaresan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Rigel empirically reverse-engineers Apple's undocumented Metal 4.1 tensor compute path on an M4 Max, revealing that fp8 matmul2d is emulated (not hardware-accelerated), the operation runs entirely on shader cores with >=fp32 accumulation, and the cooperative_tensor fragment layout is 8x8. A hand-fused GEMM+bias+GELU kernel derived from these findings achieves +6.5–12.9% speedup in cache-resident regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Apple's Metal Performance Primitives (MPP) matmul2d via cooperative_tensor fragments is documented only at the API level; critical implementation details—hardware acceleration status, physical execution location, accumulator width, and thread-to-fragment mapping—are deliberately hidden. Understanding these is essential for performance-portable kernel design and accurate benchmarking.

Method: Rigel employs a checksum-gated, provenance-tracked microbenchmark harness. Three-signal triangulation combines throughput ceiling analysis, comparison against simdgroup_matrix behavior, and per-rail power attribution to locate execution. Throughput scaling and memory bandwidth analysis determine whether fp8 is accelerated. Empirical reverse-engineering recovers the 8×8 cooperative_tensor fragment layout. Findings are validated via a hand-fused GEMM+bias+GELU kernel.

Result: Eleven hidden or contradicted specification facts are recovered. Key results: (1) fp8 (E4M3) matmul2d sustains only 0.94× fp16 throughput despite half the operand bytes, proving it is emulated; (2) execution occurs entirely on GPU shader cores with no dedicated matrix datapath or Neural Engine involvement; (3) accumulation is ≥fp32; (4) the undocumented fragment layout is 8×8. The fused kernel outperforms the decomposed path by +6.5–12.9%.

Conclusion: On pre-neural-accelerator Apple Silicon (M4 Max), Metal 4.1's fp8 matmul2d is a memory-footprint optimization, not a compute-throughput feature. Developers should avoid assuming hardware acceleration for fp8 on such generations and may benefit from hand-fused kernels informed by Rigel's recovered microarchitectural parameters. All artifacts are open-source and reproducible.

Abstract: Apple's Metal 4.1 exposes a tensor compute path: the Metal Performance Primitives (MPP) matmul2d operation over cooperative_tensor fragments, whose interface is documented but whose hardware behavior is deliberately hidden. The specification states which data-type rows are supported, never whether they are hardware-accelerated, where the operation physically executes, what its accumulator width is, or how it partitions matrix fragments across threads. We present Rigel, an empirical characterization of this path on a single Apple M4 Max (a pre-neural-accelerator generation). Using a checksum-gated, provenance-tracked microbenchmark harness, Rigel recovers eleven facts the v4.1 specification hides or contradicts. The headline finding: the Metal 4.1 fp8 (E4M3) matmul2d is emulated, not accelerated: it sustains 0.94x the throughput of fp16 despite reading half the operand bytes, so on M4 it is a memory-footprint feature, not a performance feature. We further show, via a three-signal triangulation (throughput ceiling, comparison against simdgroup_matrix, and per-rail power attribution), that matmul2d executes entirely on the GPU shader cores with no dedicated matrix datapath and no evidence of Apple Neural Engine routing; that it accumulates in >=fp32; and we reconstruct the opaque 8x8 cooperative_tensor fragment layout Apple documents nowhere. Acting on the characterization, a hand-fused GEMM + bias + GELU kernel beats the decomposed path by +6.5-12.9% in the cache-resident regime. All findings are reproducible from committed MIT-licensed code and per-cell CSVs.

</details>


### [15] [GENIE: A Fine-Grained Measure for Novelty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12790)
*Ramya Namuduri,Manya Wadhwa,Anshun Asher Zheng,Greg Durrett,Junyi Jessy Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes GENIE, a fine-grained evaluation metric for measuring the novelty of LLM-generated responses along task-specific features, demonstrating that holistic metrics fail to capture the high-dimensional nature of novelty, and applies GENIE to evaluate the effectiveness of creativity-enhancing mitigation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs consistently lack creativity and diversity. While prior work examined whether models can generate creative outputs, little attention has been paid to understanding what specifically makes model-generated content novel (or not) in a task-dependent way. The authors aim to fill this gap by investigating the underlying features that constitute novelty.

Method: The authors propose GENIE, a fine-grained evaluation metric that measures response novelty along task-specific feature dimensions relative to a reference population of responses. GENIE decomposes novelty into constituent, interpretable features rather than relying on a single holistic score.

Result: Holistic metrics fail to capture the high-dimensional nature of novelty and provide no insight into which specific properties they target. In contrast, GENIE offers interpretable, feature-level novelty assessment. The metric is then used to evaluate creativity-targeted mitigation methods, revealing where they succeed or fall short in improving novelty.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, feature-based evaluation like GENIE is necessary for meaningful assessment of novelty in LLM outputs, and can guide the development of more effective interventions for improving model creativity.

Abstract: Large Language Models have consistently demonstrated a lack of creativity and diversity across tasks. Prior work has focused on addressing whether models are capable of generating creative outputs. Here, we aim to consider novelty and investigate what makes model-generated content novel or not novel in a task-specific manner. We propose a fine-grained evaluation metric GENIE to measure the novelty of responses along task-specific features with respect to a population of responses. We show that unlike GENIE, holistic metrics struggle to capture the high-dimensionality of novelty and do not provide insight on which properties they target. Finally, we use GENIE to measure the effectiveness of mitigation methods that address creativity to better understand where these methods can improve novelty.

</details>


### [16] [Detect, Remask, Repair: Diffusion Editing for Faithful Summarization of Evolving Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12807)
*Hao Zou,Zachary Horvitz,Chandhru Karthick,Zhou Yu,Kathleen McKeown*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes DETECT-REMASK-REPAIR, a diffusion-based framework for localized faithfulness repair of outdated text summaries. Instead of full regeneration, it identifies and repairs only unsupported spans, using masked diffusion language models. The authors introduce StreamSum, a synthetic benchmark for evolving-context summarization, and demonstrate that their approach enables controllable tradeoffs between faithfulness, speed, and content preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Summaries of real-world events become outdated as new information emerges. Full regeneration is wasteful, obscures what changed, and may be unnecessary when only a few claims lose support. The authors aim to enable localized repair — updating only outdated spans while preserving supported content — as a more efficient and transparent alternative.

Method: DETECT-REMASK-REPAIR is a three-stage diffusion-based framework: (1) DETECT identifies outdated spans using faithfulness evaluation; (2) REMASK masks those spans; (3) REPAIR uses a masked diffusion language model (MDLM) to infill the masked regions conditioned on updated context. The framework supports both iterative and one-step repair, and can serve as a post-hoc correction for autoregressive systems. Evaluation uses DialogSum and the newly introduced StreamSum benchmark of synthetic event timelines.

Result: Faithfulness-steered repair improves early drafts; one-step repair reduces repair cost to under half a second. The framework enables controllable faithfulness-speed-preservation tradeoffs across datasets. It also provides effective post-hoc faithfulness correction for autoregressive summarization systems.

Conclusion: Localized diffusion-based repair offers a controllable, efficient alternative to full rewriting for updating summaries under evolving contexts, with practical benefits in speed, faithfulness, and content preservation.

Abstract: Summaries of real-world events can become outdated as contexts evolve and new information arrives. A common response is to generate a new summary from the updated context, but full regeneration discards the previous draft, can obscure what changed, and may be unnecessary when only a few claims are unsupported. We study localized faithfulness repair: updating outdated spans in an existing summary while preserving supported content. We propose DETECT-REMASK-REPAIR, a diffusion-based framework that identifies, remasks, and repairs outdated regions with masked diffusion language models. To evaluate evolving-context summarization, we introduce StreamSum, a benchmark of synthetic event timelines. Experiments on DialogSum and StreamSum show that localized diffusion repair provides a controllable alternative to full rewriting: faithfulness-steered repair improves early drafts, one-step repair reduces repair cost to under half a second, with the framework enabling faithfulness-speed-preservation tradeoffs across datasets. We also find that the framework can provide a post-hoc correction step that improves faithfulness for autoregressive systems.

</details>


### [17] [Localizing Anchoring Pathways in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12818)
*Hillary N. Owusu,Sarah Wiegreffe,Naomi H. Feldman*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Language models exhibit anchoring bias where irrelevant numbers skew judgments. Using logit-difference metrics and circuit analysis on Qwen/Llama models, the authors show edge-level attribution faithfully localizes anchor-sensitive signals, with circuits transferring across anchor directions but not reliably across base vs. instruction-tuned variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mechanistically understand where and how anchor-sensitive signals propagate inside language models during numerical reasoning tasks, and to determine whether these circuits are shared across different anchors and model variants.

Method: Authors use a controlled multiple-choice numerical reasoning setup with fixed answer options. They define a logit-difference metric (correct answer logit minus anchor-corresponding answer logit) to track anchoring. They apply attribution-based circuit localization with both node-level and edge-level patching methods on 7B-8B Qwen and Llama models (both base and instruction-tuned), then test circuit transferability across low/high anchors and across model variants.

Result: Edge-level attribution methods recover anchor-sensitive signals more faithfully than node-level methods. Low- and high-anchor circuits transfer strongly within the same model, indicating shared pathway structure across anchor directions. However, sparse transfer between base and instruction-tuned variants is less reliable, suggesting post-training alters which pathways are most relevant.

Conclusion: The paper provides a mechanistic account of anchoring effects in language models, demonstrating that anchor-related decision signals are carried through specific, localizable circuits that are largely direction-invariant but sensitive to post-training modifications.

Abstract: Irrelevant numbers in a prompt can shift language model judgments, producing anchoring effects in numerical reasoning. We study where this anchor-sensitive signal is carried inside language models using a controlled multiple-choice setup with shared answer options. We define a logit-difference metric comparing the correct answer option with the answer option corresponding to the anchor, and validate that it tracks behavioral anchoring. Using attribution-based circuit localization on 7B--8B Qwen and Llama base and instruction-tuned models, we find that edge-level methods recover this signal more faithfully than node-level methods. Low- and high-anchor circuits transfer strongly within a model, suggesting shared pathway structure across anchor direction. However, sparse transfer across base and instruction-tuned variants is less reliable, indicating that post-training changes which pathways matter most. Overall, our results provide a mechanistic account of how anchoring-related decision signals are carried inside language models.

</details>


### [18] [LoHoSearch: Benchmarking Long-Horizon Search Agents Beyond the Human Difficulty Ceiling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12837)
*Jiarui Zhao,Rongzhi Zhang,Lingchuan Liu,Hao Yang,Xunliang Cai,Xi Su*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LoHoSearch is a new benchmark for long-horizon search agents, constructed via an automated knowledge-graph pipeline covering 7M+ Wikipedia entities. It features 544 human-verified questions with structurally complex, large-search-space queries. The strongest models achieve only 34.74% accuracy, and context management strategies provide minimal gains, establishing a more demanding evaluation standard than saturated benchmarks like BrowseComp.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing search agent benchmarks (e.g., BrowseComp) have saturated rapidly, with top models exceeding 90% accuracy. These benchmarks are human-authored, meaning annotators cannot systematically maximize search space size or structural complexity due to limited global perspective on entity statistics. This creates a difficulty ceiling that is hard to break, motivating the need for a benchmark that systematically generates harder, long-horizon search tasks.

Method: LoHoSearch is constructed via an automated pipeline built on a knowledge graph of 7+ million Wikipedia entities. The pipeline selects relations with inherently large search spaces and assembles them into structurally complex questions. Each question is verified against the KG to ensure a unique answer, and all 544 questions across 11 domains are human-verified for quality and correctness.

Result: The strongest evaluated model achieves only 34.74% accuracy on LoHoSearch. Existing context management strategies yield only a +6.8% improvement, far smaller gains than observed on prior benchmarks, indicating that current techniques are insufficient for long-horizon reasoning tasks with large search spaces.

Conclusion: LoHoSearch provides a significantly more demanding standard for evaluating long-horizon reasoning and context management in search agents, addressing the saturation problem of existing benchmarks and exposing limitations in current state-of-the-art models and strategies.

Abstract: Search agent benchmarks exemplified by BrowseComp have rapidly saturated over the past year, with the strongest models surpassing 90% accuracy. Since these benchmarks are predominantly human-authored, annotators lack a global perspective on entity statistics and cannot systematically maximize search space size and structural complexity. This creates a difficulty ceiling that is hard to break. To address this, we introduce LoHoSearch (Long-Horizon Search Agents), a challenging benchmark comprising 544 human-verified questions across 11 domains. LoHoSearch is constructed via an automated pipeline built upon a knowledge graph covering over 7 million Wikipedia entities, which selects relations with large search spaces and assembles them into structurally complex questions with KG-verified unique answers. Our evaluation demonstrates that even the strongest model achieves only 34.74% accuracy, and existing context management strategies (best +6.8%) yield far smaller gains than on prior benchmarks. LoHoSearch provides a more demanding standard for evaluating long-horizon reasoning and context management in search agents.

</details>


### [19] [Small LLMs for Biomedical Claim Verification: Cost-Effective Fine-Tuning, Structural Dataset Shortcuts, and Cross-Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12854)
*Gaurav Kumar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Small LLMs (3.8B–7B) fine-tuned with QLoRA on only 1,008 examples outperform GPT-4o and GPT-5 on biomedical claim verification at a fraction of the cost, while revealing a structural artifact in the SciFact dataset that inflates in-domain performance and demonstrating that structurally clean training data enables robust cross-domain transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large proprietary LLMs like GPT-4o/5 achieve strong zero-shot biomedical claim verification but suffer from high cost and opacity, limiting scalable, reproducible use. The authors aim to determine whether small open-weight LLMs fine-tuned with parameter-efficient QLoRA can match or exceed this performance, while also investigating the role of dataset structure in cross-domain generalization.

Method: The authors fine-tune three small LLMs—Phi-3-mini (3.8B), Qwen2.5-3B, and Mistral-7B—using QLoRA on the SciFact and HealthVer datasets (1,008 training examples). They benchmark these against GPT-4o, GPT-5, and fine-tuned BioLinkBERT encoders. Evaluation includes in-domain testing and bidirectional cross-domain evaluation (SciFact→HealthVer and HealthVer→SciFact) at matched model sizes to disentangle dataset structure from data quantity effects.

Result: Mistral-7B QLoRA surpasses both GPT-4o and GPT-5 with up to 12% F1 gain. The study identifies a previously unreported structural artifact in SciFact that inflates in-domain scores. Bidirectional out-of-domain evaluation shows that models trained on structurally sound data (HealthVer) achieve robust cross-domain transfer, whereas SciFact-trained models do not generalize well.

Conclusion: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of small open LLMs offers a cost-effective, transparent, and high-performing alternative to large proprietary models for biomedical claim verification. Dataset structure, beyond size, critically impacts generalization, and the authors commit to releasing all code and adapter checkpoints to promote reproducibility.

Abstract: Large Language Models such as GPT-4o and GPT-5 achieve strong zero-shot performance on biomedical claim verification, but cost and opacity limit scalable use. We fine-tune three small LLMs: Phi-3-mini (3.8B), Qwen2.5-3B, and Mistral-7B, via QLoRA on SciFact and HealthVer, providing the first study of QLoRA models against GPT-4o and fine-tuned BioLinkBERT encoders. Mistral-7B QLoRA surpasses both GPT-4o and GPT-5 (up to 12% F1 gain) at a fractional cost using just 1,008 training examples. We conduct extensive in-domain and cross-domain evaluation: models trained on SciFact tested on HealthVer and vice versa, at matched sizes to isolate dataset structure from data quantity. We identify a previously unreported structural artifact in SciFact that inflates in-domain scores, and show through bidirectional out-of-domain evaluation that training on structurally sound data enables robust cross-domain transfer. We plan to release all code and adapter checkpoints.

</details>


### [20] [Direct Preference Optimization for Chatbot Fine-Tuning: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12881)
*Yvonne Qiu,Dezhi Yu,ShuoJia Fu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) approach for fine-tuning LLMs, showing it simplifies training, improves efficiency, and achieves competitive results, though training instability remains a concern.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the complexity and computational overhead of traditional RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) pipelines by leveraging DPO, which bypasses the need for a separate reward model.

Method: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) — a reinforcement learning technique that directly optimizes the language model against human preference data without requiring an explicit reward model, unlike standard RLHF.

Result: DPO simplifies the training pipeline and improves computational efficiency. Evaluation using BLEU, ROUGE, and cosine similarity metrics shows effective learning and convergence, with competitive performance compared to baselines. However, training instability was observed.

Conclusion: DPO is a viable and efficient alternative to traditional RLHF for LLM fine-tuning, but further work is required to mitigate training instability issues.

Abstract: We present an approach to fine-tuning large language models using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a reinforcement learning technique. Our experimental results demonstrate that DPO simplifies the training pipeline, improves computational efficiency, and achieves competitive performance. The evaluation using BLEU, ROUGE, and cosine similarity metrics indicates effective learning and convergence, though further investigation is needed to address observed training instability.

</details>


### [21] [SafeLLM: Extraction as a Hallucination-Resistant Alternative to Rewriting in Safety-Critical Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12897)
*Julia Ive,Felix Jozsa,Evridiki Georgaki,Nabeel Sheikh,Emma Cattell,Nick Jackson,Paulina Bondaronek,Ciaran Scott Hill,Richard Dobson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates extraction-based strategies as hallucination-resistant alternatives to rewriting-based RAG for LLM-driven access to safety-critical organizational documents. Line-number-based source selection achieves the strongest results (up to 95% term recall), outperforming extraction with safety annotations and multi-stage refinement pipelines. Safety-oriented approaches improve precision but introduce omissions, and performance varies with document structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed to access organizational documentation (SOPs, HR policies, clinical guidelines) via RAG systems, but free-form rewriting introduces hallucinations and unstable precision-recall trade-offs — risks that are unacceptable in safety- and compliance-critical settings. The authors seek a hallucination-resistant alternative to rewriting-based RAG.

Method: Three prompting strategies are compared: (1) line-number-based source selection, (2) extraction of relevant guideline sentences with explicit safety annotations, and (3) a multi-stage pipeline refining draft answers using supporting evidence. Experiments span local NHS acute care/oncology guidelines and UK-wide NICE guidelines, using both frontier-scale and locally deployable models. Evaluation uses automatic metrics and human expert assessment of relevance and completeness.

Result: Line-number selection consistently outperforms direct copying and safety-focused strategies across model scales, achieving up to 95% term recall with close source alignment. Safety-oriented approaches improve precision but cause systematic omissions; multi-stage filtering amplifies this precision-omission trade-off. Line-based extraction excels on protocol-like documents, while alternatives reach up to 97% term recall on verbose documents.

Conclusion: Extraction-based approaches, particularly line-number selection, offer a viable hallucination-resistant alternative to rewriting-based RAG for safety-critical document retrieval. However, no single strategy is optimal across all document types — deployment choices should be guided by document structure and the precision/recall requirements of the use case.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to access organisational documentation, including standard operating procedures (SOPs), HR policies and institutional guidelines. However, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems that rely on free-form rewriting can introduce hallucinations and unstable trade-offs between completeness and conciseness, particularly in safety- and compliance-critical settings. Objectives: To evaluate extraction as a hallucination-resistant alternative to rewriting-based RAG and compare strategies that balance precision, recall and safety across document types and model scales. Methods: We compare multiple prompting strategies, including line-number-based source selection, extraction of relevant guideline sentences with explicit safety annotations, and a multi-stage pipeline that refines draft answers using supporting evidence from source guidelines. Experiments are conducted on documents of varying length and structure, including local NHS acute care and oncology guidelines and UK-wide NICE guidelines, using both frontier-scale and locally deployable models. Performance is assessed using automatic metrics and human expert evaluation of relevance and completeness. Results: Line-number selection achieves the strongest results, outperforming direct copying and safety-focused strategies across both large and small models while maintaining high term recall (up to 95%) and close alignment with source text. Safety-oriented approaches improve precision but introduce systematic omissions, while multi-stage filtering further amplifies this trade-off. Performance varies with document structure: line-based extraction excels in protocol-like content, whereas alternative strategies perform better on more verbose documents (up to 97% term recall).

</details>


### [22] [PRISM: Prosody-Integrated Multi-Agent Reasoning Framework for Empathetic Spoken Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12902)
*Wen Zhang,Xiaocui Yang,Zhuoyue Gao,Shi Feng,Daling Wang,Yifei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PRISM is a multi-agent framework for empathetic spoken dialogue that decouples speech perception, response generation, and speech synthesis, using a prosody-to-language translation mechanism and on-demand knowledge tools to improve empathy, prosodic appropriateness, and response quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing empathetic spoken dialogue systems face two key limitations: (1) cascade pipelines lose acoustic/prosodic cues during speech-to-text conversion, and (2) end-to-end speech models lack interpretable control over emotion and knowledge integration. PRISM addresses both gaps.

Method: PRISM is a multi-agent framework with three decoupled components: speech perception, response generation, and speech synthesis. Key innovations include: (a) a prosody-to-language translation mechanism that converts prosodic features into a format that stabilizes LLM reasoning, and (b) on-demand invocation of external knowledge tools to support empathetic dialogue generation.

Result: Experimental results show consistent improvements across objective and subjective metrics in three areas: empathy, prosodic appropriateness, and text response generation quality.

Conclusion: PRISM demonstrates that decoupling spoken dialogue into coordinated components with explicit prosody translation and knowledge tool integration yields superior empathetic spoken dialogue performance compared to existing cascade and end-to-end approaches.

Abstract: Empathetic spoken dialogue systems require not only semantically appropriate responses but also emotionally aligned prosodic expression. However, cascade pipelines often discard acoustic cues during speech-to-text conversion, while end-to-end speech models lack interpretable control over emotion and knowledge integration. To address these challenges, we propose PRISM, a multi-agent framework for empathetic spoken dialogue that decouples speech perception, response generation, and speech synthesis into coordinated components. PRISM introduces a prosody-to-language translation mechanism to stabilize large language model reasoning and enables on-demand invocation of external knowledge tools for empathetic dialogue generation. Experimental results demonstrate that PRISM achieves consistent improvements in empathy, prosodic appropriateness, and text response generation quality across objective and subjective metrics. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Bxzfrm/PRISM.

</details>


### [23] [X-MADAM-RAG: Diagnosing and Handling Chinese-English Evidence Conflict in Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12903)
*Yongqi Kang,Yu Fu,Yong Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces X-RAMDocs-ZHEN, a controlled Chinese-English benchmark for diagnosing evidence conflict in multilingual RAG, and X-MADAM-RAG, an interpretable pipeline for conflict-aware answer aggregation. While X-MADAM-RAG achieves near-perfect accuracy on templated data, a stress test removing templates causes both the proposed method and baselines to collapse, revealing that document-level extraction — not conflict resolution — is the primary bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems in multilingual settings frequently encounter mutually contradictory evidence across languages (e.g., Chinese vs. English documents supporting different answers). Existing benchmarks and methods do not systematically diagnose how models handle such cross-lingual evidence conflict, motivating a controlled diagnostic framework.

Method: The authors construct X-RAMDocs-ZHEN (300 examples, 6 conditions: monolingual support, bilingual agreement, reversed conflict, conflict+noise). They propose X-MADAM-RAG, a pipeline with four stages: (1) per-document candidate extraction, (2) visible-evidence repair, (3) deterministic candidate grouping, and (4) conflict-aware aggregation. They also evaluate a zero-call rule-only extractor and construct a naturalized stress test that removes answer templates while preserving candidate strings.

Result: X-MADAM-RAG with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct achieves 0.9667 strict accuracy and 0.9767 conflict-aware success on the controlled benchmark. A rule-only extractor hits 1.0000, exposing template regularity. On the naturalized stress test (100 samples), rule-only extraction drops to 0.0000 and X-MADAM-RAG falls to 0.3000, below naive and evidence-normalized baselines. A privileged oracle remains perfect, confirming document-level extraction as the bottleneck.

Conclusion: X-RAMDocs-ZHEN and X-MADAM-RAG serve as diagnostic tools for controlled evidence conflict rather than general hallucination detection or robustness solutions. The work reveals that template overfitting and extraction failures — not conflict resolution — dominate performance degradation, highlighting the need for benchmarks that disentangle these factors.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems may receive evidence that is not merely noisy but mutually contradictory. This issue becomes particularly salient in multilingual settings, where retrieved Chinese and English evidence may support incompatible answer candidates. We study this problem through X-RAMDocs-ZHEN, a controlled Chinese-English benchmark derived from RAMDocs for diagnosing evidence conflict in RAG. The benchmark contains 300 examples across six balanced conditions, including monolingual support, bilingual agreement, reversed conflict directions, and conflict with optional noise. We further examine X-MADAM-RAG, an interpretable pipeline that decomposes evidence handling into per-document candidate extraction, visible-evidence repair, deterministic candidate grouping, and conflict-aware aggregation. On the original controlled benchmark with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, X-MADAM-RAG achieves 0.9667 strict accuracy and 0.9767 conflict-aware success, outperforming an evidence-normalized single-call baseline. However, a zero-call rule-only extractor reaches 1.0000 on the same benchmark, revealing strong template regularity. To probe this limitation, we construct a deterministic naturalized stress test that removes explicit answer templates while preserving candidate strings.
