On its 100-sample subset, rule-only extraction falls to 0.0000, but X-MADAM-RAG also drops to 0.3000 strict accuracy, below both naive and evidence-normalized baselines. A privileged oracle remains perfect, indicating that document-level extraction is the main bottleneck. These findings position X-RAMDocs-ZHEN and X-MADAM-RAG as diagnostic tools for controlled evidence conflict rather than as evidence of general hallucination detection or robustness to natural retrieval.

</details>


### [24] [SENTINEL: Failure-Driven Reinforcement Learning for Training Tool-Using Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12908)
*Ziyi Wang,Yuxuan Lu,Yimeng Zhang,Qun Liu,Chen Luo,Jiri Gesi,Hanqing Lu,Yisi Sang,Manling Li,Jing Huang,Dakuo Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SENTINEL is a failure-driven RL framework that converts an agent's rollout failures into targeted training tasks via a Controller–Proposer–Solver loop, improving tool-using LM agents more efficiently than training on fixed or general synthetic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL for training tool-using LM agents uses a fixed task distribution, which becomes increasingly mismatched with the agent's evolving policy—resulting in many uninformative rollouts. There is a need for a training paradigm that dynamically adapts tasks to the agent's current weaknesses.

Method: SENTINEL operates in a three-component loop: (1) the Controller analyzes failed trajectories and identifies recurring error patterns; (2) the Proposer generates executable, targeted tasks that stress these weaknesses; (3) the Solver (the agent) is trained via RL on these failure-driven tasks. This creates an adaptive curriculum that evolves with the policy.

Result: On Tau2-Bench Retail with Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, SENTINEL improved Pass^1 from 66.4 to 74.9 and outperformed RL on general synthetic tasks across Pass^k metrics.

Conclusion: Model failures provide an effective, scalable source of targeted training signal. Dynamically generating training tasks from error patterns yields better improvement than training on static or generic task distributions.

Abstract: Language model agents are increasingly effective in solving realistic tasks through multi-turn tool use. However, training reliable tool-using agents remains challenging in practice. While reinforcement learning provides an on-policy paradigm for improving agents from their own environment interactions, its effectiveness depends heavily on the training task distribution. When tasks are fixed before training, the task distribution can become increasingly mismatched with the policy's evolving capabilities, causing many rollouts to be spent on uninformative tasks. We propose SENTINEL, a failure-driven reinforcement learning framework that turns the Solver's rollout failures into targeted training tasks. SENTINEL follows a Controller--Proposer--Solver loop: the Controller analyzes failed trajectories and summarizes recurring error patterns, the Proposer generates executable tasks that stress these weaknesses, and the Solver is trained on the targeted tasks. On Tau2-Bench Retail with Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, SENTINEL improves Pass\^{}1 from 66.4 to 74.9 and outperforms RL on general synthetic tasks across Pass\^{}k metrics. These results demonstrate that model failures provide an effective and scalable source of targeted training signal for improving tool-using language model agents.

</details>


### [25] [PiDA: Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation for Robust Vietnamese Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12911)
*Giang Son Nguyen,Tung X. Nguyen,Hieu Minh Truong,Nhu Vo,Wray Buntine,Dung D. Le*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper systematically categorizes ASR phonetic errors in Vietnamese speech translation, quantifies their impact on NMT via linear mixed-effects modeling, and proposes Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation (PiDA) to improve robustness, achieving up to +2.04 BLEU on erroneous ASR outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cascaded ST systems propagate ASR errors to downstream NMT, yet no prior work systematically analyzes the phonetic causes of ASR errors in Vietnamese or designs targeted data augmentation to mitigate their impact on translation quality.

Method: The authors (1) manually categorize Vietnamese ASR substitution errors by phonetic cause, (2) use Linear Mixed-Effects Modelling to quantify the impact of phonetic errors on NMT performance, and (3) propose PiDA — a data augmentation technique that corrupts clean training text by substituting words with phonetically similar alternatives using phonetic word embeddings, then fine-tune NMT models on the augmented data.

Result: Phonetic confusions (not random noise) dominate ASR substitution errors and significantly degrade ST quality. Fine-tuning on PiDA-augmented FLEURS Vietnamese-English data improves BLEU by up to +2.04 on erroneous ASR outputs compared to standard fine-tuning, with a slight improvement on clean text as well.

Conclusion: Phonetically-informed data augmentation effectively robustifies NMT models against ASR errors in cascaded ST, addressing a systematic weakness rather than merely treating noise as random.

Abstract: Cascaded speech translation (ST) systems suffer from error propagation when Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) outputs incorrect transcripts. We present the first systematic categorization of ASR errors for Vietnamese ST, classifying substitution errors by phonetic cause and quantifying their impact on downstream Neural Machine Translation (NMT) performance using Linear Mixed-Effects Modelling. We confirm that most ASR substitution errors arise from phonetic confusions rather than random noise, and that these phonetic errors significantly degrade ST quality. Motivated by this finding, we propose Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation (PiDA), which generates ASR-like corruptions by substituting words with phonetically similar alternatives using phonetic word embeddings. Fine-tuning on a PiDA-augmented version of FLEURS Vietnamese-English improves translation of erroneous ASR outputs (up to +2.04 BLEU over standard fine-tuning) while also slightly improving clean-text performance.

</details>


### [26] [Polar: A Benchmark for Evaluating Political Bias in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12922)
*Sangho Kim,Heejin Kim,Yoonhee Park,Hyunggeun Jeon,Jaejin Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Polar is a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark measuring LLM political bias via option likelihoods across U.S. and South Korean contexts. Testing 38 LLMs reveals systematic bias variation by context, issue, and language; all models lean left-progressive on U.S. content but show centered patterns on South Korean content, with presentation language alone shifting measured bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Political bias in LLMs is increasingly consequential yet difficult to measure reproducibly across different political systems and languages. Existing approaches rely heavily on prompt-based generation, which introduces confounding factors. There is a need for a robust, cross-contextual, multilingual evaluation framework that can isolate and quantify political bias systematically.

Method: The authors introduce Polar, a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark grounded in the Manifesto Project's coding scheme. Bias is measured through option-level likelihoods (token probabilities) rather than generated text, reducing confounds. The benchmark spans two ideological axes and eight issue categories, with parallel evaluation across U.S. and South Korean political contexts in multiple languages. Translation experiments manipulate presentation language to isolate its effect.

Result: Across 38 LLMs, all models lean left-progressive on U.S. political content. On South Korean content, models display more centered and ideologically mixed patterns. Bias varies systematically with political context, issue category, model group, and presentation language. Translation experiments demonstrate that changing only the presentation language can significantly shift measured political bias.

Conclusion: LLM political bias is not monolithic but context-dependent, varying across political systems, issues, and languages. The findings underscore the importance of multilingual, cross-contextual evaluation frameworks like Polar for meaningful bias assessment, challenging the assumption that bias measured in one context generalizes to others.

Abstract: Political bias in large language models (LLMs) is increasingly significant, but difficult to measure reproducibly across political and linguistic contexts. We introduce Polar, a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark that measures political bias through option-level likelihoods rather than prompt-based generation. Polar covers two ideological axes and eight issue categories derived from the Manifesto Project, and evaluates models in parallel across U.S. and South Korean political contexts. Across 38 LLMs, measured bias varies systematically with political context, issue category, model group, and presentation language. All models lean left-progressive on U.S. political content, but show more centered and mixed patterns on South Korean content. Translation experiments further show that presentation language alone can shift measured bias. These findings highlight the need for multilingual and cross-contextual evaluation of political bias in LLMs.

</details>


### [27] [Multi-Turn Reasoning When Context Arrives in Pieces: Scalable Sharding and Memory-Augmented RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12941)
*Shu Tong Luo,Wenqin Liu,Rui Liu,Mingming Gong,Jiaxian Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LLMs suffer up to 65% accuracy degradation when information is fragmented across conversation turns ("Lost in Conversation"). The authors propose training models with a compact rolling memory instead of full history attention, using a sharding pipeline to convert single-turn QA into multi-turn episodes. Memory-trained models recover multi-turn accuracy, generalize zero-shot to harder tasks, and outperform full-history baselines even with full context at test time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit severe performance degradation (up to 65% accuracy drop) when task-critical information is distributed across multiple conversation turns, even though the full context is technically available. This "Lost in Conversation" phenomenon reveals a fundamental weakness in how models attend to growing histories. The goal is to mitigate this without expensive manual annotation of multi-turn data.

Method: (1) A low-cost sharding pipeline that automatically fragments single-turn QA datasets (like GSM8K) into multi-turn episodes by splitting critical reasoning steps across conversation turns. (2) Training models to maintain a compact rolling memory — a compressed representation that summarizes prior context — rather than attending to the full growing conversation history. This memory-augmented policy is trained solely on the sharded data.

Result: Memory-augmented training significantly improves multi-turn accuracy on sharded GSM8K. The approach generalizes zero-shot to harder math problems and out-of-domain long-context QA, demonstrating that the learned compression strategy transfers. Notably, memory-trained models outperform full-history baselines even when the full history is provided at test time, indicating that learning to compress fosters more robust incremental reasoning than full-context exposure.

Conclusion: The "Lost in Conversation" phenomenon is a significant vulnerability in LLMs that can be effectively addressed by training models to maintain compact rolling memories. The sharding pipeline makes this training scalable by eliminating manual annotation. Critically, the benefits extend beyond the training distribution, suggesting that compression-based incremental reasoning is a more robust paradigm than full-context attention for multi-turn settings.

Abstract: When a user reveals task-critical information across several conversation turns, LLM accuracy drops by up to 65% despite full context availability. We show that this Lost in Conversation degradation can be substantially mitigated by training models to maintain a compact rolling memory instead of attending to a growing history. To make such training scalable, we introduce a low-cost sharding pipeline that converts single-turn QA datasets into multi-turn fragmented-information episodes, eliminating the need for hours of manual annotation. Training only on sharded GSM8K, our memory-augmented policy significantly improves multi-turn accuracy and generalises zero-shot to harder math and out-of-domain long-context QA. Moreover, memory-trained models outperform full-history baselines even when given the full history at test time, suggesting that learning to compress induces more robust incremental reasoning than full-context exposure alone.

</details>


### [28] [SkillChain: Closing the Loop on Skill Evolution for Image-Based E-Commerce AI Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12984)
*Yimin Hu,Mengtao Xu,Hao Guo,Yuheng Song,Xiaoyong Zhu,Bo Zheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkillChain automates the lifecycle of Skills for production-scale image-based AI assistants on e-commerce platforms, using three stages — Skill Creator, Route Optimizer, and Body Refiner — to handle heterogeneous user intents. It improves response quality, user engagement, content consumption, and retention in online A/B tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Image-based AI assistants in e-commerce must handle diverse user intents (product search, style recommendation, visual encyclopedia, utility tools) from a single image upload. LLM-based systems conflate these modes without per-intent behavioral constraints, leading to subpar domain quality. The breadth and dynamism of the intent space render manual engineering infeasible, necessitating an automated Skill lifecycle management system.

Method: SkillChain closes the production feedback loop on Skill evolution through three automated stages: (1) Skill Creator bootstraps Skills from task specifications and interaction trajectories; (2) Route Optimizer aligns intent routing to the appropriate Skill; (3) Body Refiner iteratively refines Skill bodies using dual-path LLM-Judge evaluation, leveraging production feedback signals for continuous improvement.

Result: Deployed on a production-scale e-commerce image assistant, SkillChain substantially improves aggregate response quality, with the strongest gains observed in structural compliance and content quality. A one-week online A/B experiment confirms significant gains in user engagement, content consumption, and long-term retention.

Conclusion: SkillChain provides an effective, automated solution for managing heterogeneous Skills in production image-based AI assistants, overcoming the limitations of manual engineering and delivering measurable improvements in both offline quality metrics and online business metrics.

Abstract: Image-based AI assistants are now deployed at production scale on e-commerce platforms, where a single uploaded image can trigger fundamentally different user intents: product search, style recommendation, visual encyclopedia, or utility tool calls, each demanding its own response format, tool invocation, and domain knowledge. Without per-intent behavioral constraints, LLM-based systems conflate these heterogeneous modes and fall short of domain quality standards, while the breadth and dynamism of the intent space render manual engineering infeasible. To address this, we present SkillChain, which closes the production feedback loop on Skill evolution, automating the lifecycle of Skills through three stages: Skill Creator for bootstrapping from task specs and trajectories, Route Optimizer for routing alignment, and Body Refiner for iterative Skill Body refinement via dual-path LLM-Judge evaluation. Deployed on a production-scale e-commerce image assistant, SkillChain substantially improves aggregate response quality, with the strongest gains on structural compliance and content quality; a one-week online A/B experiment further confirms significant gains in user engagement, content consumption, and long-term retention.

</details>


### [29] [No Hidden Prompts Needed! You Can Game AI Peer Review with Presentation-Only Revisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13044)
*Xu Yang,Zhizhou Sha,Junbo Li,Jian Yu,Yifan Sun,Matthew Zhao,Jinrui Fang,Xinyue Guo,Yining Wu,Xu Hu,Yifu Luo,Qiang Liu,Zhangyang Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces "adversarial repackaging," a novel attack on AI reviewers where only presentation-level content (abstract, framing, related work, narrative) is modified while all scientific evidence remains intact. Across three AI reviewers, the attack achieves 75.1% success with a +1.21/10 mean score gain. The study reveals that AI reviewers are easier to impress than convince, and can confuse the appearance of addressing limitations with actually resolving them, exposing structural vulnerabilities beyond prompt injection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robustness research on AI-generated peer review focuses primarily on explicit attacks (hidden instructions, prompt injection). The authors identify a more policy-relevant and harder-to-detect threat: that the presentation of scientific work, rather than its substance, can be optimized to manipulate AI reviewer scores — raising concerns for the deployment of AI in real peer-review infrastructure.

Method: The authors propose adversarial repackaging: a closed-loop attack that uses AI-reviewer feedback to iteratively search for presentation-level revisions (abstract, contribution framing, related work positioning, discussion narrative) while holding all scientific evidence (methods, experiments, figures, equations, proofs, numerical results) constant. The attack is tested across three mainstream AI reviewers, and various revision strategies (related-work repositioning, analytical discussion expansion, surface-level polishing, table formatting, algorithm boxes) are systematically compared.

Result: Adversarial repackaging achieves a 75.1% attack success rate and a mean score improvement of +1.21 points on a 10-point scale across three AI reviewers. Strategic changes affecting reviewer interpretation (related-work repositioning, discussion expansion) substantially outperform surface edits (local polishing, formatting). Two structural failure modes emerge: (1) highlighting strengths reliably boosts scores while weakness mitigation frequently backfires, and (2) AI reviewers conflate the appearance of addressing limitations with genuine resolution.

Conclusion: The deployment risk of AI reviewers extends beyond malicious hidden instructions to the emergence of paper presentation as an optimization surface. The authors release a contamination-free rolling benchmark and attack framework for testing whether AI reviewers remain anchored to scientific content under presentation-only edits.

Abstract: As AI-generated reviews move from experimental tools into peer-review infrastructure, most robustness concerns have focused on explicit attacks such as hidden instructions and prompt injection. We study a harder and more policy-relevant failure mode: no hidden text, no prompt injection, and no changes to methods, experiments, figures, equations, proofs, or numerical results. The attacker modifies only presentation-level content, such as the abstract, contribution framing, related work, discussion, and narrative structure. We introduce adversarial repackaging: a closed-loop attack that uses AI-reviewer feedback to search for presentation-level revisions while keeping the scientific evidence fixed. Across three mainstream AI reviewers, adversarial repackaging achieves a 75.1% attack success rate and a mean score gain of +1.21/10. The effect is not explained by ordinary prose polishing. We also reveal that strategies that change how the reviewer interprets the paper, such as related-work repositioning and analytical discussion expansion, substantially outperform surface edits such as local polishing, table formatting, and algorithm boxes.
  Our analysis reveals two deeper structural failure modes. First, AI reviewers are easier to impress than to convince: highlighting strengths reliably increases perceived merit, while attempts to dissolve weaknesses frequently backfire. Second, AI reviewers can confuse the appearance of addressing a limitation with actually resolving it, allowing unchanged evidence to be reinterpreted as stronger scientific contribution. These results show that the deployment risk is not only malicious hidden instructions, but the emergence of paper presentation itself as an optimization surface. We release a contamination-free rolling benchmark and attack framework for testing whether AI reviewers remain anchored to scientific content under presentation-only edits.

</details>


### [30] [sebis at CRF Filling 2026: A Two-Stage Local LLM Pipeline for Medical CRF Filling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13082)
*Katharina Sommer,Tristan Till,Florian Matthes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A privacy-preserving, on-premise pipeline using MedGemma-27B with a two-stage architecture (binary classification + value extraction) achieves a macro-F1 of 0.55 on the CL4Health 2026 CRF task, securing second place among open-source local submissions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extracting structured clinical data from unstructured EHR notes is a critical bottleneck. While LLMs perform well, their clinical deployment is limited by privacy concerns, high inference costs, and hallucination beyond textual evidence. The authors aim to address these by building a fully local, domain-adapted system that enforces strict textual adherence without external API calls.

Method: A two-stage pipeline using the MedGemma-27B model. Stage one performs binary presence classification for each clinical item. Stage two performs value extraction only for items flagged as present. Both stages use item-specific, few-shot in-context learning with carefully designed prompts that enforce deterministic outputs for negated, uncertain, or unknown states. No fine-tuning or external API calls are used.

Result: The pipeline achieved a macro-F1 score of 0.55 on the official English test track of CL4Health 2026, securing second place among all locally-hosted, open-source submissions.

Conclusion: Privacy-preserving, on-premise LLM pipelines can achieve near-competitive performance with proprietary frontier models, offering a practical, data-sovereign framework for clinical NLP. The two-stage architecture effectively constrains hallucination and enforces textual evidence adherence.

Abstract: The extraction of structured clinical information from unstructured EHR notes is a persistent bottleneck in healthcare informatics. While large language models (LLMs) offer high performance, their deployment in clinical settings is hindered by privacy risks, inference costs, and the tendency to hallucinate beyond textual evidence. We address these challenges for the CL4Health 2026 Case Report Form (CRF) filling task by proposing a fully local, domain-adapted pipeline using the MedGemma-27B model. Our two-stage architecture, which separates binary presence classification from value extraction, enforces strict adherence to textual evidence and ensures deterministic outputs for negated, uncertain, or unknown states. By leveraging item-specific, few-shot in-context learning without external API calls or fine-tuning, our approach achieves a macro-F1 score of 0.55 on the official English test track. This result secures second place among all locally-hosted, open-source submissions. Our work demonstrates that privacy-preserving, on-premise LLM pipelines can achieve near-competitive performance with proprietary frontier models, providing a practical, data-sovereign framework for clinical NLP.

</details>


### [31] [LEDGER: A Long-Context Benchmark of Corporate Annual Reports for Grounded Financial Retrieval and Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13100)
*Charles Moslonka,Amaury de Vitry,Arthur Garnier,Hicham Randrianarivo,Emmanuel Malherbe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LEDGER is a corpus of 4,999 digitized corporate annual reports with 31 consolidated financial KPIs per report, designed to evaluate long-context capabilities of LLMs through three benchmarks: page-level KPI retrieval, needle-in-a-haystack single-value lookup, and full KPI extraction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing financial NLP benchmarks reduce the domain to plain-text SEC 10-K filings with simple Q&A pairs, failing to test the very-long-context capabilities of modern LLMs on realistic, multi-modal corporate documents that include figures, tables, and narrative.

Method: The authors construct LEDGER from 4,999 full corporate annual reports, labeling each with 31 financial KPIs linked to market reactions at earnings dates. They derive three benchmarks: (1) a TREC-style page-level KPI retrieval task with 118,048 natural language questions, (2) a conversational needle-in-a-haystack single-value lookup, and (3) a complete KPI extraction task from long, numerically dense documents. Human OCR-quality annotations with inter-annotator agreement metrics and a full extraction/validation/scoring toolchain are provided.

Result: The dataset comprises 4,999 reports with structured KPI labels and 118,048 retrieval questions. A case study demonstrates the dataset's utility by linking CEO-letter rhetoric to post-publication market impact.

Conclusion: LEDGER fills a critical gap in financial NLP evaluation by providing a realistic, long-context, multi-modal benchmark that goes beyond simplified SEC filings, enabling rigorous testing of LLMs' document understanding and extraction capabilities in a financially grounded setting.

Abstract: Finance reporting is a natural proving ground for large language models, and the very-long-context capabilities of recent models across all sizes make rigorous evaluation in this domain an increasingly pressing need. Yet most public financial resources reduce the task to plain-text SEC 10-K filings paired with a handful of question-answer items. We release LEDGER (Long-context Evaluation of Documents for Grounded Extraction and Retrieval), a corpus of 4,999 digitized corporate annual reports - full documents with figures, tables, and narrative, not just regulatory filings. Each report is labeled with 31 consolidated financial KPIs to be extracted and linked to the market's reaction at the earnings date. From this data we derive three evaluation benchmarks spanning the difficulty spectrum: a pure page-level KPI retrieval task with TREC-style relevance judgments over 118,048 questions in natural language, a conversational "needle-in-a-haystack" single-value lookup, and a full KPI extraction task, both from long, numerically dense reports. We additionally provide human OCR-quality annotations with inter-annotator agreement and the complete extraction, validation, and scoring toolchain. We further demonstrate the dataset's research utility with a case study linking CEO-letter rhetoric to post-publication market impact.

</details>


### [32] [MÖVE: A Holistic LLM Benchmark for the German Public Sector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13111)
*Camilla Dalerci,Thilo Michael,Robin Schaefer,Daniel Weinland*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MÖVE is a holistic benchmark for evaluating LLMs in the German public sector, assessing 39 models across performance (summarization, QA, topic extraction) and governance (hallucination, energy use, transparency, constitutional values) dimensions using ten German-language datasets. No single model excels across all criteria, and model size poorly predicts quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM adoption in public administration lacks systematic model selection guidance. Existing benchmarks are English-centric, US-focused, and evaluate only task performance, ignoring governance concerns critical to the public sector such as transparency, energy consumption, and alignment with constitutional values.

Method: The benchmark evaluates 39 LLMs on ten German-language datasets (including custom gold- and silver-standard public-administration datasets) across two dimensions: (1) Performance — summarization, question answering, topic extraction; (2) Governance — hallucination tendencies, energy consumption, provider transparency, alignment with German constitutional values, and knowledge of German political party positions. A multi-metric strategy combines classical NLP metrics, embedding-based methods, and LLM-as-a-judge approaches. The benchmark itself is analyzed for statistical precision, judge reliability, prompt sensitivity, and energy estimate validity.

Result: No single model dominates all criteria; top performers vary by task. Model size is a poor predictor of quality. The benchmark demonstrates that governance criteria reveal trade-offs absent from performance-only evaluations. Statistical analyses confirm the benchmark's reliability, though LLM-judge and prompt sensitivity require careful calibration.

Conclusion: MÖVE provides the first comprehensive, multidimensional evaluation framework for LLMs in German public administration, revealing that model selection must balance performance with governance considerations. It is maintained as a living benchmark with publicly available results, designed to guide evidence-based LLM procurement in the public sector.

Abstract: We present MÖVE (Modelle für die Öffentliche Verwaltung Evaluieren), a holistic benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in the context of the German public sector. While LLMs are increasingly adopted in public administration, model selection remains largely ad hoc, and existing benchmarks offer limited guidance: they are predominantly English-centric, US-centric in content, and focus exclusively on task performance. MÖVE addresses these gaps by evaluating 39 models across two complementary dimensions. Performance criteria cover summarization, question answering, and topic extraction. Governance criteria assess hallucination tendencies, energy consumption, provider transparency, and alignment with German constitutional values and knowledge about positions by German political parties. In total, we utilize ten German-language datasets, including gold- and silverstandard datasets that we constructed to reflect public-administration domains. We employ a multi-metric evaluation strategy combining classical NLP metrics, embedding-based methods, and LLM-as-a-judge approaches. Our results show that no single model dominates across all criteria: top performers differ between tasks, and model size alone is a poor predictor of quality. We further evaluate the benchmark itself, analyzing its statistical precision, LLM judge reliability, the impact of our private datasets on model rankings, the sensitivity of our results to prompt formulation, and the validity of our energy consumption estimates. MÖVE is designed as a living benchmark under active development; results are publicly available at https://moeve.bundesdruckerei.de/.

</details>


### [33] [G-Long: Graph-Enhanced Memory Management for Efficient Long-Term Dialogue Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13115)
*Minjun Choi,Yoonjin Jang,Sangwon Youn,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: G-Long is a graph-enhanced framework that uses a fine-tuned small language model for structured triplet extraction and associative retrieval, combined with a T5 attention-aware importance scoring mechanism, to improve long-term dialogue consistency while minimizing computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with long-term consistency in open-domain dialogue due to poor long-context reasoning and inefficient raw text processing. Existing solutions either use unstructured memory (causing information loss) or expensive LLMs (causing high latency), motivating a more structured and cost-effective approach.

Method: G-Long employs a fine-tuned small Language Model (sLM) for extracting structured triplets and performing associative retrieval over a graph-based memory. It also introduces an attention-aware importance scoring mechanism that leverages cross-attention signals from a T5 summarizer to identify and prioritize salient memories.

Result: G-Long achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks: up to 9.8% improvement in response quality on the MSC dataset and 40.8% improvement in retrieval recall on the LME dataset, while significantly reducing computational overhead.

Conclusion: G-Long demonstrates that structured graph memory combined with attention-aware importance scoring and a lightweight sLM can effectively resolve long-term consistency issues in open-domain dialogue at a fraction of the computational cost of LLM-only approaches.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have advanced open-domain dialogue systems, maintaining long-term consistency remains a challenge due to inherent limitations in long-context reasoning and the inefficiency of processing extensive raw text. Existing approaches typically rely on either unstructured memory storage, which is prone to information loss, or computationally expensive LLMs that incur high latency. To address these limitations, we propose G-Long, a graph-enhanced framework that utilizes a fine-tuned small Language Model (sLM) for structured triplet extraction and associative retrieval, significantly reducing operational costs. Furthermore, we introduce the novel attention-aware importance scoring mechanism that leverages the intrinsic cross-attention signals of a T5 summarizer to identify salient memories. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that G-Long achieves state-of-the-art performance in both response generation and memory retrieval, yielding performance gains of up to 9.8% in response quality on MSC and 40.8% in retrieval recall on LME, while significantly minimizing computational overhead.

</details>


### [34] [EvoBrowseComp: Benchmarking Search Agents on Evolving Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13120)
*Yunhan Wang,Jiaan Wang,Lianzhe Huang,Xianfeng Zeng,Fandong Meng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoBrowseComp introduces an evolving, contamination-free benchmark for search-augmented LLMs. Using a three-agent framework (QA synthesis, information filtering, high-level guidance), it generates 800 complex questions (400 English, 400 Chinese) from live-web traversal. The benchmark is auto-updatable, resistant to memorization shortcuts, and demonstrates high difficulty requiring broad horizontal search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing search-agent benchmarks like BrowseComp rely on static knowledge, making them susceptible to test-set contamination and parametric memorization. Models can exploit reasoning shortcuts via fact recall rather than genuine retrieval, obscuring true browsing competence. There is a pressing need for future-proof, contamination-resistant evaluation benchmarks that keep pace with evolving world knowledge.

Method: A three-agent collaborative framework: (1) a QA synthesis agent retrieves fresh knowledge from the live web to synthesize QA pairs; (2) an information filtering agent filters retrieved knowledge by credibility and popularity to block parametric shortcuts; (3) a high-level guidance agent formalizes questions into reasoning graphs to reduce logical redundancy and shortcuts. The framework supports fully automated synthesis, enabling regular updates for temporal freshness. The resulting EvoBrowseComp benchmark comprises 400 English and 400 Chinese questions requiring broad horizontal search.

Result: Extensive experiments confirm EvoBrowseComp's great difficulty, demonstrating that models cannot rely on parametric memorization and must perform genuine multi-step retrieval. The benchmark successfully requires broad horizontal search across diverse sources to answer questions correctly.

Conclusion: EvoBrowseComp establishes a scalable paradigm for auto-updatable, high-difficulty benchmarking that stays synchronized with evolving world knowledge and advancing agent capabilities, effectively mitigating contamination and memorization concerns in evaluating search-augmented LLMs.

Abstract: Search Agents -- large language models augmented with search tools -- have intensified the need for future-proof evaluation benchmarks. Existing benchmarks such as BrowseComp rely on static knowledge, making them vulnerable to test-set contamination and parametric memorization. Consequently, models can achieve high scores through fact recall rather than genuine retrieval, obscuring true browsing competence via reasoning shortcuts.
  In this paper, we introduce EvoBrowseComp, an evolving benchmark of 400 English and 400 Chinese contamination-free complex questions synthesized via live-web traversal. To collect these questions, we design a three-agent collaborative framework: (1) a QA synthesis agent that retrieves fresh knowledge from the live web to synthesize QA pairs; (2) an information filtering agent that filters retrieved knowledge in terms of credibility and popularity to block parametric shortcuts; and (3) a high-level guidance agent that formalizes questions into reasoning graphs to reduce logical redundancy and shortcuts in synthesized QA pairs. Because the framework supports fully automated synthesis, EvoBrowseComp can be regularly updated to prevent data contamination and maintain temporal freshness. Extensive experiments confirm its great difficulty, requiring broad horizontal search. It establishes a scalable paradigm for auto-updatable, high-difficulty benchmarking that keeps pace with both evolving world knowledge and advancing agent capabilities.

</details>


### [35] [NaturalFlow: Reducing Disruptive Pauses for Natural Speech Flow in Simultaneous Speech-to-Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13121)
*Dongwook Lee,Youngho Cho,Sangkwon Park,Heeseung Kim,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes a fluency-aware optimization framework for simultaneous speech-to-speech translation that minimizes inter-chunk silences using model-internal signals (linguistic diversity and temporal variability), achieving natural speech flow while preserving low latency and translation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simultaneous speech-to-speech translation systems overly prioritize low latency, producing fragmented, chunk-wise speech with frequent unnatural pauses that increase listeners' cognitive load. There is a need to balance low-latency benefits with the natural acoustic flow characteristic of consecutive translation.

Method: A fluency-aware optimization framework that leverages model-internal signals — specifically linguistic diversity and induced temporal variability in speech durations — to minimize inter-chunk silences and discover an optimal trade-off between latency and fluency.

Result: Experiments on both short- and long-form benchmarks demonstrate that the framework produces natural speech flow while maintaining competitive latency and translation quality.

Conclusion: The proposed fluency-aware optimization effectively bridges the gap between simultaneous and consecutive translation paradigms, achieving a sweet spot that mitigates the unnatural pausing problem without sacrificing core performance metrics.

Abstract: Simultaneous speech-to-speech translation aims to enable near-real-time communication by minimizing latency, offering a compelling, real-time alternative to the high latency of consecutive translation. However, the excessive pursuit of low latency often results in fragmented chunk-wise speech. Consequently, listeners are subjected to an unnatural acoustic flow punctuated by frequent pauses, which could increase their cognitive load. To bridge this gap, we introduce a fluency-aware optimization framework designed to discover the sweet spot between the low-latency benefits of simultaneous translation and the natural flow of consecutive translation. Our framework minimizes inter-chunk silences by leveraging model-internal signals, including linguistic diversity and induced temporal variability in speech durations. Experiments on short- and long-form benchmarks show that our framework produces natural speech flow while maintaining competitive latency and translation quality.

</details>


### [36] [HyPE: Category-Aware Hypergraph Encoding with Persistent Edge Embeddings for Persona-Grounded Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13142)
*Sangwon Youn,Yoonjin Jang,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HyPE organizes persona sentences into a hypergraph based on (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) quadruples, then uses HyperGCN with Persistent Edge Embeddings to encode structured persona representations that improve persona-consistent dialogue generation across multiple backbone LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing persona-grounded dialogue systems treat persona descriptions as a flat, unstructured set of sentences, ignoring high-order relational structure (e.g., multiple persona sentences sharing a topical category). This limits the model's ability to capture and leverage the inherent organization among persona attributes.

Method: HyPE first decomposes each persona sentence into a (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) quadruple. These elements are organized into a hypergraph where hyperedges connect persona elements sharing the same category label. A HyperGCN hypergraph neural network propagates information through this structure, producing a persona summary vector and a soft-memory bank used to condition the response generator. Persistent Edge Embeddings (PEE) — lightweight, learnable per-category priors — are fused into the HyperGCN message-passing step.

Result: Under greedy decoding on the PersonaChat benchmark, HyPE consistently outperforms sentence-level pooling baselines across GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-3B, and Qwen2.5-3B backbones, demonstrating that structured hyperedge-level persona encoding yields a transferable advantage across model scales.

Conclusion: Modeling the high-order category structure of persona attributes via hypergraphs provides consistent and transferable improvements over flat sentence-level encoding for persona-grounded dialogue generation.

Abstract: Persona-grounded dialogue systems aim to produce responses consistent with a speaker's persona, yet existing methods treat personas as a flat set of sentences and fail to model the high-order relations among persona attributes-e.g., that several persona sentences share a topical category. We propose HyPE (Hypergraph Persona Encoder), a framework that (i) analyzes each persona-bearing text as a (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) quadruple, and (ii) organizes persona elements into a hypergraph whose hyperedges are induced by shared category labels. An HyperGCN hypergraph neural network propagates this structure into a persona summary vector and a soft-memory bank that condition the response generator. We further propose Persistent Edge Embeddings (PEE), lightweight per-category learnable priors fused into the HyperGCN message-passing step. On PersonaChat under greedy decoding, HyPE consistently outperforms sentence-level pooling baselines across GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-3B, and Qwen2.5-3B backbones by demonstrating that structured hyperedge-level persona encoding provides a transferable advantage across model scales.

</details>


### [37] [NTS-CoT: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLM-based News Timeline Summarization with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13171)
*Feng Lyu,Huiqin Yan,Sijing Duan,Hao Wu,Shuang Gu,Xue Qiao,Weixu Zhang,Haolun Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies hallucination issues in LLM-based timeline summarization (TLS) — unfaithful content and information omission — and proposes NTS-CoT, a Chain-of-Thought framework with three modules (Element-CoT, Date Selection, Causal-CoT) to mitigate them, achieving SOTA results on three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations in LLM-based TLS — where generated content deviates from source news — are understudied. The authors aim to systematically identify hallucination types in TLS and develop targeted mitigation strategies.

Method: NTS-CoT framework with three modules: (i) Element-CoT captures essential news elements (who, what, where, when, why) for faithful summarization; (ii) Date Selection combines temporal saliency and event prominence for timestamp selection; (iii) Causal-CoT infers causal relationships to reduce omissions in date-event summarization.

Result: Extensive experiments on three TLS benchmarks, including quantitative analysis and human evaluation, show NTS-CoT outperforms SOTA baselines and effectively mitigates both types of hallucinations.

Conclusion: NTS-CoT's CoT-based approach effectively reduces hallucinations in LLM-driven timeline summarization, advancing the reliability of automated news event tracking.

Abstract: The rapid updates of online news make tracking event developments challenging, highlighting the need for timeline summarization (TLS). Hallucinations, where LLM-generated content deviates from source news, still remain a critical issue in LLM-based TLS and are not well studied in existing works. To bridge this gap, we identify two primary types of hallucinations: unfaithful content during news summarization and information omission in date-event summarization. Then, we propose NTS-CoT, a novel framework that leverages Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to mitigate hallucinations in TLS. The framework consists of three key modules: i) Element-CoT to capture essential news elements for faithful summarization, ii) Date Selection to combine temporal saliency and event prominence for timestamp selection, and iii) Causal-CoT to infer causal relationships and reduce omissions in date-event summarization. Extensive experiments, including quantitative analysis on three TLS benchmarks and human evaluation, demonstrate that NTS-CoT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, effectively mitigating hallucinations and improving LLM-based TLS performance. Our source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/NTS-CoT .

</details>


### [38] [MemRefine: LLM-Guided Compression for Long-Term Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13177)
*Minjae Kim,Jinheon Baek,Soyeong Jeong,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MemRefine is an LLM-guided memory management framework that keeps long-term agent memory stores within a fixed storage budget by using an LLM judge to make delete/merge/preserve decisions based on factual content, outperforming rule-based baselines under tight budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents operating over long-term interactions accumulate unbounded, redundant memory entries that inflate storage costs and degrade retrieval quality. This is particularly problematic for resource-constrained platforms with hard memory budgets, creating a need for storage-budgeted memory management.

Method: MemRefine uses surface similarity only to propose candidate pairs for potential merging/deletion, then defers the actual delete, merge, and preserve decisions to an LLM judge that evaluates factual content. The process iterates until the memory store meets the target budget, avoiding reliance on shallow similarity metrics for final decisions.

Result: Across multiple memory frameworks and long-term conversation benchmarks, MemRefine consistently meets target storage budgets while preserving downstream task performance, significantly outperforming rule-based baselines under tight budget constraints.

Conclusion: Storage-budgeted memory management is a meaningful formulation for long-term LLM agents, and LLM-guided factual reasoning — rather than surface-level heuristics — is essential for effective memory compression under hard budget constraints.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly expected to operate over long-term interactions, where information from past dialogues must be preserved and recalled to support future tasks. However, as interactions accumulate, the memory store grows without bound and fills with redundant entries that inflate storage cost and degrade retrieval by crowding out the most useful evidence. Furthermore, this is especially limiting on resource-constrained platforms with hard memory budgets, motivating us to formulate storage-budgeted memory management, the task of keeping an already constructed memory store within a fixed budget while preserving information useful for future interactions. To this end, we then propose MemRefine, an LLM-guided framework that, since surface similarity poorly reflects factual value, uses similarity only to propose candidate pairs and defers delete, merge, and preserve decisions to an LLM judge based on factual content, iterating until the budget is met. Across multiple memory frameworks and long-term conversation benchmarks, MemRefine consistently meets target budgets while preserving downstream performance and outperforming rule-based baselines under tight budgets.

</details>


### [39] [LAUKIN: A Multi-jurisdictional Common Law Contract Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13184)
*Amrita Singh,Aditya Joshi,Jiaojiao Jiang,Hye-young Paik,May Fong Cheong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LAUKIN is a cross-jurisdictional legal equivalence dataset (Australia, UK, India) with 14,727 clause pairs, 3,000 manually labelled. Built via multi-stage retrieval-reranking, it benchmarks 12 models (best macro-F1: 65.11%), revealing that shared legal heritage does not eliminate significant drafting divergences across jurisdictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multinational companies need cross-jurisdictional contract review, but existing legal NLP datasets are limited to single jurisdictions. No prior dataset addresses clause-level legal equivalence across multiple common law jurisdictions, hindering the development of automated cross-border contract analysis tools.

Method: A multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline constructs initial clause pair mappings (AU-UK, UK-IN, IN-AU) from 204 contracts across 8 agreement types. A subset of 3,000 clause pairs is manually annotated by legal experts as Equivalent or Not Equivalent (900 train, 600 dev, 1,500 test). The remaining 11,727 pairs serve as unlabelled data for semi-supervised learning. Twelve models across four techniques are evaluated on the benchmark.

Result: The best-performing model achieves a macro-F1 of 65.11%, indicating the task is challenging. Results demonstrate that despite shared common law heritage, drafting conventions diverge significantly across Australia, UK, and India, making cross-jurisdictional equivalence classification non-trivial.

Conclusion: LAUKIN establishes a novel and challenging benchmark for cross-jurisdictional legal clause equivalence classification. The substantial performance gap highlights the difficulty of the task and the need for further research. The inclusion of unlabelled data facilitates future semi-supervised learning approaches in legal NLP.

Abstract: Multinational companies increasingly require cross-jurisdictional contract review, yet existing legal NLP datasets are largely restricted to a single jurisdiction. We introduce LAUKIN (Legal equivalence dataset of Australia, UK, and INdia), a dataset of clause pairs (AU-UK, UK-IN, IN-AU) labelled for boolean legal equivalence. We develop a novel multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline to construct the initial clause pair mapping, with a subset of clause pairs subsequently annotated by legal experts as Equivalent or Not Equivalent. The dataset comprises 14,727 clause pairs from 204 contracts across 8 agreement types, of which 3,000 are manually labelled: 900 train, 600 dev, and 1,500 test. We evaluate 12 models across 4 techniques, achieving a best macro-F1 of 65.11%, establishing LAUKIN as a challenging benchmark. Results reveal that, despite shared legal heritage, drafting conventions diverge significantly across jurisdictions, making cross-jurisdictional equivalence classification non-trivial. LAUKIN also includes 11,727 unlabelled training pairs to support future semi-supervised learning research in legal NLP.

</details>


### [40] [A Context-Aware Dataset for Stance Detection in Bioethical Controversies on Reddit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13187)
*Hu Huang,Genan Dai,Fuqiang Niu,Yi Yang,Zhaoya Gong,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BioStance is a large-scale, context-aware stance detection dataset comprising 39,600 annotated Reddit Post-Comment pairs on six bioethical targets across three controversy dimensions, achieving high inter-annotator reliability (α=0.82).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stance detection research lacks large-scale, domain-specific resources that adequately capture the context-dependent nature of discourse in bioethical debates, which increasingly occur on social media.

Method: The authors constructed BioStance by collecting Post-Comment pairs from Reddit discussions on six controversial bioethical targets spanning three dimensions: fundamental value conflicts, individual liberty vs. collective responsibility, and technological uncertainty. Each instance preserves hierarchical conversational context and was independently annotated by three annotators using a three-class stance scheme (Favor, Against, None).

Result: The dataset contains 39,600 annotated instances. Inter-annotator agreement reached a mean Krippendorff's α of 0.82, indicating substantial reliability.

Conclusion: BioStance, through its combination of thematic diversity, conversational structure, and high-quality annotation, serves as a valuable resource for advancing research in context-aware stance detection, argument mining, and computational analysis of bioethical discourse.

Abstract: Bioethical debates increasingly unfold on social media, yet stance detection research lacks large-scale, domain-specific resources for modeling such context-dependent discourse. We present BioStance, a context-aware dataset of 39,600 annotated Post-Comment pairs from Reddit bioethical discussions. BioStance covers six controversial targets across three dimensions of bioethical controversy: fundamental value conflicts, individual liberty versus collective responsibility, and technological uncertainty. Each instance preserves hierarchical conversational context and is labeled by three independent annotators using a three-class stance scheme: Favor, Against, and None. The annotations achieve a mean Krippendorff's $α$ of 0.82, indicating substantial reliability. By combining thematic diversity, conversational structure, and high-quality human annotation, BioStance supports research on context-aware stance detection, argument mining, and computational analysis of bioethical discourse.

</details>


### [41] [SICI: A Semantic-Pragmatic Complexity Index Reveals Regime Shifts in LLM Stance Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13189)
*Fuqiang Niu,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SICI is a seven-dimensional metric quantifying the semantic-pragmatic complexity of stance detection examples. It reliably predicts LLM accuracy, reveals phase-transition-like error regimes (over-attribution → boundary instability → None concentration) as complexity increases, and shows that current interventions mainly shift models along the attribution–abstention axis rather than solving high-complexity cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stance detection remains challenging for LLMs even with advanced prompting, reasoning, retrieval, or debate strategies. The authors argue that harder examples are not adequately addressed by these interventions, and no principled diagnostic framework exists to characterize *why* certain target–text pairs are difficult. They propose SICI to fill this gap — a measure of the semantic-pragmatic burden that systematically explains LLM failure modes.

Method: SICI is a seven-dimensional index capturing semantic-pragmatic complexity of target–text pairs. It is validated on SemEval-2016 and VAST datasets. The authors compute inter-scorer reliability (α=0.771), test its predictive power for LLM accuracy against surface-level proxies, and analyze error-regime transitions across SICI levels for GPT-3.5, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3, and GPT-4o. A 15-method intervention study (spanning prompting, retrieval, and debate techniques) examines how these methods interact with SICI-defined complexity.

Result: SICI predicts LLM accuracy better than surface proxies and achieves strong inter-annotator reliability. As SICI increases, LLM errors undergo a phase-transition-like pattern: low-complexity → over-attribution (especially Against); intermediate → unstable boundary classification; high-complexity → concentration on None. This pattern generalizes across all four tested LLMs, though stronger models shift the transition boundaries. Interventions (prompting, retrieval, debate) tend to move models along the attribution–abstention axis rather than mitigating the high-complexity bottleneck.

Conclusion: The difficulty of stance detection is not merely a matter of unclear instructions or insufficient reasoning — it is partly a function of inherent semantic-pragmatic complexity, which SICI operationalizes. The phase-transition-like error structure suggests fundamental architectural or representational bottlenecks in current LLMs for high-complexity stance cases, and popular intervention strategies do not resolve this core limitation.

Abstract: Prompt-based LLMs are increasingly used for stance detection, but harder examples are not always repaired by clearer instructions, reasoning prompts, retrieval, or debate. We introduce SICI (Stance Inference Complexity Index), a seven-dimensional diagnostic measure of the semantic-pragmatic burden imposed by a target--text pair. Across SemEval-2016 and VAST, SICI predicts LLM accuracy better than surface proxies and shows substantial cross-scorer reliability ($α=0.771$). More importantly, LLM errors change regime as SICI increases: low-complexity examples invite over-attribution, especially Against predictions; intermediate examples form an unstable boundary; and high-complexity examples rapidly concentrate on None. This phase-transition-like structure persists across GPT-3.5, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3, and GPT-4o, although stronger models move the boundaries. A 15-method intervention study further shows that prompting, retrieval, and debate often shift models along the attribution--abstention axis rather than removing the high-complexity bottleneck.

</details>


### [42] [Layer-Resolved Optimal Transport for Hallucination Detection in NMT and Abstractive Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13216)
*Mariia Onyshchuk,Maksym-Vasyl Tarnavskyi,Marta Sumyk*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper extends optimal transport (OT) analysis of cross-attention to detect hallucinations in NMT across all decoder layers, finding that OT-based detection is complementary across hallucination types and concentrated in early-middle layers. When applied to abstractive summarization faithfulness, OT detection significantly underperforms supervised methods because unfaithful summaries can attend correctly yet misrepresent content — a failure mode invisible to attention-concentration metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work showed OT on cross-attention can detect NMT hallucinations without supervision. This paper investigates: (1) whether OT detection signals vary systematically across decoder layers and hallucination types in NMT, and (2) whether this geometric signal transfers to a related but distinct task — faithfulness detection in abstractive summarization — where the failure modes may be fundamentally different.

Method: The authors analyze the Fairseq DE-EN NMT model (N=3,414) across all six decoder layers using two OT metrics: Wasserstein-to-Uniform (Wass-to-Unif) and Wasserstein-to-Data (Wass-to-Data). They examine per-layer detection performance across hallucination types (omission, addition, mistranslation, untranslated) and decoding step dynamics. For summarization, they apply the same unsupervised OT detector to the AggreFact benchmark (N=1,116) on CNN/XSum and compare against supervised MiniCheck-Flan-T5-L. Structural experiments on T5-base probe decoder organization via attention concentration across layers and layer ablation.

Result: In NMT: Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data are complementary detectors specialized across hallucination types; detection is concentrated in layers L1–L4, with L5 being anti-predictive for subtler types; hallucinated translations lack the exploratory attention phase present in correct translations from the first decoding step. In summarization: unsupervised OT achieves only 57.2%/57.6% balanced accuracy on CNN/XSum, substantially below supervised MiniCheck (69.9%/74.3%). Structural experiments on T5-base show Layer 3 has peak attention concentration and Layer 12 is most critical for generation quality.

Conclusion: OT on cross-attention is a reliable, unsupervised hallucination detector specifically when the failure mode is source disengagement (as in NMT), and serves as a principled interpretability tool regardless of task. However, it is fundamentally limited when faithfulness failures occur downstream of attention, as in summarization where models can attend correctly to source tokens while misrepresenting their content. This establishes a principled boundary condition for OT-based detection methods.

Abstract: Optimal transport (OT) has been shown to detect hallucinations in neural machine translation (NMT) by measuring the geometric distance between cross-attention distributions and a reference distribution, without any supervision. We extend this analysis to all six decoder layers of the Fairseq DE-EN model ($N=3{,}414$), showing that Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data are complementary detectors specialised across hallucination types, that detection is concentrated in layers L1--L4 with L5 anti-predictive for subtler types, and that hallucinated translations lack the exploratory attention phase present in correct translations from the first decoding step. We further evaluate whether the geometric signal transfers to abstractive summarization faithfulness detection: our unsupervised OT detector on AggreFact ($N=1{,}116$) achieves $57.2\%$/$57.6\%$ balanced accuracy on CNN/XSum -- above chance but substantially below supervised MiniCheck-Flan-T5-L($69.9\%$/$74.3\%$). This gap is principled: unlike NMT hallucinations, unfaithful summaries can attend correctly to source tokens while misrepresenting their content, a failure mode invisible to concentration-based OT metrics by construction. Structural experiments on T5-base confirm consistent decoder organisation across depth, with Layer~3 showing peak concentration and Layer~12 being most critical for generation quality. Together, the results establish OT on cross-attention as a reliable detector when the failure mode is source disengagement, a principled interpretability tool regardless of task, and fundamentally limited when faithfulness failures occur downstream of attention.

</details>


### [43] [When Similar Means Different: Evaluating LLMs on Arabic--Hebrew Cognates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13218)
*Junhong Liang,Noor Abo Mokh,Bashar Alhafni*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SemCog Bench evaluates LLMs on Arabic-Hebrew cognate disambiguation, revealing models rely on surface-form similarity and struggle with false friends/loanwords even with sentence context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Arabic and Hebrew share true cognates, false friends, and modern loanwords due to their Semitic ancestry and language contact. This creates a challenging testbed for cross-lingual semantic reasoning in LLMs, where surface-form overlap can mislead meaning interpretation.

Method: The authors introduce SemCog Bench, a curated benchmark of 1,858 Arabic-Hebrew word pairs with sentence-level annotations. LLMs (both open-source and commercial) are evaluated across four input representations — raw script, diacritized, Romanized, and phonetic — on two tasks: cognate identification and semantic disambiguation.

Result: Models achieve high accuracy on true cognates but performance drops sharply on false friends and loanwords, indicating heavy reliance on orthographic similarity. Sentence-level context provides only modest improvements, proving insufficient to override misleading surface-form cues.

Conclusion: Current LLMs exhibit a fundamental limitation in resolving cross-lingual form-meaning conflicts. SemCog Bench establishes a rigorous benchmark for multilingual semantic reasoning, and the findings highlight the need for deeper cross-lingual understanding beyond surface-form pattern matching.

Abstract: Arabic and Hebrew, as closely related Semitic languages, share a substantial lexicon of true cognates, misleading false friends, and modern loanwords. This overlap poses a challenge for cross-lingual semantic understanding in large language models (LLMs). To evaluate this capability, we introduce SemCog Bench, a curated benchmark of 1,858 Arabic--Hebrew word pairs with sentence-level annotations for cognate identification and semantic disambiguation. We evaluate open-source and commercial LLMs across multiple input representations (raw, diacritized, Romanized, and phonetic) and reveal a critical gap in cross-lingual reasoning. While models achieve high accuracy on true cognates, performance drops sharply on false friends and loanwords, reflecting a strong reliance on surface-form similarity. Furthermore, sentence-level context yields only modest improvements, suggesting that contextual cues alone are insufficient to overcome misleading form-based signals. These findings reveal a fundamental limitation of current LLMs in resolving cross-lingual form--meaning conflicts and establish SemCog Bench as a rigorous benchmark for multilingual semantic reasoning. Our code and data are publicly available.

</details>


### [44] [PolyAlign: Conditional Human-Distribution Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13227)
*L. D. M. S. Sai Teja,Ufaq Khan,Sathira Silva,Xiao Wu,Muhammad Haris Khan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PolyAlign is a distribution-aware alignment framework that matches language models to conditional human response distributions (by language, task, response style, and length) rather than a single global assistant style, improving naturalness and distributional faithfulness in bilingual settings while maintaining task utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard post-training methods like SFT and preference optimization align LMs toward a single global assistant behavior, which suppresses the natural variation of human responses across languages, tasks, and interaction contexts. The paper frames the problem as conditional human-distribution alignment—models should match the human response distribution appropriate to the current context rather than adopting a uniform style.

Method: PolyAlign organizes bilingual interaction data into buckets defined by language, interaction track, response family, and length. It employs two components: (1) Bucket-Aware SFT, which balances optimization across heterogeneous buckets to prevent dominant distributions from overshadowing minority ones; and (2) Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO), which regularizes preference learning by using a critic-estimated distance to bucket-specific human support, encouraging the model to stay within the appropriate conditional distribution.

Result: Across a bilingual evaluation suite covering English and Chinese single- and multi-turn settings, PolyAlign improves conditional naturalness and distributional faithfulness while preserving competitive task utility compared to global alignment baselines.

Conclusion: Post-training should move beyond global alignment objectives toward interaction-aware alignment with human response distributions. Aligning to conditional, context-specific human distributions yields more natural and faithful model behavior without sacrificing utility.

Abstract: Post-training methods such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and preference optimization typically align language models toward a single global assistant behavior. While effective for improving average helpfulness, this can suppress the natural variation of human responses across languages, tasks, and dialogue settings. We study this problem as conditional human-distribution alignment: models should match the human response distribution appropriate to the current interaction context, rather than a universal response style. We introduce PolyAlign, a distribution-aware alignment framework that organizes bilingual interaction data into bucket-specific human reference distributions defined by language, interaction track, response family, and length. PolyAlign combines Bucket-Aware SFT, which balances optimization across heterogeneous buckets, with Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO), which regularizes preference learning using critic-estimated distance to bucket-specific human support. Across a bilingual evaluation suite covering English and Chinese single- and multi-turn settings, PolyAlign improves conditional naturalness and distributional faithfulness while preserving competitive task utility. The results suggest that post-training should move beyond global alignment objectives toward interaction-aware alignment with human response distributions.

</details>


### [45] [Evaluating Pluralism in LLMs through Latent Perspectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13254)
*Laura Majer,Jan Šnajder,Martin Tutek*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a domain-agnostic, multi-layered framework for unsupervised perspective extraction to quantify the pluralistic gap in LLM-generated text. Evaluated on book reviews, results show LLMs underrepresent rarer perspectives, producing distributions that diverge from human-authored text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work demonstrates LLMs reduce diversity and generate homogeneously, but this has been shown primarily via multiple-choice questionnaires or high-level text characteristics. The authors aim to operationalize perspective identification in free-form text to provide clear guidance on pluralistic alignment and more precisely characterize the pluralistic gap.

Method: A domain-agnostic, multi-layered unsupervised framework for extracting perspectives from text. The framework is evaluated on a book review dataset (highly opinionated, diverse perspectives). Multiple LLMs and prompting techniques are compared against human-authored reference distributions.

Result: Certain models and prompting strategies approach broad perspective coverage, but rarer perspectives remain systematically and disproportionately underrepresented. The resulting perspective distributions diverge significantly from those found in human text.

Conclusion: LLMs exhibit a measurable pluralistic gap characterized by the underrepresentation of minority perspectives. The proposed framework provides a principled way to detect and quantify this gap, offering a foundation for improved pluralistic alignment in future work.

Abstract: The growing need to represent diverse perspectives has increased interest in pluralistic LLM generation. Although difficult to operationalize, identifying perspectives expressed in text would provide clear guidance on pluralistic alignment and more clearly articulate the pluralistic gap in LLM generation. While models have been shown to reduce the diversity of training data and generate homogeneously, this has been demonstrated primarily on multiple-choice questionnaires or using high-level characteristics of free-form text. In this paper, we introduce and implement a domain-agnostic multi-layered framework for unsupervised extraction of perspectives suitable for identifying the pluralistic gap in LLM-generated text. We evaluate our framework on book reviews, a highly opinionated dataset representing diverse perspectives, and compare various prompts and models. Our results show that while some models and prompting techniques come close to covering a broad spectrum of perspectives, rarer perspectives remain disproportionately underrepresented, resulting in distributions that diverge from human text.

</details>


### [46] [SkillCAT: Contrastive Assessment and Topology-Aware Skill Self-Evolution for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13317)
*Kunfeng Chen,Qihuang Zhong,Juhua Liu,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkillCAT is a training-free framework for LLM agent skill self-evolution that improves over prior methods by (1) extracting causal evidence from multiple same-task trajectory pairs, (2) verifying candidate skill patches before merging, and (3) loading only task-relevant skills at inference via a routable sub-skill topology. It achieves up to 40.40% improvement across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current skill self-evolution pipelines for LLM agents suffer from three key limitations: they learn from only one trajectory per task (losing causal signal), merge candidate skill patches without prior validation (introducing noise), and load the entire skill corpus at inference time (inefficient and potentially distracting). The authors aim to address all three issues in a unified, training-free framework.

Method: SkillCAT decomposes skill evolution into three stages: (1) **Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE)** — samples multiple trajectories per task and contrasts success/failure pairs to isolate outcome-determining evidence; (2) **Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE)** — replays each candidate skill patch on clones of the source task, retaining only patches that preserve or improve outcomes before hierarchical merging; (3) **Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE)** — organizes evolved skills into a routable sub-skill topology graph, enabling inference-time loading of only capability nodes relevant to the current task.

Result: SkillCAT was evaluated on SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA, along with cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization tests. It raised the average score over baselines by up to 40.40%, demonstrating reliable skill evolution without any model training.

Conclusion: SkillCAT demonstrates that a training-free, three-stage pipeline — contrastive extraction, assessment-gated evolution, and topology-aware execution — can substantially improve LLM agent skill self-evolution, yielding strong performance gains across diverse benchmarks and generalization settings.

Abstract: Skill self-evolution methods for LLM agents aim to turn execution trajectories into reusable skill documents, but current pipelines typically learn from one trajectory per task, merge candidate skill patches before checking them, and load the full skill corpus before inference. We propose SkillCAT, a training-free framework that separates this process into three stages. Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE) samples multiple trajectories for each task and compares same-task success/failure pairs to identify evidence that explains outcome differences. Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE) replays each candidate patch on source-task clones and keeps only patches that improve or preserve task outcomes before hierarchical skill patch merging. Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE) compiles the evolved skills into a routable sub-skill topology, so inference loads only the capability nodes relevant to the task. We evaluate SkillCAT on common agent benchmarks, including SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA, and further test cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization. Across these settings, SkillCAT raises the average score over baselines by up to 40.40%, demonstrating reliable skill evolution without model training.

</details>


### [47] [Low-Latency Real-Time Audio Game Commentary System via LLM-Based Parallel Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13322)
*Ryota Kawamatsu,Anum Afzal,Yuki Saito,Shinnosuke Takamichi,Graham Neubig,Katsuhito Sudoh,Hiroya Takamura,Tatsuya Ishigaki*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A real-time audio game commentary system that reduces inter-utterance silence from 9.6s to 0.3s by parallelizing text generation with speech playback and buffering candidate utterances ahead of time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional sequential pipelines for live game commentary suffer from accumulated waiting time — each utterance must complete text generation, speech synthesis, and playback before the next begins — causing long, unnatural silences that degrade the listening experience.

Method: The proposed system runs text generation in parallel with ongoing speech playback and pre-buffers multiple candidate utterances. This enables immediate speech synthesis at playback boundaries, decoupling generation latency from inter-utterance gaps.

Result: Experiments on fast-paced game videos show mean inter-utterance silence drops from 9.6s (sequential baseline) to 0.3s. Silence timing pattern similarity to professional commentary improves by over 40%, and a user study with 120 participants confirms significantly better perceived speaking rhythm.

Conclusion: Parallelizing text generation with speech playback and look-ahead buffering effectively eliminates the latency bottleneck in real-time commentary systems, achieving near-professional silence patterns and rhythm perception.

Abstract: We present a low-latency real-time audio game commentary system that generates spoken commentary directly from live gameplay video. In this end-to-end setting, a key bottleneck is accumulated waiting time; conventional pipelines capture frames, generate text, and synthesize speech sequentially for each utterance, and do not request the next generation until speech playback has completed. This strict sequentiality causes long and unnatural silence between utterances. To address this latency bottleneck, our system runs text generation in parallel with speech playback and buffers multiple candidate utterances ahead of time, enabling immediate synthesis at playback boundaries. Experiments on fast-paced game videos show that our parallel design reduces the mean inter-utterance silence from 9.6 seconds to 0.3 seconds compared to sequential baselines. It also improves similarity to professional speaking--silence timing patterns by over 40 %, and a user study with 120 experienced game players confirms significantly improved perceived speaking rhythm. Our demo video is available at: https://youtu.be/pmrRUlvav8M.

</details>


### [48] [IVIE: A Neuro-symbolic Approach to Incremental and Validated Generation of Interactive Fiction Worlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13348)
*Micaela Vaucher,Santiago Silveira,Santiago Góngora,Luis Chiruzzo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: IVIE is a neuro-symbolic system that incrementally generates coherent interactive fiction worlds by combining LLM creativity with symbolic validation, achieving immersive player experiences while acknowledging persistent challenges in constraint enforcement and structural validity.


<details>
