  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interactive Fiction generation faces a trade-off: LLMs produce creative narratives but lack world coherence, while symbolic systems ensure consistency but are inflexible. The authors aim to reconcile these competing demands through a neuro-symbolic approach.

Method: IVIE extends PAYADOR's neuro-symbolic framework with a four-stage incremental generation pipeline: (1) setting and character creation, (2) puzzle design, (3) world state grounding via symbolic validation, and (4) assembly of interconnected locations, functional items, NPCs, and coherent puzzles around a central goal-oriented architecture. Creative decisions are delegated to LLMs while symbolic validation ensures consistency.

Result: Human evaluation demonstrates that IVIE generates immersive, thematically coherent worlds with high player engagement. The neuro-symbolic approach successfully balances generative flexibility with narrative coherence — symbolic validation constrains LLM output without eliminating creative freedom. However, LLM inconsistencies occasionally bypass puzzle constraints, and gaps in objective validation allow structurally impossible goals.

Conclusion: The neuro-symbolic paradigm is promising for interactive storytelling but requires further refinement. Key design considerations for future systems center on improving LLM reliability in constraint adherence and closing objective validation gaps to prevent structurally unsound world states.

Abstract: Computational creativity in Interactive Fiction faces a fundamental tension: Large Language Models (LLM) may produce creative narratives but struggle with world coherence, while symbolic systems ensure consistency but lack creative flexibility. We present IVIE (Incremental & Validated Interactive Experiences), a neuro-symbolic approach to generating complete and playable interactive fiction worlds from scratch. Building upon PAYADOR's neuro-symbolic framework, IVIE implements a four-stage incremental generation pipeline that delegates creative decisions--setting and character creation, puzzle design--to LLMs while grounding the world state through symbolic validation. The system generates worlds with interconnected locations, functional items, non-player characters, and coherent puzzles, all structured around a central goal-oriented architecture. Human evaluation shows the approach generates immersive, thematically coherent worlds with high player engagement. Results seem to indicate that the neuro-symbolic approach successfully balances flexibility with narrative coherence: symbolic validation grounds LLM generation without eliminating generative freedom. However, challenges remain: LLM inconsistencies occasionally bypass puzzle constraints, and objective validation gaps allow some structurally impossible goals. We identify key design considerations for future neurosymbolic interactive storytelling systems, particularly regarding LLM capabilities and their limitations.

</details>


### [49] [From Passive Generation to Investigation: A Proactive Scientific Peer Review Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13349)
*Haishuo Fang,Yue Feng,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ProReviewer frames automated peer review as a Markov Decision Process, using a structured review log to guide an LLM agent in proactively investigating papers. The 8B model, fine-tuned with RL, outperforms larger frontier LLMs and baselines across multiple quality dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based review methods lack the ability to proactively investigate suspicious parts of a paper by accumulating and acting on evidence, as human reviewers naturally do. This limits review depth and evidentiary support.

Method: The authors formulate proactive review as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and introduce ProReviewer, an agent that maintains a structured review log as a workspace to track evidence and intermediate findings. The agent is built on an 8B-parameter backbone, trained via supervised fine-tuning, and further optimized with reinforcement learning.

Result: ProReviewer achieves the highest average score across five quality dimensions, outperforming prompt-based methods with larger frontier LLMs by up to 39% and the strongest fine-tuned baseline by 16% relatively. It also achieves the highest win rates in human evaluation.

Conclusion: Enabling LLMs to perform proactive, evidence-driven investigation via an MDP framework with a structured review log significantly improves automated peer review quality, surpassing even much larger models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in automating scientific peer review. However, existing approaches often struggle to generate in-depth reviews supported by concrete evidence. We argue that a key limitation is the lack of flexibility to proactively investigate suspicious parts of a paper based on accumulated evidence, as human reviewers do. In this paper, we explore how to enable an LLM-based review agent to perform such proactive investigation. We find that this can be naturally formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), and propose ProReviewer, a scientific peer review agent that proactively reviews a paper guided by a maintained, structured review log. The structured review log serves as a workspace for the agent to track evidence and intermediate findings collected during review. Experiments show that ProReviewer with an 8B backbone, trained by supervised fine-tuning and optimized by reinforcement learning, achieves the highest average score across five quality dimensions, outperforming prompt-based methods with much larger frontier LLMs by up to 39% and the strongest fine-tuned baseline by 16% relatively. It also attains the highest win rates against baselines in human evaluation.

</details>


### [50] [An End-to-End Hybrid Framework for Rumour Detection in Low-Resources Algerian Dialect](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13411)
*Dihia Lanasri,Fatima Benbarek*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper proposes an end-to-end hybrid framework for rumour detection in Algerian dialectal social media content, leveraging a custom annotated dataset, transliteration pipelines, and domain-specific pretrained transformers, achieving an F1-score of 0.84.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rumour spread on social media is a growing problem, but detecting it in the Algerian context is particularly challenging due to the informal, code-switched nature of dialectal Arabic, scarcity of annotated resources, and poor performance of standard Arabic NLP tools on dialectal text.

Method: The authors construct a domain-specific annotated dataset combining real social media posts, synthetic data, and the FASSILA corpus, using similarity-based automatic labeling. A transliteration pipeline generates parallel Arabic-script and Arabizi datasets. They evaluate classical ML, deep learning, transformer-based, and hybrid models, with the best approach combining transformer embeddings with a classical classifier. Domain-specific pretraining on social media text is compared against larger models trained on formal Arabic.

Result: The hybrid model (transformer embeddings + classical classifier) achieves the best F1-score of 0.84. Domain-specific pretraining on social media data proves more impactful than model size, outperforming larger models trained on formal Arabic corpora.

Conclusion: Rumour detection in low-resource Algerian dialect settings is feasible. The findings highlight that domain adaptation through targeted pretraining is more critical than scaling model size, and the proposed framework offers a viable solution for under-resourced dialectal Arabic NLP tasks.

Abstract: The rapid growth of social media has intensified the spread of rumours. This issue is more challenging in the Algerian context due to the informal and code-switched nature of dialectal content, the scarcity of annotated resources, and the limited effectiveness of standard Arabic NLP tools on dialect text.
  This paper presents an end-to-end rumour detection hybrid framework for Algerian dialect social media content. We build a domain-specific annotated dataset by combining real social media posts, synthetic data, and the FASSILA corpus, with automatic labeling based on a similarity-based annotation process. A transliteration pipeline is also introduced to generate parallel datasets in Arabic script and Arabizi.
  We evaluate multiple approaches, including classical machine learning, deep learning, transformers, and hybrid models. Experimental results show that a hybrid approach combining transformer embeddings with a classical classifier achieves the best performance, reaching an F1-score of 0.84. We also find that domain-specific pre-training is more important than model size, with social media-trained models outperforming larger models trained on formal Arabic corpora.
  These results demonstrate the feasibility of rumour detection in low-resource Algerian dialect settings.

</details>


### [51] [S-GBT: Smooth Growth Bound Tensor for Certified Robustness Against Word Substitution Attacks in NLP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13439)
*Mohammed Bouri,Mohammed Erradi,Adnane Saoud*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT), a second-order defense against word substitution attacks that bounds Hessian elements element-wise, adding a curvature regularization term to training. By controlling both gradient (first-order) and its variation (second-order), S-GBT achieves up to 23.4% improvement in certified robust accuracy over prior methods on LSTM and CNN architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing defenses against word substitution attacks focus on first-order sensitivity (gradient magnitude) but overlook curvature — how sensitivity itself changes. When gradients vary sharply, models remain vulnerable despite first-order defenses. The authors argue that controlling both the gradient and its variation (second-order effects) is necessary for tighter certified robustness.

Method: The authors propose the Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT), a second-order method that computes element-wise bounds on the Hessian matrix. A regularization term minimizing these bounds is integrated directly into the training objective. The output change under word substitution is bounded by both a linear (first-order) and a quadratic (second-order) term. The method is derived for both LSTM and CNN architectures, with formal theoretical proofs provided for the resulting robustness bounds.

Result: S-GBT yields up to 23.4% improvement in certified robust accuracy compared to prior first-order-only methods across multiple benchmark datasets, while maintaining competitive clean accuracy. This demonstrates that joint first- and second-order regularization significantly tightens certified robustness against word substitution attacks.

Conclusion: Controlling both gradient magnitude and its variation (curvature) is a promising direction for building more robust NLP models. The S-GBT approach, by regularizing both first- and second-order sensitivities, provides tighter certified robustness bounds than methods focusing solely on first-order sensitivity.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in Natural Language Processing (NLP), models remain vulnerable to word substitution attacks. Most existing defenses focus on first order sensitivity and measure how much the output changes when the input is slightly perturbed. However, they ignore how this sensitivity evolves, which is described by curvature. When gradients vary sharply, models can still fail. This paper introduces the Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT), a second order method that bounds the Hessian element-wise, for which we provide formal theoretical proofs on the resulting robustness bounds. A regularization term is added during training to minimize these bounds. This yields tighter certified robustness against word substitution attacks. The change in the output under word substitution is bounded by both a linear term and a quadratic term. S-GBT is derived for two architectures: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). The method is integrated directly into the training objective. Its effectiveness is evaluated on multiple benchmark datasets. The results show that combining first and second order regularization improves certified robust accuracy by up to 23.4% compared to prior methods, while clean accuracy remains competitive. These findings indicate that controlling both the gradient and its variation is a promising direction for building more robust models.

</details>


### [52] [Ontology Memory-Augmented ASR Correction for Long Text-Speech Interleaved Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13464)
*Xinxin Li,Huiyao Chen,Meishan Zhang,Yunxin Li,Zulong Chen,Zhibo Ren,Xiaoqing Dong Baotian Hu,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes an ontology memory-augmented framework for ASR correction in long text-speech interleaved conversations, organizing dialogue history into retrievable nodes (entities, terminology, ASR confusions, semantic relations). Outperforms direct correction baselines on 9/10 backbone-setting pairs on the new RAMC-Corr benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ASR correction in long, interleaved text-speech conversations suffers from sparse evidence buried in redundant and noisy dialogue history. Existing methods that use only the current hypothesis or raw concatenated history fail to effectively locate context-grounded correction evidence.

Method: An ontology memory framework that dynamically organizes preceding interaction history into a structured, updatable memory. Nodes store entities, terminology, surface variants, potential ASR confusions, and semantic relations, enabling retrievable context-grounded correction. The authors also construct RAMC-Corr, a long-range ASR correction dataset from MAGIC-RAMC.

Result: The proposed method improves over direct correction in 9 out of 10 paired backbone-setting combinations on RAMC-Corr and yields more selective, evidence-grounded corrections for context-dependent ASR errors.

Conclusion: Structured ontology memory effectively addresses the long-range context challenge in ASR correction for interleaved conversations, outperforming direct correction approaches and producing more grounded corrections.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) correction has traditionally focused on isolated utterances or short local contexts. However, as text and speech become increasingly interleaved in long interactions, ASR correction requires conversation-level contextual evidence. Existing ASR correction methods often rely on the current hypothesis or concatenate raw dialogue history. In such contexts, sparse correction evidence can be difficult to locate amid redundancy and noise. Addressing these challenges, we propose an ontology memory-augmented ASR correction framework for long text-speech interleaved conversations. The framework organizes preceding interaction history into a dynamically updatable ontology memory, where entities, terminology, surface variants, potential ASR confusions, and semantic relations are stored as retrievable nodes for context-grounded correction. To evaluate this setting, we construct RAMC-Corr, a dataset derived from MAGIC-RAMC for long-range ASR correction with grounded context. Experiments on RAMC-Corr show that our method improves over direct correction in 9 out of 10 paired backbone-setting combinations and encourages more selective and evidence-grounded corrections for context-dependent ASR errors.

</details>


### [53] [Leveraging Audio-LLMs to Filter Speech-to-Speech Training Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13507)
*Qixu Chen,Satoshi Nakamura*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy uses a lightweight ranker to generate pseudo-labels, then trains an audio LLM to filter noisy speech pairs directly from raw audio, improving S2ST by up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale mined speech corpora for end-to-end S2ST contain noise, misalignment, and semantic errors. Filtering is essential, but manual labeling is infeasible at scale. The authors aim to develop a fully audio-based filtering model that requires no manual labels.

Method: A two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy: (1) A lightweight ranker scores noisy speech pairs using acoustic and semantic features, generating keep/drop pseudo-labels. (2) These pseudo-labels supervise an audio large language model (Audio LLM) that learns to predict keep/drop decisions directly from raw paired speech, jointly capturing acoustic fidelity and cross-lingual semantic consistency.

Result: Experiments on CVSS-C and SpeechMatrix benchmarks show consistent improvements over unfiltered training baselines, with gains up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU for end-to-end S2ST tasks.

Conclusion: Training an audio-language model via Rank-to-Distill provides an effective, scalable, and label-free approach to filtering noisy mined speech data, significantly boosting S2ST performance.

Abstract: Large-scale mined corpora provide abundant training data for end-to-end speech-to-speech translation (S2ST) but may contain noise, misalignment, and semantic errors. Filtering noisy data is crucial to maintain robust speech translation performance. We study how to train an audio-language model to make keep/drop decisions on paired speech directly from audio. To obtain reliable supervision without manual labels, we adopt a scalable two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy. A lightweight ranker generates keep/drop pseudo-labels from noisy speech pairs, then trains an audio large language model to predict keep/drop directly from raw paired speech. The resulting model jointly captures acoustic fidelity and cross-lingual semantic consistency for the selection of speech-conditioned data. Experiments on CVSS-C and SpeechMatrix show consistent improvements over unfiltered training, yielding up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU for end-to-end S2ST.

</details>


### [54] [When Does Mixing Help? Analyzing Query Embedding Interpolation in Multilingual Dense Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13537)
*Tongyao Zhu,Chao-Ming Huang,Min-Yen Kan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A ratio-controlled study on mMARCO reveals that mixing query-language embeddings predictably affects dense retrieval: optimal mixing ratios outperform monolingual queries in most non-English settings, but English-document indices are best served by pure English. English is the strongest mixing partner, and gains correlate negatively with typological distance. These patterns generalize across model families and scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mixed-language queries are common in multilingual communities, yet the sensitivity of dense retrievers to such queries is poorly understood. The authors aim to systematically characterize how embedding-level language mixing affects retrieval performance across language pairs.

Method: The authors conduct a ratio-controlled study on mMARCO using BGE-M3. They construct mixed queries via embedding-level interpolation of parallel monolingual query embeddings at varying mixing proportions, then evaluate retrieval performance against monolingual document indices across 105 language-pair configurations.

Result: An optimal mixing ratio outperforms the best monolingual endpoint in 88/105 cases. A distinct asymmetry emerges: mixing uniformly helps when retrieving from non-English indices, but English indices are best served by pure English queries. English is the strongest mixing partner for every non-English document language. When controlling for English dominance, mixing gains correlate negatively with typological distance. These patterns are validated as robust across model families and scales.

Conclusion: Language-mix sensitivity in dense retrieval is structured and predictable rather than idiosyncratic. The findings offer practical guidance for multilingual retrieval system design and highlight the outsized role of English as a mixing partner.

Abstract: While mixed-language querying is ubiquitous in multilingual communities, the sensitivity of dense retrievers to such queries remains poorly understood. We present a ratio-controlled study on mMARCO that systematically evaluates retrieval performance by varying the mixing proportion of parallel query translations via embedding-level mixing -- constructing mixed queries as an interpolation of monolingual embeddings. Experiments with BGE-M3 demonstrate that an optimal mixing ratio outperforms the best monolingual endpoint in 88/105 cases. We uncover a distinct asymmetry driven by English dominance: mixing is uniformly beneficial when retrieving from non-English document indices, whereas indices containing English are best served by pure English queries. Furthermore, English acts as the strongest mixing partner for every non-English document language. Finally, when controlling for English dominance, mixing gains correlate negatively with typological distance. We conclude that language-mix sensitivity is structured and predictable, and we validate the robustness of these patterns across model families and scales.

</details>


### [55] [ArogyaSutra: A Multi-Agent Framework for Multimodal Medical Reasoning in Indic Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13572)
*Tanmoy Kanti Halder,Akash Ghosh,Subhadip Baidya,Arijit Roy,Sriparna Saha*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ArogyaBodha is a large multilingual multimodal medical QA dataset covering 8 languages and diverse clinical domains, paired with ArogyaSutra, an actor-critic multi-agent framework using tool grounding and dual-memory for stepwise medical reasoning, significantly improving accuracy in low-resource Indic language healthcare settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs perform poorly in multilingual, low-resource healthcare contexts — particularly in rural India, where patients use native Indic languages and multimodal inputs (e.g., medical images). English-centric models fail to provide equitable AI-driven healthcare access in these scenarios.

Method: The authors construct ArogyaBodha, a dataset from 8 heterogeneous sources spanning 31 body systems, 6 imaging modalities, and 21 clinical domains across English and 7 Indic languages. They then propose ArogyaSutra, an actor-critic multi-agent framework featuring tool grounding, dual-memory mechanisms for stepwise reasoning-aware decision making, and distillation from stored actor-critic simulation trajectories.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that ArogyaBodha and ArogyaSutra jointly improve multilingual medical reasoning accuracy across all Indic languages, with ablation studies confirming the contribution of each architectural component.

Conclusion: The proposed dataset and multi-agent framework effectively bridge the gap in multilingual multimodal medical reasoning for low-resource Indic languages, advancing equitable AI healthcare assistance. Code and dataset are publicly released.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promising reasoning capabilities in general domains, yet their performance remains limited in specialized settings such as healthcare, especially in multilingual and low-resource scenarios. This gap is critical in regions like rural India, where patients often express complex medical queries in native Indic languages and rely on multimodal inputs such as medical images. Existing English-centric MLLMs struggle to support such use cases, limiting equitable access to AI-driven healthcare assistance. To address this challenge, we introduce ArogyaBodha, a large-scale multilingual multimodal medical question-answer dataset constructed from eight heterogeneous sources, covering 31 body systems, six imaging modalities, and 21 clinical domains across English and seven major Indian languages. We further propose ArogyaSutra, an actor-critic-based multi-agent framework that integrates tool grounding with dual-memory mechanisms for step-wise, reasoning-aware decision making, and uses stored actor-critic simulation trajectories for distillation. Experiments show that our dataset and framework improve multilingual medical reasoning accuracy across all Indic languages, with ablations validating the contribution of each component. The source code and dataset are available at: https://iitp-cse.github.io/ ArogyaSutra/

</details>


### [56] [LabVLA: Grounding Vision-Language-Action Models in Scientific Laboratories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13578)
*Baochang Ren,Xinjie Liu,Xi Chen,Yanshuo Liu,Chenxi Li,Daqi Gao,Zeqin Su,Jintao Xing,Zirui Xue,Rui Li,Xiangyu Zhao,Shuofei Qiao,Minting Pan,Wangmeng Zuo,Lei Bai,Dongzhan Zhou,Ningyu Zhang,Huajun Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses the gap between AI reasoning and physical lab execution by introducing RoboGenesis (a simulation-based data engine for generating lab robotics demonstrations) and LabVLA (a two-stage VLA model using FAST pretraining and flow matching on Qwen3-VL-4B). LabVLA achieves state-of-the-art success rates on the LabUtopia benchmark in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems can reason about experiments (reading literature, generating hypotheses, planning protocols), but the physical execution of protocols at the bench remains human-dependent. Existing VLA models are trained on household/tabletop tasks and fail in laboratory settings involving instruments, transparent liquids, and fixed protocol workflows. Two central bottlenecks are identified: (1) lack of lab-specific supervision data, and (2) the need for a unified learning framework accommodating diverse robot embodiments.

Method: Data Engine (RoboGenesis): A simulation-based workflow that composes lab workflows from atomic skills, validates/filters rollouts, and exports structured demonstrations across supported robot profiles. Policy (LabVLA): A two-stage training recipe — Stage 1 uses FAST action token pretraining to make the Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct backbone action-aware before continuous control learning; Stage 2 employs flow matching posttraining, attaching a DiT action expert under knowledge insulation. Evaluation is performed on the LabUtopia benchmark under both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

Result: LabVLA achieves the highest average success rate among all evaluated baselines on the LabUtopia benchmark, outperforming alternatives in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution conditions.

Conclusion: RoboGenesis and LabVLA together address the dual bottlenecks of lab-specific data scarcity and embodiment diversity, enabling robust vision-language-action policies for autonomous scientific experimentation. The two-stage training strategy (FAST pretraining + flow matching with knowledge insulation) proves effective for transferring VLM capabilities to continuous robot control in lab settings.

Abstract: Scientific laboratories increasingly rely on AI systems to reason about experiments, but the physical act of doing science remains largely outside their reach. AI can help read literature, generate hypotheses, and plan protocols, yet the execution of those protocols at the bench still requires a human operator. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models provide one possible interface between written protocols and robot execution, but existing policies are trained mostly on household and tabletop demonstrations and rarely encounter the instruments, transparent liquids, or fixed protocol workflows found in scientific laboratories. Closing this gap requires both laboratory-specific supervision and a unified learning framework that can accommodate the diverse robot embodiments used to execute experimental protocols. We therefore identify data and embodiment as central bottlenecks alongside model design. To address the data side, we build RoboGenesis, a simulation-based workflow and data engine that composes configured laboratory workflows from atomic skills, validates and filters rollouts, and exports structured demonstrations across supported robot profiles. On the policy side, we present LabVLA, trained with a two-stage recipe: FAST action token pretraining first makes the Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct backbone action aware before any continuous control is learned, and flow matching posttraining then attaches a DiT action expert under knowledge insulation. On the LabUtopia benchmark, LabVLA achieves the highest average success rate among all evaluated baselines under both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

</details>


### [57] [One Polluted Page Is Enough: Evaluating Web Content Pollution in Generative Recommenders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13610)
*Minghao Luo,Liang Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FORGE benchmark reveals search-augmented LLMs are highly vulnerable to recommending fake products when retrieval results contain polluted web content, with fooled rates up to 73.8% under full top-3 pollution. Reasoning exacerbates vulnerability by fabricating spurious justifications, and common defenses like skepticism prompting and consensus filtering prove unreliable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As search-augmented LLMs increasingly mediate consumer product recommendations, a critical risk emerges: these models may retrieve and propagate polluted web content (e.g., fake reviews, promotional pages) that promotes counterfeit or non-existent products. No existing benchmark systematically measures this vulnerability, motivating the creation of FORGE.

Method: The authors introduce FORGE, a benchmark that simulates web-content pollution by locally rewriting real product information in retrieved web pages into fake products. It spans 225 real-world products across 15 categories and 5 consumer scenarios. The benchmark measures "fooled rate" — how often an LLM recommends the fake product after receiving polluted search results. Evaluated across 12 commercial and open-weights LLMs, with three defenses tested: skepticism prompting, model-prior consensus filtering, and cross-document evidence filtering.

Result: A single polluted page yields fooled rates up to 27%; full top-3 replacement raises this to 73.8%. Vulnerability varies by product category, correlating inversely with models' stable prior knowledge. Reasoning-capable models do not resist better; instead they generate spurious social proof (fabricated reviews, testimonials) to justify false recommendations. Skepticism prompting can backfire and increase vulnerability, while consensus filtering risks suppressing legitimate products.

Conclusion: Search-augmented LLMs are systemically vulnerable to web-content pollution, acting as unwitting promoters of fake products. Current defenses are insufficient — skepticism can worsen outcomes, and filtering introduces collateral damage to legitimate recommendations. The authors release FORGE as an open benchmark to facilitate further research.

Abstract: Search-augmented LLMs increasingly mediate everyday consumer recommendations by retrieving live web content. This creates a new risk: generative recommenders may consume polluted web content, such as fake reviews and promotional pages crafted to mislead recommendations. We ask: to what extent do search-augmented LLMs become unwitting promoters of fake products when consuming polluted retrieval results? To answer this, we introduce FORGE (Fake Online Recommendations in Generative Environments), a benchmark for measuring fake-product promotion under controlled web-content pollution. Given an upstream search result, FORGE locally rewrites real products in retrieved web pages into fake ones to simulate web-content pollution, and measures how often the LLM recommends the fake product. FORGE covers 225 real-world products across 15 categories and 5 consumer scenarios. Across 12 commercial and open-weights LLMs, all models are vulnerable: a single polluted page yields fooled rates of up to 27%, while the full top-3 replacement raises this to 73.8%. Vulnerability varies substantially across categories, increasing when models lack stable prior knowledge of the relevant products. Reasoning does not mitigate this vulnerability; instead, it often generates spurious social proof to justify false recommendations. We evaluate three defenses: skepticism prompting and consensus filtering (over model priors or cross-document evidence). Skepticism can exacerbate vulnerability, much like reasoning, while filtering risks suppressing legitimate products. We release FORGE at https://github.com/leoluolol/forge-benchmark.

</details>


### [58] [Beyond Uniform Tokens: Adaptive Compression for Time Series Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13624)
*Jialin Gan,Xin Qiu,Guangzhe Chen,Xue Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper proposes an adaptive token budgeting framework for time series language models that asymmetrically compresses TS tokens (via frequency-domain redundancy removal) and progressively prunes prompt tokens across layers, achieving up to 7.68× inference speedup with performance gains in 78% of evaluated settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time series tokens and prompt tokens have fundamentally different information structures, yet existing TS-LLMs treat them uniformly. TS tokens exhibit highly uneven spectral contributions (many share redundant frequency patterns, few carry critical temporal evidence), and prompt-token influence attenuates with model depth. This motivates asymmetric, structure-aware token compression rather than uniform processing.

Method: An adaptive token budgeting framework with two asymmetric mechanisms: (1) TS token compression that leverages frequency-domain structure to identify and prune spectrally redundant tokens while preserving critical ones; (2) progressive prompt-token reduction across layers, where prompt tokens are gradually dropped in deeper layers where their influence naturally decays. Both are governed by an adaptive budgeting strategy.

Result: Experiments across forecasting, classification, imputation, and anomaly detection tasks demonstrate up to 7.68× inference acceleration. Performance improvements are observed in 78% of evaluated settings, validating that asymmetric compression not only speeds up inference but also enhances task performance.

Conclusion: Asymmetric token compression — exploiting the distinct spectral properties of TS tokens and depth-attenuating influence of prompt tokens — is an effective and scalable strategy for building efficient TS foundation models, offering both substantial speedups and competitive or superior accuracy.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled time series (TS) analysis by jointly modeling numerical observations and textual context through a shared token interface. However, TS tokens and prompt tokens exhibit fundamentally different information structures, making uniform token processing inefficient. In this paper, we study token efficiency in TS language modeling from an asymmetric-token perspective. We show that TS tokens have highly uneven spectral contributions, where many tokens share redundant frequency patterns while a small subset preserves critical temporal evidence. We also observe that prompt-token influence attenuates with model depth, suggesting that full prompt retention across all layers is unnecessary. Based on these findings, we develop an adaptive token budgeting framework that compresses TS tokens via frequency-domain structure and progressively reduces prompt tokens across layers. Experiments across forecasting, classification, imputation, and anomaly detection demonstrate up to \textit{\textbf{7.68$\times$}} inference acceleration and performance gains in \textit{\textbf{78\%}} of evaluated settings, showing the effectiveness of asymmetric token compression for scalable TS foundation models.

</details>


### [59] [From Tokens to Faces: Investigating Discrete Speech Representations for 3D Facial Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13630)
*Pedro Correa,Olivier Perrotin,Samir Sadok,Paula Costa,Thomas Hueber*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper systematically evaluates four families of speech representations for 3D facial animation, finding that phonetic-class encoding benefits prediction accuracy. Based on label-based representations, the authors introduce an Audio-Visual Text-to-Speech (AVTTS) pipeline using discrete representations as a shared space for decoding both speech and 3D facial motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speech-driven 3D facial animation critically depends on the choice of speech representation, yet different representation families encode fundamentally different types of information (segmental, acoustic, semantic, phonetic). A systematic understanding of which representation properties best serve facial animation synthesis is lacking.

Method: The authors evaluate four speech representation families — SSL features, neural codec latents, ASR-style label-based spaces, and tokenized representations — across two facial decoders. They use objective metrics and perceptual evaluation for facial reconstruction quality, plus probing analyses linking tokenized representations to phonetic units and articulatory deformations.

Result: Representations that encode phonetic classes yield more accurate facial animation prediction. Both semantic (SSL) and label-based (ASR-style) representations achieve comparable facial animation quality. Tokenized representations correlate strongly with phonetic units and articulatory deformations.

Conclusion: Phonetic-class encoding is key for accurate speech-driven facial animation. Leveraging this insight, the proposed AVTTS pipeline demonstrates that discrete representations can serve as an effective shared latent space for jointly decoding speech and 3D facial motion.

Abstract: The choice of speech representation is critical in speech-driven 3D facial animation. Representations differ in what they encode: SSL features emphasize segmental and semantic cues, neural codecs yield latents optimized for acoustic reconstruction, and ASR-style objectives produce label-based spaces. We evaluate four speech representation families for 3D facial synthesis, comparing their facial reconstruction quality across two facial decoders using objective metrics and a perceptual evaluation. We additionally conduct probing analyses that relate tokenized representations to phonetic units and to articulatory deformations. We found that encoding phonetic classes is beneficial for accurate facial animation prediction on both semantic and label-based representations with comparable facial animation quality. From the latter, we introduce an Audio Visual Text-to-Speech (AVTTS) pipeline that leverages, as a shared space, discrete representations to decode speech and 3D facial motion.

</details>


### [60] [Operads for compositional reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13634)
*Nathaniel Bottman,Kyle Richardson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes operads as a rigorous mathematical framework for modeling question decomposition in LLM reasoning, defining a questions operad where operations are question templates and composition is substitution of sub-answers. It introduces operadic consistency—measuring agreement across partial collapses of a decomposition tree—as a new diagnostic metric, empirically validated in a companion paper to correlate strongly with accuracy and outperform temperature-based self-consistency baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question decomposition is a widely used but mathematically ungrounded strategy for improving LLM multi-step reasoning. The authors seek to provide rigorous formal foundations for this practice and to uncover new analytical tools from that formalization.

Method: The authors use operad theory—algebraic structures modeling many-in/one-out operations and their compositions. They define the questions operad Q, where operations are question templates and operadic composition encodes substituting sub-answers into parent questions. QA models are then interpreted as algebras over Q (structure-preserving maps from Q to a semantic domain). They introduce operadic consistency, which checks whether a model's answers agree when a decomposition tree is collapsed along different partial evaluation paths.

Result: The companion paper (Bottman, Liu, and Richardson, 2026) shows operadic consistency is strongly correlated with accuracy across twelve LLMs and four multi-hop QA datasets, outperforming standard temperature-based self-consistency baselines.

Conclusion: Operads provide the natural mathematical home for question decomposition, and invariants like operadic consistency open new directions for analyzing and improving the reliability of multi-step LLM reasoning.

Abstract: Question decomposition, i.e. breaking a complex query into simpler sub-queries whose answers are composed to produce a final answer, is a widely used strategy for improving LLM reasoning, yet it currently lacks a rigorous mathematical foundation. In this paper, we propose operads, mathematical structures that model many-in, one-out operations and compositions thereof, as a natural framework for describing question decomposition. We define the questions operad $Q$, in which operations correspond to question templates and composition corresponds to substitution of sub-answers, and show how QA models can be interpreted as algebras over $Q$. Beyond reframing existing practice, this operadic perspective points toward new methods, in particular a notion of operadic consistency, which measures whether a QA model's answers agree across the partial collapses of a question decomposition tree. Empirical evaluation of operadic consistency is reported in our companion paper (Bottman, Liu, and Richardson, 2026), which finds it strongly correlated with accuracy across twelve LLMs and four multi-hop QA datasets and outperforming standard temperature-based self-consistency baselines. We argue that operads are the natural mathematical home for question decomposition, and that invariants such as operadic consistency open new directions for analyzing and improving the reliability of multi-step reasoning.

</details>


### [61] [Recursive Agent Harnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13643)
*Elias Lumer,Sahil Sen,Kevin Paul,Vamse Kumar Subbiah*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Recursive Agent Harness (RAH), a code-first extension of recursive language models where the recursive unit is a full agent with filesystem tools, code execution, and planning. RAH significantly outperforms non-recursive baselines on long-context reasoning benchmarks, with gains attributable to the harness architecture rather than the underlying model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two independent trends — recursive language models (RLMs) for long-context reasoning and production coding agents spawning subagents at scale — converge on the same pattern: recursion as a strategy for complex task decomposition. The authors aim to formalize and evaluate this pattern where the recursive unit is elevated from a bare model call to a fully tooled agent harness.

Method: RAH frames recursion at the harness level rather than the model level. A parent agent generates and executes a script that spawns subagent harnesses in parallel for fine-grained workloads, using structured function calls for smaller subtasks. The authors evaluate RAH on Oolong-Synthetic (199 samples, 13 context-length buckets up to 4M tokens), comparing against Codex and RLM baselines with GPT-5 as the fixed backbone, and also test with Claude Sonnet 4.5.

Result: With GPT-5 as backbone, RAH improves the Codex coding-agent baseline from 71.75% to 81.36% on Oolong-Synthetic. With Claude Sonnet 4.5, RAH achieves 89.77%. The authors attribute the gain to the harness design rather than model capability.

Conclusion: Harness-level recursion, where agents spawn and orchestrate subagents with tools, is a powerful and generalizable pattern that substantially advances long-context reasoning performance beyond what model-level recursion alone can achieve.

Abstract: Recursive language models (RLMs) showed that recursion over model calls is an effective strategy for long-context reasoning, and production coding agents have begun to write code that spawns subagents at scale, most recently in Anthropic's dynamic workflows. We name and study the pattern between these two lines of work, where the recursive unit is a full agent harness with filesystem tools, code execution, and planning rather than a model call with no tools. We call this the Recursive Agent Harness (RAH) and frame it as harness recursion, the code-first extension to the model recursion of RLMs. A parent agent generates and runs an executable script that spawns subagent harnesses in parallel for fine-grained workloads and uses structured function calls for small subtasks. We provide a controlled evaluation on long-context reasoning. With the backbone held fixed at GPT-5 to match the published Codex and RLM baselines, RAH improves the Codex coding-agent baseline from 71.75% to 81.36% on Oolong-Synthetic (199 samples, 13 context-length buckets up to 4M tokens), a gain attributable to the harness rather than the model. With a stronger backbone, Claude Sonnet 4.5, the same design reaches 89.77%.

</details>


### [62] [SkMTEB: Slovak Massive Text Embedding Benchmark and Model Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13647)
*Marek Šuppa,Andrej Ridzik,Daniel Hládek,Natália Kňažeková,Viktória Ondrejová*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces SkMTEB, the first comprehensive Slovak text embedding benchmark (31 datasets, 7 task types). Evaluates 31 models, finding multilingual instruction-tuned models perform best. Develops two efficient, open-source Slovak embedding models (e5-sk-small and e5-sk-large) via vocabulary trimming and fine-tuning, achieving competitive performance with proprietary APIs while being locally deployable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Slovak is a low-resource West Slavic language with insufficient benchmark coverage for text embeddings — existing multilingual benchmarks offer limited depth. There is also a lack of efficient, locally-deployable embedding models tailored for Slovak, which is needed for applications like semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).

Method: 1) Construct SkMTEB, a benchmark with 31 datasets spanning 7 task types (e.g., classification, clustering, pair classification, reranking, retrieval, STS, summarization). 2) Evaluate 31 embedding models on SkMTEB. 3) Develop e5-sk-small (45M) and e5-sk-large (365M) by applying vocabulary trimming to Multilingual E5 models and fine-tuning on Slovak data, achieving parameter reductions of up to 62%.

Result: Large instruction-tuned multilingual models achieve the strongest performance on SkMTEB. Slovak-specific NLU models transfer poorly to embedding tasks. The e5-sk-small and e5-sk-large models achieve competitive performance with proprietary APIs despite significant size reduction, while remaining fully locally deployable.

Conclusion: SkMTEB fills a critical gap for Slovak embedding evaluation. Vocabulary trimming plus fine-tuning offers an effective, replicable strategy for building compact, high-quality embeddings for under-resourced languages. All artifacts (benchmark, models, datasets, code) are released openly to facilitate adoption and replication for other low-resource languages.

Abstract: We introduce SkMTEB, the first comprehensive MTEB-style text embedding benchmark for Slovak, a low-resource West Slavic language, comprising 31 datasets across 7 task types -- nearly 4$\times$ the depth of existing multilingual benchmark coverage for Slovak. Our evaluation of 31 embedding models reveals that large instruction-tuned multilingual models achieve the strongest performance, while existing Slovak-specific models trained for NLU tasks transfer poorly to embedding tasks. To address the need for efficient, locally-deployable Slovak embeddings, we develop \texttt{e5-sk-small} (45M parameters) and \texttt{e5-sk-large} (365M) by applying vocabulary trimming and fine-tuning to Multilingual E5 models. Despite size reductions of up to 62\%, our open-source models achieve competitive performance with proprietary APIs while remaining locally deployable for semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). We release the benchmark, models, datasets, and code openly, hoping our approach offers a replicable path for other under-resourced languages.

</details>


### [63] [Operadic consistency: a label-free signal for compositional reasoning failures in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13649)
*Nathaniel Bottman,Yinhong Liu,Kyle Richardson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces operadic consistency (OC), a novel confidence signal for detecting LLM reasoning failures without ground-truth labels. OC checks whether a model's direct answer to a compositional query matches the answer obtained by composing answers to sub-questions derived from a decomposition. Across 12 LLMs and 4 multi-hop QA datasets, OC achieves Pearson r ∈ [0.86, 0.94] with accuracy, outperforming or complementing established baselines like chain-of-thought self-consistency and semantic entropy, and yields significant selective-prediction improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing confidence baselines for detecting LLM reasoning failures at inference time (e.g., self-consistency, semantic entropy, P(True)) rely on within-question sampling and self-evaluation. These methods struggle on certain complex reasoning datasets. Operad theory—the mathematics of compositional systems—suggests a complementary structural diagnostic: an LLM's direct answer to a compositional query should agree with the answer derived by composing answers to explicitly decomposed sub-questions. This compositional constraint is underexploited as a failure-detection signal.

Method: The authors propose operadic consistency (OC): for a given multi-hop question, the question is decomposed into sub-questions (tree-structured), each is answered independently, and the sub-answers are composed to produce a final answer. OC measures whether this composed answer agrees with the model's direct answer to the original question. Agreement is treated as a per-question confidence signal. The decomposition can be externally provided (for instruction-tuned models) or extracted from the model's own chain-of-thought (for frontier thinking models). OC is compared against baselines including chain-of-thought self-consistency (CoT-SC), semantic entropy, and P(True), using Pearson correlation with accuracy, regression analysis, and selective-prediction metrics (AUARC, AUROC).

Result: OC is strongly correlated with accuracy across all 12 models and 4 datasets (Pearson r ∈ [0.86, 0.94], all p ≤ 0.0004); it is the only signal with r ≥ 0.85 uniformly. CoT-SC matches OC on HotpotQA (r=0.93) and DROP (r=0.87) but drops to r ≈ 0.45 on MuSiQue and StrategyQA. Per-question regression shows OC contributes unique information beyond CoT-SC and semantic entropy (cluster-robust p ≤ 10⁻¹⁶). At equal K=3 budget, OC yields AUARC lifts of +0.086 to +0.096 and AUROC lifts of +0.092 to +0.164 over tuned CoT-SC, with 95% CIs excluding zero. On frontier thinking models with self-extracted decompositions, positive selective-prediction lift is observed on all 16 tested cells, with 12 of 16 having 95% CIs excluding zero.

Conclusion: Operadic consistency provides a structurally grounded, complementary signal for detecting LLM reasoning failures. It strongly correlates with accuracy, captures information orthogonal to sampling-based baselines, and yields practical selective-prediction gains at equal cost. The approach is viable even when decompositions are extracted from the model's own chain of thought, making it applicable to frontier reasoning models without external decomposition pipelines.

Abstract: Detecting LLM reasoning failures at inference time without ground-truth labels has motivated a wide range of confidence baselines, including self-consistency, semantic entropy, and P(True), built on within-question sampling and self-evaluation. Operad theory, the formalism for systems built by iterated substitution, suggests a complementary diagnostic: a model's direct answer to a compositional query should agree with the answer it produces by composing a stated decomposition of the same query. We instantiate this idea as operadic consistency (OC), a per-question signal. Across twelve instruction-tuned LLMs (4B to 671B parameters, open-weights and closed-source) on four multi-hop QA datasets, OC is strongly correlated with accuracy on every dataset (Pearson $r \in [0.86, 0.94]$, all $p \leq 0.0004$), and is the only signal we evaluate with $r \geq 0.85$ uniformly across all four datasets. Chain-of-thought self-consistency (CoT-SC; Wang et al., 2023) matches OC on HotpotQA and DROP ($r = 0.93, 0.87$) but drops to $r \approx 0.45$ on MuSiQue and StrategyQA. At the per-question level, OC contributes information beyond CoT-SC and semantic entropy on every dataset (cluster-robust $p \leq 10^{-16}$ for the OC coefficient), and the conclusion is robust to additionally controlling for constructed decomposition-aware baselines ($p \leq 10^{-13}$). The same signal yields selective-prediction improvements (accuracy at fixed coverage) over a tuned CoT-SC baseline at the equal-cost $K = 3$ budget (AUARC lifts of +0.086 to +0.096 and AUROC lifts of +0.092 to +0.164; 95% CIs exclude zero on every cell). On five frontier thinking models, where the decomposition is extracted from the model's own chain of thought, the same equal-cost comparison gives positive selective-prediction point-estimate lift on all 16 (dataset, budget, metric) cells tested, with 95% CIs excluding zero on 12 of the 16.

</details>


### [64] [HyperTool: Beyond Step-Wise Tool Calls for Tool-Augmented Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13663)
*Yaxin Du,Yifan Zhou,Yujie Ge,Jiajun Wang,Xianghe Pang,Shuo Tang,Tuney Zheng,Bryan Dai,Jian Yang,Siheng Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HyperTool proposes a new tool interface for LLM agents where multi-step tool workflows are encapsulated into a single code-block invocation, reducing context consumption and eliminating the need for the model to manage low-level dataflow in the reasoning trace. Trained on synthesized trajectories, HyperTool significantly boosts multi-step tool-use accuracy on MCP-Universe benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agents perform step-wise atomic tool calls, exposing each invocation, observation, and value transfer in the main reasoning trace. This creates an execution-granularity mismatch: locally deterministic tool workflows are unfolded into repeated model-visible decisions, wasting context length and forcing the model to micromanage low-level dataflow rather than focusing on high-level reasoning.

Method: The authors introduce HyperTool, a unified executable MCP-style tool interface. A model invokes HyperTool by writing a code block that can call existing tools via their original schemas, manipulate return values, and pass intermediate results locally — all folded into a single outer call, hiding deterministic subroutines from the main trace. Training data is synthesized by generating HyperTool-format trajectories from cross-tool compositional tasks and verifying them in real MCP environments.

Result: On the MCP-Universe benchmark, HyperTool improves average accuracy from 15.69% to 35.29% on Qwen3-32B and from 9.93% to 33.33% on Qwen3-8B. It also surpasses GPT-OSS and Kimi-k2.5 on average accuracy.

Conclusion: HyperTool demonstrates that raising the granularity of model-visible tool execution — from atomic step-wise calls to compound code-block invocations — can substantially improve multi-step tool use in LLM agents, particularly for compositional tasks requiring cross-tool orchestration.

Abstract: Tool-augmented LLM agents commonly rely on step-wise atomic tool calls, where each invocation, observation, and value transfer is exposed in the main reasoning trace. This creates an \emph{execution-granularity mismatch}: locally deterministic tool workflows are unfolded into repeated model-visible decisions, consuming context and forcing the model to manage low-level dataflow in the trace. We introduce \textbf{HyperTool}, a unified executable MCP-style tool interface that changes the model-visible unit of tool execution. A model invokes HyperTool with a code block that can call existing tools through their original schemas, manipulate returned values, and pass intermediate results locally, folding deterministic tool subroutines into a single outer call. To train models to use this interface, we synthesize HyperTool-format trajectories from cross-tool compositional tasks and verify them in real MCP environments. On MCP-Universe, HyperTool improves average accuracy from 15.69\% to 35.29\% on Qwen3-32B and from 9.93\% to 33.33\% on Qwen3-8B, and surpass GPT-OSS and Kimi-k2.5 on average accuracy, showing that our HyperTool can substantially improve multi-step tool use.

</details>


### [65] [Influcoder: Distilling Decoders' Gradient Influence Rankings into an Encoder for Data Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13668)
*Dimitri Kachler,Damien Sileo,Pascal Denis*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Influcoder proposes a fast, cost-effective method for influence-function-based Data Attribution on large-scale LLM training datasets, addressing the speed and storage limitations of existing influence function approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM capabilities grow, there is increasing demand to curate high-quality training datasets by identifying which training samples influence specific model behaviors (e.g., toxic outputs). Existing influence-function-based Data Attribution methods are effective but suffer from prohibitive processing speed and storage requirements, making them impractical for large-scale datasets.

Method: The authors propose Influcoder, a novel approach designed to enable influence-based Data Attribution at scale with improved computational efficiency and reduced storage footprint, though specific architectural or algorithmic details are not elaborated in this abstract.

Result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract; the paper claims Influcoder is a quick and cost-effective solution for influence-based DA at scale.

Conclusion: Influence functions can be adapted for practical, large-scale Data Attribution through efficient design choices, enabling fast and storage-friendly identification of influential training samples in LLM datasets.

Abstract: With the growth of LLMs' (Large Language Models) capabilities, there has been an increasing push to curate high quality datasets by filtering samples in the training data. In general, Data Attribution (DA) methods aim to estimate how individual samples in a training dataset can precondition a model to generate certain outputs. As an example, one might be interested in which samples in the data could be the source of toxic behavior after training the LLM. Many methods quantify this conditioning through the paradigm of influence functions. While methods of this family are effective in its function, they lack the necessary processing speed and storage compactness to be practically implemented on large datasets. We propose a method, Influcoder, as a quick and cost-effective approach to influence-based Data Attribution at scale.

</details>


### [66] [Learning to Reason by Analogy via Retrieval-Augmented Reinforcement Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13680)
*Zilin Xiao,Qi Ma,Chun-cheng Jason Chen,Xintao Chen,Avinash Atreya,Hanjie Chen,Vicente Ordonez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RA-RFT trains LLMs to reason by analogy: a retriever is trained via gold-relevance distillation to rank contexts by expected reasoning benefit (not semantic similarity), then the policy model is fine-tuned with RL using retrieved analogous demonstrations, yielding significant gains on math reasoning benchmarks over standard RL fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional RAG relies on lexical/semantic similarity, which fails for complex reasoning: similar problems may need different strategies, while superficially different problems may share reasoning patterns. There is a need for retrieval that identifies contexts based on reasoning benefit rather than surface-level similarity.

Method: RA-RFT is a two-stage post-training framework: (1) Gold-relevance distillation trains a retriever to score contexts by expected reasoning utility using verifiable outcome rewards as supervision; (2) The policy model undergoes reinforcement fine-tuning (e.g., GRPO) with retrieved analogous demonstrations as prompts, learning to exploit reasoning traces under outcome-based reward signals.

Result: RA-RFT consistently outperforms standard RL fine-tuning (GRPO) on challenging math benchmarks. On AIME 2025, it improves average@32 accuracy by +7.1 points for Qwen3-1.7B and +2.8 points for Qwen3-4B. Reasoning-aware retrieval also surfaces complementary solution strategies that serve as distinct reasoning scaffolds.

Conclusion: Reasoning-aware retrieval is a complementary improvement axis orthogonal to advances in reward design or training curricula. RA-RFT demonstrates that teaching models to reason by analogy via retrieval of reasoning-relevant contexts yields substantial and consistent gains.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has become a standard mechanism for grounding language models in external knowledge, yet conventional retrieval based on lexical or semantic similarity is poorly suited for complex reasoning tasks: a semantically similar problem may demand an entirely different solution strategy, while a superficially different problem may share the same underlying reasoning pattern. We propose Retrieval-Augmented Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RA-RFT), a post-training framework that teaches language models to reason by analogy. RA-RFT uses gold-relevance distillation to train a retriever that ranks contexts by expected reasoning benefit rather than semantic overlap, and then fine-tunes the policy model via reinforcement fine-tuning methods with retrieved analogous demonstrations, so the model learns to leverage reasoning traces under verifiable outcome rewards. We further analyze the diversity of retrieved contexts and find that reasoning-aware retrieval surfaces complementary solution strategies that provide distinct reasoning scaffolds for individual problems. Across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, RA-RFT consistently outperforms standard reinforcement fine-tuning methods. For example, it improves AIME 2025 average@32 accuracy by 7.1 and 2.8 points over GRPO for Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B respectively -- suggesting that reasoning-aware retrieval is a complementary axis of improvement and orthogonal to advances in reward design or training curricula.

</details>


### [67] [EvoArena: Tracking Memory Evolution for Robust LLM Agents in Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13681)
*Jundong Xu,Qingchuan Li,Jiaying Wu,Yihuai Lan,Shuyue Stella Li,Huichi Zhou,Bowen Jiang,Lei Wang,Jun Wang,Anh Tuan Luu,Caiming Xiong,Hae Won Park,Bryan Hooi,Zhiyuan Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoArena is a benchmark for evaluating LLM agents in dynamic environments with progressive updates across terminal, software, and social domains. The authors propose EvoMem, a patch-based memory that records structured update histories, improving agent performance on evolving tasks and standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most LLM agent evaluations assume static environments, but real-world deployment is dynamic, requiring agents to continually adapt to changing conditions. Existing benchmarks fail to model this environmental evolution, creating a gap between evaluation and real-world requirements.

Method: The authors introduce EvoArena, a benchmark suite modeling environment changes as progressive update sequences across three domains: terminal (command-line), software (GUI), and social (preference shifts). They also propose EvoMem, a patch-based memory paradigm that stores memory evolution as structured update histories, allowing agents to reason about changes through memory diffs rather than retaining only final states.

Result: Current agents average only 39.6% accuracy on EvoArena. EvoMem yields a 1.5% average gain on EvoArena and improves GAIA by 6.1% and LoCoMo by 4.8%. It also improves chain-level accuracy by 3.7%, where agents must complete consecutive sequences of related evolutionary subtasks. Mechanistic analysis shows EvoMem enhances evidence capture in memory, preserving complete evolving environment states.

Conclusion: Modeling environmental evolution is critical for both evaluation and memory design in reliable LLM agent deployment. Patch-based, evolution-aware memory proves beneficial not only for dynamic benchmarks but also for static ones by better preserving state information.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents have achieved strong performance on a wide range of benchmarks, yet most evaluations assume static environments. In contrast, real-world deployment is inherently dynamic, requiring agents to continually align their knowledge, skills, and behavior with changing environments and updated task conditions. To address this gap, we introduce EvoArena, a benchmark suite that models environment changes as sequences of progressive updates across terminal, software, and social domains. We further propose EvoMem, a patch-based memory paradigm that records memory evolution as structured update histories, enabling agents to reason about environmental evolution through changes in their memory. Experiments show that current agents struggle on EvoArena, achieving an average accuracy of 39.6% across evolving terminal, software, and social-preference domains. EvoMem consistently improves performance, yielding an average gain of 1.5% on EvoArena and also improving standard benchmarks such as GAIA and LoCoMo by 6.1% and 4.8%. Beyond individual tasks, EvoMem further improves chain-level accuracy by 3.7% on EvoArena, where success requires completing a consecutive sequence of related evolutionary subtasks. Mechanistic analysis shows that EvoMem improves evidence capture in the memory, indicating better preservation of complete evolving environment states. Our results highlight the importance of modeling evolution in both evaluation and memory for reliable agent deployment.

</details>


<div id='cs.SE'></div>

# cs.SE [[Back]](#toc)

### [68] [Characterizing Tests in IoT Software: Practices, Challenges and Opportunities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12592)
*Rufeng Chen,Hengcheng Zhu,Wuqi Zhang,Zixu Zhou,Lili Wei*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: First empirical study on IoT software testing reveals that despite numerous test cases, their effectiveness is limited due to challenges with external dependencies. Mocking emerges as a promising solution aligned with these challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT software bugs can cause severe consequences (e.g., unauthorized access via buggy smart locks), yet little is known about how IoT software is tested in practice. This knowledge gap motivates the first empirical study on test cases in open-source IoT software.

Method: The authors conducted an empirical study on open-source IoT software, evaluating (1) test case effectiveness, (2) challenges inherent in IoT software testing, and (3) usage patterns of mock objects in IoT test suites.

Result: IoT software often has a considerable number of test cases but limited effectiveness. The primary testing challenges involve managing complex interactions with external dependencies such as network-reliant IoT components, file systems, operating systems, and databases. Mock object usage closely aligns with these identified challenges.

Conclusion: Mocking demonstrates strong potential as a solution to improve test coverage and address the complexity of IoT software testing, given its alignment with the primary testing challenges identified.

Abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is experiencing rapid growth. Smart devices are emerging in smart homes and industrial applications, performing mission-critical tasks. Bugs in IoT software can lead to severe consequences. For example, a buggy smart lock can allow unauthorized access to a private property. Testing is a primary practice to expose software bugs and ensure software quality. However, little is known about how IoT software is tested. To bridge this gap, we conducted the first empirical study on test cases in open-source IoT software. Specifically, we evaluated the effectiveness of test cases in IoT software, explored the challenges inherent in testing IoT software, and analyzed the usage of mock objects. Our results indicate that while IoT software often contains a considerable number of tests, their effectiveness remains limited. We identified the primary challenges in testing IoT software as managing complex interactions with various external dependencies, such as other network-reliant IoT components, file systems, operating systems, and databases. We also observed that the use of mock objects in IoT software closely aligns with our identified testing challenges. This alignment demonstrates the potential of mocking as a solution to enhance test coverage and address the complexities of IoT software testing.

</details>


### [69] [HybridCodeAuthorship: A Benchmark Dataset for Line-Level Code Authorship Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12620)
*Luke Patterson,Li Wang,Adam Faulkner*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Introduces HybridCodeAuthorship, a benchmark of Python code files with interleaved human- and AI-authored lines that better reflects real-world AI code assistant usage. Evaluates two detection algorithms, with the best achieving only 0.48 (chunk-level) and 0.56 (line-level) F1 scores, highlighting the benchmark's difficulty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI-generated code detection benchmarks use academic/LeetCode-style problems and assume entire snippets are either fully human- or AI-authored, failing to reflect real industry codebases where AI and human code are interleaved. A realistic benchmark is needed for fine-grained location detection of AI-authored code within files.

Method: The authors construct HybridCodeAuthorship by leveraging CodeSearchNet (a collection of GitHub open-source repository links). They build a dataset pipeline that produces Python code files with interleaved human- and AI-authored lines, then benchmark two state-of-the-art AI-generated code detection algorithms at both line-level and chunk-level granularity.

Result: HybridCodeAuthorship proves to be a challenging benchmark. The top-performing algorithm, AIGCode Detector, achieves only an F1 score of 0.48 at chunk-level detection and 0.56 at line-level detection, indicating substantial room for improvement in fine-grained AI code detection.

Conclusion: The benchmark fills a critical gap by providing a realistic, interleaved code authorship dataset. The low detection scores underscore the difficulty of the task and call for more advanced algorithms capable of fine-grained AI-generated code localization in authentic industry-style codebases.

Abstract: Thanks to the rapid adoption of AI code assistants powered by large language models (LLMs), industry codebases are, increasingly, a hybrid of AI- and human-authored code. For risk management and productivity analysis purposes, it is crucial to enable fine-grained location detection of AI-generated code. To develop algorithms for this task, quality benchmarks are needed to assess performance. However, existing benchmarks tend to comprise academic, LeetCode-style problems and presume a code snippet is either completely human-authored or completely AI-authored, which is not reflective of the diverse intents and styles of industry codebases utilizing AI code assistants. To fill these gaps, we introduce HybridCodeAuthorship, a novel benchmark of Python code files with interleaved human- and AI-authored lines of code to simulate authentic utilization of AI code assistants. In this paper, we first present our dataset construction pipeline, which leverages CodeSearchNet, a massive collection of links to open sourced repositories on GitHub. We then benchmark the performance of two state-of-the-art AI-generated code detection algorithms at both the line- and chunk-level. Experimental results demonstrate that HybridCodeAuthorship is a challenging benchmark with a top-scoring algorithm, AIGCode Detector, obtaining a highest F1 score of 0.48 and 0.56 on chunk-level and line-level code detection tasks, respectively.

</details>


### [70] [Beyond Problem Solving: UOJ-Bench for Evaluating Code Generation, Hacking, and Repair in Competitive Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12864)
*Tingqiang Xu,Hangrui Zhou,Tianle Cai,Alex Gu,Kaifeng Lyu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: UOJ-Bench evaluates LLMs on code generation, hacking, and repair using real-world competitive programming submissions. While test-time scaling boosts error detection to >90%, it remains computationally costly. Frontier LLMs can already identify errors in 5%+ of full-score submissions missed by standard judges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs have shown strong performance in competitive programming, but their potential to support human learning — specifically identifying errors in human-written code — is underexplored. Traditional online judge systems rely solely on test case execution for feedback, and this work investigates whether LLMs can provide complementary, educationally valuable signals beyond pass/fail judging.

Method: The authors introduce UOJ-Bench, a benchmark with three tasks: code generation, code hacking (identifying errors in human submissions), and code repair (fixing identified errors). It is built from real-world code submissions on the Universal Online Judge (UOJ) and uses UOJ's native judging infrastructure for evaluation. Models are evaluated under one-shot and test-time scaling settings.

Result: Under one-shot evaluation, even the strongest models fail to identify errors in >50% of incorrect submissions. Test-time scaling improves success rates above 90%, but with substantial inference costs. Best-performing models under test-time scaling can uncover errors in over 5% of full-score submissions across ~30 problems, demonstrating complementary capability to standard judging systems.

Conclusion: While LLMs are not yet practical for large-scale educational deployment due to computational costs, frontier models already show promise in providing complementary error detection signals beyond traditional online judge systems, pointing toward future AI-assisted programming education.

Abstract: Despite strong performance in competitive programming, the role of Large Language Models (LLMs) in supporting human learning in the same setting remains largely unexplored. In this work, we introduce UOJ-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate not only the problem-solving ability of LLMs, but also their ability to identify errors in human-written code -- a crucial educational activity traditionally supported by running test cases over online judge systems. UOJ-Bench consists of three distinct tasks: code generation, code hacking, and code repair, all constructed from real-world code submissions on the Universal Online Judge (UOJ) and evaluated through UOJ's native judging infrastructure. Our results show that under one-shot evaluation, even the strongest models fail to identify errors in more than 50% of a set of submissions that have been found to be incorrect by UOJ users. While test-time scaling improves success rates to above 90%, the substantial computational costs incurred from model inference limit its practicality for large-scale deployment. Despite these limitations, we find that the best-performing models under test-time scaling can uncover errors in over 5% of full-score submissions across roughly 30 problems, suggesting that frontier LLMs can already provide complementary signals beyond standard judging systems.

</details>


### [71] [The Rise of AI-Native Software Engineering: Implications for Practice, Education, and the Future Workforce](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12986)
*Mamdouh Alenezi*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A systematic review of 48 publications (2016–2026) on AI's impact on software engineering, yielding a conceptual framework, a competency model, a curriculum roadmap, workforce strategies, and a research agenda — concluding that education must emphasize judgment, verification, and orchestration over mere code generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI, LLMs, and Agentic AI represent a disruptive transformation in software engineering, yet the evidence on productivity effects is contradictory and context-dependent. There is an urgent need to synthesize the literature to guide practice, reshape university curricula, and inform workforce development for the AI-native era.

Method: The authors conducted a systematic review of 48 peer-reviewed publications (2016–2026) from top venues in SE, ML, computing education, HCI, and productivity. A four-agent research workflow was employed: Literature Discovery, Scientometric Analysis, Curriculum Transformation, and Workforce Impact. All studies were verified against primary sources and synthesized along nine themes and three trajectories (practice, education, workforce). A scientometric inflection was identified post-late-2022.

Result: (i) A conceptual framework — AI-native SE organized around intent, collaboration, and verification. (ii) A nine-dimension competency model covering specification, critical evaluation, agent orchestration, and metacognition. (iii) A four-phase university curriculum roadmap with AI-resilient assessment. (iv) Faculty-development and workforce-transformation strategies. (v) A prioritized agenda of eleven research gaps. The evidence base reveals internally contradictory findings on productivity effects, emphasizing context-dependence.

Conclusion: The central challenge of the AI-native era is not code production but educating engineers for judgment, verification, and orchestration. Benefits of AI in SE are strongly context-dependent, and universities must adapt curricula, assessment, and faculty development accordingly.

Abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI), Large Language Models (LLMs), and emerging Agentic AI constitute the most disruptive transformation in the history of software engineering (SE), reshaping development processes, required competencies, professional roles, and the educational outcomes that universities must deliver. This paper presents a systematic review of 48 verified, influential peer-reviewed publications (2016--2026) drawn from leading venues in software engineering, machine learning, computing education, human--AI collaboration, and software productivity.
