Studies were discovered, screened, and analyzed through a four-agent research workflow (Literature Discovery, Scientometric Analysis, Curriculum Transformation, and Workforce Impact) and were verified against primary sources. We synthesize the evidence along nine themes and three trajectories -- practice, education, and workforce -- and report a scientometric inflection in which annual LLM-for-SE output grew roughly five-fold after late 2022. From this synthesis we contribute: (i) a conceptual framework for AI-native software engineering organized around \emph{intent}, \emph{collaboration}, and \emph{verification}; (ii) a nine-dimension competency model spanning specification, critical evaluation, agent orchestration, and metacognition; (iii) a four-phase university curriculum roadmap with AI-resilient assessment; (iv) faculty-development and workforce-transformation strategies; and (v) a prioritized agenda of eleven research gaps. The evidence base is internally contradictory on the magnitude and direction of productivity effects, underscoring that benefits are strongly context-dependent and that educating engineers for judgment, verification, and orchestration -- rather than code production alone -- is the central challenge of the AI-native era.

</details>


### [72] [The End of Code Review: Coding Agents Supersede Human Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13175)
*Martin Monperrus*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper argues that LLM-based coding agents have surpassed the capability threshold where traditional human code review remains necessary, claiming that agents can fulfill every stated goal of code review at lower cost and higher throughput, and that the hybrid model of agents writing code with mandatory human review is unsustainable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the five-decade-old orthodoxy of mandatory human code review in light of advances in LLM-based coding agents, and to argue that persisting with human reviewers in an agent-driven development pipeline is both ineffective and unscalable.

Method: The paper constructs a conceptual argument based on two claims: (1) mapping every stated goal of human code review (defect detection, knowledge sharing, compliance, etc.) to equivalent or superior agent capabilities; (2) demonstrating through a reductio-style argument that the naive hybrid model — agents writing, humans reviewing — fails on both assurance and scalability grounds.

Result: The argument concludes that coding agents can serve all code review goals at lower cost and higher throughput, and that human-in-the-loop review bottlenecks are incompatible with the throughput of AI-assisted code generation.

Conclusion: Traditional human code review as a mandatory quality gate is obsolete; organizations should transition to agent-driven quality assurance pipelines to fully realize the productivity gains of LLM-based software development.

Abstract: Code review has been the primary quality gate in software development since Fagan formalised code inspection in 1976. For five decades, having a human examine and comment on a colleague's changes before merge has been a cornerstone practice at organisations of every size. Coding agents are large language model (LLM)-based autonomous systems capable of reading, writing, testing, and repairing software. We argue that coding agents have crossed a threshold of capability at which traditional human code review is no longer a necessary component of a software quality pipeline. Our argument rests on two claims: every stated goal of code review can be served by agents at lower cost and higher throughput; the naive integration in which agents write code and humans remain the mandatory reviewers is a dead end because it neither provides meaningful assurance nor scales with AI-assisted throughput.

</details>


### [73] [ComAct: Reframing Professional Software Manipulation via COM-as-Action Paradigm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13239)
*Jiaxin Ai,Tao Hu,Xuemeng Yang,Shu Zou,Hairong Zhang,Daocheng Fu,Yu Yang,Hongbin Zhou,Nianchen Deng,Pinlong Cai,Zhongyuan Wang,Botian Shi,Kaipeng Zhang,Licheng Wen*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes COM-as-Action, a paradigm that reframes professional software interaction as deterministic program synthesis using the Component Object Model (COM), rather than fragile GUI-based control. It introduces ComCADBench, a CAD benchmark revealing that GUI agents fail while COM-based execution succeeds, and presents ComActor, a self-correcting agent trained via a three-stage framework on the ComForge platform, achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current computer-use agents face fundamental limitations: GUI-based agents suffer from fragile visual grounding and error accumulation in long-horizon tasks, while API-based approaches struggle with heterogeneous protocols and inaccessible commercial interfaces. There is a need for a unified, robust abstraction for professional software manipulation.

Method: The authors identify the Component Object Model (COM) as a unified executable abstraction and propose COM-as-Action, reframing software interaction as deterministic program synthesis. To validate this, they introduce ComCADBench, the first benchmark for agents operating real industrial CAD software. They develop ComActor, a self-correcting agent trained through a progressive three-stage framework (syntactic correctness to geometric accuracy), and ComForge, a scalable training platform using Windows containers.

Result: Experiments reveal a substantial paradigm gap: frontier proprietary models achieve near-zero success under GUI-based interaction, while COM-based execution yields substantial immediate gains. ComActor achieves state-of-the-art performance on ComCADBench, demonstrates strong resilience in long-horizon tasks where baselines collapse, and generalizes to external CAD benchmarks.

Conclusion: The COM-as-Action paradigm effectively addresses the limitations of both GUI-based and API-based agents in professional software. By leveraging COM as a deterministic executable abstraction and combining it with a self-correcting agent trained on a scalable platform, the approach achieves robust, generalizable performance on complex industrial CAD tasks, particularly in long-horizon scenarios.

Abstract: Existing computer-use agents remain fundamentally limited in professional software manipulation: GUI-based agents suffer from fragile visual grounding and long-horizon error accumulation, while API-basedapproaches struggle with heterogeneous protocols and inaccessible commercial interfaces. In this work,we identify the Component Object Model (COM) as a unified executable abstraction, proposing COM-as-Action: a new paradigm that reframes professional software interaction as deterministic program synthesisrather than sequential visual control. To validate this paradigm in the most demanding environments, weintroduce ComCADBench, the first benchmark for agents operating real industrial CAD software. Ourexperiments reveal a substantial paradigm gap: frontier proprietary models achieve near-zero successunder GUI-based interaction, whereas COM-based execution yields substantial immediate gains. Tobridge the remaining gap between syntactic correctness and geometric accuracy, we develop ComActor, aself-correcting agent trained through a progressive three-stage framework, alongside ComForge, a scalableplatform for large-scale training in Windows containers. Extensive experiments show that ComActorachieves state-of-the-art performance on ComCADBench, with strong resilience in long-horizon taskswhere baselines collapse, and generalizes to external CAD benchmark.

</details>


### [74] [Mining Architectural Quality Under Agentic AI Adoption: A Causal Study of Java Repositories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13298)
*Oliver Aleksander Larsen,Mahyar T. Moghaddam*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A causal study of 151 open-source Java repositories finds that agentic AI coding tool adoption does not improve software architecture: total architectural smells remain unchanged, while lines of code increase ~13%, yielding a misleading density decline driven entirely by a denominator effect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While prior causal work has examined AI coding tools' effects on code-level outcomes (complexity, static analysis warnings), no causal evidence exists on whether these effects propagate to architectural-level degradation. The authors aim to fill this gap, particularly given the rising popularity of "vibe coding" with agentic AI tools.

Method: The authors mined 151 open-source Java repositories (74 with detectable agentic AI adoption, 77 propensity-matched controls) across a 13-month window, producing 1,811 monthly Arcan architectural snapshots. They applied a staggered difference-in-differences design with the Borusyak imputation estimator to estimate the causal effect of adoption on architectural smell density (ASD), supplemented by robustness checks including wild cluster bootstrap, Lee bounds, and stale-observation sensitivity analysis.

Result: Total architectural smell counts are essentially unchanged (+1.1%, p = 0.82), while lines of code grow significantly (+12.8%, p = 0.003). The resulting 6.7% decline in ASD (p = 0.004) is therefore a denominator effect (more code, same smells) rather than genuine architectural improvement. Pre-trends are flat (Wald p = 0.90), supporting parallel trends. Per-type smell estimates and all robustness checks corroborate this pattern.

Conclusion: Density-normalized metrics can mislead when treatment affects system size; studies of AI tool adoption must report raw counts and explicitly decompose density changes to distinguish genuine improvement from denominator artifacts. AI coding tools appear to increase code volume without corresponding architectural degradation — but also without architectural improvement.

Abstract: AI coding tools are now used by a majority of developers, and agentic use of these tools has popularized the practice colloquially called "vibe coding". Yet causal evidence on their effect on software architecture is scarce. Prior causal work has measured code-level outcomes (complexity, static analysis warnings); whether such degradation propagates to architecture-level outcomes remains unknown. We mine 151 open-source Java repositories, 74 with detectable agentic AI adoption (identified via configuration files and Co-Authored-By commit trailers) and 77 propensity-matched controls, across a 13-month per-repository window yielding 1,811 monthly Arcan snapshots. We estimate the causal effect of adoption on architectural smell density (ASD) with a staggered difference-in-differences design and the Borusyak imputation estimator, applying a causal design recently used for code-level metrics to the architecture level. Total smell counts are essentially unchanged (+1.1%, p = 0.82) while lines of code grow +12.8% (p = 0.003); the resulting 6.7% ASD decline (p = 0.004) is therefore a denominator effect rather than an architectural improvement. Per-type estimates and robustness checks (wild cluster bootstrap, Lee bounds, stale-observation sensitivity) corroborate the pattern; pre-trends are flat (Wald p = 0.90), consistent with parallel trends. Density-normalized outcomes can mislead when treatment affects system size: raw counts and explicit decomposition are required for causal mining studies of AI tool adoption. The complete replication package, including the curated 151-repository monthly panel, is publicly available.

</details>


### [75] [Toward Instructions-as-Code: Understanding the Impact of Instruction Files on Agentic Pull Requests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13449)
*Ali Arabat,Mohammed Sayagh*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether providing instruction files to AI coding agents (like GitHub Copilot) improves the quality of agent-generated pull requests. Analyzing 15,549 agentic PRs from 148 projects, the authors find no consistent benefit: roughly equal proportions of projects saw improvements vs. declines in merge rates after introducing instructions. However, projects that did improve tended to have longer, better-structured instruction files, motivating the concept of "Instructions-as-Code" as a deliberate software engineering practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents are increasingly used to generate pull requests, and developers create instruction files to guide these agents. However, it is unclear whether these instructional efforts actually translate into better agent performance — specifically, higher merge rates, ability to handle more complex tasks, and reduced merging effort. The paper seeks to empirically validate this assumed relationship.

Method: The authors analyzed 15,549 agentic PRs from 148 projects in the AIDev dataset. They performed a before-and-after comparison within each project, evaluating three dimensions: (1) success rate (merge rate), (2) task complexity (code churn, number of modified files), and (3) merging effort (time to merge, number of comments). They further explored characteristics of instruction files — length and structural organization (sections/subsections) — to identify factors correlating with improved outcomes.

Result: Introducing instruction files produced mixed results: 27.7% of projects increased merge rate by ≥20%, while 26.35% saw a decrease of similar magnitude. Similar inconsistent patterns emerged for code churn, modified files, merge time, and comment counts. However, exploratory analysis revealed that projects achieving higher merge rates had substantially longer instruction files with more sections and subsections, suggesting that instruction quality and structure matter more than mere presence.

Conclusion: Simply creating instruction files does not guarantee better AI-agent performance. The findings motivate the need for a systematic, engineering-oriented approach to crafting agent instructions — termed "Instructions-as-Code" — treating instruction development as a first-class software engineering activity requiring research into best practices, structure, and maintainability.

Abstract: AI-agents (e.g., GitHub Copilot) collaborate as teammates in different software engineering tasks, including code generation proposed through pull requests (Agentic-PRs). For better agent efficiency, developers create instruction files that guide the AI-agents, including how to navigate the project, locate the right components, run tests, respect best practices, and more. In this paper, we investigate the relationship between the creation of these instructions and the performance of AI-agents in creating better pull requests, which have a higher chance of success (i.e., the merge rate), address more complex tasks (e.g., code churn), and require less effort to be merged (e.g., time to merge). To this end, we analyze 15,549 agentic PRs from 148 projects in the AIDev dataset. Using the three dimensions, we compare each project before and after the creation of the instruction files. We find that specifying instructions for AI-agents does not necessarily lead to better results. With the instruction files, 27.7\% of the projects increased their merge rate by at least 20\%, while 26.35\% decreased it. The same observation is seen with the amount of changes (e.g., code churn, number of modified files) and with the efforts to merge an agentic PR (e.g., merge time and number of comments). From a first exploration, we find that projects that managed to increase their merge rate have substantially longer instruction files, which are also well structured into a higher number of sections and sub-sections. Our results motivate the need for research to assist practitioners in framing the development of instruction files as a software engineering activity (aka, \textbf{Instructions-as-Code}).

</details>


### [76] [Understanding the Rejection of Fixes Generated by Agentic Pull Requests -- Insights from the AIDev Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13468)
*Mahmoud Abujadallah,Ali Arabat,Mohammed Sayagh*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: 46.41% of AI-agent-generated pull requests are rejected. Through qualitative and quantitative analysis of 306 non-merged PRs from Copilot, Devin, Cursor, and Claude, the authors identify 14 rejection reasons across four high-level categories: incorrect implementation, CI/test failures, agent inability to perform, and low priority. They provide guidance for improving agent prompting and task prioritization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI coding agents are increasingly used to generate PRs, but nearly half of their proposed fixes are rejected, wasting significant human review effort, compute resources, and CI validation. Understanding why these failures occur is essential for integrating AI agents as effective teammates.

Method: The authors conduct a qualitative study on a representative sample of 306 non-merged PRs created or co-authored by Copilot, Devin, Cursor, and Claude agents, drawn from the AIDev dataset. This is followed by a quantitative analysis to categorize and measure the frequency of rejection reasons.

Result: Fourteen distinct rejection reasons were identified, grouped into four high-level categories: (1) incorrect implementation (e.g., incomplete, wrong approach), (2) failure to pass CI pipelines and tests, (3) agent inability to perform the implementation (e.g., no code generated, lost sessions), and (4) low-priority fixes. These findings highlight key failure modes of current AI coding agents.

Conclusion: Better guidance is needed at three levels: (1) providing hints about the correct approach for fixing an issue, (2) outlining constraints on approaches to avoid, and (3) instructing agents on CI-based self-validation. Additionally, effective task prioritization is crucial to avoid wasted human review and agent resources (tokens, compute, request limits).

Abstract: AI coding agents are increasingly used to generate pull requests (PRs) that propose code fixes in software projects. From a first exploration of the AIDev dataset, we find that 46.41\% of the fixes proposed by the agents Copilot, Devin, Cursor, and Claude are rejected. This represents a significant amount of wasted resources that require human reviews, verifications, and running tests and validations for fixes that are merely discarded. Our goal in this paper is to understand the failure modes of AI-agents, an understanding that is crucial for better integrating AI-agents as efficient teammates. In this paper, we conduct a qualitative study on a representative sample of 306 non-merged pull requests created or co-authored by the agents mentioned earlier, followed by a quantitative analysis of the reasons for rejection. Our qualitative findings identify 14 reasons divided into four high-level categories for rejecting AI-agent fixes. We observe that developers can reject fixes due to fixes whose implementation is incorrect (e.g., incomplete, wrong approach), fixes that do not pass the continuous integration (CI) pipelines and fail tests, fixes for which the agent is unable to perform the implementation (e.g., no code generated, sessions lost), and fixes whose priority is low. Our results shed light on the importance of better guiding the model at these levels: (1) proposing hints about the approach to follow for fixing an issue, (2) outlining constraints or limitations regarding the approaches that should not be taken, and (3) instructing the agent on how to validate the implementation through CI pipelines and without introducing a breaking change. Our results suggest the need for good prioritization of tasks so that generated fixes do not lead to wasted human review efforts or wasted agent resources (e.g., tokens, compute, or allowed number of requests).

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [77] [ToolSense: A Diagnostic Framework for Auditing Parametric Tool Knowledge in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12451)
*Ashutosh Hathidara,Sai Shruthi Sistla,Sebastian Schreiber,Sahil Bansal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Parametric tool retrieval models perform well on standard benchmarks with verbose queries but collapse dramatically under realistic, ambiguous queries, revealing a knowledge-retrieval dissociation — strong retrieval does not imply true tool understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tool-retrieval benchmarks use fully-specified, verbose queries and constrained decoding, masking whether LLM-based parametric retrievers genuinely understand tool semantics. There is a need for diagnostic benchmarks that probe true tool comprehension under realistic conditions.

Method: The authors introduce ToolSense, an open-source framework that automatically generates three diagnostic benchmarks from any tool catalog: (1) a Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with queries at three ambiguity tiers, (2) an MCQ probing benchmark, and (3) a QA probing benchmark. They apply ToolSense to ToolBench (~47k tools) and evaluate five parametric model training configurations.

Result: On RRB queries, several parametric configurations collapse by ~50–64 percentage points compared to fully-specified ToolBench benchmarks, falling below the embedding-based baseline. Some models scored near-random on factual probing benchmarks despite strong retrieval performance, demonstrating a knowledge-retrieval dissociation.

Conclusion: Current parametric tool retrieval models do not truly understand the tools they retrieve; strong benchmark scores are an artifact of verbose queries and constrained decoding. The ToolSense framework provides a more rigorous diagnostic pipeline for evaluating tool comprehension in LLM-based agents.

Abstract: Large language models deployed as agents over large tool catalogs face a critical tool-retrieval bottleneck. As embedding-based retrieval approaches rely on compact encoders that may under-capture specialized tool semantics, parametric tool retrieval addresses this by encoding each tool as a virtual token appended to the LLM vocabulary, fine-tuned in two stages (memorization then retrieval SFT) to use the LLM as a retriever, achieving strong performance on standard ToolBench retrieval benchmarks. Yet these benchmarks use verbose, fully-specified queries, and their evaluation applies constrained decoding that restricts outputs to valid token paths, neither reveals whether the model actually understands its tools. We introduce \textbf{ToolSense}, an open-source LLM-powered diagnostic framework that takes any tool catalog as input and automatically generates three benchmarks: a Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with queries at three ambiguity tiers, an MCQ probing benchmark, and a QA probing benchmark. Applying ToolSense to ToolBench (~47k tools) and evaluating five parametric model training configurations reveals a knowledge-retrieval dissociation: on RRB queries, several configurations collapse by ~50-64 percentage points compared to fully-specified ToolBench benchmarks, falling below the embedding-model baseline. Additionally, despite strong retrieval performance, some models score near-random on factual probes, suggesting a knowledge-retrieval dissociation. We open-source the ToolSense framework and the ToolBench diagnostic benchmarks at https://github.com/SAP/toolsense.

</details>


### [78] [Arbor: Tree Search as a Cognition Layer for Autonomous Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12563)
*Neha Prakriya,Chaojun Hou,Zheng Gong,Huasha Zhao,Xi Zhao,Mou Li,Zhenyu Gu,Emad Barsoum*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Arbor is a multi-agent framework that uses structured tree search as a shared cognitive layer for autonomous optimization across large, stateful action spaces. Applied to full-stack LLM inference optimization, it achieves up to 193% throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor baselines, significantly outperforming single-agent approaches while maintaining stability across multiple hardware generations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior autonomous optimization systems operate on isolated targets with stateless evaluation, limiting their ability to coordinate complex, multi-layer optimization. Full-stack LLM inference optimization demands coordinated effort across application, framework, compiler, kernel, and hardware layers — a challenge that existing single-agent or stateless approaches cannot reliably address without crashing or plateauing.

Method: Arbor introduces a structured tree search as a cognition layer, maintaining an explicit search tree of scored hypotheses as shared working memory across agents. It employs a dual-agent architecture: an Orchestrator agent drives optimization by delegating to Domain Specialists across the inference stack, while a Critic agent provides stability through root-cause analysis, introspection, and measurement validation. Failures are treated as diagnostic signals that reshape subsequent exploration, and prior successes shift the bottleneck distribution. Agent capabilities are decomposed into hard skills (domain expertise) and soft skills (coordination protocols).

Result: Arbor achieves up to 193% inference throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor-optimized baselines. A single agent without the harness plateaus at +33% throughput improvement and crashes irrecoverably within hours. Run-to-run variance is within 2 percentage points, and the method generalizes across multiple hardware generations.

Conclusion: The structured tree search cognition layer, combined with the checks-and-balances Orchestrator-Critic architecture, enables robust, reproducible, and hardware-agnostic autonomous optimization at scale. Decomposing agent capabilities into hard and soft skills proves effective for sustaining fully autonomous multi-day optimization campaigns.

Abstract: Arbor is a multi-agent framework that introduces structured tree search as a cognition layer for autonomous agents operating in large, stateful action spaces. Prior autonomous optimization systems operate on isolated targets with stateless evaluation. Arbor instead maintains an explicit search tree of scored hypotheses that serves as the shared working memory across agents, evolving with every measurement, treating failures as diagnostic signal that reshapes subsequent exploration, and expanding as prior successes shift the bottleneck distribution.
  We validate Arbor on full-stack LLM inference optimization, a domain where achieving peak performance has historically required coordinated effort from engineering teams across the application, framework, compiler, kernel, and hardware stack. Arbor pairs an Orchestrator agent, which drives optimization by delegating to Domain Specialists across the inference stack, with a Critic agent that safeguards stability through root-cause analysis, introspection, and measurement validation -- a checks-and-balances architecture where neither agent can unilaterally drive the system. Agent capabilities are decomposed into hard skills (domain expertise) and soft skills (coordination protocols that determine how contributions compose), enabling fully autonomous multi-day campaigns. Arbor achieves up to 193% inference throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor-optimized baselines, while a single agent without the harness plateaus at +33% throughput improvement and crashes irrecoverably within hours. Arbor generalizes to multiple generations of hardware platform, and run-to-run variance is within 2 percentage points demonstrating that the method is hardware-agnostic and reproducible.

</details>


### [79] [Strategic Decision Support for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12587)
*Shayan Kiyani,Sima Noorani,George Pappas,Hamed Hassani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper flips the traditional decision-support paradigm: instead of humans using AI as support, it studies how AI agents should strategically seek support (from humans or tools) to remain reliable. The authors propose an optimization framework that minimizes support usage while controlling a counterfactual missed-support error, derive an optimal threshold policy, and develop online algorithms with calibration-on-the-fly for practical deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In modern agentic systems, AI agents act autonomously on behalf of users, with humans and tools serving as support. This role reversal raises reliability concerns: agentic errors can be consequential, and agent behavior must align with human goals. The classical decision-support principles (cost–value tradeoff, uncertainty quantification) need to be revisited for this inverted setting where the AI is the central actor deciding when to seek help.

Method: The authors formulate strategic decision support as an optimization problem: minimize support usage subject to controlling a counterfactual missed-support error (the probability that the agent acts alone when support would have materially improved its output). At the population level, they prove the optimal policy is a threshold rule on the value of support. They then develop (1) an online algorithm that adaptively thresholds a support-value score using randomized exploration to control error without distributional assumptions, and (2) a calibration-on-the-fly method that reduces unnecessary support calls online. The framework is instantiated across information gathering, human–AI collaboration, and tool use.

Result: Experiments across diverse scenarios (information gathering, human–AI collaboration, tool use) demonstrate that the proposed method reliably controls the target missed-support error while substantially reducing support usage in practice compared to baselines.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a principled framework for strategic decision support in agentic AI systems, showing that a threshold-based policy with online adaptive calibration can effectively balance reliability and efficiency. The unified lens applies broadly across heterogeneous agentic use cases.

Abstract: Traditionally, decision support studies how humans use machine learning models to make better decisions. In modern agentic systems, this division of roles is increasingly reversed: AI agents act on behalf of users, while humans and tools becomes support mechanisms around them. This role reversal brings reliability concerns to the forefront, since agentic errors can be consequential and agent behavior must remain aligned with human goals and constraints. Departing from the classical view of decision support, we revisit its two basic principles, the cost--value tradeoff of seeking support and the role of uncertainty quantification, in a setting where AI agents are the central actors. We propose a framework for strategic decision support for AI agents through an optimization problem that minimizes support usage subject to controlling a counterfactual missed-support error: the probability that the agent acts alone on instances where support would have materially improved its output. At the population level, we show that the optimal policy is a threshold rule on the value of support. Building on this structure, we develop an online algorithm that adaptively thresholds such a score and uses randomized exploration to control missed-support error without distributional assumptions. We further introduce a calibration-on-the-fly method that reduces unnecessary support calls online. We instantiate this framework across diverse scenarios, including information gathering, human--AI collaboration, and tool use, showing how each can be modeled through the same strategic decision-support lens. Experiments across these settings show that our method reliably controls the target error while substantially reducing support usage in practice.

</details>


### [80] [Pythagoras-Prover: Advancing Efficient Formal Proving via Augmented Lean Formalisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12594)
*Joshua Ong Jun Leang,Zheng Zhao,Mihaela Cătălina Stoian,Qiyuan Xu,Haonan Li,Wenda Li,Shay B. Cohen,Eleonora Giunchiglia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Pythagoras-Prover introduces compute-efficient open-source Lean theorem provers (4B and 32B autoregressive, plus a 4B diffusion-based prover) that use curriculum SFT, dynamic proof filtering, and a data augmentation technique called ALF (Augmented Lean Formalisation) to achieve state-of-the-art results on MiniF2F-Test and PutnamBench while dramatically reducing parameter counts and training costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern Lean theorem provers require substantial training and inference compute due to scarce verified proof data and long reasoning traces. Both SFT and proof sampling are expensive. The authors aim to build a compute-efficient, open-source family of provers that operates within practical compute budgets while maintaining or exceeding state-of-the-art performance.

Method: (1) A Lean-verified corpus stratified by difficulty (easy/medium/hard) for curriculum SFT, training models progressively from shorter to longer proofs. (2) Dynamic proof-reasoning filtering that preserves informative traces while keeping each instance within an 8k-token context budget. (3) Augmented Lean Formalisation (ALF), which expands scarce verified corpora by mutating formal statements and populating them via self-distillation, providing extra training signal without formally verifying every mutated instance. (4) Two generation paradigms: autoregressive models (4B, 32B) and a proof-of-concept diffusion-based prover (4B) that iteratively refines proofs at inference time.

Result: Pythagoras-Prover-4B surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B at pass@32 on MiniF2F-Test (86.1% vs 82.4%) with ~167x fewer parameters. Pythagoras-Prover-32B achieves 93.0% on MiniF2F-Test (open-source SOTA) and solves 93/672 PutnamBench problems. On MiniF2F-ALF, a contamination-sensitive ALF-mutated benchmark, the 32B model remains strongest and the 4B model matches the prior SOTA (Goedel-Prover-V2-32B).

Conclusion: Compute-efficient training strategies — curriculum SFT, context-budgeted proof filtering, and ALF data augmentation — enable small models to match or exceed much larger models on formal theorem proving benchmarks. The diffusion-based prover offers a promising alternative generation paradigm. The MiniF2F-ALF benchmark reveals that all evaluated models suffer accuracy drops, suggesting contamination sensitivity and the need for robust evaluation.

Abstract: Modern Lean theorem provers achieve strong performance only with substantial training and inference compute, driven in part by scarce verified proof data and the long reasoning traces of formal proof search, making both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and sampling expensive. We introduce Pythagoras-Prover, a compute-efficient open-source family of Lean theorem provers built for practical compute budgets. The family spans two generation paradigms: autoregressive models at 4B and 32B parameters, and a first proof-of-concept diffusion-based prover (4B) that iteratively refines Lean proofs at inference time. For training efficiency, we build a Lean-verified corpus stratified into easy, medium, and hard problems for curriculum SFT, so models acquire proof skills progressively from shorter, simpler proofs to longer, harder ones. During SFT, a dynamic proof-reasoning filtering scheme preserves informative proof traces while keeping each instance within an 8k-token context budget. We also introduce Augmented Lean Formalisation (ALF), which expands scarce verified corpora into variants of formal statements, populated via self-distillation for extra training signal without formally verifying every mutated instance. By perturbing known problems while preserving their formal character, ALF reduces reliance on any statement's surface form. Empirically, Pythagoras-Prover-4B surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B at pass@32 on MiniF2F-Test (86.1% vs 82.4%) with ~167x fewer parameters, while Pythagoras-Prover-32B sets the open-source state of the art at 93.0% on MiniF2F-Test and solves 93 of 672 PutnamBench problems. We release MiniF2F-ALF, an ALF-mutated contamination-sensitive benchmark on which every evaluated model loses accuracy; here our 32B remains strongest and our 4B matches the prior state of the art, Goedel-Prover-V2-32B.

</details>


### [81] [The Illusion of Multi-Agent Advantage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13003)
*Prathyusha Jwalapuram,Hehai Lin,Chuyuan Li,Fangkai Jiao,Sudong Wang,Yifei Ming,Zixuan Ke,Chengwei Qin,Giuseppe Carenini,Shafiq Joty*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automatic multi-agent systems (MAS) consistently underperform single-agent CoT-SC baselines across reasoning and interactive benchmarks despite being up to 10× more expensive. A custom diagnostic dataset reveals that expert-designed MAS outperform auto-generated ones, exposing that current automated MAS design produces architecturally bloated, superficially complex systems misaligned with core multi-agent principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The prevailing assumption that MAS are inherently superior to SAS lacks rigorous empirical validation. Existing benchmarks focus on isolated reasoning tasks that fail to test claimed MAS advantages (context protection, parallel processing, distributed decision-making). Additionally, automated MAS generation methods claim improved generalizability over manual designs, but their actual performance relative to simpler SAS baselines like CoT-SC remains unsubstantiated.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic empirical evaluation comparing automatically generated MAS against Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC) across two categories: (1) traditional reasoning datasets, and (2) interactive multi-step workflow tasks (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus). To control for task structure limitations, they introduce a synthetic diagnostic dataset explicitly designed with task decomposition, context separation, and parallelization potential. They then compare expert-architected MAS against automatically generated architectures on this dataset, measuring both raw performance and cost-efficiency. Finally, they perform a systematic architectural deconstruction of generated MAS to identify structural deficiencies.

Result: Automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC across all tested benchmarks while incurring up to 10× higher computational cost. On the diagnostic dataset, expert-architected MAS outperform automatically generated architectures in both performance and cost-efficiency. Architectural deconstruction reveals that automated design paradigms generate bloated architectures with superficial complexity that fails to provide functional utility, indicating a fundamental misalignment with multi-agent design principles.

Conclusion: Existing evaluation frameworks mask critical architectural gaps and cost-inefficiencies in complex MAS by failing to account for the marginal utility of increased computation. The field requires benchmarks that genuinely test multi-agent advantages (decomposition, context isolation, parallelism) and design methods that align architectural complexity with functional necessity rather than superficial elaboration.

Abstract: Prevailing wisdom posits that Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) are superior to Single-Agent Systems (SAS), citing advantages like context protection, parallel processing and distributed decision-making. However, empirical support for this claim relies primarily on comparisons with SAS baselines using benchmarks that prioritize isolated reasoning tasks, which do not adequately assess these advantages. Focusing on automatically generated MAS that are designed for enhanced generalizability over manually-designed counterparts, we perform a rigorous, systematic evaluation against SAS, specifically Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC). Across traditional reasoning datasets and tasks with interactive multi-step workflows (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus), we demonstrate that automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC despite being up to 10x more expensive. To isolate these failures from limitations inherent to task structure, we introduce a diagnostic synthetic dataset tailored for MAS featuring explicit task decomposition, context separation and parallelization potential. We show that expert-architected MAS consistently outperforms automatically generated architectures in both raw performance and cost-efficiency on this dataset, demonstrating that existing evaluation frameworks mask critical architectural gaps and inefficiencies of complex MAS by failing to account for the marginal utility of increased computational cost. Critically, systematic deconstruction of the generated MAS architectures reveals that current automated design paradigms produce architectural bloat that prioritizes superficial complexity which does not translate into functional utility, exposing a fundamental misalignment with multi-agent principles.

</details>


### [82] [PersonaDrive: Human-Style Retrieval-Augmented VLA Agents for Closed-Loop Driving Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12616)
*Mahmoud Srewa,Praneetsai Iddamsetty,Mohammad Abdullah Al Faruque,Salma Elmalaki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PersonaDrive conditions a VLA driving agent on retrieved human demonstrations from a style-instructed dataset (aggressive, neutral, conservative) using a lightweight retrieval head over per-style databases. A single backbone is fine-tuned to treat retrieved context as in-context demonstrations, enabling style switching at inference without retraining. It achieves state-of-the-art driving scores on Bench2Drive across all styles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing non-ego traffic agents in closed-loop simulators are homogeneous, using either rule-based managers or single-mode learned models. Recent style-variation attempts rely on post-hoc labels or LLM-inferred reward weights as proxies rather than actual human demonstrations of explicitly instructed driving styles. There is a need for agents that exhibit genuine, human-demonstrated stylistic diversity without requiring per-style retraining.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (i) offline triplet mining on per-style human driving data using combined image-text similarity; (ii) training a lightweight retrieval head that fuses frozen visual features with a small control encoder over per-style databases; (iii) fine-tuning a single VLA backbone to use retrieved context points as in-context behavioral demonstrations for waypoint prediction. At inference, style is selected by swapping which per-style database the retrieval head queries — no retraining needed.

Result: On Bench2Drive, PersonaDrive (no style) improves driving score by 4.6% over SimLingo and 2.5% over HiP-AD. Under style conditioning, it attains the highest driving score in every style within a ~2% band; its weakest style surpasses the strongest baseline (DMW) by 5.4%. Average speed and acceleration increase 18% and 25% from conservative to aggressive instructions.

Conclusion: PersonaDrive demonstrates that conditioning a single VLA backbone on retrieved human style demonstrations enables effective, diverse stylistic driving without per-style retraining, outperforming both style-agnostic and style-conditioned baselines while producing measurable behavioral differences across style instructions.

Abstract: Closed-loop driving simulators typically populate their environments with non-ego traffic agents that behave largely the same way, produced either by rule-based traffic managers or by learned models trained toward a single behavioral mode. Recent work introduces style variation through post-hoc labels on observational data or LLM-inferred reward weights, but these signals act as proxies for what a style should reward rather than demonstrations of humans explicitly asked to drive in that style. We introduce PersonaDrive, a pipeline that conditions a vision-language-action (VLA) driving agent on retrieved demonstrations from a style-instructed human driving dataset, in which participants drive CARLA leaderboard routes under aggressive, neutral, and conservative instructions on a driver-in-the-loop rig. The pipeline has three stages: (i) offline triplet mining over per-style human driving data using a combined image-text similarity score; (ii) training a lightweight retrieval head that fuses frozen visual features with a small control encoder over per-style databases; and (iii) fine-tuning a single VLA backbone to treat retrieved context points as in-context behavioral demonstrations during waypoint prediction. At inference, the same backbone is conditioned on any style by swapping which per-style database the retrieval head queries, so selecting a style requires no per-style retraining while enabling human-style, style-diverse non-ego agents for closed-loop simulation. On Bench2Drive, PersonaDrive (no style) improves the driving score by 4.6% over SimLingo and 2.5% over HiP-AD, and under style conditioning attains the highest driving score in every style within a roughly 2% band (its weakest style surpassing the strongest baseline, DMW, by 5.4%), while average speed and acceleration rise by 18% and 25% from the conservative to the aggressive instruction.

</details>


### [83] [LLM-as-an-Investigator: Evidence-First Reasoning for Robust Interactive Problem Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13220)
*Fabrizio Marozzo,Pietro Liò*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces "LLM-as-an-Investigator," an evidence-first agentic AI methodology that counters user-driven sycophancy — where LLMs prematurely reinforce user hypotheses instead of testing alternatives. A Solution Investigator Agent iteratively asks targeted clarification questions, updating hypothesis probabilities until one explanation dominates, and is evaluated on a benchmark of solved technical forum threads across mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains, demonstrating superior diagnostic accuracy over direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs acting as technical assistants exhibit "user-driven sycophancy": they prematurely align with incomplete or unverified user-provided hypotheses and propose solutions without sufficient evidence gathering. This bias undermines robust problem diagnosis, especially in technical domains where incorrect assumptions can lead to wasted effort or wrong fixes.

Method: The authors propose an agentic methodology with a Solution Investigator Agent that: (1) estimates ambiguity of the initial problem description, (2) generates multiple candidate hypotheses, (3) asks targeted clarification questions, and (4) updates hypothesis probabilities after each user answer via Bayesian-style reasoning. The agent defers diagnosis until evidence makes one hypothesis clearly dominant. Evaluation uses a three-agent pipeline: a Problem-Solution Extractor Agent converts solved technical forum threads into structured cases; a Ground-Truth Evaluator Agent simulates the user while concealing the known solution; and the tested assistant attempts solution recovery through dialogue. Benchmarks span mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains, comparing standard assistants, reasoning-oriented LLMs, and the investigator model across different LLM backbones.

Result: The investigator-based approach achieves higher diagnostic accuracy than both direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines. Analysis confirms that standard assistants more readily follow misleading user hypotheses, while the evidence-first protocol effectively reduces user-induced conversational bias.

Conclusion: An evidence-first, hypothesis-driven investigation protocol significantly improves LLM diagnostic accuracy in technical domains by mitigating user-driven sycophancy. The approach demonstrates that structured information-gathering — asking the right clarifying questions before committing to a diagnosis — is a viable and effective strategy for deploying LLMs as reliable technical assistants.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as interactive assistants for technical problem solving. However, when users provide incomplete descriptions or plausible but unverified explanations, LLMs may prematurely align with these assumptions and propose solutions before collecting sufficient evidence. We refer to this behavior as user-driven sycophancy: the tendency of an LLM to reinforce a user-provided hypothesis instead of testing alternative explanations. This paper introduces LLM-as-an-Investigator, an evidence-first agentic AI methodology for robust problem diagnosis. The approach is implemented through a Solution Investigator Agent, which estimates the ambiguity of an initial problem description, generates candidate hypotheses, asks targeted clarification questions, and updates hypothesis probabilities after each answer. Rather than producing an immediate response, the agent continues the investigation until the evidence makes one candidate explanation stronger than the alternatives. To evaluate the approach, we build a benchmark from solved technical forum threads in mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains. We use a three-agent evaluation pipeline in which a Problem-Solution Extractor Agent converts solved threads into structured cases, a Ground-Truth Evaluator Agent simulates the user while hiding the known solution, and the tested assistant attempts to recover the solution through dialogue. The experiments compare standard assistants, reasoning-oriented LLMs, and the proposed investigator-based model across LLM backbones. In addition to diagnostic accuracy, we analyze how standard assistants follow misleading user hypotheses in diagnostic cases. The results show that the proposed approach identifies the problem more accurately than direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines, while its evidence-first protocol helps reduce user-induced conversational bias.

</details>


### [84] ["Did you lie?" Evaluating Lie Detectors across Model Scale and Belief-Verified Model Organisms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12618)
*Alan Cooney,David Africa,Geoffrey Irving*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Current lie detectors for language models are unreliable. The authors create better testbeds with verified model beliefs (13 reasoning model organisms and a prompted-lying dataset) and evaluate four detectors, finding that while all scale positively with model capability on prompted lies, activation- and logprob-based detectors fail on trained organisms. Only chain-of-thought judging remains strong, partly due to verification artifacts. They conclude current detectors cannot support high-confidence claims about model beliefs and suggest future research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing lie detection testbeds often fail to ensure models verifiably believe the opposite of what they say, making prior results (both positive and negative) difficult to interpret. Reliable testbeds are essential for auditing, monitoring, and post-hoc investigation of model behavior.

Method: The authors introduce (1) 13 reasoning model organisms with hidden beliefs verified via chain-of-thought and shown to generalize to held-out tasks, and (2) Varied Deception, a prompted-lying testbed covering diverse lie-inducing motivations. Four detectors are evaluated: a chain-of-thought judge, a logprob classifier, and two activation probes—including a new method called Did-You-Lie (DYL) for training follow-up probes. Evaluation spans 31 open-weight models (2B to 1T parameters).

Result: On prompted lying, all four detectors show positive scaling with model capability. However, on trained model organisms, all activation- and logprob-based detectors drop sharply; DYL retains the most signal. Only the chain-of-thought judge remains strong (0.82 balanced accuracy), partly as an artifact of the verification process favoring CoT-readable beliefs.

Conclusion: Current lie detectors cannot support high-confidence claims about model beliefs. The verification process itself may bias evaluations toward CoT-based methods. The authors release all datasets, model organisms, and trained detectors to facilitate further research addressing these limitations.

Abstract: Robust lie detectors for language models could enable powerful techniques for auditing, monitoring, and post-hoc investigation of model behaviour, but evaluating them requires testbeds where models verifiably believe the opposite of what they say. We show that existing trained model organisms often fail this requirement, leaving prior positive and negative detection results difficult to interpret. We address this with 13 reasoning model organisms whose hidden beliefs are verified in chain-of-thought and shown to generalise to held-out tasks, alongside Varied Deception, a prompted-lying testbed covering a broad range of lie-inducing motivations. On these testbeds we evaluate four detectors: a chain-of-thought judge, a logprob classifier, and two activation probes, including Did-You-Lie (DYL), a new method for training follow-up probes. On prompted lying, across 31 open-weight models spanning 2B to 1T parameters, all four detectors show positive scaling with model capability. However, every activation- and logprob-based detector drops sharply on our trained model organisms, with DYL retaining the most signal; only the chain-of-thought judge remains strong, with 0.82 balanced accuracy, partly as an artefact of our verification process favouring CoT-readable beliefs. Current lie detectors therefore cannot support high-confidence claims about model beliefs, and we suggest research directions that may address some of their current limitations. We release our datasets, model organisms, and trained detectors.

</details>


### [85] [Can I Buy Your KV Cache?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13361)
*Luoyuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes precomputing and reusing KV caches for documents across AI agents instead of each agent recomputing prefill from scratch. This yields 9–50x compute savings with zero accuracy loss, and hosting KV caches provider-side (like prompt caching) eliminates egress costs, turning the approach into a viable "agent-native prefill CDN."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Millions of AI agents repetitively recompute identical KV caches for the same documents via expensive prefill operations (attention scaling with L²), wasting enormous compute. The authors seek to eliminate this redundancy with a simple reuse mechanism.

Method: The authors propose a system where a publisher precomputes a document's KV cache once, and subsequent agents load it to skip prefill entirely. They evaluate token-exact fidelity (greedy decoding and logit-level matching), measure compute savings on Qwen3-4B, test shipping KV caches (which fails due to incompressibility and egress costs), and propose provider-side hosting analogous to production prompt-caching. A cost model compares re-prefill vs. reuse for serving one document to 80M agents.

Result: Loading a precomputed KV cache matches prefilling from scratch exactly (24/24 greedy tokens, logit-level match). Reuse is 9–50x cheaper in compute than prefill on Qwen3-4B, with savings growing with document length. Shipping KV caches fails: near-incompressibility makes per-load egress costlier than prefill. Provider-side hosting removes egress. Serving one 3774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M via re-prefill vs. ~$0.03M via reuse (49.7x less).

Conclusion: KV cache reuse is a simple, accurate, and economically compelling mechanism. The authors frame an "agent-native prefill CDN" and identify lossless KV compression and a cross-party payment layer as the remaining open problems.

Abstract: Right now, across the world, AI agents are repeating the same absurd act: to read one document, they each recompute it from scratch. Every agent re-runs prefill, the most compute-intensive step a large model takes, over identical text, only to rebuild a key-value (KV) cache identical to the one the agent before it just built. The same answer, computed a million times. We make a proposal that is almost offensively simple: compute it once. Let a publisher precompute a document's KV cache, and let every other agent buy the right to load it and skip prefill. It works, and it is token-exact: loading a precomputed KV and continuing matches prefilling from scratch (24/24 greedy tokens, and at the logits level), with no accuracy cost. On Qwen3-4B, reuse is 9-50x cheaper in compute than prefill, and the gap widens with length (prefill's attention scales with L^2), so a single reuse already pays it back. Then the part that matters: where the KV lives. Shipping it fails, because KV is nearly incompressible, so per-load egress costs more than the prefill it saves. Hosting it provider-side, exactly as production prompt-caching works, removes egress entirely. The size of the prize is set by our measured compute saving: serving one hot 3774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M to re-prefill but only ~$0.03M of reuse compute (49.7x less). The 0.1x cache-read tariff APIs charge passes a 10x discount to users while sitting inside this measured envelope, so the 10x is a floor that the measured ~50x compute saving clears, and the gap to the physical ~50x is provider margin: millions of dollars per popular document. We frame the resulting agent-native prefill CDN and leave lossless KV compression and a cross-party payment layer as the open problems.

</details>


### [86] [TrajGenAgent: A Hierarchical LLM Agent for Human Mobility Trajectory Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12657)
*Siyu Li,Toan Tran,Lingyi Zhao,Khurram Shafique,Li Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TrajGenAgent is a hierarchical LLM-agent framework that generates realistic human mobility trajectories without fine-tuning. It uses a two-stage design combining LLM-based activity chain synthesis with deterministic spatiotemporal grounding, and introduces anomaly-detection-based evaluation to assess behavioral and semantic plausibility beyond aggregate statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale human trajectory collection is costly and privacy-constrained. Existing LLM-based generators either use prompt engineering (lacking fine-grained spatiotemporal grounding) or trajectory-level fine-tuning (computationally expensive, may degrade general reasoning). A method is needed that achieves high realism without parameter updates.

Method: TrajGenAgent employs a two-stage orchestrator-worker architecture. Stage 1: An LLM synthesizes an individual- and weekday-conditioned activity chain from historical evidence via in-context learning. Stage 2: A deterministic workflow grounds each activity into a complete visit using personalized POI retrieval, distance-aware location selection, kinematics-aware travel-time propagation, and LLM-based duration estimation. Evaluation uses two complementary anomaly detectors to assess behavioral and semantic plausibility.

Result: TrajGenAgent outperforms representative neural and LLM-based baselines on spatiotemporal fidelity, semantic coherence, and individual-specific behavioral realism, as demonstrated on both benchmark and large-scale simulation datasets.

Conclusion: A semantic-aware hierarchical agent framework can generate realistic synthetic trajectories by combining LLM reasoning with deterministic grounding, avoiding fine-tuning costs while achieving strong realism across multiple dimensions validated by a novel anomaly-detection-based evaluation framework.

Abstract: Human mobility data is important for transportation, urban planning, and epidemic control, but large-scale trajectory collection is often costly and privacy-constrained, motivating realistic synthetic trajectory generation. Existing LLM-based generators typically rely on either prompt engineering, which preserves zero-shot reasoning but lacks fine-grained spatiotemporal grounding, or trajectory-level fine-tuning, which improves statistical precision but incurs substantial computational cost and may weaken general reasoning. We propose TrajGenAgent, a semantic-aware hierarchical LLM-agent framework for human mobility trajectory generation without model fine-tuning. TrajGenAgent uses a two-stage orchestrator-worker design: an LLM first synthesizes an individual- and weekday-conditioned activity chain from historical evidence via in-context learning, and a deterministic workflow then grounds each activity into a complete visit using personalized POI retrieval, distance-aware location selection, kinematics-aware travel-time propagation, and LLM-based duration estimation. To evaluate realism beyond aggregate spatiotemporal statistics, we introduce an anomaly-detection-based evaluation framework using two complementary detectors to assess behavioral and semantic plausibility. Experiments on benchmark and large-scale simulation datasets show that TrajGenAgent improves spatiotemporal fidelity, semantic coherence, and individual-specific behavioral realism over representative neural and LLM-based baselines, while avoiding parameter updates.

</details>


### [87] [Neuro-Symbolic Agents for Regulated Process Automation: Challenges and Research Agenda](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13405)
*Alexander Rombach,Chantale Lauer,Nijat Mehdiyev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes "compliance-by-construction," a neuro-symbolic paradigm where regulations and process models are embedded as core architectural components of LLM agents, preventing control-flow violations at the structural level rather than relying solely on external guardrails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents are entering regulated industries with judgment-intensive processes. Current approaches treat regulations as external guardrails for post-hoc monitoring, but this is insufficient — semantic guardrails cannot reliably prevent structural control-flow violations. The paper argues that symbolic structures (regulations, typed process models, compliance constraints) already present in these domains should be leveraged as first-class architectural elements.

Method: The paper introduces compliance-by-construction as a complementary paradigm to guardrail-based monitoring. It identifies a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges at both foundational and capability levels, arguing these must be addressed jointly. The approach integrates symbolic process models, regulatory constraints, and type systems directly into agent architecture design rather than as external wrappers.

Result: The paper does not present empirical results; it is a position paper that formulates the compliance-by-construction paradigm, delineates the problem space, and maps out a structured research agenda for the neuro-symbolic community.

Conclusion: Compliance-by-construction offers a structural foundation that prevents control-flow violations, while guardrails remain necessary for semantic errors. The authors call on the neuro-symbolic community to engage with regulated process automation as a high-impact research domain requiring joint advances across foundational and capability-level challenges.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are entering regulated industries where they automate judgment intensive quality management processes. We argue that symbolic structures already embedded in these domains, including regulations, typed process models, and compliance constraints, should be treated not merely as external monitoring mechanisms but as core architectural components that shape the agent's decision-making and behavior. We propose compliance-by-construction as a complementary paradigm to guardrail-based monitoring: a structural foundation that prevents control-flow violations, while guardrails remain essential for catching semantic errors. We identify a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges on foundational and capability level and show that addressing them jointly enables compliance-by-construction. We call on the neuro-symbolic community to engage with regulated process automation as a high impact research domain.

</details>


### [88] [Evoflux: Inference-Time Evolution of Executable Tool Workflows for Compact Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12674)
*Kushal Raj Bhandari,Ling Yue,Ching-Yun Ko,Dhaval Patel,Shaowu Pan,Pin-Yu Chen,Jianxi Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Evoflux uses inference-time evolutionary search over typed workflow graphs to repair tool-use plans for small LMs, boosting execution feasibility from ~3% to 17-24% on live MCP benchmarks, outperforming SFT/DPO distillation and offering more reliable, cost-efficient grounding than ReAct under limited teacher traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small language models used as tool agents often produce plausible but non-executable workflow graphs due to failures in tool discovery, schema validation, dependency tracking, or grounding. Standard small-corpus distillation (a few hundred teacher traces) teaches workflow format but not the recovery/repair behaviors needed when plans fail against dynamic tool catalogs.

Method: Evoflux is an inference-time evolutionary search method that frames tool use as repair of executable typed workflow graphs. It operates through structured graph edits, execution feedback, adaptive mutation intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning—iteratively evolving candidate workflows until they pass execution checks, without requiring additional training.

Result: On held-out MCP-Bench tasks (live MCP servers, 250 tools), Evoflux lifts execution feasibility from ~3% (base small planners) to 17-24%. SFT and SFT+DPO on the same search-mined data match, underperform, or fall below zero-shot. ReAct achieves higher peaks but with greater variance and token cost.

Conclusion: Execution-grounded evolutionary search is more reliable than distillation-based fine-tuning for compact tool-use agents when teacher-trace budgets are scarce, as it directly optimizes for executability and adapts online to changing tool catalogs.

Abstract: Compact language models (LMs) reduce cost, latency, and deployment risk for tool agents. Yet MCP-style tool use requires more than isolated function calling: an agent must discover tools from live catalogs, satisfy schemas, preserve dependencies across intermediate outputs, and ground final responses in executed evidence. Small planners often generate plausible workflow graphs that fail under tool resolution, parameter validation, dependency tracking, or execution. We argue that this failure mode is poorly handled by small-corpus distillation. A few hundred teacher traces can teach workflow format, but rarely cover the recovery behavior needed to repair failed plans over changing tool catalogs. We introduce Evoflux, an inference-time evolutionary search method that treats compact tool use as the repair of executable tool workflows. It evolves typed workflow graphs through structured edits, execution feedback, adaptive intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning. On held-out MCP-Bench tasks spanning live MCP servers and 250 tools, Evoflux raises execution feasibility from roughly 3% to 17-24% across small planners. In contrast, SFT and SFT+DPO on the same search-mined data match, underperform, or collapse below zero-shot performance; ReAct reaches higher peaks, but with higher variance and token cost. These results show that execution-grounded search is more reliable under scarce teacher-trace budgets.

</details>


### [89] [Multiagent Protocols with Aggregated Confidence Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13591)
*Ali Elahi,Barbara Di Eugenio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces three protocols for aggregating confidence signals in multiagent debate (MAD) systems into a single, system-level confidence score. Using soft voting or Bayesian fusion on transformed, comparable signals, the aggregated confidence significantly improves discriminability (AUARC) over single agents and standard debate baselines, while maintaining F1-score and recovering MAD losses on ambiguous tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite widespread use of confidence in NLP for reliability and downstream decisions, no method exists to produce or evaluate a confidence score for the output of an entire multiagent system. Prior work only applies confidence within debates (e.g., weighting messages, triggering debate, calibrating individual agents) but never aggregates these into a single system-level confidence.

Method: Three protocols are proposed: (1) raw confidence signals are first transformed (via parametric and non-parametric calibrators) to make them comparable across models; (2) transformed signals are then combined via soft voting or Bayesian fusion (a probability fusion method). Two confidence estimators are analyzed: sequence probability and self-report. Evaluation spans six homogeneous and heterogeneous debating pairs, five benchmarks, and four task types.

Result: Aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (higher AUARC) than the best single agent or standard debate baselines. F1-score remains stable, and the method recovers losses that MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Calibration improves F1 for both estimators, while AUARC is less reliant on calibration.

Conclusion: System-level confidence aggregation for multiagent debate is both feasible and beneficial, offering improved discriminability without sacrificing correctness. The approach bridges a critical gap in multiagent NLP systems by enabling reliable confidence outputs for downstream decision-making.

Abstract: Confidence is used for reliability, oversight, and a range of downstream decision tasks in Natural Language Processing (NLP), yet no existing method produces or evaluates a confidence for the output of a multiagent system. Prior work uses confidence within multiagent debate (MAD) to weight messages, trigger debate, or calibrate individual agents, but it never aggregates these into a single confidence for the system itself. We introduce three protocols that produce a final answer along with a single aggregated confidence by first transforming raw confidence signals to make them comparable across models, then combining them via soft voting or a probability fusion we call Bayesian fusion. This aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (AUARC) than that of the best single agent or the standard debate baselines, while correctness (F1-score) stays stable and recovers the losses MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Analyzing two estimators, sequence probability and self-report, alongside parametric and non-parametric calibrators, we find that calibration improves F1 for both estimators while AUARC is less reliant on it. We evaluate six homogeneous and heterogeneous debating pairs per benchmark, across five benchmarks and four task types, spanning a range of model capabilities and sizes.

</details>


### [90] [From AGI to ASI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12683)
*Tim Genewein,Matija Franklin,Alexander Lerchner,Laurent Orseau,Samuel Albanie,Adam Bales,Cole Wyeth,Stephanie Chan,Iason Gabriel,Joel Z. Leibo,Allan Dafoe,Marcus Hutter,Thore Graepel,Shane Legg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This report examines the transition from human-level AGI to artificial general superintelligence (ASI), outlining four potential pathways (scaling, paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, multi-agent collectives), identifying frictions and bottlenecks, and arguing that ASI may emerge as a series of transformative societal changes rather than a single step change, necessitating a global interdisciplinary effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The imminent prospect of human-level AGI raises profound societal questions. The paper is motivated by the need to understand how AI development will continue post-AGI along the continuum toward Universal AI, specifically the under-explored transition from AGI to ASI, and to identify open research questions about frictions that could accelerate or decelerate this transition.

Method: The report employs conceptual and theoretical analysis grounded in the formal understanding of Universal AI. It characterizes ASI intuitively as a system more capable than large human organizations, then systematically maps four distinct pathways from AGI to ASI (scaling, paradigm shifts, recursive self-improvement, and large-scale multi-agent emergence). It further analyzes potential frictions and bottlenecks along these pathways and frames them as concrete open research questions.

Result: The analysis reveals that predicting ASI progress is highly uncertain, and it cannot be ruled out that AI progress will continue accelerating. Consequently, the notion of a single transformative "step change" at AGI may be misleading; a more accurate picture is a series of cascading, AI-driven societal transformations across science and technology.

Conclusion: The transition from AGI to ASI may unfold through multiple pathways, each subject to significant uncertainties and frictions. Rather than a singular event, AGI's introduction is better understood as catalyzing an ongoing series of transformative changes. Preparing for this prospect demands a massively interdisciplinary, globally-scoped endeavor.

Abstract: Over the last decade, building human-level artificial general intelligence has moved from far-fetched speculation to being a concrete next-decade target for many of the largest AI organisations. Achieving this goal would have profound and far-reaching impacts on human society, which raises many complex questions for the decade ahead. This report investigates how AI itself might continue to develop in a post-AGI world along the continuum of machine intelligence. The endpoint of this continuum, Universal AI, is theoretically well understood, which provides some formal grounding for the main focus of this report: the transition from human-level AGI to artificial general superintelligence, which, intuitively, can be understood as a system that is more intelligent and cognitively capable than large organisations of humans. After characterizing ASI, the report discusses four potential pathways from AGI to ASI: scaling AGI, AI paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, and ASI emerging from large-scale multi-agent collectives. The report then discusses possible frictions and bottlenecks along these pathways. Determining whether the impact of these frictions will be negligible or substantial raises a number of concrete open research questions. Due to large uncertainties for predicting ASI progress, it cannot be ruled out that AI progress might continue to accelerate over the next years. This could imply that the image of a single transformative step change, caused by the introduction of human-level AGI into our society, could be inaccurate. More apt might be the prospect of a series of transformative societal changes caused by AI-enabled progress and breakthroughs across many areas of science and technology. Preparing for this prospect requires a massively interdisciplinary endeavour of global scope and interest.

</details>


### [91] [Reward Modeling for Multi-Agent Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13598)
*King Yeung Tsang,Zihao Zhao,Vishal Venkataramani,Haizhou Shi,Zixuan Ke,Semih Yavuz,Shafiq Joty,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OrchRM is a self-supervised framework that evaluates multi-agent orchestration quality by constructing win-lose pairs from intermediate execution artifacts, enabling efficient reward-guided orchestrator training without human annotations. It achieves up to 10× training efficiency gains and up to 8% accuracy improvement in MAS test-time scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training orchestrators for LLM-based multi-agent systems is challenging due to limited supervision (no human-annotated labels for orchestration quality) and high computational cost from sub-agent rollouts. Existing approaches rely heavily on expensive token-level exploration at the sub-agent level, motivating a more efficient orchestration-level solution.

Method: OrchRM (Orchestration Reward Modeling) is a self-supervised framework that uses intermediate artifacts (e.g., sub-agent outputs, interaction traces) from multi-agent executions to automatically construct win-lose comparison pairs. These pairs are then used to train a Bradley-Terry reward model that scores orchestration quality. Critically, OrchRM operates directly at the orchestration level rather than requiring costly sub-agent rollouts, enabling efficient reward-guided training and test-time scaling.

Result: OrchRM improves training token efficiency by up to 10× compared to baselines while boosting MAS test-time scaling accuracy by up to 8%. These improvements generalize across three diverse domains: mathematical reasoning, web-based question answering, and multi-hop reasoning.

Conclusion: Orchestration-level reward modeling is a scalable and effective approach for training robust multi-agent orchestrators. By bypassing expensive sub-agent rollouts and leveraging self-supervised win-lose pairs, OrchRM establishes a promising direction for efficient and high-performing MAS coordination.

Abstract: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) built on Large Language Models (LLMs) require effective orchestration to coordinate specialized agents, yet training such orchestrators is hindered by limited supervision and high computational cost. We propose Orchestration Reward Modeling (OrchRM), a self-supervised framework for evaluating orchestration quality without human annotations. OrchRM leverages intermediate artifacts from multi-agent executions to construct win-lose pairs for Bradley-Terry reward model training. Unlike existing MAS test-time scaling and orchestrator training frameworks that rely on costly sub-agent rollouts, OrchRM operates directly at the orchestration level, enabling efficient and high-performing reward-guided orchestrator training and MAS test-time scaling. OrchRM improves training efficiency by up to 10x in token usage while improving MAS test-time scaling performance by up to 8% in accuracy. These gains consistently transfer across multiple domains, including mathematical reasoning, web-based question answering, and multi-hop reasoning, demonstrating orchestration-level reward modeling as a scalable direction for robust multi-agent orchestration. Code will be available at https://github.com/Wang-ML-Lab/OrchRM.

</details>


### [92] [Deployment-Centered Evaluation: Predicting Query-Level Rejection Risk in a Clinical LLM System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12702)
*Alyssa Unell,Miguel Fuentes,Brenna Li,Bridget Lin,Meena Jagadeesan,Sanmi Koyejo,Nigam Shah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A deployment-centered evaluation of an LLM system in electronic health records shows that incorporating deployment-specific context (provider type, department, model used) alongside query content enables prediction of user rejection (AUROC 0.719), supporting targeted guardrails and abstention mechanisms in clinical settings where feedback is sparse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static clinical NLP benchmarks measure correctness rather than real-world user acceptance, aggregate performance across queries, and require dense annotations — creating blind spots for evaluating LLM systems in actual deployment.
