The authors address the need to evaluate based on sparse, real-world user feedback signals that reflect genuine clinical rejection behavior.

Method: The authors train a pre-response classifier that predicts the risk of user rejection before generating an LLM response. The model uses query content plus deployment-specific context (provider type, department name, language model used). A prospective analysis is conducted over 4.5 months of user feedback at an academic medical center, with downstream evaluation in two use cases: guardrail triggering and abstention.

Result: The rejection prediction model achieved an AUROC of 0.719. The key finding is that deployment-specific context features (provider type, department, model) significantly improve predictive performance compared to using query content alone, demonstrating the feasibility of anticipating user dissatisfaction before generation.

Conclusion: Predicting user rejection using deployment-specific context is feasible and opens the door to targeted guardrails in clinical LLM systems. This shifts evaluation from static correctness benchmarking toward real-world, deployment-aware monitoring of user acceptance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into clinical systems, making it essential to evaluate the real-world utility of these systems. However, static benchmarks tend to measure correctness rather than user acceptance, aggregate performance across queries, and require densely annotated datasets -- leading to major blind spots for evaluating clinical systems. In this work, we perform a deployment-centered evaluation of an LLM system embedded within electronic health records at an academic medical center, where user feedback is sparse but closely reflects the deployment conditions. Specifically, we train a pre-response classifier that estimates the risk that a future interaction will result in the user rejecting the LLM response, based on query content and deployment-specific context available before generation. We conduct a prospective analysis of our model over 4.5 months of user feedback, finding that our prediction model achieves an AUROC of 0.719. Further, we estimate the benefit of such predictions in two downstream use cases (guardrail triggering and abstention). Our key conceptual insight is that making use of deployment-specific context (i.e., the provider type, department name, language model used for response), as opposed to only query content, improves the ability to predict whether the user will reject the system output. Altogether, our empirical case study demonstrates the feasibility of predicting user rejection using deployment-specific context, opening the door to targeted guardrails.

</details>


### [93] [Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning from Delayed Marketplace Feedback for Objective-Weight Adaptation in Three-Sided Dispatch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13604)
*Haochen Wu,Yi Hou,Shiguang Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DoorDash deployed an offline RL system that learns store-level dispatch weights to balance delivery speed and batching efficiency, using delayed marketplace feedback. The policy selects a discrete multiplier for the combinatorial optimizer, trained via Double Q-learning with conservative regularization, and showed improved batching and courier time without harming delivery quality in a live experiment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Three-sided marketplace dispatch involves tradeoffs among delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. Manual tuning of dispatch objective weights is brittle and cannot scale. The authors seek to automatically adapt these weights using reinforcement learning from delayed operational feedback, without replacing the production combinatorial optimizer or violating operational constraints.

Method: The system uses a store-level policy that selects a discrete multiplier to shift the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. A shared value function is trained offline from centralized logged data with decentralized store-level execution, using Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer (CQL-style) to mitigate overestimation on out-of-distribution actions. The interface treats the combinatorial solver as a fixed component, preserving feasibility constraints.

Result: In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increased batching rates and reduced courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality metrics.

Conclusion: World feedback from live economic and logistics systems can be used to safely adapt dispatch decision policies online. A lightweight policy interface (discrete multiplier) atop an existing optimizer, combined with conservative offline RL, provides a practical path to deploying learned dispatch control in large-scale marketplaces.

Abstract: Dispatch in three-sided marketplaces provides a natural setting for reinforcement learning from world feedback: decisions are evaluated by delayed operational outcomes such as delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. We present a deployed reinforcement learning system at DoorDash that adapts dispatch objective weights in a large-scale food-delivery marketplace using delayed signals. Rather than replacing the combinatorial assignment optimizer, a store-level policy learned from logged marketplace data selects a discrete multiplier that shifts the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. This interface enables offline policy learning under noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback while preserving production feasibility constraints and operational safeguards. We train a shared value function using centralized offline data and decentralized store-level execution, with Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer to reduce out-of-distribution value overestimation. In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality. Results illustrate how world feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be used to safely adapt decision policies online.

</details>


### [94] [Is It You or Your Environment? A Bayesian Inference Framework for Genomically-Anchored Personalized Physiological Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13556)
*Aruna Dey,Suraj Biswas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A cold-start problem in personalized health AI is addressed by using an individual's genomic profile as a fixed Bayesian prior to initialize physiological set points, enabling immediate interpretation of deviations before behavioral data accumulate, with the prior dynamically decaying as empirical data grow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized health AI systems require weeks of individual behavioral data before they can distinguish constitutional (innate) variation from environmentally driven deviations — the cold-start problem. Without a personalized baseline, the same physiological measurement (e.g., HRV of 55 ms) cannot be correctly interpreted as either suppression or enhancement for different individuals.

Method: A causal inference framework using genomic profiles as exogenous genetic anchors. The approach: (1) computes a personalized physiological set-point prior Ĝ = μ + Σ(βᵢ × gᵢ) using GWAS-derived effect sizes (βᵢ) and risk-allele counts (gᵢ); (2) interprets each incoming measurement P as a non-constitutional deviation δ = P − Ĝ; (3) dynamically decays the prior via Ĝ_t = w(t)·Ĝ_genomic + [1−w(t)]·P̄_t, transitioning from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference. Priors are graded by evidence strength, distinguishing robust anchors (FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5) from contested genes (SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2).

Result: The architecture is developed across six physiological domains. Using the framework, an observed HRV of 55 ms correctly yields a suppression hypothesis for someone with a genomic prior of 80 ms and an enhancement hypothesis for someone with a prior of 30 ms — a contextually appropriate reversal impossible without a personalized anchor. Four deployment constraints are defined: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output.

Conclusion: Genomic anchoring resolves the cold-start problem by providing an immediate, causally valid personalization mechanism that is exogenous, immune to reverse causation, and available pre-observation. The framework distinguishes association from Mendelian randomization and individual token causation, and requires disciplined deployment with evidence grading and dynamic prior decay rather than static genomic determinism.

Abstract: Personalized health AI systems face a fundamental cold-start problem: machine learning models for physiological interpretation require weeks of individual behavioral data before they can distinguish constitutional variation from environmentally driven deviation. We propose a solution grounded in causal inference and Bayesian prior design. An individual's genomic profile serves as an exogenous genetic anchor -- a domain-informed, personalized prior that is fixed at conception, immune to reverse causation, and available before a single behavioral observation is collected. The anchor initializes a Bayesian belief state over an individual's physiological set point G-hat = mu + sum(beta_i * g_i), where beta_i are GWAS-derived effect sizes and g_i are risk-allele counts. Each incoming physiological measurement P produces a non-constitutional deviation delta = P - G-hat that separates the signal attributable to environment and state from the constitutionally fixed baseline. As behavioral data accrue, the prior decays according to G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t, transitioning from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference. The same observed HRV of 55 ms generates a suppression hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 80 ms, and an enhancement hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 30 ms -- a reversal impossible without a personalized anchor. We develop this architecture across six physiological domains, grading genomic priors by evidence strength, distinguishing robustly replicated anchors (FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5) from contested candidate genes (SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2). We address the inference boundary between association, Mendelian randomization, and individual token causation, and define four constraints for deployment: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output.

</details>


### [95] [Definitional alignment before capability alignment: a Design-Science framework for adjudicating claims about AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12713)
*J. E. Aguilera Briones*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper develops DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual framework for evaluating the adjudicative fitness of AGI definitions. It provides five ordinal criteria and a governance audit structure, demonstrates the artifact on five measurement families, and stress-tests it against a 2024-2025 claim that current generative systems constitute AGI. The claim was certifiable only under performance-based operationalizations. The paper introduces "definitional sovereignty" as a governance concept.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: "AGI" lacks a single shared, stable referent; competing operationalizations yield contradictory verdicts on the same system. AGI arrival claims (both affirmative and skeptical) draw on overlapping but incompatible evidence. This under-specification is reframed as a design and governance problem requiring structured adjudicative tools, rather than a purely definitional debate.

Method: The paper follows Design Science Research Methodology (DSRM) to construct DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual artifact with two components: (1) five ordinal criteria for assessing adjudicative fitness of candidate AGI definitions (precision, stability, evaluability, ecological validity, and non-circularity), and (2) a structured governance audit covering authorship, interest, certification, external verification, and revision authority. The artifact is demonstrated on five prominent measurement families (performance-based, capability-ontology, psychometric, skill-acquisition, and economic) plus one deflationary boundary position, using a documented 2024-2025 corpus. It is then stress-tested against a stylized strong arrival claim.

Result: Under the stylized claim that current generative systems constitute AGI because they outperform well-educated adults on many cognitive tasks, the claim was certifiable only under a performance-based operationalization. Capability-ontology, psychometric, and skill-acquisition approaches did not certify it; the economic family remained indeterminate; and the deflationary position refused binary adjudication entirely. The artifact successfully discriminated among definitional families and revealed where and why adjudication breaks down.

Conclusion: The contribution is a novel integration and operationalization, not an empirical validation. Independent application, inter-rater reliability testing, and author-external case studies remain necessary. The paper proposes "definitional sovereignty" as an enabling component of algorithmic sovereignty: the institutional capacity to contest, certify, and revise imported technological categories under public accountability, framing AGI definition as a governance choice rather than a purely technical matter.

Abstract: Claims that artificial general intelligence has already arrived and claims that it remains decades away are often defended from overlapping evidence. "AGI" lacks a single shared and stable referent and competing operationalizations can return different verdicts on the same system. This article treats that under-specification as a design and governance problem. Following Design Science Research Methodology, it develops DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual artifact with two coupled components: five ordinal criteria for assessing the adjudicative fitness of candidate definitions and a structured governance audit of authorship, interest, certification, external verification and revision authority. The artifact is demonstrated on five prominent measurement families and one deflationary boundary position in a documented corpus and then stress-tested against a stylized strong arrival claim: that current generative systems constitute AGI because they outperform a well-educated adult on many cognitive tasks. On evidence from the cited 2024-2025 sources, the claim was certifiable only under a performance-based operationalization; capability-ontology, psychometric and skill-acquisition approaches did not certify it, the economic family remains indeterminate and the deflationary position refuses binary adjudication. The contribution is a novel integration and operationalization, not an empirical validation: independent application, inter-rater testing and author-external cases remain necessary. The paper further proposes definitional sovereignty as an enabling component of algorithmic sovereignty: the institutional capacity to contest, certify and revise imported technological categories under public accountability.

</details>


### [96] [Beyond Runtime Enforcement: Shield Synthesis as Defensibility Analysis for Adversarial Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13621)
*Achraf Hsain,Sultan Almuhammadi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper reframes shielded RL from a runtime safety mechanism to a design-time analytical tool. Using a constrained two-player safety game for network defense, it produces a "defensibility fingerprint" combining formal attractor-based metrics with operational behavior from adversarial MARL, enabling architectural what-if analysis that reveals formal and operational security are distinct dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional shielded RL treats the compiled temporal-logic specification as a runtime constraint on agent actions. The authors argue this is a misapplication: the automata-theoretic machinery is more valuable as a design-time instrument for analyzing whether, where, and how a system can be defended, providing structural insights rather than deployment-time restrictions.

Method: A constrained two-player safety game is formulated where defender and attacker specifications are enforced asymmetrically: the defender spec defines unsafe regions, the attacker spec restricts adversary actions during attractor computation. Solving yields a defensibility verdict, winning region, and shield. Attractor structure provides topology-level metrics, combined with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial MARL to form a "defensibility fingerprint." A what-if analysis examines how architectural changes affect formal vs. operational security.

Result: Formal defensibility and operational effectiveness capture distinct security dimensions: small architectural changes produce large operational shifts while formal safety margins remain nearly unchanged. This demonstrates that shield synthesis is most valuable for answering architectural questions about system defensibility rather than as a deployment mechanism for safe agents.

Conclusion: Shield synthesis should be repositioned as a design-time analytical framework. The primary output is the defensibility verdict — a formal certificate about whether a topology-specification pair is defensible — along with structural metrics, rather than a runtime-safe policy. This enables principled architectural reasoning about system security.

Abstract: Shielded reinforcement learning is typically presented as a runtime safety mechanism that compiles temporal-logic specifications into automata restricting an agent's actions. We argue this is the wrong product. The same automata-theoretic machinery -- specification compilation, product game construction, attractor computation, and winning-region extraction -- is better read as a design-time analytical instrument whose outputs are structural insights about a system rather than runtime constraints on a deployed agent.
  We instantiate this through a constrained two-player safety game for network defense. The two specifications are enforced asymmetrically: the defender specification defines the unsafe region of the game, whereas the attacker specification restricts the adversary's legal actions during attractor computation. Solving the game yields a defensibility verdict -- a formal certificate that a topology-specification pair is or is not defensible -- with the associated winning region and shield.
  Beyond the binary verdict, we derive topology-level metrics from the attractor structure and combine them with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial multi-agent reinforcement learning. Together these form a defensibility fingerprint capturing both a network's formal safety properties and its operational behavior under adaptive play.
  A what-if analysis shows that formal defensibility and operational effectiveness capture distinct aspects of security: small architectural changes can produce large shifts in operational outcomes while leaving formal safety margins nearly unchanged. Shield synthesis is thus most valuable not as a deployment mechanism for safe agents, but as a framework for answering architectural questions about whether, where, and how a system can be defended. The defensibility verdict is the output, not the safe policy.

</details>


### [97] [The Theory of Mind Utility: Formal Specification of a Mentalizing Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12721)
*Nikolos Gurney,Stacy Marsella*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ToM-U formalizes epistemic state inference by constructing and evaluating Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) — directed typed graphs tracking agents, information access histories, and epistemic relationships — to determine how mentalizing computes belief states without commitment to implementation details.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing accounts (Bayesian Theory of Mind, simulation theory, theory-theory) either presuppose belief states or lack formal mechanisms for epistemic state inference. ToM-U fills this gap by specifying what mentalizing computes at the computational level (Marr's level), deriving rather than assuming belief states from observable behavior.

Method: ToM-U introduces five formal definitions: (1) LEWM structure as directed typed graphs with agent/state nodes and epistemic relations; (2) agent node properties including ordered information access history; (3) a bounded proliferation mechanism for recursive mentalizing; (4) three inference procedures; and (5) a residue function capturing structured traces of failed mentalizing. Candidate LEWMs are evaluated against observed behavior until confidence thresholds are met.

Result: The architecture generates directional, falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failure that follow from structural model properties rather than auxiliary assumptions. ToM-U positions itself as a domain-agnostic mechanism upstream of goal inference and other social cognitive processes.

Conclusion: ToM-U provides a computational-level formalization of epistemic state inference that derives belief states from observable behavior using structured graph representations, offering testable predictions about mentalizing failures and serving as a foundational mechanism for downstream social cognition.

Abstract: Inferring others' beliefs requires more than reading surface signals; it requires tracking who told them what, in what order, and how credibly. The Theory of Mind Utility (ToM-U) formalizes this epistemic state inference problem at the computational level of analysis, specifying what mentalizing computes and why without commitment to algorithmic or neural implementation. ToM-U achieves this by constructing Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) -- directed typed graphs that represent agents, state nodes, and the epistemic relationships among them -- and evaluating discrete candidate LEWMs against observed behavior until one achieves sufficient confidence. Five formal definitions specify the LEWM structure, agent node properties including ordered information access history, a bounded proliferation mechanism for recursive mentalizing, three inference procedures, and a residue function that captures the structured trace left by failed mentalizing attempts. ToM-U differs from Bayesian Theory of Mind and adjacent formal accounts, which presuppose rather than derive belief states, and from simulation theory and theory-theory, which lack a formal apparatus for epistemic state inference. The architecture generates directional, falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failure that follow from structural properties of the model rather than auxiliary assumptions, and positions ToM-U as a domain-agnostic mechanism upstream of goal inference and other downstream social cognitive processes.

</details>


### [98] [Rethinking Psychometric Evaluation of LLMs: When and Why Self-Reports Predict Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12730)
*Rafal Kocielnik,Pengrui Han,Peiyang Song,Myrl G. Marmarelis,Ramit Debnath,Dean Mobbs,Anima Anandkumar,R. Michael Alvarez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper shows that Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) achieves human-level coherence between LLM self-reports and behavior within shared conversations, unlike Big 5 personality traits. However, across separate conversations, coherence only persists for training-anchored behaviors (e.g., implicit bias) and collapses for context-primed behaviors (e.g., sycophancy). Persona prompting makes self-reports more consistent but fails to align behavior, suggesting deployment safety requires task-specific psychometric instruments evaluated across diverse contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work found substantial dissociation between LLM self-reports and actual behavior using Big 5 personality traits, questioning whether LLMs can reliably self-report. However, Big 5 traits weakly predict specific behaviors even in humans. The authors investigate whether the observed dissociation stems from using the wrong psychometric framework and insufficient context matching, rather than a fundamental LLM limitation.

Method: The authors contrast Big 5 with the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) across four behavioral tasks (implicit bias, sycophancy, etc.) using 11 frontier LLMs. They systematically vary session context (same vs. separate conversations) and identity induction (persona prompting) to test when SR-behavior coherence emerges or breaks down.

Result: Three key findings: (1) Within a shared conversation, TPB achieves human-level SR-behavior coherence; Big 5 does not. (2) Across separate conversations, coherence survives only for behaviors anchored in training (implicit bias) but collapses for context-primed behaviors (sycophancy). (3) Persona prompting increases self-report consistency across conversations but does not improve SR-behavior alignment.

Conclusion: Coarse personality frameworks like Big 5 are inadequate for predicting deployment behavior in LLMs. More task-specific, behavior-targeted instruments (like TPB) are needed, and even these must be validated across diverse tasks and conversational contexts to ensure reliable behavioral anticipation for safe AI deployment.

Abstract: Anticipating LLM behavioral tendencies from low-cost psychometric probes is critical for safe deployment, but only if self-reports (SR) reliably predict behavior. Recent work documented substantial SR-behavior dissociation in LLMs, but relied on broad personality traits (Big 5) that predict specific behaviors weakly, even in humans. Furthermore, the isolation of conversational sessions combined with weak context matching left open whether LLMs truly lack coherence or whether the conditions needed to detect such coherence were not met. We contrast Big 5 with the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), which measures intention targeted to a specific behavior and predicts human behavior substantially better than broad traits. We run experiments across four behavioral tasks and 11 frontier LLMs, while also varying session context and identity induction. We find that SR-behavior coherence exists but is selective. 1) Within a shared conversation, the Theory of Planned Behavior reaches human-level coherence; Big 5 does not. 2) Across separate conversations, coherence survives only for behaviors anchored outside the immediate prompt, such as implicit bias shaped by training, and collapses when behavior is strongly primed by context, as with sycophancy. 3) Persona prompting makes self-reports more consistent across conversations, but does not bring behavior into alignment. These findings suggest that coarse personality frameworks, such as Big 5 may not be the best tools for testing deployment behavior. More task- and behavior-specific instruments are needed, and even these must be evaluated across tasks and contexts.

</details>


### [99] [Benchmarking AI Agents for Addressing Scientific Challenges Across Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12736)
*Tianyu Liu,Allen Xin Wang,Antonia Panescu,Lisa Xinyi Chen,Wenxin Long,Xinyu Wei,Yueqian Jing,Ziyao Zeng,Jihang Chen,Sihan Jiang,Ziqing Wang,Siyi Gu,Siyu Chen,Xinyang Hu,Haoran Shao,Leqi Xu,Wangjie Zheng,Zhiyuan Cao,Ada Fang,Botao Yu,Kunyang Sun,Rex Ying,Arman Cohan,Qingyu Chen,Lingzhou Xue,Kaize Ding,Yuanqi Du,Wengong Jin,Zhuoran Yang,Marinka Zitnik,James Zou,Hua Xu,Hongyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciAgentArena is a benchmark of ~200 real-world scientific tasks with stepwise verification and an interactive environment for evaluating AI agents, revealing that current agents perform well on structured data-analysis tasks but struggle with novel insight generation, self-directed exploration, and open-ended research problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI agent benchmarks fail to capture the complexity, heterogeneity, and extended reasoning required by real scientific research, while scientific task benchmarks oversimplify research into static, direct problems with limited interactive evaluation. There is a need for a benchmark that bridges this gap.

Method: The authors introduce SciAgentArena, a systematic benchmark comprising approximately 200 tasks with stepwise verification and an interactive, agent-agnostic environment. Tasks are drawn from emerging needs across multiple scientific domains and are designed to reflect real-world research scenarios. The platform supports evaluation of diverse AI agents.

Result: Current AI agents perform effectively on well-specified data-analysis workflows with clear task structures and evaluation criteria. However, performance is uneven across scientific contexts: agents struggle with generating novel insights, sustaining self-directed exploration, and formulating robust solutions for open-ended research questions. Common failure modes are characterized across agents.

Conclusion: SciAgentArena provides a practical framework for measuring and guiding progress in AI agents for science. It highlights key areas needing improvement—reliability, autonomy, and scientific reasoning—to enable future agents capable of tackling complex scientific challenges.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly being developed to accelerate scientific discovery, yet their practical capabilities in real research settings remain poorly understood. Existing benchmarks for AI agents rarely capture the complexity, heterogeneity, and extended reasoning required by scientific work, whereas benchmarks for scientific tasks often reduce research to static, direct problems and provide limited support for interactive evaluation. Here, we introduce SciAgentArena, a systematic benchmark for evaluating AI agents in real-world scientific research scenarios drawn from emerging needs across multiple domains. SciAgentArena comprises approximately 200 tasks with stepwise verification and an interactive, agent-agnostic environment for assessing diverse AI agents. Using this benchmark, we find that current agents can contribute effectively to well-specified data-analysis workflows, particularly when the task structure and evaluation criteria are clear. However, their performance remains uneven across scientific contexts: agents struggle to generate genuinely novel insights, sustain self-directed exploration, and formulate robust solutions for open-ended research questions. We further characterize common failure modes across agents and identify opportunities for improving their reliability, autonomy, and scientific reasoning. Together, SciAgentArena provides a practical framework for measuring progress in AI agents for science and for guiding the design of future agents capable of addressing complex scientific challenges. Full codes, tasks, and datasets can be accessed via this link: https://sciagentarena.github.io/.

</details>


### [100] [Reducing the Complexity of Deep Learning Models for EEG Analysis on Wearable Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12742)
*Farough Shayeste Roodi,Parham Zilouchian Moghaddam,Mahdi Mohammadi-nasab,Mehdi Modarressi,Mostafa Ersali Salehi Nasab,Masoud Daneshtalab*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates the feasibility of deploying deep neural networks for EEG-based epileptic seizure detection on resource-constrained wearable devices by analyzing the accuracy-complexity trade-offs achieved through parameter quantization and electrode reduction techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wearable healthcare IoT devices rely on ECG and EEG signal analysis, but deep neural networks demand computational, energy, and memory resources far exceeding what these devices can provide, hindering practical deployment of deep learning in wearable services.

Method: The authors explore the trade-off between accuracy and computational complexity by applying parameter quantization and electrode reduction methods to several state-of-the-art DNN models designed for EEG-based epileptic seizure detection.

Result: When applied judiciously, parameter quantization and electrode reduction techniques significantly reduce DNN complexity with minimal adverse effects on classification accuracy, revealing explicit trade-offs between accuracy and complexity reduction for online EEG analysis.

Conclusion: Deploying DNN-based EEG analysis on resource-constrained wearable devices is feasible when appropriate compression and reduction techniques are employed, as they enable substantial complexity reduction while preserving acceptable accuracy levels.

Abstract: Wearable healthcare devices are the fastest-growing Internet of Things (IoT) sector. Many automated healthcare services rely on two crucial biological signals, namely ECG and EEG, which reflect the activity of the heart and brain, respectively. Although deep neural networks are considered the primary way to process and analyze these signals, the very tight energy and computational power constraints in wearable devices are far below the computational, energy, and memory bandwidth demands of DNN models, thereby impeding the deployment of deep learning in many practical wearable services. This paper investigates the feasibility of deploying state-of-the-art DNN models in resource-constrained wearable devices. Notably, we explore the trade-off between accuracy and computational complexity of DNNs when parameter quantization and electrode reduction methods are used. Our investigation centers on several state-of-the-art DNN models designed for EEG signal analysis, specifically for detecting epileptic seizures. Our findings demonstrate that, when applied judiciously, these techniques can significantly reduce the complexity of the DNNs under consideration with minimal adverse effects on accuracy. These results reveal the explicit trade-offs between accuracy and complexity reduction encountered when adapting DNN-based online EEG analysis for wearable devices.

</details>


### [101] [Prefill Awareness in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12747)
*Andy Wang,Parv Mahajan,David Demitri Africa,Alexandra Souly,Jordan Taylor,Robert Kirk*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frontier language models can detect when their prior assistant messages have been tampered with or prefilled (a capability termed "prefill awareness"), with Claude Opus 4.5 detecting opposing prefills 9-35% of the time at zero false positives. This poses a significant confound for safety methods that rely on prefill-based evaluation protocols.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety evaluations (alignment, jailbreaking, AI control) often depend on prefilling model outputs. If models can recognize tampered prefills and adjust behavior accordingly, the validity of these safety methods is compromised. The authors aim to systematically measure whether frontier models possess this "prefill awareness" capability and understand its underlying mechanisms.

Method: The authors construct a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms (preference-opposing, preference-neutral, and preference-aligned), filtering for cases where models show consistent stances. They measure detection rates and false positive rates, conduct controlled ablations to disentangle the roles of stylistic mismatch versus preference mismatch, and examine realistic agentic settings including misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories.

Result: Claude Opus 4.5 detects preference-opposing prefills in 9-35% of cases with 0% false positive rate. Models often revert toward baseline behavior without explicitly flagging the prefill as foreign. Ablations reveal that stylistic mismatch primarily drives detection (flagging as foreign), while preference mismatch primarily drives behavioral reversion. In agentic settings, models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns, with behavior depending strongly on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts.

Conclusion: Prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for some prefill-based safety methods. The authors recommend that model developers systematically track this capability in frontier systems to ensure the continued validity of alignment evaluations, jailbreaking tests, and AI control protocols.

Abstract: Safety-relevant studies of language models, including alignment and jailbreaking evaluations and AI control protocols, often rely on prefilling model outputs. If AI models can recognize and act on the fact their prior assistant messages have been inserted or edited, the effectiveness and validity of these methods could be compromised. We investigate whether frontier language models can distinguish between tampered and untampered assistant-side context, a capability we call prefill awareness. To do so, we construct a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms, filtering for cases where models show consistent stances. We find that frontier models show substantial prefill awareness: Claude Opus 4.5 detects prefills opposing its preferences in 9-35% of cases with a 0% false positive rate when prompted; additionally, models often revert towards baseline behavior without explicitly reporting that the prefill was foreign. Controlled ablations later also show that detection and resistance rely on different cues, where stylistic mismatch mainly affects whether models flag a prefill as foreign, while preference mismatch mainly affects whether they revert toward their baseline answer. We also examine more realistic agentic settings such as misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories, where frontier models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns in ways that depend strongly on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts. Our results indicate that prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for some prefill-based methods. We recommend that model developers track this capability in frontier systems.

</details>


### [102] [Constructing Evaluation Datasets for Procedural Reasoning: Balancing Naturalness, Grounding, and Multi-Hop Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12767)
*Sarah Elshabrawy,Rahul K. Dass,Ashok K. Goel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper compares three strategies for generating question-answer datasets to evaluate procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems, finding that strict TMK-based generation yields the highest grounding (96.5%) and usability (92.6%), while more natural strategies improve learner-likeness but sacrifice representational fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems requires QA datasets that are simultaneously learner-like and grounded in the instructional knowledge representations (TMK models) that the system uses. Existing generation approaches trade off between these two qualities, and no systematic comparison exists to guide dataset construction.

Method: The authors compare three QA generation strategies across 23 instructional topics and 690 question-answer pairs: (1) strict generation directly from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models, (2) transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and (3) TMK-aware generation combining transcripts with structured TMK guidance. They introduce a grounding validation framework using closed-set evidence units extracted from TMK models, measuring answer support, question self-containedness, and multi-hop procedural reasoning targeting.

Result: Strict TMK generation achieves the best overall quality: 96.5% grounded questions and 92.6% usable questions. Transcript-first generation produces more learner-like but more context-dependent and weakly grounded items. TMK-aware generation yields the highest raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding scores. The results demonstrate that procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding.

Conclusion: Explicit representation-aware validation is essential for constructing evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning. Procedural richness and natural phrasing alone are insufficient proxies for quality; grounding against the underlying knowledge representation must be directly measured. The proposed validation framework offers a principled approach for this purpose.

Abstract: Evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems requires question-answer datasets that are both learner-like and grounded in the instructional knowledge the system is expected to use. We study how TMK-based question generation strategies affect dataset quality for procedural and multi-hop reasoning.
  We compare three strategies: strict generation from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models, transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and TMK-aware generation that combines transcripts with structured guidance. To evaluate generated items, we introduce a grounding validation framework based on closed-set evidence units extracted from TMK models. The framework measures whether answers are supported by the underlying representation, whether questions are self-contained, and whether they target multi-hop procedural reasoning.
  Across 23 instructional topics and 690 generated question-answer pairs, strict TMK generation achieves the strongest overall quality, with 96.5% grounded questions and 92.6% usable questions. Transcript-first generation produces more learner-like questions but more context-dependent or weakly grounded items, while TMK-aware generation yields high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding. These results show that procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding, motivating explicit representation-aware validation for evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning.

</details>


### [103] [A Tutorial on World Models and Physical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12783)
*Il-Seok Oh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This tutorial paper presents a unified framework for world modeling in AI, distinguishing between explicit models (structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning) and implicit models (predictive structure within learned representations). It positions world modeling as foundational for physical AI, discusses foundation models as a path toward unified perception-prediction-action systems, and identifies key open challenges in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World modeling is emerging as a central principle for building intelligent systems that go beyond reactive control to achieve prediction, reasoning, and decision making. The paper is motivated by the need to organize and unify diverse approaches to world modeling under a coherent conceptual framework, clarifying the complementary roles of explicit and implicit paradigms and charting a path toward artificial general intelligence.

Method: The paper adopts a tutorial-style conceptual synthesis rather than proposing a novel algorithm. It introduces a unifying framework organized around a core distinction: explicit world models that learn structured, interpretable dynamics supporting rollout-based planning, versus implicit world models that encode predictive structure within scalable learned representations (e.g., latent-space models). The framework unifies these paradigms through shared predictive structure while differentiating them by representation and exploitation strategy. It further incorporates the role of foundation models as integrative systems spanning perception, prediction, and action.

Result: The framework demonstrates that explicit and implicit world models are not competing but complementary paradigms for physical AI in domains such as robotics and autonomous driving. Foundation models offer a promising pathway toward unified systems. However, major unresolved challenges persist in three areas critical to AGI: hierarchical reasoning across abstraction levels, long-horizon planning under uncertainty, and autonomous goal formation without human specification.

Conclusion: World modeling provides a foundational principle for intelligent systems, with explicit and implicit approaches offering complementary strengths. Unified through shared predictive structure, these paradigms enable physical AI but require advances in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation to progress toward artificial general intelligence. Foundation models represent a promising integrative direction.

Abstract: World modeling is emerging as a central principle for building intelligent systems capable of prediction, reasoning, and decision making. A central distinction can be drawn between explicit world models, which learn structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning and planning, and implicit world models, which encode predictive structure within scalable learned representations. These complementary paradigms provide a foundation for physical AI in domains such as robotics and autonomous driving, enabling intelligence beyond reactive control under real-world constraints. Recent foundation models further suggest a pathway toward unified systems integrating perception, prediction, and action. Despite rapid progress, major challenges remain in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation, which are critical for advancing toward artificial general intelligence. This tutorial presents a coherent framework in which diverse world modeling approaches are unified through shared predictive structure and differentiated by how such structure is represented and exploited.

</details>


### [104] [The Containment Gap: How Deployed Agentic AI Frameworks Fail Public-Facing Safety Requirements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12797)
*Md Jafrin Hossain,Mohammad Arif Hossain,Weiqi Liu,Nirwan Ansari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An audit of three leading agentic LLM frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK) reveals zero native containment guarantees. A memory-poisoning attack on a simulated benefits agent achieves 88.9% targeted wrongful denial rates while preserving aggregate accuracy. Two lightweight defenses (<0.2ms overhead) eliminate both attack vectors, highlighting that current frameworks lack secure-by-default properties for high-stakes deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic LLM systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes public-facing domains (government services, healthcare, finance). The authors ask whether the frameworks used to build these systems provide architectural-level structural safety guarantees — specifically, containment mechanisms that would prevent one compromised component from corrupting others or persistent memory.

Method: The authors derive six containment principles from a compositional model of agentic architectures (covering isolation, memory integrity, policy enforcement, etc.). They audit three frameworks — LangChain, AutoGPT, and OpenAI Agents SDK — against these principles. They then empirically validate findings by constructing a simulated government benefits agent in LangChain, executing a memory-poisoning attack that injects corrupt data into persistent memory, and measuring both targeted wrongful denial rates and aggregate accuracy under single-factor and complex five-factor policies. Finally, they prototype two lightweight defenses: a memory integrity validator and a policy gate.

Result: None of the three frameworks natively implement any of the six containment principles, including memory integrity — a defense against one of the most prevalent vulnerability classes. In the LangChain benefits agent, a single memory-poisoning write produces persistent corruption across all seeds and backends, yielding an 88.9% wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants. Under a five-factor policy, the attack increases targeted wrongful denials 3.5x while preserving aggregate accuracy, making detection via standard monitoring difficult. The proposed memory integrity validator and policy gate eliminate both attack vectors with sub-millisecond per-call overhead (<0.2ms).

Conclusion: The current agentic framework ecosystem does not meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments. The authors call for priority architectural interventions — specifically, built-in containment mechanisms like memory integrity and policy gates — to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications.

Abstract: Agentic large language model systems that autonomously invoke tools, maintain persistent memory, and execute multi-step plans are increasingly deployed in public-facing domains, including government services, healthcare triage, and financial advising. We ask whether the frameworks used to build these systems provide architectural-level structural safety guarantees. Applying six containment principles derived from a compositional model of agentic architectures, we audit three dominant frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, and OpenAI Agents SDK) and find no native compliance in any of them. Memory integrity, a defense against one of the most prevalent vulnerability classes, is not observed in any of the three evaluated frameworks. We validate these findings empirically: in a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, a single memory-poisoning write induces persistent targeted corruption across all tested seeds and backends, increasing the wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants to 88.9%. Under a complex five-factor policy, the same attack preserves aggregate accuracy while increasing targeted wrongful denials by 3.5x, rendering the corruption difficult to detect through standard monitoring. We then introduce two lightweight containment mechanisms: a memory integrity validator and a policy gate, which eliminate both attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2ms per call). We conclude that the current agentic framework ecosystem may not yet meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments and outline priority architectural interventions to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications.

</details>


### [105] [MLUBench: A Benchmark for Lifelong Unlearning Evaluation in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12809)
*He Li,Haoang Chi,Qizhou Wang,Yunxin Mao,Zhiheng Zhang,Jie Tan,Tongliang Liu,Wenjing Yang,Bo Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MLUBench, a comprehensive benchmark for multimodal LLM lifelong unlearning with 127 entities across 9 classes, revealing that existing methods suffer cumulative degradation. Proposes LUMoE, which mitigates this degradation while preserving multimodal alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data owners increasingly request removal of specific content from MLLMs, and these unlearning requests arrive sequentially over time. Existing benchmarks are too limited in scale and scope to capture the complexities of this lifelong unlearning setting.

Method: Authors introduce MLUBench (a large-scale benchmark with 127 entities across 9 classes) and propose LUMoE, a method designed to address the unique challenge of preserving multimodal alignment during sequential unlearning, where unlearning from one modality can otherwise degrade the entire model.

Result: Extensive experiments on MLUBench reveal that existing unlearning methods suffer severe, cumulative degradation over time. LUMoE significantly mitigates this degradation compared to baselines.

Conclusion: MLLM lifelong unlearning poses a unique challenge distinct from unimodal unlearning due to the need to preserve cross-modal alignment. LUMoE effectively addresses this, and the open-sourced MLUBench facilitates further research.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are trained on massive multimodal data, making data unlearning increasingly important as data owners may request the removal of specific content. In practice, these requests often arrive sequentially over time, giving rise to the challenging problem of MLLM Lifelong Unlearning. However, most existing benchmarks are limited in scale and scope, failing to capture the complexities of MLLM lifelong unlearning. To fill this gap, we introduce the MLUBench, a large-scale and comprehensive benchmark featuring 127 entities across 9 classes under lifelong unlearning requests. We perform extensive experiments using MLUBench and reveal that existing unlearning methods suffer from severe, cumulative degradation. More critically, we further identify the unique challenge of this problem: unlike in unimodal models, MLLM lifelong unlearning is constrained by the need to preserve multimodal alignment. Continually unlearning from one modality could degrade the entire model. To alleviate this challenge, we propose LUMoE, an effective method. Experiments demonstrate that LUMoE significantly mitigates the degradation problem faced by baselines. The source code and the MLUBench dataset are open-sourced in https://github.com/lihe-maxsize/Lifelong_Unlearning_main.

</details>


### [106] [Teach-and-Repeat: Accurately Extracting Operational Knowledge from Mobile Screen Demonstrations to Empower GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12817)
*Yudong Zhang,Lei Hu,Daoyang Liu,Jiawei Liu,Yangfan Luo,Xingyu Liu,Zuojian Wang,Zhilin Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Teach VLM translates mobile screen demonstration videos into step-wise operational knowledge (natural-language sentences describing actions, UI targets, arguments, and execution order). A data flywheel addresses training data scarcity, and a Chinese benchmark enables fine-grained evaluation. The Teach-and-Repeat paradigm uses this generated knowledge to guide downstream execution agents, achieving SOTA on operation semantics prediction and improving task success rates in Android World.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile UI understanding is shifting from static perception to dynamic action comprehension. Existing VLMs struggle to infer underlying operations from demonstration videos due to highly diverse and heterogeneous UI designs across applications. There is also a scarcity of aligned training data for this task.

Method: The authors propose Teach VLM, a model that extracts and analyzes operation-related keyframes from mobile screen demonstration videos to translate trajectories into operational knowledge. A systematic data flywheel enables scalable data acquisition. A novel Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark is introduced for fine-grained evaluation. The Teach-and-Repeat paradigm then uses the generated operational knowledge as an interpretable procedural reference to guide downstream screen-based execution agents.

Result: Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in operation semantics prediction. The Teach-and-Repeat paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents in Android World experiments.

Conclusion: Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm together provide a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation on mobile devices.

Abstract: Understanding the digital world on mobile devices is shifting from static UI perception to dynamic action comprehension. This capability enables models to convert visual state transitions into operational knowledge, defined as short natural-language sentences that describe action types, target UI elements, textual arguments, and execution orders. However, due to the highly diverse and heterogeneous UI designs across applications, existing vision-language models (VLMs) struggle to accurately infer these underlying operations. To bridge this gap, we introduce Teach VLM, a core model designed to translate mobile screen trajectories into step-wise operational knowledge by extracting and analyzing operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos. To address the scarcity of aligned training data, we develop a systematic data flywheel for scalable data acquisition. We further introduce a novel Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark for fine-grained evaluation. Building upon Teach VLM, we propose the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm, where the generated operational knowledge serves as an interpretable procedural reference to guide downstream screen-based execution agents. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in operation semantics prediction. Furthermore, experiments in Android World show that our paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents. Together, Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm offer a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation.

</details>


### [107] [GeoNatureAgent Benchmark: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Environmental Geospatial Analysis Across Frontier and Open-Weight Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12821)
*Gabriel Diaz-Ireland,Diego Prieto-Herráez,Mario García Peces,Javier Velázquez,Devika Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces GeoNatureAgent Benchmark, the first benchmark evaluating LLM-based AI agents on environmental analysis via structured tool calls against a real, self-hostable geospatial API. Evaluates 7 LLMs across 93 tasks in 18 categories, finding Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8%, comparison tasks remain unsolved, and structured tool calling is far more discriminative than general GIS benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Environmental scientists spend excessive time on data wrangling rather than analysis. AI agents that could automate geospatial workflows lack validation: no existing benchmark evaluates agents operating through structured tool calling against real APIs, leaving a critical evaluation gap for environmental analysis automation.

Method: The benchmark comprises 93 tasks across 18 categories (municipality analysis, multi-turn conversation, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling/recovery, ranking, comparison, multilingual understanding, habitat analysis, task rejection). It uses an open, self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators (CO2, erosion, land cover) across Spain and Portugal via 16 tools. Seven LLMs are evaluated under three temperature-1.0 seeds, with both capability (accuracy) and per-case cost measured as orthogonal axes. Extensibility is demonstrated via BigEarthNet V2 land cover integration.

Result: Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8% ± 0.8%, followed by DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% ± 3.1%; no other model exceeds 51%. The cost-accuracy Pareto frontier is dominated by open-weight models; DeepSeek V3.2 achieves 93% of Claude's capability at 11× lower cost ($0.011/case). Comparison tasks remain universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons), exposing systematic reasoning limits. Structured tool calling against a real API is 25–35 points more discriminative than general-purpose GIS benchmarks.

Conclusion: Structured tool calling against real geospatial APIs provides a stringent, discriminative evaluation framework for environmental AI agents. Open-weight models approach frontier-model performance at dramatically lower cost, but fundamental reasoning weaknesses (e.g., comparison tasks) persist across all models. The benchmark and API are publicly released to spur further research.

Abstract: Environmental scientists spend disproportionate effort on data wrangling rather than analysis, and AI agents that automate geospatial workflows remain unvalidated: no benchmark evaluates agents operating through structured tool calling against real APIs. We introduce the GeoNatureAgent Benchmark, the first benchmark for environmental analysis agents that operate via structured tool calls to a production-style geospatial API. It comprises 93 tasks across 18 categories, covering municipality analysis, multi-turn conversation, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling and recovery, ranking, comparison, multilingual understanding, habitat analysis, and task rejection. Tasks are evaluated against an open, self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators across Spain and Portugal via sixteen tools. We evaluate seven LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3.2, GLM-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, Llama 4 Scout) under three temperature-1.0 seeds, reporting capability and per-case cost as orthogonal axes. We find: (1) Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8% +/- 0.8%, followed by DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% +/- 3.1%, with no other model above 51%; (2) the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier is occupied mostly by open-weight models, with DeepSeek V3.2 offering 93% of Claude's capability at 11x lower cost ($0.011/case); (3) comparison tasks remain universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons), exposing systematic reasoning limits; and (4) structured tool calling against a real API is more discriminative than general-purpose GIS benchmarks, with accuracies 25-35 points lower. We further show extensibility by integrating BigEarthNet V2 land cover for Portugal alongside Spanish CO2 and erosion indicators. The benchmark, harness, and self-hostable API are publicly available.

</details>


### [108] [Topical Phase Transitions in Artificial Intelligence Research: Large-Scale Evidence and an Early-Warning Signature for Emerging Topics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12828)
*Rasul Khanbayov,Hasan Kurban*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Analysis of 80,814 AI papers (2017–2025) from five top conferences reveals that major AI topics exhibit "phase transitions" — sudden, cross-venue surges after years of marginality — rather than gradual growth. LLMs and diffusion models exemplify this pattern, while reinforcement learning grew smoothly. The authors develop an early-warning signature that retrospectively predicts transitions with 63% recall and flag reasoning, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, RAG, and world models as candidates for 2026–2028.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether AI research topics grow gradually or through abrupt, detectable shifts, and to characterize how research reorganizes across venues at scale. A secondary goal is to develop an early-warning system that can identify emerging phase transitions before they peak.

Method: The authors analyze 80,814 accepted papers from ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, and NeurIPS (2017–2025). They define four publication-dynamics criteria (frozen on 2017–2021 data) as an early-warning signature for topical phase transitions, then evaluate this signature out-of-sample on 2023–2025 transitions. The method distinguishes genuine phase transitions (e.g., LLMs, diffusion models, vision-language models) from smooth compound growth (e.g., reinforcement learning) using cross-venue publication trajectories.

Result: Major AI topics exhibit topical phase transitions: remaining marginal for years then surging across venues within 1–3 years. The early-warning signature achieves 27% precision and 63% recall against a 13.5% base rate. Applied to 2025 data, the signature flags reasoning/test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models as topics likely to surge during 2026–2028.

Conclusion: AI research does not advance uniformly but through abrupt, cross-venue phase transitions. These transitions leave detectable early-warning footprints, enabling systematic identification of emerging topics before they peak. The flagged 2025 topics represent the most probable candidates for the next wave of AI research reorganization.

Abstract: Do research topics in artificial intelligence grow gradually, or do they advance through abrupt, detectable jumps? Analyzing 80,814 accepted main-track papers from five premier AI conferences (ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS) spanning 2017 to 2025, we show major AI topics advance through topical phase transitions: remaining marginal for years, then surging across venues within one to three years. Large language models became the dominant cross-venue topic by 2025, diffusion models rose with comparable abruptness, and language-model methods crossed into computer vision via vision-language models, whereas reinforcement learning compounded smoothly, distinguishing genuine phase transitions from ordinary growth. This structure is our primary contribution: a large-scale, cross-venue characterization of how AI research reorganizes. We then ask whether a transition leaves a detectable footprint before it peaks. We define an early-warning signature, four publication-dynamics criteria frozen on 2017-2021 data, and evaluate it out of sample on 2023-2025 transitions, obtaining a precision of 27% and recall of 63% against a 13.5% base rate. Applied to 2025 data, the signature flags reasoning and test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models as topics to monitor over 2026-2028. The source code is also publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/KurbanIntelligenceLab/ai-phase-transitions.

</details>


### [109] [Fantastic Scientific Agents and How to Build Them: AgentBuild for Rietveld Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12834)
*Woong Shin,Craig A. Bridges,Marshall T. McDonnell,Rafael Ferreira da Silva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentBuild frames scientific agent construction as a workflow stage governed by a scientist-authored contract (rubric + curriculum + knowledge base). A rubric-driven judge and meta-optimizer iteratively edit an agent within declared boundaries, compiling the agent without burying the scientist's judgment. Demonstrated on Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data, the system progresses through an LLZO signal-to-noise ladder, treating frontier failures as contract failures rather than optimization failures. The contract remains the durable, reusable asset as base models evolve.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As scientific workflows transition from deterministic executables to LLM-based agents, prevailing development practices (fine-tuning, RL, prompt-and-go) obscure or bypass the scientist's judgment. The authors aim to preserve scientific expertise as a version-controlled, auditable, and durable asset rather than having it dissolved into opaque model weights or ad-hoc prompts.

Method: AgentBuild treats agent construction as a compilation stage. The scientist authors a **contract** comprising three components: (1) a version-controlled rubric specifying scoring criteria, (2) a difficulty-graded curriculum of tasks, and (3) a curated external knowledge base. A **rubric-driven judge** evaluates candidate agents against the curriculum, and a **meta-optimizer coding agent** edits the agent within a declared boundary. The build process iterates — the judge gates progress, the optimizer revises — until the agent satisfies the contract. The system is instantiated for Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data via GSAS-II, using MCP (Model Context Protocol) and A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocols, tested on a lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide (LLZO) signal-to-noise ladder culminating in a 4-hour scan frontier case.

Result: The blank-harness AgentBuild run successfully progressed through the LLZO signal-to-noise ladder up to a 4-hour scan frontier case. The rubric scores both fit credibility and trajectory scope, so the frontier limitation manifests as a **contract failure** (the contract cannot be satisfied under current constraints) rather than a pattern-fitting failure. This exposes workflow-scope limits transparently. As base models improve, re-running AgentBuild constitutes a re-tune (leveraging the existing contract) rather than a full rebuild.

Conclusion: AgentBuild demonstrates that treating agent construction as a contract-compilation workflow stage preserves the scientist's judgment as a durable, version-controlled asset. The contract — not the agent weights or prompts — becomes the primary scientific artifact. This approach provides auditability, reproducibility, and graceful evolution as foundation models improve, while making failure modes interpretable as contract-boundary violations rather than opaque optimization gaps.

Abstract: As scientific workflows shift from deterministic executables to LLM-based agents, the development practices on offer, such as fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and prompt-and-go, bury the scientist's judgment. We propose treating agent construction as a workflow stage and introduce AgentBuild, which builds a scientific agent from a contract the scientist authors. The contract is a version-controlled rubric, a difficulty-graded curriculum, and a curated external knowledge base. A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the agent within a declared boundary, so the build compiles the agent, not the scientist's judgment. We instantiate this for Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data through GSAS-II behind MCP and A2A, where a blank-harness construction run progresses through a lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide (LLZO) signal-to-noise ladder, reaches the 4 hour scan as a frontier case, and exposes the workflow-scope limits that remain. The same rubric that rewards credible fits also scores trajectory scope, making the frontier a contract failure rather than a pattern-fitting failure. As base models evolve, re-running AgentBuild is a re-tune, not a rebuild, and the scientist's authored contract remains the durable asset.

</details>


### [110] [(Human) Attention Is (Still) All You Need: Human oversight makes AI-assisted social science reliable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12848)
*Chen Zhu,Xiaolu Wang,Weilong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HLER, a human-in-the-loop architecture with pre-commitment, deterministic computation, and human decision gates, reduces critical failure rates in AI-assisted economic research from 72% to 16% compared to an unconstrained multi-agent baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed for research tasks traditionally requiring trained researchers, but reliability depends on how cognitive labor is structured between humans and machines, not merely model capability. The authors aim to identify architectural constraints that make AI-assisted research more trustworthy.

Method: A pre-specified 2×4 factorial experiment with 280 complete research runs across four datasets, comparing an unconstrained multi-agent LLM baseline against the HLER architecture. HLER imposes three commitments: (1) LLMs reason but do not execute data work, (2) data and estimation are handled deterministically, and (3) three human decision gates bind the workflow. An 80-run ablation study isolates the contributions of deterministic computation and human gates. Fisher's exact test assesses statistical significance.

Result: The unconstrained baseline produced critical failures in 72% of runs. HLER reduced the failure rate to 16% (p<0.001, Fisher's exact test). Reliability gains were largest on the least publicly represented dataset (Qing-dynasty population register), consistent with a task-based production model with Fréchet-distributed output quality. The ablation suggests deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently, with exploratory evidence of complementarity.

Conclusion: HLER functions as a research harness rather than an autonomous AI scientist: it sharply reduces failures, makes residual weaknesses more visible, and prevents unreliable claims from being advanced as publication-ready outputs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for tasks once reserved for trained researchers, including hypothesis generation, specification choice, and drafting conclusions. We argue that the reliability of AI-assisted research depends not only on model capability, but also on how cognitive labour is structured between humans and machines. We study this problem through Human-in-the-Loop Economic Research (HLER), a decision architecture based on pre-commitment, decision sequencing, accountability, and attention allocation. In a pre-specified 2*4 factorial experiment with 280 complete research runs across four datasets, an unconstrained multi-agent baseline produced critical failures in 72% of runs. Using the same underlying model, the same agent decomposition, and identical prompts for the shared reasoning agents, HLER reduced the failure rate to 16% by imposing three architectural commitments: LLMs reason but do not execute data work, data and estimation are handled deterministically, and three human decision gates bind the workflow. Fisher's exact test rejects equality of failure rates at p<0.001. Reliability gains were largest on the least publicly represented dataset, a Qing-dynasty population register, consistent with a task-based production model with Frechet-distributed output quality. An 80-run ablation suggests that deterministic computation and human gates contribute independently, with exploratory evidence of complementarity. We interpret HLER as a research harness rather than an autonomous AI scientist: it sharply reduces failures, makes residual weaknesses more visible, and prevents unreliable claims from being advanced as publication-ready outputs.

</details>


### [111] [WISE: A Long-Horizon Agent in Minecraft with Why-Which Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12852)
*Renmin Cheng,Changhao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: WISE is a long-horizon embodied agent framework that enhances low-level controllers with a Causal Event Graph combining episodic memory with causal reasoning (what-where-when + which-why). It features an Opportunistic Task Scheduler for dynamic subtask re-prioritization and a multi-scale progressive exploration strategy, significantly improving task success and efficiency on sparse, long-horizon Minecraft tasks requiring adaptive decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-augmented hierarchical agents for Minecraft suffer from low-level controller performance bottlenecks due to repeated execution failures. The authors identify that existing approaches lack not only episodic memory but also the integration of episodic (what-where-when) memory with causal (which-why) reasoning. Prior methods like MrSteve rely on feature-similarity-based retrieval, which fails under viewpoint changes and cannot support opportunistic task reordering.

Method: WISE introduces three components: (1) a Causal Event Graph that augments episodic memory with explicit causal structure linking observations to task relevance, enabling robust recall under viewpoint changes and causal reasoning; (2) an Opportunistic Task Scheduler that dynamically re-prioritizes subtasks when causally relevant opportunities are detected; and (3) a multi-scale progressive exploration strategy that provides spatially comprehensive observations for downstream reasoning.

Result: WISE largely improves task success and efficiency on long-horizon sparse tasks, particularly in settings requiring adaptive decision-making.

Conclusion: Augmenting low-level controllers with causally structured episodic memory (the Causal Event Graph) and coupling it with opportunistic scheduling and comprehensive exploration significantly mitigates the performance bottlenecks in hierarchical embodied agents, enabling more robust and adaptive long-horizon task execution in open-ended environments like Minecraft.

Abstract: Rapid advances have been made in developing general-purpose embodied agent in environments like Minecraft through the adoption of LLM-augmented hierarchical approaches. Despite their promise, low-level controllers often become performance bottlenecks due to repeated execution failures. We argue that a key limitation is not only the lack of episodic memory, but also the decoupling of \textit{what-where-when} memory from \textit{which-why} reasoning. To address this, we propose \textbf{WISE} (Which-Why Informed Semantic Explorer), a long-horizon agent framework with an enhanced low-level controller equipped with a Causal Event Graph that augments episodic memory with explicit causal structure linking observations to task relevance. Unlike prior work such as MrSteve, which relies on feature similarity for retrieval, WISE enables robust recall under viewpoint changes and supports opportunistic task reordering through causal reasoning. Building on this memory, we propose an Opportunistic Task Scheduler that dynamically re-prioritizes subtasks when causally relevant opportunities are detected. We further equip WISE with a multi-scale progressive exploration strategy to provide spatially comprehensive observations for downstream reasoning. Experiments show that WISE largely improves task success and efficiency on long-horizon sparse tasks, particularly in settings requiring adaptive decision-making.

</details>


### [112] [DailyReport: An Open-ended Benchmark for Evaluating Search Agents on Daily Search Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12871)
*Jingxuan Han,Wei Liu,Mingyang Zhu,Youpeng Wang,Ziwen Wang,Lin Qiu,Xuezhi Cao,Xunliang Cai,Zheren Fu,Licheng Zhang,Zhendong Mao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DailyReport is a benchmark with 150 open-ended daily search tasks and 3,546 cascade rubrics that evaluates search agents across disentangled dimensions, revealing that current agentic systems still fail to meet real-world user expectations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SA benchmarks focus on narrow, specialized tasks that don't reflect real-world user needs, and rely on coarse task-level rubrics that lack interpretability. There is a need for an open-ended, realistic, and fine-grained evaluation framework.

Method: DailyReport comprises 150 open-ended tasks capturing real-world information needs. Each task is decomposed into subtasks and evaluated using cascade rubrics across multiple disentangled dimensions. A cascade performance attribution mechanism and user-centric aggregation produce interpretable dimension-level scores and an overall user preference score. The benchmark was applied to evaluate 17 agentic systems.

Result: Evaluation of 17 agentic systems on DailyReport shows that current search agents still fall short of meeting user expectations across multiple dimensions.

Conclusion: DailyReport provides a more realistic, interpretable, and fine-grained benchmark for SA evaluation. The performance gap highlights significant room for improvement in building search agents that satisfy real-world user demands.

Abstract: Search Agents (SAs) typically leverage large language models (LLMs) to support complex information-seeking tasks by autonomously exploring web sources and synthesizing information into comprehensive responses. For SAs evaluation, prior benchmarks mainly focus on specialized tasks that are unlikely to arise in real-world user scenarios. Moreover, their reliance on coarse task-level rubrics often limits evaluation interpretability. To bridge this gap, we introduce DailyReport, an open-ended benchmark to evaluate SA capabilities on daily search tasks. It contains 150 open-ended tasks with 3,546 associated rubrics, capturing widely discussed and timely information demands of real-world users. Each task is decomposed into subtasks and evaluated with cascade rubrics across disentangled dimensions.
