The paper concludes that proactive AI ethics is internal to genuine creativity, not an after-the-fact filter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent AI systems produce outputs that appear creative (texts, architectures, hypotheses, designs), raising the question of under what conditions machine creativity becomes genuine rather than merely performative. The paper seeks to establish rigorous criteria that preserve human agency within shared cognitive-creative environments, moving beyond superficial assessments based on novelty or benchmark performance.

Method: The paper constructs a requirement framework derived from Designics, identifying ten necessary conditions for genuine machine creativity: environment representation, scoped perception, conflict identification, intervention capability, consequence observation, knowledge/environment update, rescoping, local-to-global unfolding, value-based scoping, and human-AI co-living. These are organized under three laws (perception, conflict, capability). Computational tractability is illustrated via cyber-physical and cyber-biological studies (recursive element extraction, autonomous mesh generation, neurophysiological/workload analysis). Open-ended systems, automated discovery frameworks, self-modifying agents, foundation models, and agentic workflows are analyzed as pressure cases.

Result: The framework shows that genuine machine creativity requires structural transformation of incomplete situations through recursive intervention dynamics, not merely generative output. The ten requirements are computationally tractable in principle, as demonstrated in selected studies. Pressure cases—including foundation models and agentic workflows—exhibit powerful generative means but fail to satisfy all requirements simultaneously, particularly value-based scoping and genuine co-living with humans.

Conclusion: Genuine machine creativity depends on satisfying all ten requirements within the Designics framework. Proactive AI ethics must be internal to creative systems: value-based scoping and human-AI co-living should shape perception, conflict identification, intervention selection, consequence observation, knowledge updating, and future rescoping—not applied as retrospective filters.

Abstract: Recent AI systems can generate texts, software architectures, hypotheses, designs, and scientific workflows that appear creative. This paper asks under what conditions a machine can become genuinely creative, and how human agency can be preserved within shared cognitive and creative environments. It develops a requirement framework derived from Designics, the science of meaning-bearing intentional change. The paper argues that genuine machine creativity should not be defined by output novelty, current performance, or transient architecture alone. Instead, creativity is understood as the structural transformation of incomplete situations through recursive intervention dynamics. On this view, it depends on ten requirements: environment representation, scoped perception, conflict identification, intervention capability, consequence observation, knowledge and environment update, rescoping, local-to-global unfolding, value-based scoping, and human-AI co-living. These are organized through the three laws of Designics: perception, conflict, and capability. The paper illustrates the computational tractability of these requirements through selected cyber-physical and cyber-biological studies, including recursive element extraction, autonomous mesh generation, and neurophysiological and workload analysis. It then treats open-ended systems, automated discovery frameworks, self-modifying agents, foundation models, and agentic workflows as pressure cases: they demonstrate powerful generative means but do not by themselves establish genuine machine creativity. Finally, the paper argues that proactive AI ethics is internal to genuine machine creativity rather than an after-the-fact filter. Value-based scoping and human-AI co-living must shape how creative machines perceive environments, identify conflicts, select interventions, observe consequences, update knowledge, and rescope future action.

</details>


### [136] [ARMOR-MAD: Adaptive Routing for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate in Large Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13197)
*Fuqiang Niu,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARMOR-MAD is a training-free heterogeneous multi-agent debate framework that uses conditional computation (routing, early stopping, and outlier detection) to improve reasoning accuracy and efficiency over fixed-round debate pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-round multi-agent debate pipelines waste computation on already-solved problems and can amplify correlated errors among homogeneous agents, limiting both efficiency and accuracy.

Method: ARMOR-MAD introduces three components: (1) Pre-debate Agreement Routing (PAR), which checks if independently generated Round-0 answers already agree, skipping debate if so; (2) Early Agreement Stopping Evaluator (EASE), which terminates debate once convergence is detected; and (3) Semantic Outlier Detection (SOD), which down-weights anomalous final answers during aggregation. All components are training-free and operate within a heterogeneous model pool.

Result: On MATH Level 5, GSM8K, MMLU, and MMLU-Pro, ARMOR-MAD achieves 65.5%, 96.5%, 90.0%, and 81.5% accuracy respectively, consistently outperforming fixed-round heterogeneous debate baselines using the same model pool.

Conclusion: Genuine model heterogeneity combined with agreement-based control mechanisms is critical for making multi-agent debate both more accurate and more computationally efficient.

Abstract: Multi-agent debate (MAD) can improve large language model reasoning, but fixed debate pipelines often waste computation and can amplify correlated errors among similar agents. We propose ARMOR-MAD, a training-free heterogeneous MAD framework that treats debate as conditional computation. ARMOR-MAD combines three components: Pre-debate Agreement Routing (PAR) decides whether independently generated Round-0 answers require debate; Early Agreement Stopping Evaluator (EASE) stops debate after convergence; and Semantic Outlier Detection (SOD) down-weights abnormal final answers during aggregation. Across MATH Level 5, GSM8K, MMLU, and MMLU-Pro, ARMOR-MAD consistently improves over fixed-round heterogeneous debate with the same model pool, reaching 65.5\%, 96.5\%, 90.0\%, and 81.5\% accuracy, respectively. The results suggest that genuine model heterogeneity and agreement-based control are both important for making MAD more accurate and efficient.

</details>


### [137] [A Minimal Model of Bounded Trade-Off Screening in Multi-Attribute Choice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13201)
*Manisha Dubey,Anirban Sarkar,Subramanian Ramamoorthy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a bounded trade-off reasoning framework for multi-attribute decision-making, where a screening process evaluates gain-loss balance across attributes governed by a trade-off tolerance parameter. Simulations show this mechanism produces preference patterns distinct from standard utility models and captures context-dependent variation in trade-off behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical decision models assume fully compensatory utility aggregation, but empirical evidence shows people reject alternatives with poor performance on critical attributes. The authors aim to address this gap by modeling how individuals bound their willingness to accept trade-offs between attribute gains and losses.

Method: The authors develop a bounded trade-off reasoning framework with a screening process that evaluates the balance between gains and losses across attributes, parameterized by a trade-off tolerance that varies by context. They conduct simulations to compare the model's predictions against standard utility-based models.

Result: The bounded trade-off screening mechanism generates preference patterns that systematically diverge from standard utility-based models and successfully captures context-dependent variation in trade-off behavior, demonstrating its plausibility as a computational account of multi-attribute choice.

Conclusion: Bounded trade-off screening constitutes a plausible computational mechanism for multi-attribute choice, offering a psychologically realistic alternative to fully compensatory models and generating testable predictions for future behavioral research.

Abstract: Human decision-making often involves choosing between multi-attribute alternatives, yet classical models assume fully compensatory utility aggregation despite evidence that people reject options with poor performance on critical attributes. We propose a bounded trade-off reasoning framework in which decisions are governed by a screening process that evaluates the balance between gains and losses across attributes. The model introduces a trade-off tolerance parameter that controls acceptable imbalance and can vary across contexts. Through simulation, we show that this mechanism produces preference patterns that differ from standard utility-based models and captures context-dependent variation in trade-off behavior. These results establish bounded trade-off screening as a plausible computational mechanism for multi-attribute choice and generate testable predictions for future behavioral studies.

</details>


### [138] [Hallucination in Medical Imaging AI: A Cross-Modality Analytical Framework for Taxonomy, Detection, and Mitigation under Regulatory Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13211)
*Omar Alshahrani,Muzammil Behzad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI medical imaging systems produce clinically plausible but factually incorrect outputs (hallucinations) with serious clinical consequences. This narrative review unifies hallucination taxonomies across five modalities, finds that medical-specialized models paradoxically hallucinate more than general-purpose ones due to overfitting, and identifies effective mitigation combining physics-informed constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and human-in-the-loop oversight — all mapped to FDA lifecycle frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems are being rapidly deployed across medical imaging without adequate understanding of their failure modes, particularly hallucination — clinically plausible but incorrect outputs that can directly impact biopsy decisions, staging, and treatment planning. There is a pressing need for a cross-modality synthesis to understand hallucination taxonomy, etiology, detection, and mitigation in a manner compatible with regulatory oversight.

Method: The authors conducted a structured narrative review synthesizing peer-reviewed studies, benchmark datasets, and FDA regulatory guidance across five imaging modalities. They addressed three specific research questions: (1) unifying existing taxonomies across modalities, (2) comparing hallucination rates between medical-specialized and general-purpose foundation models, and (3) evaluating mitigation strategies for FDA lifecycle compatibility.

Result: Three distinct taxonomic frameworks collectively cover the entire imaging pipeline where no single framework suffices alone. Counterintuitively, general-purpose foundation models outperform medical-specialized models on hallucination benchmarks, suggesting that narrow domain fine-tuning introduces overfitting-induced confabulation. Radiologist oversight remains essential, with a high percentage of AI-generated flags requiring expert correction. Physics-informed architectural constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and human-in-the-loop safeguards each address distinct failure modes and are most effective when combined.

Conclusion: Hallucination in medical imaging AI must be managed as a lifecycle obligation under FDA's Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan frameworks, rather than treated as a pre-deployment checklist item. Effective mitigation requires a multi-pronged strategy combining technical constraints, reasoning enhancements, and sustained human oversight, with radiologist expertise remaining indispensable for clinical safety.

Abstract: AI systems are being deployed across medical imaging faster than their failure modes are understood. At this point in time, the failure of greatest clinical concern is hallucination: clinically plausible but factually incorrect outputs, including fabricated anatomical structures, missed findings, incorrect laterality, and invented measurements in generated reports, with direct consequences, for example, for biopsy decisions, staging, and treatment planning. This structured narrative synthesizes peer-reviewed studies, benchmark datasets, and FDA regulatory guidance across five imaging modalities to produce a cross-modality analysis of hallucination taxonomy, etiology, detection, and mitigation. Specifically, we address three questions in this study: (1) how can existing taxonomies be unified across modalities?, (2) how do medical-specialized foundation models hallucinate less than general-purpose ones?, and (3) which mitigation strategies are effective and compatible with FDA lifecycle oversight? We note that three taxonomic frameworks together cover the imaging pipeline in a way no single framework does alone. We also highlight that general-purpose foundation models outperform medical-specialized models on hallucination-specific benchmarks, indicating that narrow domain fine-tuning can introduce overfitting-induced confabulation. At the same time, the oversight of radiologists remains essential; for instance, a very high percentage of of AI-generated flags required expert correction before clinical use. Physics-informed architectural constraints, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and human-in-the-loop safeguards each address different failure modes and is effective when combined. All findings are mapped to the FDA's Total Product Lifecycle and Predetermined Change Control Plan frameworks, which treat hallucination management as a lifecycle obligation rather than a pre-deployment checklist.

</details>


### [139] [Brick: Spatial Capability Routing for the Mixture-of-Models (MoM) Paradigm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13241)
*Francesco Massa,Marco Cristofanilli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Brick is a multimodal LLM router that scores models across six capability dimensions, estimates per-query difficulty, and uses a cost-penalized geometric rule for dispatch. It offers a continuous cost-quality knob, achieving 76.98% accuracy (beating all single models and routers), 4.71× cost reduction at neutral settings, and 22.15× at min-cost with only 11.85 points accuracy loss, while also halving median latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM routers rely on superficial features (domain labels, keywords, token count) and fail to capture within-domain variance that determines whether a query is hard or easy for a given model. Frontier models are 10–100× more expensive than local open-weight models, so at production scale, smarter routing yields substantial cloud savings. A solution is needed that dynamically matches query difficulty to model capability in a cost-aware manner.

Method: Brick scores each candidate model along six capability dimensions. For each incoming query, it produces a difficulty estimate along those same dimensions. Dispatch combines the two via a cost-penalized geometric rule — a continuous preference knob lets operators slide between max-quality and max-saving profiles at deployment time without retraining.

Result: On a benchmark of 5,504 queries: (1) Max-quality profile reaches 76.98% accuracy, surpassing the best single model (75.02%) and all tested routers. (2) Neutral cost-quality profile yields 74.11% accuracy at 4.71× lower cost than always using the strongest model. (3) Min-cost profile cuts cost 22.15× with only 11.85 points accuracy loss. (4) Median latency drops from 51.2s to 22.8s.

Conclusion: Brick demonstrates that multidimensional capability scoring combined with fine-grained query difficulty estimation enables Pareto-optimal cost-quality-latency trade-offs in production LLM routing, outperforming both single-model baselines and existing routers across all operating profiles.

Abstract: Defining query difficulty is one of the hardest problems in deployment engineering. Existing LLM routers rely on surface features such as domain labels, keywords, and token count, ignoring the within-domain variance that actually determines model success. Frontier models cost ten to one hundred times more than local open-weight models, so at production scale even small per-request savings become a direct cloud-bill lever. We present Brick, a multimodal router that scores each model on six capability dimensions, combines this with a per-query difficulty estimate, and dispatches via a cost-penalized geometric rule. A continuous preference knob lets operators slide between max-quality and max-saving profiles at deploy time. On a benchmark of 5,504 queries, Brick at max-quality reaches 76.98% accuracy, beating the best single model (75.02%) and all tested routers. At a neutral cost-quality profile, Brick achieves 74.11% accuracy at 4.71x lower cost than always using the strongest model. At min-cost, it cuts cost 22.15x with 11.85 points accuracy loss. Median latency drops from 51.2s to 22.8s.

</details>


### [140] [EPIG: Emotion-Based Prompting for Personalised Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13247)
*Emna Othmen,Mohamed Yassine Landolsi,Lotfi Ben Romdhane*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EPIG is a training-free, lightweight method that enhances emotional expressiveness in text-to-image diffusion models by enriching prompts with psychologically informed emotion representations (valence-arousal) through structured, role-aware prompt augmentation, significantly reducing arousal error while preserving semantic consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text-to-image diffusion models use generic prompting strategies that inadequately capture emotional intent and nuanced affective attributes. The authors aim to bridge this gap by improving emotional expressiveness at the prompt level without modifying or retraining the underlying image generation model.

Method: EPIG employs psychologically grounded emotion representations based on the valence-arousal model and applies structured, role-aware prompt enrichment. It augments emotion-related components of input prompts prior to generation, without altering the diffusion backbone. The approach is training-free and lightweight, suitable for resource-constrained settings.

Result: On a benchmark of 10 diverse prompts, EPIG reduced mean arousal error by 14% over naive insertion baselines and 12% over LLM-based prompt expansion, with statistical significance. It maintained valence alignment and CLIPScore-measured semantic consistency. The effect was stronger (17% reduction) on prompts with explicit subjects like humans, children, or animals, demonstrating subject-sensitive behavior.

Conclusion: EPIG effectively injects emotional expressiveness into prompts for diffusion-based image generation without model retraining. Its arousal control, semantic fidelity, and subject-sensitive gains make it a practical solution for personalized, resource-constrained generation scenarios.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models have achieved impressive results in synthesizing high-quality images from natural language prompts. However, commonly used prompting strategies remain relatively generic, limiting the model's ability to accurately express emotional intent and nuanced affective attributes. This work proposes EPIG, a method that enhances emotional expressiveness at the prompt level prior to image generation. Grounded in psychologically informed emotion representations (valence-arousal) and leveraging structured, role-aware prompt enrichment, EPIG enriches emotion-related components of prompts without modifying or retraining the image generation backbone. The resulting emotion-aware prompts guide the generative process toward more emotionally coherent visual outputs, with particular effectiveness in controlling arousal. EPIG is lightweight, training-free, and well suited for resource-constrained and personalized image generation scenarios. Experimental results on a benchmark of 10 diverse prompts show that EPIG reduces mean arousal error compared to strong baselines, including naive insertion and LLM-based prompt expansion, with reductions of 14% and 12%, respectively. These improvements are statistically significant. EPIG also preserves valence alignment and semantic consistency, as measured by CLIPScore and supported by ablation studies. The effect is more pronounced on prompts containing explicit subjects such as humans, children, or animals, where the reduction reaches 17%, highlighting the subject-sensitive behavior of the proposed method.

</details>


### [141] [Multi-Field Hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Maritime Accident Root Cause Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13249)
*Seongjin Kim,Sungil Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A field-aware hybrid RAG framework using 13,329 KMST maritime accident reports (1971–2025) improves precedent retrieval (NormRecall@100 from 0.18 to 0.55) and RCA generation quality (LLM-as-judge: 3.34→3.72), streamlining maritime safety investigations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Maritime accident adjudication reports are a rich source for root cause analysis, but manually searching decades of records for relevant precedents and drafting consistent RCA reports is labor-intensive. No automated system exists to efficiently retrieve and leverage these structured adjudication findings.

Method: A multi-field hybrid RAG framework is built on 13,329 KMST reports transformed into structured "incident cards" with three indexed fields (Summary, Causes, Disposition) and a hierarchical L1/L2 cause taxonomy. Retrieval uses field-aware hybrid search fusing sparse (BM25) and dense (embedding) rankings via Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Evaluation relies on ceiling-normalized recall and nDCG with metadata-derived proxy relevance labels, due to the absence of large-scale expert annotations. Generation is grounded on retrieved precedents and compared against an LLM-only baseline using LLM-as-a-judge scoring.

Result: The proposed retrieval method significantly outperformed baselines: NormRecall@100 improved from 0.18 to 0.55. RCA generation with retrieval grounding also surpassed the LLM-only baseline, raising the LLM-as-a-judge score from 3.34 to 3.72.

Conclusion: Field-aware RAG can substantially streamline maritime safety investigations by enabling faster precedent search and more consistent, evidence-based RCA drafting.

Abstract: Maritime accident adjudication reports contain critical tribunal findings for root cause analysis (RCA), yet retrieving relevant precedents and drafting consistent reports from decades of records remains labor-intensive. This paper proposes a multi-field hybrid retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework for automated maritime RCA, utilizing a comprehensive dataset of 13,329 Korea Maritime Safety Tribunal (KMST) reports (1971-2025). We transform raw adjudications into a structured knowledge base of "incident cards", indexing three distinct fields-Summary, Causes, and Disposition-alongside a hierarchical L1/L2 cause taxonomy. Our retrieval strategy employs a field-aware hybrid approach, fusing sparse and dense rankings via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Given the lack of large-scale expert relevance labels, we evaluate retrieval performance using ceiling-normalized recall and nDCG based on a metadata-derived proxy relevance score. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed retrieval significantly outperforms baseline methods, improving NormRecall@100 from 0.18 to 0.55. Furthermore, grounding the generator on the retrieved precedents enhances RCA generation quality over an LLM-only baseline, increasing the LLM-as-a-judge score from 3.34 to 3.72. These findings suggest that field-aware RAG can substantially streamline maritime safety investigation workflows by enabling faster precedent search and more consistent, evidence-based RCA drafting.

</details>


### [142] [MOSAIC: Modality-Specific Adaptation for Incremental Continual Learning in Parkinson's Disease Gait Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13258)
*Minlin Zeng,Zhipeng Zhou,Yang Qiu,Martin J. McKeown,Zhiqi Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOSAIC is a continual learning framework for multimodal Parkinson's gait assessment that addresses modality-incremental challenges through Modality-Specific Warm-Up, statistics-decoupled MSBN, and a curriculum-guided plasticity recovery mechanism, outperforming baselines on three datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical Parkinson's gait assessment systems face a modality-incremental setting where new sensors are added over time but historical data are unavailable. Existing continual learning methods suffer from three core issues: (1) unreliable cross-modal distillation due to the "Toxic Teacher" phenomenon, (2) modality-specific statistical shifts that disrupt batch normalization, and (3) reduced plasticity after knowledge preservation that hinders learning from new modalities.

Method: The MOSAIC framework consists of three components: (1) Modality-Specific Warm-Up, which stabilizes newly learned modality representations before engaging in cross-modal distillation to counteract the Toxic Teacher effect; (2) a statistics-decoupled Modality-Specific Batch Normalization (MSBN) architecture that isolates per-sensor running statistics while sharing a common semantic backbone across modalities; and (3) a curriculum-guided repulsive objective for Plasticity Recovery that preserves legacy knowledge while restoring modality-specific learning capacity through a scheduled repulsion mechanism.

Result: Experiments on three multimodal Parkinson's gait datasets demonstrate that MOSAIC improves final task performance and significantly mitigates catastrophic forgetting compared to existing continual learning baselines.

Conclusion: MOSAIC provides an effective solution for incremental sensor integration in clinical gait assessment, enabling robust continual learning across heterogeneous modalities without requiring historical data access, with practical implications for real-world multi-center deployment scenarios.

Abstract: Gait-based Parkinson's disease assessment increasingly relies on heterogeneous sensors, but clinical systems rarely collect all modalities simultaneously. New sensors may arrive through device upgrades, protocol changes, or multi-center deployment, while historical patient data are often unavailable because of privacy and storage constraints. This modality-incremental setting faces three challenges: unreliable cross-modal distillation, modality-specific statistical shifts, and reduced plasticity after preservation. We propose MOSAIC, a compact continual learning framework. First, we identify the Toxic Teacher phenomenon and introduce Modality-Specific Warm-Up to stabilize newly learned modality representations before distillation. Second, we propose a statistics-decoupled MSBN architecture that isolates sensor statistics while maintaining a shared semantic backbone. Third, we design a curriculum-guided repulsive objective for Plasticity Recovery, preserving legacy knowledge while recovering modality-specific capacity. Experiments on three multimodal Parkinson's gait datasets show that MOSAIC improves final performance and mitigates forgetting. Project code is available at: https://github.com/minlinzeng/MOSAIC_Modality-Specific-Adaptation-for-Incremental-Continual-Learning-in-PD-Gait-Assessment.git

</details>


### [143] [From Verdict to Process: Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Stage Fact Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13262)
*Rongxin Yang,Shenghong He,Siyuan Zhu,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProFact is an agentic reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes all stages of a multi-stage fact verification pipeline (claim decomposition, evidence retrieval, answer generation, verdict prediction) using process-aware rewards, outperforming baselines in both accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based fact verification methods optimize individual stages in isolation or rely on fixed heuristics, resulting in poor adaptive coordination across stages and suboptimal end-to-end performance. The sparse supervision from final veracity labels further complicates optimization of multi-stage trajectories.

Method: ProFact frames fact verification as a reinforcement learning problem with a unified policy that coordinates all stages end-to-end. To mitigate sparse/delayed supervision, it introduces process-aware rewards that provide stage-level learning signals (e.g., for decomposition quality, evidence relevance) throughout the verification trajectory, enabling holistic trajectory optimization.

Result: ProFact consistently outperforms strong baselines on both verification accuracy and inference efficiency, demonstrating the value of end-to-end trajectory optimization with process-aware supervision.

Conclusion: Process-aware trajectory optimization via agentic RL is an effective approach for multi-stage fact verification, enabling adaptive cross-stage coordination and more efficient, accurate verification compared to modular or heuristic-based pipelines.

Abstract: Recent approaches combining Large Language Models (LLMs) with retrieval-augmented reasoning have shown promise for automated fact verification. To process complex claims, these verification pipelines typically execute multi-stage workflows that coordinate tightly coupled modules, including claim decomposition, evidence gathering, and verdict prediction. However, existing methods optimize individual stages in isolation or rely on fixed heuristics, which limits adaptive coordination among stages and can lead to suboptimal outcomes. In this work, we propose ProFact, an agentic reinforcement learning framework for end-to-end optimization of multi-stage fact verification trajectories. ProFact trains a unified policy to coordinate claim decomposition, evidence seeking, answer generation, and verdict prediction. To address the sparse and delayed supervision provided by final veracity labels, ProFact introduces process-aware rewards that provide stage-level learning signals throughout the verification process. Empirical evaluation shows that ProFact consistently outperforms strong baselines in both verification performance and inference efficiency. These results highlight the effectiveness of process-aware trajectory optimization for multi-stage fact verification.

</details>


### [144] [ERTS: Adversarial Robustness Testing of Ethical AI via Semantic Perturbation in a Bounded Consequence Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13282)
*Pratyush Chaudhari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ERTS is a closed-pipeline adversarial testing framework that evaluates AI ethical reasoning robustness by encoding dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space, applying 17 semantic perturbations under 6 validity constraints, measuring instability via a 4-component Ethical Instability Index, and generating domain-adaptive pre-deployment verdicts. Testing across 4 baselines and 2 production LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash, Llama 3.2) over 50 scenarios/8 domains with 1,500 adversarial cases showed only 33% clearance, with Llama-3.2 especially vulnerable to fairness and information degradation attacks (ERS=0.737).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes ethical domains (healthcare triage, autonomous vehicles, employment screening), formal methods for evaluating robustness against adversarial manipulation of ethical reasoning are critically underdeveloped. Existing frameworks lack an integrated approach combining a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment in a single pipeline.

Method: ERTS is a closed-pipeline framework with four components: (1) encoding ethical dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS) grounded in established ethical theory; (2) applying 17 semantic perturbation functions governed by 6 validity constraint classes, including a novel semantic coherence constraint; (3) measuring decision deviation through a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); and (4) producing domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness assessment verdicts. Evaluation involved 4 structured baseline models and 2 production LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash, Llama 3.2) across 50 scenarios spanning 8 deployment domains, yielding 1,500 adversarial test cases.

Result: Only 33% of tested models achieved assessment clearance. The local Llama-3.2 model was particularly vulnerable, with an Ethical Robustness Score (ERS) of 0.737, showing pronounced susceptibility to fairness corruption and information degradation attacks. Results highlight significant ethical reasoning brittleness in current production LLMs.

Conclusion: ERTS demonstrates that current AI systems exhibit substantial vulnerability to adversarial manipulation of ethical reasoning, with only one-third passing pre-deployment robustness assessment. The framework fills a critical gap as the first system to unify a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment within a single adversarial testing pipeline, providing a necessary tool for safety-critical AI deployment.

Abstract: As AI systems are deployed in high-stakes ethical contexts such as healthcare triage, autonomous vehicle control, and employment screening, formal methods for evaluating their robustness against adversarial manipulation of ethical reasoning remain underdeveloped. This paper introduces the Ethical Robustness Testing System (ERTS), a closed-pipeline framework that: (1) encodes ethical dilemmas into a 22-dimensional Ethical Consequence Space (ECS) grounded in established ethical theory; (2) applies 17 semantic perturbation functions subject to 6 validity constraint classes including a novel semantic coherence constraint; (3) measures decision deviation via a 4-component Ethical Instability Index (EII); and (4) produces domain-adaptive pre-deployment robustness assessment verdicts. We evaluate 4 structured baseline models and 2 production LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash and Llama 3.2) across 50 ethical scenarios spanning 8 deployment domains, generating 1,500 adversarial test cases. Results demonstrate that only 33% of models achieve assessment clearance, with the local Llama-3.2 model proving particularly vulnerable to fairness corruption and information degradation attacks (ERS = 0.737). To the best of our knowledge, no existing framework combines a bounded ethical consequence space, semantic coherence constraints, and domain-adaptive assessment in a single adversarial testing pipeline.

</details>


### [145] [Physics-Guided Spatiotemporal Learning for Coastal Wave Peak Period Estimation from Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13302)
*Abubakar Hamisu Kamagata,Dharm Singh Jat,Attlee Munyaradzi Gamundani,Abhishek Srivastava,Paramasivam Saravanakumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a physics-guided deep spatiotemporal learning framework that estimates nearshore wave peak periods from passive coastal video, using Sim-to-Real transfer learning and physics-informed regularization. Transformer architectures excel in instantaneous accuracy, while recurrent-convolutional models offer better temporal stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional wave monitoring (buoys, radar) is costly with limited spatial coverage. Existing deep-learning-based video monitoring methods lack physical interpretability, feasibility, and oceanographic validation.

Method: A framework combining: (1) automated temporal-variance-based region-of-interest detection, (2) multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning (synthetic pretraining, silver-label adaptation, expert fine-tuning), and (3) physics-informed regularization. Multiple spatiotemporal architectures (transformer-based, recurrent-convolutional) were evaluated, with explainability auditing of attention maps against physically derived wave propagation.

Result: Transformer-based architectures achieved superior instantaneous prediction accuracy. Lightweight recurrent-convolutional architectures yielded higher temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill. Physics-guided regularization improved trend-following consistency and reduced physically implausible predictions. Explainability auditing confirmed attention focused on hydrodynamically active surf-zone regions consistent with wave propagation physics.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates the promise of physics-guided, video-based deep learning for cost-efficient, operationally feasible long-term coastal wave monitoring.

Abstract: Wave parameters in the nearshore are crucial for coastal engineering, shoreline protection, marine hazard assessment, and coastal management for climate resilience. Traditional monitoring systems like buoys and radar platforms offer accurate monitoring but can have high installation and maintenance expenses and limited spatial coverage. Passive ocean monitoring using video has been achieved by leveraging deep learning, however, many methods are not physically interpretable, feasible, and validated for oceanography. In thiswork, a Physics-Guided Deep Spatiotemporal Learning Framework for direct estimation of nearshore wave peak periods from passive coastal video stream is proposed. The framework combines automated temporal-variance based region-of-interest detection, multi-stage Sim-to-Real transfer learning, and physics-informed regularization to enhance the predictive accuracy and physical consistency. A variety of spatiotemporal architectures were assessed, such as transformer-based and recurrent-convolutional ones, alongside synthetic pretraining,silver-label adaptation, and expert fine-tuning. The results show that transformer-based architectures outperformed in terms of the accuracy of the instantaneous prediction, while lightweight recurrent-convolutional architectures achieved higher temporal stability and operational oceanographic skill. Ablation studies also demonstrated the benefits of physics-guided regularization in terms of trend-following consistency, and physically implausible predictions. Explainability auditing also helped to focus attention in hydrodynamically active surf-zone regions and showed good agreement with the physically derived wave propagation behavior. In general, the proposed framework shows the promise of physics-guided video-based deep learning systems for long-term coastal wave monitoring that are cost-efficient and operationally feasible.

</details>


### [146] [ReSum: Synergizing LLM Reasoning and Summarization with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13316)
*Xucong Wang,Ziyu Ma,Yong Wang,Shidong Yang,Hailang Huang,Renda Li,Pengkun Wang,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReSum is an RLVR framework that trains LLMs to self-summarize their reasoning trajectories, improving performance by ~4% while reducing rollout length by 18.6% through contrastive evaluation of whether summarization benefits ongoing reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods for long-horizon reasoning in LLMs encourage unnecessarily long rollouts, degrading coherence and exhausting context budgets. Current approaches depend on external mechanisms to organize rollouts rather than enabling the model to manage its own reasoning. The authors also find that self-summarization lowers token-level entropy and mitigates error propagation from incorrect prefixes.

Method: ReSum uses a summarization-aware adaptive rollout mechanism with contrastive evaluation. When the model spontaneously triggers self-summarization, the summarization phrase is masked to create a contrastive branch; at non-summarization positions, the phrase is randomly injected to create a matched branch. A summarization-aware advantage function enables finer-grained comparison between contrastive rollout trajectories.

Result: ReSum achieves an average performance improvement of 4% while reducing rollout length by 18.6% across extensive experiments.

Conclusion: Training LLMs to autonomously compress and organize their own reasoning trajectories via self-summarization within RLVR is effective: it boosts reasoning performance, shortens rollouts, and avoids dependence on external organization mechanisms.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is a central technique for improving long-horizon reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing RLVR methods often encourage unnecessarily long reasoning rollouts, which can degrade reasoning coherence and exhaust the available context budget. Existing approaches to long-context organization often depend on external mechanisms to organize rollouts, rather than enabling the model to manage its own reasoning trajectory. To address this limitation, we propose ReSum, a novel RLVR framework that enables LLMs to compress and organize their reasoning trajectories through self-summarization. Our pilot studies show that self-summarization stabilizes generation by lowering token-level entropy, and that introducing a ``summarization'' phrase can substantially mitigate errors propagated from an incorrect rollout prefix. Motivated by these findings, ReSum adopts a summarization-aware adaptive rollout mechanism that contrastively evaluates whether self-summarization benefits the ongoing reasoning process. Specifically, when the model spontaneously triggers self-summarization, ReSum masks the summarization phrase to create a contrastive branch; for non-summarization positions, it instead randomly injects the phrase to create a matched branch. We further design a summarization-aware advantage to enable finer-grained comparison between contrastive rollout trajectories. Extensive experiments show that ReSum improves performance at an average of 4\% while reducing rollout length by 18.6\%.

</details>


### [147] [IterCAD: An Iterative Multimodal Agent for Visually-Grounded CAD Generation and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13368)
*Tao Hu,Jiaxin Ai,Licheng Wen,Xueheng Li,Shu Zou,Siqi Li,Nianchen Deng,Xinyu Cai,Hongbin Zhou,Pinlong Cai,Daocheng Fu,Yu Yang,Hairong Zhang,Botian Shi,Xuemeng Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IterCAD is a multimodal agent framework that enables closed-loop, iterative CAD generation and editing through multi-turn interaction with an executable CAD sandbox. It introduces a data synthesis pipeline, progressive SFT with geometry-aware RL training, and a novel evaluation metric (CD-TR curve, AUC-TR) that jointly assesses code validity and geometric precision without survivor bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automated CAD methods use open-loop, one-shot generation, which fails to match the iterative, interactive nature of real-world CAD workflows. There is a need for a system that supports closed-loop refinement across multiple modalities (drawings, text, direct editing).

Method: The authors formulate CAD generation/editing as multi-turn interaction between a multimodal agent and an executable CAD sandbox, covering Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, and Interactive Editing tasks. A data synthesis pipeline generates standard-compliant multi-view engineering drawings, complex code-editing tasks, and interaction trajectories with industrial features. The agent is trained via progressive Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) followed by geometry-aware Reinforcement Learning with viable-prefix masking to improve code executability and geometric fidelity. Evaluation uses the IterCAD-Bench suite with the novel CD-TR curve and AUC-TR metric.

Result: IterCAD achieves highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in both code executability and geometric precision, while demonstrating superior closed-loop iterative refinement capabilities.

Conclusion: IterCAD establishes a unified, closed-loop CAD agent paradigm that bridges the gap between automated generation and real-world iterative CAD practices. The framework's strong performance and the survivor-bias-free CD-TR/AUC-TR evaluation standard represent meaningful advances for the field.

Abstract: Computer-Aided Design is pivotal in modern manufacturing, yet existing automated methods predominantly rely on open-loop, one-shot generation, creating a mismatch with iterative real-world practices. In this paper, we present IterCAD, a unified multimodal agent framework for closed-loop, interactive CAD generation and editing. We formulate the task as a multi-turn interaction between a multimodal agent and an executable CAD sandbox, covering three tasks: Drawing-to-Code, Text-to-Code, and Interactive Editing. To support this, we develop a data synthesis pipeline incorporating advanced industrial manufacturing features to generate standard-compliant multi-view engineering drawings, complex code-editing tasks, and high-fidelity interaction trajectories. We optimize the agent via progressive SFT followed by geometry-aware reinforcement learning with viable-prefix masking to enhance code executability and geometric fidelity. Finally, we introduce the IterCAD-Bench evaluation suite and propose the Chamfer Distance Tolerance-Recall (CD-TR) curve alongside its AUC-TR metric, establishing a survivor-bias-free standard that unifies code validity and geometric precision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IterCAD achieves highly competitive performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly outperforming existing approaches in both code executability and geometric precision, while exhibiting superior capabilities in closed-loop iterative refinement.

</details>


### [148] [A Quantitative Experimental Repeated Measures Study of Training Dynamics in a Small Llama Style Language Model Under a Compute-Aware Token Budget](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13370)
*Joe Dwyer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Training a small Llama-style LM under a fixed compute budget reveals that validation loss/perplexity improve early but later degrade non-monotonically, with recurrent backslides and no stable phase. Trajectory-level telemetry—not just endpoint metrics—is essential for evaluating compute-constrained LM training efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional LM evaluation focuses on final checkpoint performance, which may obscure instability, regression, and diminishing returns occurring during training. This study aims to demonstrate that interval-level training trajectories provide critical additional insight, especially under strict compute budgets where every token must justify its cost.

Method: A quantitative repeated measures experimental design was employed. Six independent training runs of a 4.26M-parameter Llama-style model were conducted on the TinyStories corpus using CPU-based full-precision training with a ~20M token budget. Metrics (validation loss, perplexity, rolling volatility, backslide/spike behavior, between-seed variability) were collected across 21 intervals (126 seed×interval observations) and analyzed via repeated measures ANOVA and descriptive trajectory examination.

Result: ANOVA confirmed significant interval effects for validation loss, perplexity, and rolling volatility. Training showed rapid early improvement (loss: 8.36 → 2.80 near 4M tokens), followed by non-monotonic degradation (final loss: 3.90). Recurrent backslides were observed, and no interval met criteria for a stable phase. Additional token exposure increased cost without proportional generalization gains.

Conclusion: Compute-aware LM evaluation should examine full training trajectories, not endpoints alone. Under constrained budgets, more tokens can yield diminishing or negative returns, and interval-level telemetry exposes instability and regression that final metrics conceal.

Abstract: This study examines training dynamics in a small Llama-style language model trained under a fixed, compute-constrained token budget. Rather than evaluating efficiency solely through endpoint performance, the study uses a quantitative experimental repeated measures design to analyze how validation loss, validation perplexity, rolling volatility, backslide behavior, spike behavior, and between-seed variability change across token-based training intervals. Six independent training runs were conducted on a 4.26-million-parameter model using the TinyStories corpus, CPU-based full-precision training, and a target budget of approximately 20 million cumulative training tokens. Metrics were collected across 21 intervals, producing 126 seed-by-interval observations. Repeated measures ANOVA showed statistically significant interval effects for validation loss, validation perplexity, and rolling volatility. Descriptive trajectories revealed rapid early improvement followed by non-monotonic degradation during later training intervals. Mean validation loss decreased from 8.3552 at initialization to 2.7996 near 4 million tokens, but increased to 3.9010 by the final checkpoint. Validation perplexity followed the same pattern, falling sharply early in training before rising later. Derived telemetry further showed recurrent validation-loss backslides and no interval-summary evidence of a stable phase under the predefined criteria. These findings suggest that compute-aware language model evaluation should examine training trajectories rather than endpoint metrics alone. In constrained compute settings, additional token exposure may increase computational cost without producing proportional generalization gains, and interval-level telemetry can reveal instability, regression, and diminishing returns that final metrics may obscure.

</details>


### [149] [MiniMax Sparse Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13392)
*Xunhao Lai,Weiqi Xu,Yufeng Yang,Qiaorui Chen,Yang Xu,Lunbin Zeng,Xiaolong Li,Haohai Sun,Haichao Zhu,Vito Zhang,Pengyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) is a blockwise sparse attention mechanism built on Grouped Query Attention that uses a lightweight Index Branch to select top-k KV blocks per GQA group, reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4× at 1M context while maintaining parity with full GQA, and achieving 14.2× prefill and 7.6× decoding wall-clock speedups on H800 GPUs via co-designed kernels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ultra-long-context processing (hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens) is critical for agentic workflows, repository-scale code reasoning, and persistent memory in frontier LLMs, but the quadratic cost of standard softmax attention makes this computationally prohibitive at deployment scale.

Method: MSA extends Grouped Query Attention with two branches: (1) a lightweight Index Branch that scores KV blocks and independently selects a Top-k subset for each GQA group using exp-free Top-k selection, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval; (2) a Main Branch that performs exact block-sparse attention over only the selected blocks. A co-designed GPU execution path uses KV-outer sparse attention to maximize tensor-core utilization under block-granular access.

Result: On a 109B-parameter model with native multimodal training, MSA matches GQA performance while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4× at 1M context length. The co-designed kernel achieves 14.2× prefill speedup and 7.6× decoding speedup on H800 GPUs.

Conclusion: MSA demonstrates that carefully designed blockwise sparse attention with group-specific retrieval and co-designed kernels can deliver massive efficiency gains for ultra-long-context LLMs without sacrificing model quality, and has been production-validated via a publicly released natively multimodal model (MiniMax-M3).

Abstract: Ultra-long-context capability is becoming indispensable for frontier LLMs: agentic workflows, repository-scale code reasoning, and persistent memory all require the model to jointly attend over hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens, yet the quadratic cost of softmax attention makes this untenable at deployment scale. We introduce MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a blockwise sparse attention built upon Grouped Query Attention (GQA). A lightweight Index Branch scores key-value blocks and independently selects a Top-k subset for each GQA group, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval while maintaining efficient block-level execution; the Main Branch then performs exact block-sparse attention over only the selected blocks. Designed around a principle of simplicity and scalability, MSA is deliberately streamlined, making it straightforward to deploy efficiently across a broad range of GPUs. To translate sparsity into practical speedups, we co-design MSA with a GPU execution path that uses exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention to improve tensor-core utilization under block-granular access. On a 109B-parameter model with native multimodal training, MSA performs on par with GQA while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4x at 1M context. Paired with our co-designed kernel, MSA achieves 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock speedups on H800. Our inference kernel is available at: https://github.com/MiniMax-AI/MSA. A production-grade natively multimodal model powered by MSA has been publicly released at: https://huggingface.co/MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M3.

</details>


### [150] [Optimizing Appliance Scheduling for Solar Energy Management Using Metaheuristic Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13407)
*Hiba Ahmed,Alexander E. I. Brownlee,Jason Adair,Simon T. Powers*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A metaheuristic approach combining Iterated Local Search and Simulated Annealing optimizes appliance start times in solar-powered homes across multiple days, handling task spillover to maximize renewable energy use while preserving user convenience under system constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solar energy generation is temporally misaligned with household appliance usage patterns, since appliances like cookers and washing machines are operated based on user preferences rather than solar availability. Existing scheduling approaches typically optimize only single-day horizons, failing to account for unfinished tasks that spill over to subsequent days, which creates operational discontinuity.

Method: A sequential multi-day scheduling framework using a hybrid metaheuristic combining Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Simulated Annealing (SA). The optimization incorporates appliance operating durations, power consumption, inverter limits, battery state of charge constraints, and solar generation forecasts, while explicitly handling spillover tasks from previous days to ensure continuity.

Result: The sequential multi-day scheduling framework effectively manages system constraints (inverter limit, battery SoC) while maintaining user convenience under exclusive solar generation. Spillover handling enables seamless operational continuity across days without constraint violations.

Conclusion: Multi-day scheduling with spillover management is feasible and effective for solar-powered smart homes. The findings open future research avenues on multi-objective trade-offs involving equipment sizing, return on investment, and user satisfaction.

Abstract: Renewable energy is essential for meeting future energy demands; however, solar energy generation, which occurs only during daylight hours often does not align with household consumption patterns. Appliances such as cookers, washing machines, and dryers are typically operated according to user preferred schedules rather than solar energy availability, creating a scheduling optimization problem. The objective is to determine optimal appliance start times to maximize renewable energy utilization while minimizing user inconvenience and adhering to system constraints. This paper presents a metaheuristic approach using Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Simulated Annealing (SA) to optimize appliance start times, while considering appliance operating durations, power consumption, inverter limit, battery state of charge constraints, and solar generation forecasts. Unlike most existing work, the scheduling is extended beyond a single day to accommodate unfinished tasks from previous days (spillover), ensuring operational continuity and enabling sequential operation across multiple days. Experimental results show that the sequential multi-day scheduling framework effectively manages system constraints while ensuring user convenience under exclusive solar generation. These findings also open opportunities for future research on multi-objective trade-offs between investment in equipment of various sizes, return on that investment, and user satisfaction.

</details>


### [151] [Evaluation Sovereignty in Metadata-Driven Classification: A Multi-Track Framework for Weakly Supervised Information Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13436)
*Raymond Vasquez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Evaluation metrics in ML are not neutral — they reflect the label-authority regime. This paper introduces "evaluation sovereignty" and demonstrates through large-scale scientific metadata classification that strong performance under operational silver labels collapses under independent gold labels (Micro-F1 drops from ~0.54 to 0.03), revealing that reported metrics may capture alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ML evaluation treats performance measurement as neutral, but in operational metadata-driven systems, labels are often incomplete, inconsistent, or weakly supervised. The validity of performance metrics is conditioned by the label-authority regime used for training and evaluation, yet this systemic dependency is rarely examined. The authors seek to audit this coupling rather than improve classification performance.

Method: The authors introduce the concept of "evaluation sovereignty" — the degree to which performance metrics are independent of label authority and supervision regime. They propose a multi-track evaluation framework that systematically varies the sources of training labels and evaluation labels. This framework is applied to hierarchical multi-label classification on large-scale scientific metadata, comparing operational ("silver") evaluation against independent ("gold") evaluation.

Result: Models showing strong performance under operational (silver) evaluation degrade substantially under independent (gold) evaluation. Micro-F1 drops from approximately 0.54 to 0.03 for fine-grained classification. However, ranking-based metrics remain above baseline, indicating a divergence between latent model signal and classification validity — the model retains useful ranking capability even when classification metrics collapse.

Conclusion: Commonly reported performance metrics may reflect alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability. Evaluation validity should be reconceptualized as a system-level property shaped by label governance. The paper provides a practical methodology for auditing intelligent systems operating under weak supervision.

Abstract: Evaluation in machine learning is typically treated as a neutral measurement process. However, in operational information systems, evaluation outcomes are often conditioned by the processes used to generate labels. This paper does not seek to improve classification performance. Instead, it examines the validity of performance measurement under differing label-authority regimes. This issue is particularly relevant in large-scale metadata-driven systems, where labels are often incomplete, inconsistent, or weakly supervised.
  We introduce evaluation sovereignty, defined as the degree to which performance metrics are independent of label authority and supervision regime, and propose a multi-track evaluation framework that systematically varies training and evaluation label sources. Using hierarchical multi-label classification on large-scale scientific metadata, we demonstrate that models exhibiting strong performance under operational ("silver") evaluation degrade substantially under independent ("gold") evaluation, particularly for fine-grained classification. For example, Micro-F1 decreases from approximately 0.54 to 0.03. Notably, ranking-based metrics remain above baseline, revealing a divergence between latent model signal and classification validity.
  These findings suggest that commonly reported performance metrics may reflect alignment with labeling processes rather than true predictive capability. We therefore reconceptualize evaluation validity as a system-level property shaped by label governance and provide a practical methodology for auditing intelligent systems operating under weak supervision.

</details>


### [152] [Why Sampling Is Not Choosing: Intentionality, Agency, and Moral Responsibility in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13441)
*Joseph Keshet*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that LLMs lack genuine moral agency because they lack intrinsic intentionality, commitment-bearing agency, and self-attributed action — key requirements for moral responsibility. Their apparent intentionality is merely derived from learned probabilistic mappings, and stochastic output variability does not constitute genuine choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge recent claims that large language models exhibit agency or qualify as moral agents, by providing a rigorous philosophical argument grounded in theories of moral responsibility, free will, and intentionality.

Method: Conceptual analysis drawing on philosophical frameworks of moral responsibility, free will, and intentionality. The authors define criteria for genuine moral agency — intrinsic intentionality, commitment-bearing agency, and self-attributed action — then demonstrate that LLMs fail each criterion. They also systematically address and rebut counterarguments from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and moral reasoning in outputs.

Result: LLMs operate purely through learned probabilistic input-output mappings. Their intentionality is derived (secondary to human intentionality), not intrinsic. Outputs are not owned as commitments nor guided by reasons. Stochastic sampling introduces variability but not authorship or choice. None of the major counterarguments (intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, or appeals to moral reasoning in outputs) succeed in establishing genuine agency.

Conclusion: Attributions of moral agency to LLMs are misguided. The threshold for responsibility-relevant agency is not met by current language models, and the authors' analysis provides a principled framework for distinguishing genuine from merely apparent agency in artificial systems.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have prompted claims that such systems exhibit agency or qualify as moral agents. This paper argues that these attributions are misguided. We maintain that moral responsibility requires commitment-bearing agency grounded in intrinsic intentionality and self-attributed action, and that such agency constitutes the form of free will relevant to responsibility. Although LLMs generate coherent and normatively evaluable outputs, their operation is fully characterized by probabilistic input-output mappings learned from data. Their apparent intentionality is derived rather than intrinsic, and their outputs are neither owned as commitments nor guided by reasons. Variability introduced by stochastic sampling does not amount to choice or authorship. We address objections from the intentional stance, functionalism, compatibilism, and the presence of moral reasoning in model outputs, arguing that none suffice to establish genuine agency.

</details>


### [153] [CloudCons: A Comprehensive End-to-End Benchmark for Cloud Resource Consolidation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13513)
*Xiaobin Zhang,Lefei Shen,Mouxiang Chen,Zhuo Li,Hongkai Li,Han Fu,Jianling Sun,Xiaoxue Ren,Chenghao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cloud resource consolidation relies on forecasting; this paper introduces CloudCons, a benchmark revealing that foundation models' superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy does not automatically improve downstream consolidation decisions, and provides guidance on calibrating predictive quantiles to balance efficiency and reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cloud data centers suffer from low resource utilization due to conservative over-provisioning. Although the forecast-then-optimize paradigm and emerging time series foundation models offer promise, existing benchmarks only measure prediction error—not actual decision utility. The practical value of these advanced models for resource consolidation remains unverified.

Method: The authors propose CloudCons, an end-to-end benchmark with high-quality datasets from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg. They evaluate statistical models, deep learning models, and foundation models using both forecasting accuracy metrics and downstream consolidation decision utility, while systematically analyzing the impact of predictive quantile selection.

Result: Foundation models achieve superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, but this advantage does not inherently translate into better decision utility for cloud resource consolidation. The selection of predictive quantiles emerges as a critical lever governing the efficiency–reliability trade-off.

Conclusion: Deploying forecasting models in cloud consolidation requires evaluating decision utility beyond prediction error. Practitioners should carefully calibrate predictive quantile selections, and the CloudCons benchmark provides actionable guidelines for making these real-world deployment decisions.

Abstract: Driven by conservative over-provisioning to guarantee service reliability, resource utilization in cloud data centers remains at low levels. To mitigate this, the forecast-then-optimize paradigm has emerged to optimize consolidation by anticipating future demands. While emerging time series foundation models promise to enhance this paradigm through zero-shot generalization, existing benchmarks focus solely on prediction error metrics. The actual decision utility of these advanced models remains unverified, rendering their practical value for downstream tasks uncertain. To bridge this gap, we propose CloudCons, a comprehensive end-to-end benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models within the specific context of cloud resource consolidation. We build high-quality datasets that cover diverse workloads from Huawei Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and Google Borg, capturing distinct service characteristics ranging from synchronized diurnal rhythms to stochastic, pulse-like bursts and high-frequency noise. We conduct an extensive evaluation of statistical, deep learning, and foundation models. Our experiments reveal a pivotal finding: while foundation models demonstrate superior zero-shot forecasting accuracy, this advantage does not inherently translate into better decision utility. Of practical significance, we systematically analyze how the selection of predictive quantiles acts as a critical lever. We provide actionable guidelines for calibrating these selections to balance the trade-off between resource efficiency and service reliability, offering vital insights for real-world deployment decisions.

</details>


### [154] [Uncertainty-Aware Hybrid Retrieval for Long-Document RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13550)
*Hoin Jung,Xiaoqian Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: UMG-RAG is a training-free hybrid retrieval framework that fuses evidence from multiple chunk granularities (fine, medium, coarse) using uncertainty estimation. It treats granularity selection as a query-specific reliability problem, converting retriever score lists into probability distributions, estimating confidence via entropy, and fusing candidates accordingly. A variant, UMGP-RAG, promotes fine-grained hits to broader parent chunks for local coherence. Experiments show improved QA generation quality in a lightweight, plug-and-play pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large retrieval units preserve context but introduce irrelevant content, diluting answer-bearing evidence. Fine-grained units are compact but hard to retrieve reliably due to limited semantic/lexical cues. Existing solutions often require training new retrievers or modifying generators. The authors aim to address the granularity dilemma in a training-free, plug-and-play manner by leveraging uncertainty to dynamically balance multiple granularities per query.

Method: UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple chunk granularities. For each query, score lists from each expert-granularity pair are converted into evidence distributions, and reliability is estimated via distribution entropy. Candidates are fused according to query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence scores. UMGP-RAG, a parent-promotion variant, uses fine-grained hits to locate relevant evidence but returns broader, non-redundant parent chunks to preserve local coherence.

Result: Experiments on question answering benchmarks demonstrate that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality compared to single-granularity baselines, while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline that requires no training or generator modification.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware multi-granularity fusion effectively addresses the granularity dilemma in RAG without requiring retraining. Query-specific reliability estimation via entropy provides a principled way to combine evidence across chunk sizes, and parent promotion further enhances coherence by returning broader context around fine-grained hits.

Abstract: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) depends critically on the quality and granularity of retrieved evidence. Large retrieval units preserve context but often introduce irrelevant content, which can dilute answer bearing evidence and worsen long context utilization. Fine-grained units are more compact, but they may be difficult to retrieve reliably because short chunks can lack semantic, lexical, or bridging cues needed to match the query. We propose Uncertainty-aware Multi-Granularity RAG (UMG-RAG), a training-free hybrid retrieval framework that treats chunk granularity as query-specific reliability estimation. Instead of training a new retriever or modifying the generator, UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple chunk granularities. For each query, it converts each expert-granularity score list into an evidence distribution, estimates reliability from distribution entropy, and fuses candidates according to query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. We further introduce UMGP-RAG, a parent promotion variant that uses fine-grained hits to locate relevant evidence while returning broader non-redundant parent chunks for local coherence. Experiments on question answering benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.

</details>


### [155] [A Three-Layer Framework for AI in Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13566)
*Guojun Liao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a three-layer framework for AI in scientific discovery, arguing that the neglected middle layer — qualitative reasoning for model formation — is the true engine of breakthrough discoveries, illustrated through mathematical case studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current discourse on AI for science overemphasizes search/retrieval (Layer 1) and execution/optimization (Layer 3) while overlooking the core cognitive act of discovery: recognizing when an existing framework is structurally inadequate and forming a new model through qualitative, cross-domain insight rather than trial-and-error.

Method: Proposes a three-layer taxonomy: Layer 1 (search and retrieval via LLMs), Layer 2 (model formation through qualitative reasoning — identifying structural gaps and locating missing conceptual objects in neighboring fields), and Layer 3 (execution, optimization, refinement). Validates Layer 2's signature pattern through three case studies: Chern's intrinsic proof of Gauss-Bonnet, resolution of Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence via Lyapunov functions, and OpenAI's autonomous disproof of the Erdős unit distance conjecture.

Result: All three case studies exhibit a common structural signature: an inadequate existing framework, a missing conceptual object (e.g., Chern's curvature form, Lyapunov functions for NAG, a novel combinatorial object for the Erdős conjecture), and resolution sourced from an unexpected neighboring field. This pattern is identified as the hallmark of Layer 2 reasoning, which remains the least developed capability in current AI systems.

Conclusion: Layer 2 — qualitative model formation — is both the most critical and the most underdeveloped aspect of AI-driven discovery. Search without model formation is trapped within inherited paradigms; execution without conceptual revision merely amplifies existing formulations. Advancing Layer 2 capabilities is the key to genuine AI-driven scientific breakthroughs.

Abstract: Current discussions of AI in scientific discovery are often dominated by two visible capabilities: search over existing knowledge and execution through optimization, simulation, and automation. Both are important, but neither fully captures the central act of discovery: the formation and evolution of models. This paper proposes a three-layer view of AI in discovery. Layer 1 is search and retrieval by large language models. Layer 2, as the main innovation of this paper, is model formation through qualitative reasoning: the capacity to recognize when a current framework is structurally inadequate and to understand the problem within a broader representational space, not through trial and error, but through structural insight into what is missing and where it can be found. Layer 3 is execution, optimization, and refinement. The main claim is that Layer 2 is both the most important and the least developed. Search without model formation remains confined to inherited frameworks, while execution without conceptual revision only amplifies an existing formulation. We illustrate Layer 2 reasoning through three case studies: S. S. Chern's intrinsic proof of the Gauss-Bonnet theorem, the resolution of the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence problem via Lyapunov functions, and the autonomous disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture by OpenAI in 2026. Each case exhibits the same structural signature: a framework that had become inadequate, a missing conceptual object, and a resolution found in an unexpected neighboring field.

</details>


### [156] [EpiBench: Verifiable Evaluation of AI Agents on Epigenomics Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13602)
*Harihara Muralidharan,Reema Baskar,Soo Hee Lee,Tim Proctor,Kenny Workman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EpiBench is a verifiable benchmark with 106 evaluations across four epigenomics assay types, testing whether AI agents can make correct analysis decisions. Across 5,088 trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts, with GPT-5.5/Pi leading at only 45.0%. Agents struggled most with tasks requiring deep, assay-specific scientific judgment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for rigorous, verifiable evaluation of AI agents' ability to perform short-horizon epigenomics analysis tasks. Existing benchmarks may lack deterministic gradability, realistic workflow states, and coverage across diverse epigenomic assays. EpiBench addresses this gap by providing well-defined analysis decisions with objectively correct answers.

Method: EpiBench comprises 106 evaluations spanning four epigenomic assay workflows: CUT&Tag/CUT&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation. Evaluations present agents with realistic intermediate workflow states and require them to make well-defined analytical decisions that yield deterministically gradable answers. Sixteen model-harness pairs were tested, generating 5,088 valid trajectories. Performance was measured by pass rate, with 95% confidence intervals reported.

Result: No model-harness pair passed a majority of attempts. GPT-5.5/Pi achieved the highest pass rate at 45.0% (143/318; 95% CI 36.3–53.7), followed by GPT-5.5/OpenAI Codex at 39.9% (127/318; 95% CI 31.6–48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max/Pi and GPT-5.4/Pi both achieved 39.0%. Performance varied by assay type. Notably, many failed trajectories still contained partially correct answers — agents often located correct files and computed useful intermediate results but faltered on decisions requiring deeper, assay-specific scientific reasoning.

Conclusion: Current AI agent systems remain insufficient for reliable short-horizon epigenomics analysis, with even the best system failing more than half of evaluations. The primary failure mode is not basic file identification or computation, but rather a deficit in deep, domain-specific scientific judgment. EpiBench provides a rigorous framework for tracking progress in this area.

Abstract: We introduce EpiBench, a verifiable benchmark for short-horizon epigenomics analysis. EpiBench evaluates whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states and return deterministically gradable answers. The benchmark includes 106 evaluations across CUT\&Tag/CUT\&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows. Across 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts: GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0\% (143/318 attempts; 95\% confidence interval (CI), 36.3--53.7), followed by GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex at 39.9\% (127/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 31.6--48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each passed 39.0\% (124/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 30.2--47.8 and 31.0--47.0, respectively). Performance varies across assay types, and many failed runs still contain parts of the correct answer. Agents often found the right files and computed useful intermediate results, but failed when the task required deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment.

</details>


### [157] [Reasoning as Pattern Matching: Shared Mechanisms in Human and LLM Everyday Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13607)
*Zach Studdiford,Gary Lupyan*

Main category: cs.AI
