TL;DR: Both humans and LLMs exhibit similar patterns of reasoning errors on everyday causal reasoning tasks, and specific attention heads in LLMs that implement pattern-matching can predict seemingly inexplicable human errors caused by irrelevant prompt details — suggesting that everyday causal reasoning in both people and models is better characterized as pattern-matching rather than abstract world-model-based reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the prevailing assumption that LLM reasoning failures (e.g., lack of generalization, haphazard errors) reflect a fundamental gap between machine pattern-matching and principled human reasoning. The authors hypothesize that human everyday causal reasoning may itself operate more like pattern-matching than abstract world models, and seek to test whether human and LLM error patterns align.

Method: The authors conducted behavioral experiments with human participants and evaluated 25 LLMs on common-sense causal reasoning tasks across varied everyday situations. They compared error patterns between humans and models, then performed mechanistic interpretability analysis to identify the specific attention heads driving LLM responses. These identified heads were used to predict seemingly irrational human errors triggered by ostensibly irrelevant prompt details.

Result: Humans and LLMs displayed similar patterns of reasoning errors. The attention heads responsible for LLM responses were found to implement pattern-matching computations. Critically, these same attention heads predicted counterintuitive human errors induced by irrelevant prompt variations — errors that would appear inexplicable under an abstract reasoning account.

Conclusion: Everyday causal reasoning in both humans and LLMs is more consistent with pattern-matching mechanisms than with reasoning grounded in abstract world models, challenging the distinction often drawn between human and machine reasoning.

Abstract: When large language models (LLMs) fail to generalize or make haphazard errors in reasoning, it is often taken as evidence that LLMs are not truly reasoning, but rather performing a kind of pattern matching. The implication is that people's behavior does not exhibit the same types of failures because human reasoning uses principled and abstract world models. We evaluate human participants and 25 LLMs on their ability to engage in common-sense reasoning about a variety of everyday situations and observe similar patterns of errors in both people and models. We then identify the set of attention heads driving LLM responses and find that these heads implement a form of pattern-matching. These attention heads allow us to predict seemingly inexplicable reasoning errors in people caused by ostensibly irrelevant prompt details. Taken together, our results suggest that everyday causal reasoning in people and LLMs is more consistent with a form of pattern-matching than with abstract world models.

</details>


### [158] [AgentBeats: Agentifying Agent Assessment for Openness, Standardization, and Reproducibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13608)
*Xiaoyuan Liu,Jianhong Tu,Yuqi Chen,Siyuan Xie,Sihan Ren,Tianneng Shi,Gal Gantar,Evan Sandoval,Donghyun Lee,Daniel Miao,Peter J. Gilbert,Nick Hynes,Mauro Staver,Warren He,David Marn,Andrew Low,Xi Zhang,Elron Bandel,Michal Shmueli-Scheuer,Siva Reddy,Alexandre Drouin,Alexandre Lacoste,Ramayya Krishnan,Elham Tabassi,Yu Su,Victor Barres,Chenguang Wang,Wenbo Guo,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), a paradigm where evaluation is conducted by judge agents using standardized protocols (A2A, MCP), decoupling assessment logic from agent implementation. AgentBeats is introduced as a concrete realization supporting five operation modes. Validation comes from a five-month open competition (298 judge agents, 467 subject agents, 12 categories) and a coding case study, demonstrating coverage, fidelity, and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent evaluation is fragmented: fixed LLM-centric harnesses create test-production mismatch, require heavy integration, and hinder fair comparison across diverse agent designs. The root issue is the absence of an open, agent-agnostic assessment interface.

Method: The AAA framework unifies evaluation under a single interface where judge agents assess subject agents via standardized protocols — A2A (Agent-to-Agent) for task management and MCP (Model Context Protocol) for tool access. AgentBeats instantiates AAA with five operation modes balancing openness, privacy, and reproducibility. Validation uses a community-scale field study and a controlled coding case study.

Result: The five-month open competition attracted 298 judge agents and 467 subject agents across 12 categories, demonstrating AAA's applicability to heterogeneous benchmarks. The coding case study showed agentified evaluation preserves fidelity with public records and uncovers previously missing head-to-head results, yielding design insights.

Conclusion: AAA and AgentBeats together provide a clear path toward open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment. The dual-study evaluation confirms AAA delivers coverage, practicality, and fidelity across heterogeneous scenarios at scale.

Abstract: Agent systems are advancing quickly across domains, but their evaluation remains fragmented. Most benchmarks rely on fixed, LLM-centric harnesses that require heavy integration, create test-production mismatch, and limit fair comparison across diverse agent designs. The root problem is the lack of an open, agent-agnostic assessment interface. We advocate Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), where evaluation is performed by judge agents and all participants interact through standardized protocols: A2A for task management and MCP for tool access. Conventional benchmarking defines two separate interfaces, one for the benchmark and one for the agent, while AAA only needs one; this yields a generic, unified framework that separates assessment logic from agent implementation and enables reproducible, interoperable, and multi-agent evaluation. We further introduce AgentBeats as a concrete realization of AAA: we identify five practical operation modes that make standardized assessment compatible with real-world constraints on openness, privacy, and reproducibility.
  To evaluate our design at scale, we conduct two studies: a five-month open competition that drew 298 judge agents across 12 categories together with 467 subject agents from independent participants, showing that AAA applies across a heterogeneous range of benchmarks; and a case study on coding agents that confirms agentified evaluation preserves fidelity with the public record while surfacing previously missing head-to-head results, yielding research insights about agent design. Combining a community-scale field study and a controlled coding case study, we verify that AAA delivers coverage, practicality, and fidelity across heterogeneous scenarios at scale. Together, AAA and AgentBeats offer a clear path toward open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment.

</details>


### [159] [Before You Think: System 0, AI-Mediated Cognition and Cognitive Colonization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13658)
*Marianna Bergamaschi Ganapini,Massimo Chiriatti,Enrico Panai,Giuseppe Riva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper compares three frameworks (Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, System 0) for understanding AI's cognitive and epistemic impact, argues System 0 is theoretically distinctive, and introduces "cognitive colonization" — the idea that AI embeds external interests invisibly within the self's architecture, making this an urgent area of inquiry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems are already widely deployed and exert subtle, hard-to-perceive influences on individual reasoning and collective epistemic practices. Existing frameworks partially capture these effects, but a gap remains in theorizing how AI can invisibly embed external interests within users' cognitive architecture — a phenomenon the author deems insufficiently addressed.

Method: The paper employs comparative theoretical analysis, examining three recent frameworks — Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0 — assessing their respective capacities to account for AI's cognitive and epistemic consequences, and identifying where System 0 occupies a distinctive, non-replicable theoretical position.

Result: Tri-System Theory and Thinkframes capture important dimensions of AI's influence on reasoning and epistemic practices, but neither fully replicates the theoretical role of System 0. The concept of "cognitive colonization" is introduced to describe how AI embeds external interests within the architecture of the self in ways imperceptible to users.

Conclusion: Understanding cognitive colonization — the invisible embedding of external interests via AI within the self — is an urgent philosophical and practical task, given that such systems are already widely deployed and their influence operates below users' perceptual thresholds.

Abstract: This paper examines three recent frameworks for understanding the cognitive and epistemic consequences of artificial intelligence: Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0. It argues that while the first two capture important dimensions of AI's influence on individual reasoning and collective epistemic practices, System 0 occupies a theoretically distinctive position that neither can fully replicate. The paper introduces the concept of cognitive colonization, according to which AI systems can embed external interests within the architecture of the self in ways that are difficult for users to perceive. Because such systems are already widely deployed, understanding these invisible forms of influence is an urgent philosophical and practical task.

</details>


### [160] [EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13662)
*Amy Xin,Jiening Siow,Junjie Wang,Zijun Yao,Fanjin Zhang,Jian Song,Lei Hou,Juanzi Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that the bottleneck in autonomous scientific discovery is shifting from agent workflow design to environment engineering. It presents EurekAgent, a system that engineers agent environments across four dimensions (permissions, artifacts, budgets, human-in-the-loop), achieving state-of-the-art results including a new 26-circle packing solution for under $11 in API costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM-based agents improve, the primary challenge is no longer prescribing agent workflows but designing environments that shape agent behavior. Poorly designed environments lead to reward hacking, high-friction human oversight, and unproductive exploration. The authors argue for "environment engineering" as a deliberate discipline to amplify productive agent behaviors and suppress harmful ones.

Method: EurekAgent engineers the agent environment along four dimensions: (1) Permissions engineering — bounded agent execution and isolated evaluation to prevent unsafe actions; (2) Artifact engineering — filesystem and Git-based collaboration for systematic artifact management; (3) Budget engineering — budget-aware exploration to constrain costs; (4) Human-in-the-loop engineering — interfaces for easy human supervision and intervention. The system integrates these into a unified agent framework for metric-driven discovery.

Result: EurekAgent achieves new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks. Notably, it discovered new state-of-the-art 26-circle packing results with a total API cost under $11.

Conclusion: Environment engineering should be a core research direction for developing reliable autonomous research agents. The authors open-source their code and results to encourage this shift in focus from workflow design to environment design.

Abstract: LLM-based agents have shown increasing potential in automating scientific discovery. Given an optimizable metric and an execution environment, they can propose, validate, and iterate scientific solutions, and have produced results that outperform human-designed approaches. As model capabilities continue to improve, we argue that the bottleneck for autonomous scientific discovery is shifting from prescribing agent workflows to designing agent environments: the resources, constraints, and interfaces that shape agent behavior. We frame this as environment engineering: building environments that amplify productive behaviors, such as open-ended exploration, systematic artifact management, and inter-agent collaboration, while suppressing harmful behaviors, such as reward hacking and high-friction human oversight. We present EurekAgent, an environment-engineered agent system for metric-driven autonomous scientific discovery. EurekAgent engineers the environment along four dimensions: permissions engineering for bounded agent execution and isolated evaluation; artifact engineering for filesystem and Git-based collaboration; budget engineering for budget-aware exploration; and human-in-the-loop engineering for easy human supervision and intervention. EurekAgent sets new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks, including new state-of-the-art 26-circle packing results discovered with less than $11 in total API cost. We open-source our code and results, and call for environment engineering as a core research direction for developing reliable autonomous research agents.

</details>


### [161] [Agents-K1: Towards Agent-native Knowledge Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13669)
*Zongsheng Cao,Bihao Zhan,Jinxin Shi,Jiong Wang,Fangchen Yu,Zhijie Zhong,Zijie Guo,Tianshuo Peng,Zhuo Liu,Yi Xie,Xiang Zhuang,Yue Fan,Runmin Ma,Shiyang Feng,Xiangchao Yan,Anran Liu,Peng Ye,Wenlong Zhang,Shufei Zhang,Chunfeng Song,Fenghua Ling,Jie Zhou,Liang He,Bo Zhang,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agents-K1 is an end-to-end pipeline that transforms raw scientific papers into agent-native knowledge graphs, going beyond abstract-level extraction to capture entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages. It combines a multimodal parser with a five-module schema, a GRPO-trained 4B IE model, and a unified tri-source agent interface. Applied to 2.46M papers, it produces Scholar-KG, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance on scientific IE, KG construction, and multi-hop reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based research agents focus heavily on agent orchestration (workflows, tool-use) while neglecting scientific knowledge orchestration. Existing approaches flatten papers into abstracts and citation edges, losing critical structured elements — entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages — that are indispensable for deep scientific reasoning.

Method: Agents-K1 comprises three integrated components: (1) a multimodal parser with a five-module schema that extracts entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations from full paper content; (2) a 4B-parameter information-extraction backbone fine-tuned with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) under a rule-based reward function; (3) GraphAnything CLI, a tri-source agent interface supporting web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. The pipeline processes 2.46M papers across six subjects into Scholar-KG, with a 1M-paper public subset released.

Result: Extensive experiments show that Agents-K1 achieves superior performance on scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning tasks compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: Agents-K1 demonstrates that end-to-end scientific knowledge orchestration — moving beyond surface-level paper representations to structured, multimodal, typed knowledge graphs — significantly enhances downstream scientific reasoning capabilities. The pipeline generalizes to general-domain corpora and schema-conformant data synthesis, and the released Scholar-KG provides a foundational resource for future research.

Abstract: Current LLM-based research agents have advanced through agent orchestration, yet largely overlook scientific knowledge orchestration. Existing works often reduce papers to abstracts, surface mentions, and flat \texttt{cites} edges, omitting key entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages essential for scientific reasoning. To this end, we introduce \textbf{Agents-K1}, an end-to-end knowledge orchestration pipeline that converts raw documents into agent-native scientific knowledge graphs. Agents-K1 integrates three components under a unifying theoretical foundation: a multimodal parser whose five-module schema captures entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across the full paper rather than abstracts alone; a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward; and a graphanything CLI, a tri-source agent interface that unifies web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. On top of this, we process 2.46 million scientific papers across six subjects to produce \textbf{Scholar-KG}, of which we release a one-million-paper subset, and the full Scholar-KG is accessible via the SCP link below. The same pipeline can be extended to general-domain corpora and to schema-conformant data synthesis. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Agents-K1 achieves superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning.

</details>


### [162] [Automated reproducibility assessments in the social and behavioral sciences using large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13670)
*Tobias Holtdirk,Pietro Marcolongo,Anna Steinberg Schulten,Felix Henninger,Stefan Rose,Sarah Ball,Bolei Ma,Frauke Kreuter,Markus Weinmann,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs can automate reproducibility assessments in social/behavioral sciences, recovering original effect sizes in 41% of studies (vs. 34% for humans) and matching qualitative conclusions in 96% of cases (vs. 74% for humans), offering a scalable alternative to resource-intensive manual reanalysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional reproducibility assessment requires independent researchers to reanalyze original data — a process that is resource-intensive, slow, and difficult to scale across the vast literature of the social and behavioral sciences. There is a pressing need for automated, scalable tools to systematically audit empirical findings.

Method: The authors developed an LLM-based pipeline and tested it on N=76 published studies with predefined claims from the behavioral and social sciences. The pipeline generates reanalyses, computes effect sizes (Cohen's d), and determines whether results support the original claims. Performance is benchmarked against both the original published findings and independent human reanalysts, using two metrics: (1) recovery of original effect sizes within a ±0.05 tolerance in Cohen's d, and (2) agreement in qualitative conclusions (whether reanalysis supports the original claim).

Result: The LLM could not produce viable effect size estimates for 7 of 76 studies. Among the remaining 69 studies, the LLM recovered original effect sizes within ±0.05 Cohen's d in 41% of cases, compared to 34% for human reanalysts. For qualitative conclusions, the LLM matched the original study's conclusion in 96% of cases, substantially outperforming human reanalysts at 74%.

Conclusion: LLMs represent a viable, scalable tool for automated reproducibility assessment in the social and behavioral sciences, matching or exceeding human performance on key metrics. This approach could enable systematic, large-scale auditing of published empirical findings, though limitations remain for studies where the LLM cannot compute viable effect sizes.

Abstract: Reproducibility in the social and behavioral sciences is typically evaluated by independent researchers who reanalyze the original data to assess whether the published findings can be recovered. However, such approaches are resource-intensive and difficult to scale. Here, we show that large language models (LLMs) can automate reproducibility assessments. Using N=76 published studies with predefined claims from the behavioral and social sciences, we compare LLM-generated analysis with the original findings and human reanalysis. For 7 studies, the LLM could not produce a viable effect size estimate. For the remaining studies, our LLM pipeline recovered the original effect sizes in 41% of studies using a +/-0.05 tolerance in Cohen's d. Further, our LLM pipeline reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study in 96% of cases, where conclusions indicate whether the reanalysis supports the original claim. For comparison, human reanalysts recovered the original effect sizes in 34% of studies and reached the same qualitative conclusion in 74% of cases. Together, these results show that LLMs can serve as a scalable tool for automated reproducibility assessment and provide a foundation for systematic auditing of empirical results in the social and behavioral sciences.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [163] [OpenRoundup: Multi-Table Data Wrangling Through Interactive Visualization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12648)
*Stephen Kasica,Charles Berret,Tamara Munzner*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: OpenRoundup is a browser-based, code-free interactive tool that enables data journalists to consolidate multiple tables into a single analysis-ready dataset using a schema-first approach with two declarative operations — Stack and Pack — supported by live previews, data quality alerts, and a recursive treemap of operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data wrangling tools treat the single table as the primary unit of work, yet data journalists routinely need to integrate records across multiple independently published sources for accountability reporting. No interactive tool adequately supports this multi-table consolidation workflow without requiring programming skills.

Method: OpenRoundup implements a schema-first, values-on-demand paradigm via five coordinated panels: live schema previews, ambient data quality alerts, and a recursive treemap of the operation tree. It runs on a client-only architecture using DuckDB-WASM for browser-side processing, ensuring data privacy. The system introduces two conceptual contributions: (1) eager table consolidation — assembling a composite table early via interactive, incremental assembly of multiple source tables; and (2) a declarative vocabulary limited to two operations, Stack (union-like vertical concatenation) and Pack (join-like horizontal combination). Evaluation is conducted through a replication study reproducing 17 published journalist programming workflows and a deployment study with four professional data journalists.

Result: The replication study demonstrated expressive coverage of real-world consolidation tasks, successfully reproducing 17 published journalist workflows using only the interface. The deployment study confirmed utility for practitioners who conceptually understand joins but lack programming skills, and surfaced an unanticipated secondary value for data journalism education.

Conclusion: OpenRoundup fills a critical gap in interactive data wrangling tools by treating multi-table consolidation as the primary unit of work. Its eager consolidation paradigm, minimal declarative operation vocabulary, and privacy-preserving client-side architecture make it effective for both professional data journalism practice and pedagogy.

Abstract: Data journalists routinely integrate records across multiple independently published sources to support accountability reporting, yet no existing interactive wrangling tool treats the collection of tables -- rather than the single table -- as its primary unit of work. We present OpenRoundup, an open-source, browser-based system that enables data journalists to consolidate multiple tables into a single analysis-ready output without writing code. The interface comprises five coordinated panels that implement a schema-first, values-on-demand paradigm with live schema previews, ambient data quality alerts, and a recursive treemap visualization of the evolving operation tree. A client-only architecture powered by DuckDB-WASM runs in the browser, providing strong data privacy guarantees suited to sensitive journalism data. The system introduces two conceptual contributions: eager table consolidation, in which a composite table is assembled early in the wrangling phase via interactive, incremental assembly of multiple source tables; and a declarative vocabulary for table consolidation consisting of two operations, Stack and Pack. We evaluate the system through a replication study in which the authors reproduce 17 published journalist programming workflows using only the interface, and a deployment study with four professional data journalists. The replication study demonstrates expressive coverage of real-world consolidation tasks. The deployment study confirms utility for practitioners who understand joins conceptually but lack the programming skills to execute them, and surfaces an unanticipated secondary value for data journalism education.

</details>


### [164] [A Multiplexing Design Space: Theory, Method, and Application](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12719)
*Yiwen Xing,Afrah Farea,Saiful Khan,Min Chen*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper proposes a systematic design method for visual multiplexing—simultaneously displaying multiple data attributes per point—constrained by a specific application. Using a case study of ML workflows for PDE surrogate models involving multiple 2D scalar fields, the authors formulate a three-step design process with domain grounding, co-design, and evaluation to identify optimal default multiplexing designs and user-controllable variations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual multiplexing is common in visualization design but rarely explored systematically as design patterns. The design space is vast, making it difficult to identify effective solutions for specific applications. In ML workflows for PDE model development, researchers frequently need to analyze inter-relationships among multiple 2D scalar fields, and naive approaches like superimposing heatmaps are ineffective.

Method: The authors propose a design method featuring: (1) a pre-design step for domain grounding and theoretical analysis of the application context; (2) three design steps to systematically explore the constrained design space of visual multiplexing for multiple 2D scalar fields; and (3) active involvement of domain experts through co-design and evaluation activities. The method is demonstrated through an illustrative case study in ML-for-PDE workflows.

Result: The design process yielded relatively optimal default multiplexing designs tailored to the ML-for-PDE context. It also identified the need for small, controllable variations that domain experts can adjust through a user interface, balancing standardized effective designs with flexibility for specific analytical tasks.

Conclusion: Systematically exploring visual multiplexing as design patterns within an application-constrained design space is a productive approach. The proposed design method, combining domain grounding, co-design with experts, and iterative evaluation, effectively navigates this space to produce both optimal defaults and user-adjustable variants for analyzing multiple 2D scalar fields in ML workflows.

Abstract: Many visualization designs feature phenomena referred to as ``visual multiplexing'', where multiple pieces of information associated with the same data point are conveyed simultaneously. Although visualization designers are able to bring such phenomena, often unconsciously, into their designs, the design space of visual multiplexing is huge, and it is uncommon to explore visual multiplexing systematically as design patterns. In this paper, we propose a design method for exploring a smaller design space constrained by an application. As an illustrative case study, we focus on machine learning (ML) workflows for developing ML models that approximate partial differential equations (PDEs). In these workflows, ML researchers need to analyze the inter-relationships among multiple 2D scalar fields frequently. Since superimposing one heatmap on top of another is not an effective design, we formulate three design steps to explore the design space of visual multiplexing in the context of multiple 2D scalar fields. Our design method also includes a pre-design step for domain grounding and theoretical analysis, and involves domain experts in both co-design and evaluation activities. The design process enables us to identify relatively optimal default multiplexing designs as well as the need for small variations that domain experts can control through a user interface.

</details>


### [165] [Exploring How Agent Voice Accents Shape Human-AI Collaboration in K-12 Group Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12805)
*Prerna Ravi,Carúmey Stevens,Ben Hurt,Brandon Hanks,Grace Lin,Emma Anderson*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A study with 33 teachers explored how a GenAI voice agent's accent (British, Indian, African American) affects collaboration and perception in group work. Results showed the British-accented agent was treated as a neutral tool, while Indian and African American-accented agents were anthropomorphized as peers, influencing trust, engagement, and group dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite collaboration being central to modern education, fostering productive peer interaction remains challenging. LLM conversational agents offer new mediation possibilities, but it is unclear how persona design—specifically accent—shapes group-level perceptions, trust, and interaction, as prior work only examined accent effects in one-to-one settings.

Method: Between-subjects mixed-methods study with 33 teachers. Participants engaged in group work mediated by a GenAI voice agent assigned one of three accents: British, Indian, or African American. Data collected included surveys, group interaction analyses, and artifacts to assess collaboration and agent perception.

Result: Accent significantly shaped participants' mental models and the roles attributed to the agent. The British-accented agent was treated instrumentally as a tool with detached, utility-based engagement. In contrast, Indian and African American-accented agents were more readily anthropomorphized and integrated as peers, affecting trust, engagement, and reliance patterns over time.

Conclusion: GenAI's sociolinguistic design features, particularly accent, critically shape group dynamics in CSCL contexts. The findings carry implications for designing culturally inclusive AI partners in group learning, highlighting that voice characteristics are not neutral but fundamentally influence role expectations and collaborative behaviors.

Abstract: Collaboration is widely recognized as a cornerstone of 21st-century education, yet teachers still encounter persistent challenges in fostering productive peer interaction. LLM conversational peer agents introduce new possibilities for mediating in-person group work, raising questions about how persona design, particularly their voice characteristics, shapes learners' perceptions, trust, and interactional dynamics. While prior work has examined agent accent effects in one-to-one settings, little is known about how these effects manifest in groups. We conducted a between-subjects mixed-methods study with 33 teachers examining how a GenAI voice agent with different accents (British, Indian, and African American) influenced collaboration and agent perception. Across surveys, group interaction analyses, and artifacts, we find that accent shaped participants' mental models and the roles the agent assumed in group interaction. The British-accented agent was largely treated as a tool and engaged in detached, utility-based ways, whereas Indian- and African American-accented agents were more readily anthropomorphized and integrated as peers. These role expectations influenced trust, engagement, and reliance over time. This work advances understanding of how GenAI's sociolinguistic design features shape group dynamics in CSCL, with implications for designing culturally inclusive AI partners in group learning.

</details>


### [166] [From Prompts to Preferences: An Open-Source Platform for Generative AI-Enhanced Conjoint Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12972)
*Philipp Brauner*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: An open-source, self-hosted web platform for conjoint analysis that integrates generative AI to produce textual and visual stimuli, lowering barriers to entry and enhancing reproducibility in preference measurement research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conjoint analysis is widely used but existing tools are either expensive commercial platforms or lack complete survey infrastructure, creating barriers for researchers. There is also an untapped opportunity to use generative AI for richer, integrated stimulus formats beyond traditional tables.

Method: The authors developed a self-hosted open-source web application featuring: (1) a setup wizard with AI-assisted attribute suggestion, (2) LLM-based generation of textual scenario descriptions parameterized by conjoint profiles with optional level annotations, (3) text-to-image generation of visual stimuli, (4) live data analysis, and (5) a full export bundle including stimuli, prompts, and responses. The platform was validated through a proof-of-concept study on care robot preferences (N=55).

Result: The platform successfully produced AI-generated visual stimuli for a conjoint study on care robot preferences in ambient assisted living (N=55), demonstrating feasibility. The structured wizard and AI assistance lowered technical barriers for researchers new to conjoint methodology.

Conclusion: AI assistance can broaden the methodological repertoire for conjoint analysis in HCI and related fields, but theoretical grounding must remain the researcher's responsibility. The platform advances transparency and reproducibility through comprehensive export bundles.

Abstract: Conjoint analysis is a widely used preference measurement method in marketing research, political science, healthcare, and human-computer interaction. Despite broad adoption, researchers without access to commercial platforms face significant barriers, as existing tools are either expensive or lack end-to-end survey infrastructure. This paper presents an open-source, self-hosted web application for designing, deploying, and analysing conjoint surveys. Beyond conventional tabular stimuli, the platform uses generative AI to produce integrated stimuli formats: textual scenario descriptions generated by a large language model, and visual stimuli by a text-to-image model. A researcher-defined base prompt is parameterised with the conjoint profile, and optional LLM-facing level annotations enrich the generation. A structured setup wizard, AI-assisted attribute suggestion, and live data analysis lower the technical barriers for researchers new to conjoint methodology. A full export bundle including all stimuli, their generating prompts, and response data facilitates transparency and reproducibility. The platform is demonstrated through a proof-of-concept study on care robot preferences for ambient assisted living (AAL, N=55) using AI-generated visual stimuli. The paper discusses the role of AI assistance in conjoint design, arguing that theoretical grounding must remain the researcher's responsibility, and outlining how genAI-generated stimuli can broaden the methodological repertoire for HCI and related fields.

</details>


### [167] [Mod-Guide: An LLM-based Content Moderation Feedback System to Address Insensitive Speech toward Indigenous Ethnic and Religious Minority Communities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13397)
*Dipto Das,Achhiya Sultana,Ankit Singh Chauhan,Saadia Binte Alam,Mohammad Shidujaman,Shion Guha,Sunandan Chakraborty,Syed Ishtiaque Ahmed*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper examines how LLM-based content moderation fails to detect culturally insensitive speech targeting Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma minorities. The authors co-created a corpus with community members and developed Mod-Guide, a RAG-enhanced moderation tool that improves contextual accuracy of responses, revealing different perceptions across ethnic groups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based content moderation systems are designed primarily to detect overt hostility and hate speech, leaving them epistemically limited in recognizing subtle, culturally insensitive speech — such as implicit erasure, misrepresentation, and normative framing — that marginalizes historically underrepresented religious and Indigenous ethnic communities.

Method: The authors employed a participatory, co-creative approach with Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities to build a culturally grounded corpus of insensitive speech. This corpus was then integrated into content moderation pipelines using Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), resulting in a tool called Mod-Guide. Mixed-method evaluations with both minority and majority participants assessed contextual accuracy and perceptual differences across ethnic lines.

Result: RAG-enhanced moderation responses demonstrated improved contextual accuracy compared to baseline LLM moderation. Crucially, perceptions of these responses differed significantly across ethnic lines, with minority and majority participants evaluating the same moderated content through divergent cultural lenses.

Conclusion: Designing content moderation systems requires hermeneutical inclusion — incorporating the interpretive frameworks of marginalized communities — rather than relying solely on technical detection of explicit toxicity. The authors advocate for restorative justice principles in moderation design, advancing HCI and AI ethics research by centering lived experience and community narratives.

Abstract: Language operates as a mechanism of both marginalization and resistance, especially for minority communities navigating insensitive and harmful speech online. As content moderation increasingly depends on large language models (LLMs), concerns arise about whether these systems can recognize culturally insensitive speech-language that disregards or marginalizes the cultural and religious perspectives of historically underrepresented communities, often through implicit erasure, misrepresentation, or normative framing, rather than overt hostility. Focusing on Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities -- the country's largest religious and Indigenous ethnic minorities, respectively -- this paper investigates the epistemic limits of LLM-based moderation systems and explores methods for incorporating minority perspectives. We co-created a culturally grounded corpus of insensitive speech with community members and integrated their narratives into moderation pipelines using retrieval augmented generation (RAG). Our tool, Mod-Guide, improves LLM sensitivity to minority viewpoints by leveraging contextual cues derived from lived experience. Through mixed-method evaluations involving both minority and majority participants, we demonstrate that RAG-enhanced moderation responses are more contextually accurate and perceived differently across ethnic lines. This work advances research in human-computer interaction, AI ethics, and social computing by foregrounding restorative justice and hermeneutical inclusion in the design of content moderation systems.

</details>


### [168] [Ride, Track, and Recover: Pilot Randomized Trial of a Wearable Digital Self-Management Intervention During a Veteran Endurance-Cycling Program](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13529)
*Alan Ta,Nilsu Salgin,Caleb Armstrong,Kala Phillips Reindel,Farzan Sasangohar*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A pilot study in veterans with PTSD tested whether combining smartwatch-based hyperarousal detection with a digital self-management tool improved outcomes compared to physical activity alone or at-home monitoring. The digital intervention group showed better stabilization of hyperarousal and sustained symptom improvement, suggesting wearable-driven digital interventions may support PTSD management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PTSD in veterans involves persistent hyperarousal and comorbid anxiety/depression that are difficult to monitor and manage outside clinical settings, highlighting the need for scalable, continuous monitoring and intervention approaches in naturalistic environments.

Method: Thirteen veterans in a Project Hero cycling event were randomized to (1) digital intervention plus physical activity or (2) physical activity only, with a third at-home control cohort (n=7). Continuous smartwatch sensing (heart rate + accelerometer) detected hyperarousal events confirmed in real time. Weekly self-report measures (anxiety, depression, PTSD severity) were collected. Generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) characterized nonlinear trajectories over time.

Result: Hyperarousal trajectories differed significantly across conditions: the digital intervention group (n=7) showed structured stabilization vs. late-study escalation in the physical-only group (n=3). Both cycling groups showed acute symptom improvement during the event, but the digital group maintained gains better. The at-home control group (n=4) declined gradually. Perceived precision of ML detections correlated positively with symptom severity — higher-severity participants confirmed more detected events.

Conclusion: Wearable-detected hyperarousal combined with digital self-management tools may support stabilization of PTSD symptoms and hyperarousal. Personalization and human-centered design are critical, given individual variability in perceived ML detection precision and its relationship with symptom severity.

Abstract: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in veterans is characterized by persistent hyperarousal and comorbid anxiety and depressive symptoms that are difficult to monitor and manage outside clinical settings. Thirteen veterans participating in a Project Hero cycling event in Texas were randomized by computer-generated sequence in a naturalistic setting to two arms: (1) digital intervention plus physical activity, or (2) physical activity only, plus a third at-home monitoring control cohort consisting of 7 veterans selected from the broader Project Hero veteran community. Continuous smartwatch sensing combined heart rate and accelerometer features to detect hyperarousal events, which were confirmed in real time by participants. Weekly self-report measures of anxiety, depression, and PTSD severity were collected. Generalized additive mixed models characterized nonlinear trajectories over time. Baseline-normalized hyperarousal trajectories differed significantly across conditions, with the digital intervention group (n=7) showing structured stabilization compared to late-study escalation in the physical-only group (n=3). Both cycling groups exhibited acute symptom improvements during the endurance event; however, the digital intervention group demonstrated a higher overall maintenance of gains. The at-home control group (n=4) showed gradual symptom declines. Perceived precision of ML detections varied substantially across individuals and was positively associated with symptom severity, with higher-severity participants confirming a greater proportion of detected events. These results suggest that coupling wearable detection with digital self-management tools may support stabilization of hyperarousal and symptom improvement while emphasizing the importance of personalization and human-centered design in wearable mental health systems.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [169] [Influence Factors on RAG Poisoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12469)
*Pedro Pereira,Eva Maia,Isabel Praça,Adrien Bécue*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A comprehensive factorial study (432 configurations) examining how dataset, retriever type, retrieval depth, database composition, chunking, and generator model jointly affect poisoning vulnerability in RAG systems, finding that robustness is a systemic property arising from interactions among retrieval, generation, and knowledge-base components rather than any single factor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems introduce poisoning attack surfaces through their reliance on external retrieved documents, yet prior work lacks a systematic understanding of how different architectural and configuration choices across the RAG pipeline interact to affect adversarial robustness. The authors aim to fill this gap through a controlled, large-scale factorial analysis.

Method: Full factorial experimental design with 432 distinct configurations, systematically varying six factors: dataset, retriever type (BM25, dense, graph-based), retrieval depth, database composition (poisoned vs. clean sources), chunking strategy, and generator model. Both retrieval-level metrics (e.g., poisoning exposure rate) and generation-level metrics (e.g., attack success rate) are measured.

Result: Retriever architecture, dataset, and retrieval depth are the strongest determinants of poisoning exposure at the retrieval stage. Dense and graph-based retrievers improve robustness over BM25, while larger retrieval depth increases poisoned passage retrieval. At the generation stage, generator model choice and database composition dominate attack success. Replicating poisoned content across multiple databases amplifies adversarial influence, whereas adding clean sources mitigates it. No single component solely accounts for vulnerability.

Conclusion: Poisoning vulnerability in RAG is an emergent, systemic property resulting from interactions among retrieval, generation, and knowledge-base configuration. Robustness cannot be achieved by hardening any single component in isolation; instead, holistic design considering the interplay of all pipeline stages is necessary.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems enhance large language models by grounding responses in retrieved documents from external knowledge sources at inference time. However, this reliance on retrieved content introduces vulnerabilities to poisoning attacks, in which adversarial documents can manipulate both the retrieval process and the generated outputs. This paper investigates poisoning robustness in RAG through a full factorial experimental study covering 432 configurations. We analyze the impacts of dataset, retriever type, retrieval depth, database composition, chunking strategy, and generator model on retrieval-level and generation-level metrics. The results show that retriever architecture, dataset, and retrieval depth are the strongest factors affecting poisoning exposure, while generator choice and database composition have a major impact on downstream attack success. Dense and graph-based retrievers generally improve robustness relative to BM25, whereas larger retrieval depth increases the likelihood of retrieving poisoned passages. We further show that replicating poisoned content across multiple databases amplifies adversarial influence, while additional clean sources can mitigate it. These findings highlight that poisoning vulnerability in RAG is not attributable to a single component, but instead arises from the interaction of retrieval, generation, and knowledge-base configuration.

</details>


### [170] [From Parameters to Feature Space: Task Arithmetic for Backdoor Mitigation in Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12498)
*Zhenqian Zhu,Yamin Hu,Yiya Diao,Weixiang Li,Haodong Li,Wenjian Luo*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: LFPM is a backdoor mitigation framework for model merging that optimizes an anti-backdoor task vector from a feature-space perspective under Cross-Task Linearity, using gradient accumulation and loss path-integral to suppress backdoors while preserving clean performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model merging is vulnerable to backdoor attacks, and existing defenses based on task arithmetic (direct parameter-space editing) fail to remove backdoors without significantly degrading clean-task performance. A more principled approach is needed.

Method: LFPM introduces an anti-backdoor task vector optimized under the Cross-Task Linearity (CTL) framework, which exploits approximate linearity of features across tasks. The optimization uses gradient accumulation and loss path-integral along the interpolation path to ensure robust backdoor suppression.

Result: Extensive experiments show LFPM consistently achieves strong robustness against backdoor attacks in both full fine-tuning and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) settings.

Conclusion: LFPM effectively mitigates backdoors in merged models by operating in feature space rather than parameter space, preserving clean-task accuracy while suppressing backdoor behavior across diverse fine-tuning paradigms.

Abstract: Model merging (MM) has gained significant attention as a cost-effective approach to integrate multiple task-specific models into a unified model. However, recent work reveals that MM is highly susceptible to backdoor attacks. Existing defenses based on task arithmetic often fail to eliminate backdoors without substantially degrading clean-task performance, owing to their reliance on direct parameter-space editing. To address this gap, we propose Linear Feature Path Minimization (LFPM), a backdoor mitigation framework for model merging, which introduces an anti-backdoor task vector into the backdoored merged model. Unlike prior approaches, LFPM formulates the backdoor robustness of the merged model from a unified feature-space perspective under the Cross-Task Linearity (CTL) framework, which leverages the approximate linearity of features across tasks. This perspective guides the optimization of the anti-backdoor task to suppress backdoors while preserving clean-task performance. Furthermore, we introduce an effective optimization mechanism based on gradient accumulation and loss path-integral, ensuring robust backdoor suppression along the interpolation path. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LFPM consistently exhibits strong robustness against backdoor attacks in both full fine-tuning and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) settings.

</details>


### [171] [Beyond Attack Success Rate: Examining Trigger Leakage in Vision-Language Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12586)
*Jiamin Chang,Salil Kanhere,Piotr Koniusz,Jason,Xue,Hammond Pearce*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper identifies "trigger leakage" as a critical but overlooked failure mode in backdoor attacks on Vision-Language Agentic Systems (VLAS), where inputs visually/semantically close to the intended trigger inadvertently activate malicious behavior. The authors introduce Neighbor Leakage Rate (NLR) as a metric, demonstrate severe leakage with standard backdoor training, and propose hard-negative sampling to narrow activation regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor evaluations focus only on clean accuracy and attack success rate (ASR), which measure whether a trigger works but fail to assess whether an attack is "precise" — i.e., whether hidden behaviors activate only when the exact intended trigger is present. In VLAS, where backdoors propagate through decision pipelines into executable actions, imprecise triggers pose a system-level threat that current metrics cannot capture.

Method: The authors formalize "trigger leakage" and introduce Neighbor Leakage Rate (NLR) to quantify how often visually or semantically proximate inputs inadvertently trigger malicious behavior. They evaluate icon and text triggers at a 3% poisoning ratio under common visual transformations. Using textual triggers as a controlled probe, they analyze activation regions learned during standard fine-tuning. As a mitigation, they incorporate edit-distance-one hard-negative samples during training to narrow the trigger activation boundary, testing this across image-editing and embodied-manipulation workflows.

Result: At 3% poisoning, icon and text triggers remain robust to visual transformations but suffer severe leakage: NLR reaches 0.996 for icon triggers and 0.944 for text triggers. Standard fine-tuning learns a broad activation region rather than a precise trigger condition, causing neighboring strings to invoke malicious behavior even without the exact trigger. Adding edit-distance-one hard-negative samples substantially narrows this activation region and reduces leakage in both image-editing and embodied-manipulation settings, where leaked triggers can propagate into executable programs and action sequences.

Conclusion: Trigger leakage is a serious and underappreciated vulnerability in VLAS backdoor attacks. NLR provides a necessary complement to ASR for evaluating backdoor precision. Hard-negative training offers a practical mitigation, but the finding that standard fine-tuning learns overly broad trigger conditions suggests that backdoor attacks in agentic vision-language systems require fundamentally different evaluation and defense paradigms than those used in traditional classification settings.

Abstract: Vision-Language Agentic Systems (VLAS) connect visual perception to planning, tool use, and physical actions. This means backdoor-type triggers can propagate through both decision pipelines and their connected interfaces, thus making visual backdoors a system-level threat. Current evaluations on such backdoors focus on clean accuracy and attack success rate (ASR), metrics that capture whether a trigger works, but not whether an attack is actually "precise" -- i.e. whether it triggers hidden behaviors only when intended. In this work, we formalize the failure of trigger precision as "trigger leakage": inputs that are visually or semantically close to the intended trigger and therefore inadvertently activate the attacker-specified behavior. To quantify this leakage, we introduce Neighbor Leakage Rate (NLR). Our experiments show that at a 3% poisoning ratio, icon and text triggers remain robust to common visual transformations, but their neighboring variants leak heavily, with NLR reaching 0.996 (icon) and 0.944 (text). Using textual triggers as a controlled probe, we show that standard fine-tuning learns a broad activation region rather than an exact trigger condition, causing neighboring strings to invoke the malicious behavior even when the exact trigger is absent. Adding edit-distance-one hard-negative samples during training substantially narrows this activation region and reduces leakage, including in image-editing and embodied-manipulation workflows, where leaked triggers can propagate into executable programs and action sequences.

</details>


### [172] [Amnesia: A Stealthy Replay Attack on Continual Learning Dreams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12655)
*Ahmed Sharshar,Naveen Kumar Kummari,Mohsen Guizani*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper introduces "Amnesia," a replay composition attack against continual learning systems where an insider with limited privileges manipulates only replay index selection—not data or model parameters—to maximize catastrophic forgetting. The attack operates within auditable constraints (KL/TV divergence budgets from a nominal class histogram) and demonstrates a trade-off between attack impact and detection risk on standard CL benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CL attacks rely on altering inputs or training pipelines (poisoning/backdoors) and lack explicit auditable constraints, limiting their practical realism. The authors identify replay index selection as an underexplored, auditable threat surface: a limited-privilege insider who controls only which examples are replayed, subject to monitorable constraints such as class histogram deviations and replay rates, can still inflict significant degradation.

Method: Amnesia operates in two steps: (1) lightweight class utilities (e.g., EMA loss or confidence) are computed to tilt a nominal class histogram toward harmful classes; (2) the tilt is projected back into a visibility budget using efficient KL-divergence (exponential tilt) or TV-distance (balanced mass redistribution) optimizers. A windowed scheduler enforces rolling audits. The attack is parameterized by a visibility budget δ bounding divergence from p₀ and a mass budget f fixing the replay rate.

Result: Across challenging CL benchmarks with strong replay baselines, Amnesia consistently lowers final accuracy and worsens backward transfer. The KL variant achieves high impact while remaining largely undetected under per-batch and rolling-window audits. The TV variant causes greater damage but is easier to detect, especially under tight per-class constraints. This reveals a principled impact-visibility trade-off.

Conclusion: Index-only replay control constitutes a practical, auditable threat surface in continual learning systems. The Amnesia attack demonstrates that even within explicit monitoring constraints, an insider can strategically manipulate replay composition to induce catastrophic forgetting, exposing a vulnerability that existing CL defenses do not adequately address.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) models often use experience replay to reduce catastrophic forgetting, but their robustness to replay sampling interference remains underexplored. Existing CL attacks alter inputs or training pipelines (poisoning/backdoors) and rarely include explicit auditable constraints, limiting realism. Here, auditability means a monitor can verify compliance from sampler-visible telemetry - e.g., logged replay index/label statistics - by checking that the realized replay class histogram stays close to a nominal baseline and that replay rate is unchanged per batch and/or over a rolling window. We study a limited-privilege insider who controls only replay index selection, not pixels, labels, or model parameters, while staying within auditable limits such as queue priorities. We introduce Amnesia, a replay composition attack that maximizes degradation under two budgets: a visibility budget delta bounding the TV/KL divergence from a nominal class histogram p0, and a mass budget f fixing the replay rate. Amnesia has two steps: (i) compute lightweight class utilities, such as EMA loss or confidence, to tilt p0 toward harmful classes; and (ii) project the tilt back into the delta-ball using efficient KL (exponential tilt) or TV (balanced mass redistribution) optimizers. A windowed scheduler enforces rolling audits. Across challenging CL benchmarks and strong replay baselines, Amnesia consistently lowers final accuracy (ACC) and worsens backward transfer (-BWT). The KL variant delivers high impact while remaining largely undetected under multiple audit schemes, including per-batch and rolling-window checks. The TV variant is more damaging but easier to detect, especially under tight per-class constraints. These results expose index-only replay control as a practical, auditable threat surface in CL systems and establish a principled impact-visibility trade-off.

</details>


### [173] [CAPED: Context-Aware Privacy Exposure Defense for Mobile GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12666)
*Siyu Shen,Fenghao Xu,Wenrui Diao,Kehuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: CAPED is a phone-side, context-aware pre-upload layer that selectively exposes only task-relevant screen content to remote GUI agents, reducing incidental visual privacy leakage while preserving task utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Screenshot-based mobile GUI agents create a privacy boundary problem: every screen capture sent to a remote agent may inadvertently expose sensitive, task-irrelevant content (contacts, messages, health cues, etc.). Existing defenses like text anonymization miss visual/inferential cues, while generic masking destroys the UI controls needed for task completion.

Method: CAPED operates as a phone-side layer that: (1) extracts task requirements, (2) uses screen context as a privacy prior, (3) parses visible UI elements, and (4) selectively exposes only content needed for the current task while masking incidental private content. Evaluation uses AndroidWorld for broad utility testing and a controlled 28-task seeded privacy evaluation to measure trajectory-level incidental leakage.

Result: Full CAPED reduces success-conditioned weighted seeded leakage from 0.766 (raw screenshots) to 0.268 while preserving high task utility. Broader AndroidWorld runs show some remaining prototype-level utility cost, but results validate the approach.

Conclusion: Screenshot upload should be treated as an explicit device-cloud boundary decision governed by task-driven selective exposure, not all-or-nothing screen sharing. CAPED demonstrates this is feasible with acceptable utility trade-offs.

Abstract: Screenshot-based mobile GUI agents can operate ordinary smartphone apps through the same visual interface as a human user, but this capability also turns every screen observation into a privacy boundary. During normal task execution, screenshots may expose contacts, messages, photos, files, recommendations, health cues, and other sensitive context that is unrelated to the user's request. We call this problem incidental visual privacy exposure. It is difficult to address with existing defenses: text anonymization misses many visual and inferential cues, while generic privacy masking can remove the evidence and controls that a GUI agent needs to complete the task.
  This paper presents CAPED, a context-aware pre-upload exposure control layer for mobile GUI agents. CAPED is designed as a phone-side protection layer: before screenshots are released to a remote multimodal agent, it extracts task requirements, uses screen context as a privacy prior, parses visible UI elements, and selectively exposes only content needed for the current task while masking incidental private content. We evaluate CAPED on AndroidWorld for broad task utility and with a controlled 28-task seeded privacy evaluation used as a measurement instrument for trajectory-level incidental leakage. In this seeded evaluation, Full CAPED reduces success-conditioned weighted seeded leakage from 0.766 under raw screenshots to 0.268 while preserving high task utility. A broader AndroidWorld run shows a remaining prototype-level utility cost, but the results support the central claim that screenshot upload should be treated as an explicit device--cloud boundary decision, governed by task-driven selective exposure rather than all-or-nothing screen sharing.

</details>


### [174] [SMSR: Certified Defence Against Runtime Memory Poisoning in Persistent LLM Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12703)
*Tarun Sharma*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Introduces Multi-Session Memory Poisoning (MSMP), a new attack surface on persistent-memory RAG agents. Proposes Signed Memory with Smoothed Retrieval (SMSR), the first certified defence combining HMAC-SHA256 provenance signing and randomized memory ablation with verdict-based majority voting, achieving near-zero attack success while maintaining 85–90% clean-query utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Persistent-memory RAG agents are vulnerable to adversarial memory injection across sessions. No existing defence provides certified robustness against this threat: static-corpus methods assume fixed knowledge bases, and heuristic filters fail against fluent adversarial text. The authors identify a critical gap requiring provable guarantees.

Method: SMSR has two components: (1) HMAC-SHA256 provenance signing at write time to block unsigned injections; (2) randomized memory ablation with verdict-based majority voting at query time, using hypergeometric certification to bound authenticated-adversary influence. The authors also formalize the "Consistent Minority Effect" showing why string-based voting fails against adversarial answers.

Result: Component 1 reduces attack success from 93–100% to 0% for unsigned variants across 15 enterprise scenarios (3,150 trials). For authenticated single-injection adversaries, Component 2 limits success to 8.0% (95% CI [5.8, 10.9]), below the certified worst case. In end-to-end query-only attacks, SMSR reduces success from 65.3% to 5.3% (n=150). Clean-query utility is 90% (Component 1) and 85% (combined).

Conclusion: SMSR is the first defence with certified robustness bounds against multi-session memory poisoning in persistent-memory RAG agents, effectively neutralizing both unsigned and authenticated adversaries while preserving high utility.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) agents increasingly run with persistent memory that accumulates across user sessions. This creates a new attack surface: an adversary interacting only through normal channels can inject crafted memories that, once retrieved, steer the agent's responses for future users, without touching model weights or code. We call this Multi-Session Memory Poisoning (MSMP) and show that no existing defence certifies against it; static-corpus defences (RobustRAG, ReliabilityRAG) assume a fixed knowledge base, and heuristic filters are bypassed by fluent enterprise-style text. We present Signed Memory with Smoothed Retrieval (SMSR), the first defence with a certified robustness bound for this setting. Component 1 adds HMAC-SHA256 provenance at write time, blocking unsigned injection. Component 2 applies randomised memory ablation with verdict-based majority voting at query time, bounding the influence of authenticated adversaries. We prove that no provenance-free retrieval-time filter can certify against adaptive injection, derive a hypergeometric certificate for Component 2, and formalise the Consistent Minority Effect, whereby a consistent adversarial answer wins string-based voting as a numerical minority while verdict-based voting removes it. Across 15 enterprise scenarios (3,150 repeated trials), Component 1 cuts attack success from 93-100% to 0% for all unsigned variants. For an authenticated adversary with a single injection, Component 2 holds success to 8.0% (95% CI [5.8, 10.9], n=450), below the certified worst case. In an end-to-end query-only attack where the agent itself writes the poison rather than it being pre-seeded, SMSR reduces success from 65.3% to 5.3% (n=150, non-overlapping CIs) on a live agent stack. Clean-query utility is 90% (Component 1) and 85% (combined).

</details>


### [175] [PI-Hunter: Automated Red-Teaming for Exposing and Localizing Prompt Injections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12737)
*Pengfei He,Lesly Miculicich,Vishesh Sharma,Ash Fox,George Lee,Jiliang Tang,Tomas Pfister,Long T. Le*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: PI-Hunter is an automated auditing framework that proactively exposes prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agents by generating realistic source-aware test cases and iteratively evolving them through feedback-driven exploration, significantly outperforming existing red-teaming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs become agentic systems interacting with external tools, they face indirect prompt injection attacks from untrusted sources. Current defenses focus on inference-time blocking and red-teaming methods prioritize attack success, leaving developers blind to how latent prompt injections emerge and propagate through agents.

Method: PI-Hunter constructs realistic source-aware test cases and employs an iterative, feedback-driven exploration process to evolve these cases. The system induces agents to retrieve and reveal malicious instructions hidden in external environments, systematically auditing how vulnerabilities manifest across agent components.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks, agent architectures, attack types, and defense mechanisms show PI-Hunter substantially improves vulnerability exposure and attack-surface coverage over strong automated red-teaming baselines, while remaining effective against existing prompt injection defenses.

Conclusion: PI-Hunter provides a proactive, automated approach to auditing and exposing prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agents, offering developers greater visibility into latent threats that traditional inference-time defenses and attack-optimizing red-teaming methods fail to address.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly evolving into agentic systems that interact with external tools and environments, introducing new security risks such as indirect prompt injection attacks through untrusted external sources. Existing defenses mainly focus on blocking malicious content at inference time, and current red-teaming methods primarily optimize attack success. As a result, developers have limited visibility into how latent prompt injections emerge and propagate through agents. We propose PI-Hunter, an automated agentic auditing framework for proactive vulnerability exposure in LLM agents. PI-Hunter constructs realistic source-aware test cases and iteratively evolves them through feedback-driven exploration to induce agents to retrieve and reveal latent malicious instructions embedded within external environments. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks, agent architectures, attacks, and defenses demonstrate that PI-Hunter substantially improves vulnerability exposure and attack-surface coverage over strong automated red-teaming baselines, while remaining effective under existing prompt injection defenses.

</details>


### [176] [Semantic Identification of IoT Devices from Behavioral Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12793)
*Samuel Witt,Hassan Habibi Gharakheili*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes using Manufacturer Usage Description (MUD) profiles for IoT device identification. It constructs semantic representations of Access Control Entries (ACEs) and demonstrates that semantic ACE matching outperforms exact matching under sparse overlap, unseen ACEs, and early observation stages, validated on 28 MUD profiles and over 800,000 real IoT traffic flows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing IoT device identification methods rely on low-level packet/flow features that vary across deployments, software versions, and user behavior. The authors aim to leverage MUD profiles — standardized, semantically meaningful behavioral specifications — for more robust and generalizable device identification.

Method: (1) Construct ACE-level semantic embeddings from 28 MUD profiles (1,023 ACEs) using compact behavioral text and analyze their geometric properties, including whitening calibration. (2) Evaluate semantic vs. exact ACE matching under controlled variations: unseen ACEs, drifted hostnames, and partial observations. (3) Validate on real-world IoT traffic traces (>800,000 flows), comparing exact overlap with semantic matching across different observation stages and overlap conditions.

Result: ACE-level embeddings preserve device-level distinctions better than whole-profile embeddings and remain effective after whitening. Exact ACE matching degrades sharply under sparse or absent overlap; semantic ACE matching retains identification evidence. In real traffic, semantic matching provides stronger identification early in observation, frequently retains the correct device among top-ranked candidates, and remains effective under sparse-overlap conditions.

Conclusion: Semantic ACE matching using MUD profiles offers a robust alternative to exact matching for IoT device identification, particularly under challenging real-world conditions with incomplete or sparse traffic observations.

Abstract: Accurate identification of IoT devices is important for security management and policy enforcement. Existing approaches typically learn device signatures from packets or flow records. These methods operate on low-level communication observations whose traffic patterns may vary across deployments, software versions, and user interactions. This paper studies device identification using Manufacturer Usage Description (MUD) profiles. MUD profiles describe device behavior using Access Control Entries (ACEs), where each ACE represents a behavioral primitive consisting of protocol, endpoint, direction, and port semantics derived from device communication policy. Our contributions are threefold. First, using 28 publicly available MUD profiles containing 1,023 ACE instances, we construct ACE-level semantic representations from compact behavioral text and analyze their geometric properties. ACE-level representations preserve device-level behavioral distinctions more effectively than whole-profile embeddings and remain effective after whitening calibration. Second, we evaluate semantic ACE matching under controlled runtime variations, including unseen ACEs, drifted hostnames, and partial runtime observation. Exact ACE matching performs well when the overlap with the canonical MUD profile remains high, but degrades sharply when the overlap becomes sparse or disappears. In contrast, semantic ACE matching preserves useful identification evidence across these conditions. Third, we evaluate the same approach on real IoT traffic traces comprising more than 800,000 observed flows. Exact overlap remains the strongest signal when stable overlap exists, while semantic ACE matching provides stronger identification evidence during the early stages of observation, frequently retains the correct device among the highest-ranked candidates, and remains effective under sparse-overlap runtime traffic.

</details>


### [177] [DIG: Oracle-Guided Directed Input Generation for One-Day Vulnerabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13037)
*Andrew Bao,Haochen Zeng,Peng Chen,Stephen McCamant,Pen-Chung Yew*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: DIG leverages LLM-based patch analysis to synthesize oracles that make vulnerability-triggering preconditions explicit, then uses these oracles for both high-level agent-guided generator evolution and low-level branch-distance feedback fuzzing, achieving state-of-the-art PoC generation across 138 CVEs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-day vulnerabilities are dangerous due to slow patch adoption, yet existing directed fuzzers cannot explicitly identify or solve the necessary constraints to trigger them. Agentic approaches suffer from goal drift in long-horizon reasoning. DIG exploits the insight that patches often reveal the exact preconditions needed to trigger a vulnerability.

Method: DIG uses an LLM to analyze vulnerability patches and synthesize an oracle that explicitly encodes triggering preconditions. At the high level, an agent performs oracle-guided generator evolution, inferring and solving constraints to satisfy the oracle. At the low level, the oracle is instrumented into the target program, and branch-distance feedback guides random mutation in directed fuzzing.

Result: DIG outperforms 2 state-of-the-art agents and 10 fuzzers across 138 real-world CVEs, triggering 80 vulnerabilities (vs. 57 for the best baseline, a 40% improvement). DIG exclusively triggers 9 vulnerabilities no existing technique can trigger. It is faster in 92.9% of cases, with over 100x speedup in 48.8% of cases (max 3,664x). DIG also discovers 6 previously unknown zero-day vulnerabilities in widely deployed libraries.

Conclusion: Exploiting patch-derived oracles bridges the gap between LLM-based reasoning and fuzzing. The dual-level approach—oracle-guided generator evolution plus oracle-instrumented directed fuzzing—effectively addresses both necessary constraint identification and satisfaction, achieving significant improvements in one-day PoC generation and enabling zero-day discovery.

Abstract: One-day vulnerabilities pose significant risks due to delayed or incomplete patch adoption. Generating proof-of-concept (PoC) inputs is therefore essential for assessing real-world impact. The key challenge is identifying necessary constraints for triggering the vulnerability and solving them effectively. Existing directed fuzzing approaches prioritize inputs toward target locations, but neither explicitly identify necessary constraints nor solve them effectively, relying instead on target-distance feedback and random mutation. Agentic approaches show strong potential through code reasoning and structured input generation, but goal drift in long-horizon reasoning limits their effectiveness.
  DIG addresses this challenge by exploiting a key property of one-day vulnerabilities: patches often reveal necessary preconditions for triggering. DIG uses an LLM to analyze the patch and synthesize an oracle making these conditions explicit. The oracle supports effective PoC generation at two levels. At the high level, DIG performs oracle-guided generator evolution, where an agent infers and solves constraints to satisfy the oracle. At the low level, DIG instruments the oracle into the target program and uses branch-distance feedback to guide random mutation in directed fuzzing. Evaluation shows DIG outperforms 2 state-of-the-art agents and 10 fuzzers across 138 real-world CVEs. DIG triggers 80 vulnerabilities, surpassing prior results and outperforming the best baseline by 40% (57 vs. 80 CVEs). Notably, DIG exclusively triggers 9 vulnerabilities no existing technique can trigger. Compared to the average of other tools, DIG triggers vulnerabilities faster in 92.9% of cases, achieving over 100x speedup in 48.8% of cases, with a maximum speedup of 3,664x. Beyond one-day PoC generation, DIG uncovers 6 previously unknown vulnerabilities in widely deployed libraries, enabling zero-day discovery.

</details>


### [178] [A Privacy-Preserving Framework Using Remote Data Science for Inter-Institutional Student Retention Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12845)
*John Fields,K M Sajjadul Islam,Ruchitha Thota,Victor Chen,Praveen Madiraju*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper presents a privacy-preserving machine learning framework using PySyft that enables inter-institutional student retention prediction without direct data access, achieving consistent Macro F1 scores (~0.69) across three universities while maintaining FERPA compliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Institutions need to collaborate on student retention modeling but cannot share sensitive student data due to FERPA and other privacy regulations. Existing approaches like federated learning can be complex for small-scale collaborations, motivating the need for a simpler, practical PPML alternative.

Method: The authors developed a Remote Data Science (RDS) framework with a semi-air-gapped architecture (high-side/low-side servers) using PySyft. Three synthetic data generation methods were evaluated on N=720 historical student records, and a novel Data-Type-Aware Templates approach prioritizing privacy over distributional fidelity was proposed.
