Result: Consistent classification performance across institutions with Macro F1 scores ranging from 0.690 to 0.695, demonstrating that the RDS-based PPML framework maintains predictive accuracy while ensuring strict FERPA compliance.

Conclusion: RDS-based privacy-preserving ML is technically feasible for educational settings and offers a practical, less complex alternative to federated learning for small-scale inter-institutional collaborations on sensitive student data.

Abstract: This study explores privacy-preserving machine learning (PPML) techniques using the PySyft platform to enable collaborative prediction of student retention between institutions. We developed a remote data science (RDS) framework with a semi-air-gapped architecture consisting of high-side and low-side servers, allowing researchers from three universities to build predictive models on sensitive student data without direct data access. Using historical data from a small private university (N=720), we evaluated three synthetic data generation approaches and validated the framework through inter-institutional collaboration. The results demonstrate consistent classification performance across institutions (Macro F1: 0.690--0.695) while maintaining strict Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) compliance. We also propose Data-Type-Aware Templates, a novel synthetic data method that prioritizes privacy over distributional fidelity. Our findings confirm that RDS-based PPML is technically feasible for educational settings and offers a practical alternative to federated learning for small-scale inter-institutional collaborations. The code is available at https://github.com/jtfields/NAIRR240195-Privacy-Preserving-Machine-Learning.

</details>


### [179] [Who Pays the Price? Stakeholder-Centric Prompt Injection Benchmarking for Real-world Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13385)
*Zihao Wang,Yiming Li,Yutong Wu,Zheyu Liu,Kangjie Chen,Fok Kar Wai,Pin-Yu Chen,Vrizlynn L. L. Thing,Bo Li,Dacheng Tao,Tianwei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: StakeBench is a stakeholder-centric benchmark for evaluating prompt-injection attacks on LLM-based web agents, revealing that current agents are universally vulnerable and that conventional attack-centric evaluations miss critical harm distribution patterns across users, sellers, and platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prompt-injection security benchmarks adopt an attack-centric perspective focused on technical feasibility, ignoring how harms distribute differently across stakeholders. The same attack can produce asymmetric consequences for users, sellers, and platforms, but no framework captures this victim-dependent risk landscape.

Method: StakeBench systematically categorizes affected entities (user, seller, platform), decomposes attacks into concrete objectives, and evaluates each case using complementary outcome-level and process-level metrics. It identifies distinct failure modes: stealthy parasitism (attack succeeds without task disruption), misaligned disruption (task disrupted without attack success), and compounded failure (both objectives violated).

Result: Not a single attack objective is reliably resisted by current LLM-based web agents. Failures distribute across qualitatively distinct modes — stealthy parasitism, misaligned disruption, and compounded failure — which conventional evaluations systematically miss.

Conclusion: Stakeholder-aware assessment is essential for real-world LLM agent deployments; current agents exhibit substantial, heterogeneous vulnerabilities that demand evaluation frameworks capturing the full distribution of harms across affected parties.

Abstract: Web agents driven by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world environments, where they operate over untrusted web content and execute actions with direct consequences. This makes them vulnerable to prompt-injection attacks, in which seemingly benign content embeds adversarial instructions that manipulate agent behaviour. Existing security benchmarks adopt an \textit{attack-centric} perspective, focusing on the technical feasibility of injections while overlooking the nuanced distribution of resulting harms. In practice, however, prompt-injection risk is victim-dependent: a single exploit can produce asymmetric consequences for different stakeholders, and the same attack pattern may exhibit substantially different effectiveness depending on whom it targets. To capture these properties, we introduce \textbf{\sysname}, a \textit{stakeholder-centric} benchmark to systematically categorize and attribute harm in real-world web agent systems. It distinguishes between affected entities (e.g., user, seller, platform), decomposes the attacks into concrete objectives, and evaluates each case with complementary outcome- and process-level metrics. Our results reveal substantial and heterogeneous vulnerabilities: not a single attack objective is reliably resisted by current agents, and failures distribute across qualitatively distinct modes ranging from \emph{stealthy parasitism} (attack succeeds without disrupting the user's delegated task) to \emph{misaligned disruption} (task disrupted without attack success) and \emph{compounded failure} (both adversarial objective and task integrity simultaneously violated). These patterns are missed by conventional evaluation, highlighting the need for stakeholder-aware assessment of LLM-based agents in real-world deployments. Benchmark is available at https://github.com/StakeBench/SBC.

</details>


### [180] [LNTest: A Testbed for Evaluating Bitcoin Lightning Network-Based Botnets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12887)
*Thomas Bakaysa,Ahmet Kurt,Abdul-Salem Beibitkhan,Jesus Maria Romo Diaz de Leon,Tag Kalat,Joshua Kramer,Estela Rodriguez,Abraham Watkins,Abdullah Aydeger*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: LNTest is the first reusable testbed for Lightning Network-based botnets, revealing that D-LNBot's autonomous formation creates fragile clustered chains, command propagation scales linearly rather than as previously claimed, and overlay topology dictates takedown resilience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior LN-based botnet designs (LNBot, D-LNBot) were only simulated proofs-of-concept, leaving unanswered questions about real-world topology formation, propagation complexity, and resilience to takedowns. A realistic, reproducible experimental platform was needed.

Method: LNTest: a containerized testbed using Core Lightning nodes with Docker over a shared Bitcoin Core regtest chain. Supports three overlay topology modes (deterministic chain, autonomous peer discovery, user-supplied graphs). Experiments measure topology formation, command propagation latency versus botnet size, and takedown resilience under random failure and targeted removal.

Result: (1) D-LNBot's autonomous formation yields clustered chains with bridge-node-linked cliques, not uniform chains. (2) Command propagation is Θ(n) linear, not O(m log n), with no benefit from higher neighbor degree. (3) Uniform chains resist targeted removal but fragment under random failure; scale-free topologies show the inverse; autonomous clustered chains are fragile under both, making them the most vulnerable.

Conclusion: LN-based botnet behavior is highly sensitive to overlay topology formation dynamics, and the previously assumed efficiency and resilience claims do not hold under realistic experimentation. LNTest provides an open-source platform for reproducible research on LN botnet defenses.

Abstract: Bitcoin's Lightning Network (LN) can be exploited as a covert, low-cost command-and-control (C&C) channel for botnets, as demonstrated by the LNBot and D-LNBot designs. However, both remain proof-of-concept prototypes evaluated only through simulation, leaving key questions about real-world topology formation, propagation complexity, and resilience to takedowns unanswered. We present LNTest, the first reusable testbed for LN-based botnets, built from Core Lightning nodes containerized with Docker over a shared Bitcoin Core regtest chain. LNTest supports three overlay topology modes (a deterministic chain, autonomous peer discovery, and user-supplied graphs), enabling controlled experiments across different botnet structures. Using LNTest, we report three main findings. First, D-LNBot's autonomous formation protocol does not produce the uniform chain from its design; instead, it creates a clustered chain in which cliques are linked by bridge nodes whose removal fragments the network. Second, command propagation scales linearly with botnet size ($Θ(n)$), not the $O(m \log n)$ previously claimed, and gains nothing from higher neighbor connectivity. Third, the overlay topology determines the effectiveness of takedown strategies: uniform-degree chains resist targeted removal but fragment under random failure, scale-free topologies show the opposite pattern, and the autonomous clustered chain is fragile under both, making it the most vulnerable of the three. LNTest is released as open source, with a script that reproduces all our experiments, to support reproducible research on LN-based botnet defenses.

</details>


### [181] [MAStrike: Shapley-Guided Collusive Red-Teaming on Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12918)
*Chejian Xu,Zhaorun Chen,Jingyang Zhang,Freddy Lecue,Avni Kothari,Sarah Tan,Wenbo Guo,Bo Li*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: MAStrike is a closed-loop red-teaming framework for hierarchical multi-agent systems that uses Shapley value analysis to identify vulnerable agent coalitions and generate coordinated, collusive adversarial attacks, outperforming heuristic baselines across finance, software engineering, and CRM domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing red-teaming for MAS relies on heuristic target selection and isolated message perturbation, failing to answer which agents are most critical to system safety and how compromised agents can collude to bypass defenses. Hierarchical MAS deployed in high-stakes domains have an expanded attack surface under coordinated adversarial behaviors like privilege escalation and cross-agent collusion.

Method: MAStrike introduces: (1) agent-level Shapley value analysis to quantify each agent's marginal contribution to system robustness; (2) identification of vulnerable agent coalitions guided by Shapley attribution; (3) generation of coordinated, role-aware adversarial manipulations; (4) iterative refinement via structured causal diagnosis that attributes failures to uncompromised agents blocking adversarial attempts. A comprehensive benchmark with controllable hierarchical topologies across finance, software engineering, and CRM is also constructed.

Result: MAStrike substantially outperforms heuristic baselines across MAS built on multiple frontier models. The analysis reveals non-trivial Shapley value distributions and higher-order interaction structures among agents, uncovering critical vulnerabilities and coordination patterns overlooked by prior single-agent or template-based methods.

Conclusion: Agent-level Shapley value attribution effectively identifies safety-critical agents in hierarchical MAS, and collusive, role-aware adversarial strategies expose significantly deeper vulnerabilities than isolated single-agent attacks. This underscores the need for coalition-aware defenses in production MAS deployments.

Abstract: Hierarchical multi-agent systems (MAS) are rapidly being deployed in high-stakes workflows across domains such as finance and software engineering. In these systems, safety and security are inherently distributed across role-specialized agents, significantly expanding the attack surface, particularly under coordinated adversarial behaviors such as privilege escalation and cross-agent collusion. Existing red-teaming approaches for MAS remain limited: they rely on heuristic selection of target agents and perturb isolated message streams, leaving critical questions unanswered as which agents are most responsible for system safety, and how compromised agents can coordinate to bypass defenses. We propose MAStrike, a closed-loop framework for collusive red-teaming in hierarchical MAS. We propose the first agent-level Shapley value analysis for MAS, quantifying each agent's marginal contribution to system robustness under task-specific distributions. GGuided by this attribution, MAStrike identifies vulnerable agent coalitions and generates coordinated, role-aware adversarial manipulations. These attacks are iteratively refined through structured causal diagnosis, attributing failure cases to uncompromised agents that block adversarial attempts. We further build a comprehensive MAS red-teaming benchmark and controllable environments spanning diverse hierarchical topologies and domains, including finance, software engineering, and CRM. Extensive experiments across MAS built on multiple frontier models show that MAStrike substantially outperforms heuristic baselines. Our analysis further uncovers non-trivial Shapley value distributions and higher-order interaction structures among agents, revealing critical vulnerabilities and coordination patterns that are overlooked by prior single-agent or template-based methods.

</details>


### [182] [ViPER: Vision-based Packing-Aware Encoder for Robust Malware Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12949)
*Fatima Qaiser,Bisma Tahir,Muhammad Abid Mughal,Nauman Shamim*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: ViPER is a vision-based malware detection system using a ViT-B/14 backbone with dual-head architecture that jointly learns malware classification and packing detection. A packing-aware gating mechanism adjusts decision boundaries based on packing state, and frequency-weighted losses with stratified sampling address label imbalance. On 200K PE byteplot images, it achieves 0.8521 balanced accuracy, 0.9260 ROC-AUC, and 0.9949 packing detection AUC, outperforming SOTA baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing visualization-based malware detectors fail on packed executables because packing produces high-entropy byteplot images that obscure structural patterns. Since packing is common in both benign and malicious software, packing state alone cannot reliably indicate maliciousness. No prior work addresses this challenge within a unified supervised framework.

Method: ViPER uses a LoRA-adapted ViT-B/14 backbone with a dual-head architecture: one head for malware classification, one for packing detection. A packing-aware gating mechanism conditions malware predictions on the inferred packing state, creating distinct decision boundaries for packed vs. unpacked inputs. Training employs frequency-weighted losses and stratified sampling over joint class-packing strata to handle packing label skew.

Result: On 200,000 Windows PE byteplot images: balanced accuracy 0.8521, ROC-AUC 0.9260, AUPR 0.9279, and packing detection AUC 0.9949. Outperforms representative SOTA baselines across all primary metrics.

Conclusion: ViPER effectively mitigates the packing failure mode in visualization-based malware detection by jointly modeling malware class and packing state, with gated conditioning enabling robust decisions for both packed and unpacked binaries.

Abstract: Visualization-based malware detection maps raw binary bytes to grayscale images and applies learned visual classifiers, providing an evasion-resistant and disassembly-free alternative to conventional analysis pipelines. However, executable packing remains a critical failure mode: packed binaries produce high-entropy images that obscure the structural patterns these models rely on. Because packing is also prevalent in benign software (e.g., for compression or copy protection), packing state alone is not a reliable indicator of maliciousness, and existing approaches do not address this challenge within a unified supervised framework. We present ViPER, a Vision-based Packing-Aware Encoder for Robust malware detection. ViPER builds on a LoRA-adapted ViT-B/14 backbone with a dual-head architecture that jointly learns malware classification and packing detection. A packing-aware gating mechanism conditions malware predictions on the inferred packing state, enabling distinct decision boundaries for packed and unpacked inputs. To address packing label skew during training, we employ frequency-weighted losses with stratified sampling over joint class-packing strata. Evaluated on 200,000 Windows PE byteplot images, ViPER achieves a balanced accuracy of 0.8521, ROC-AUC of 0.9260, and AUPR of 0.9279, outperforming representative state-of-the-art baselines across all primary metrics, while attaining a packing detection AUC of 0.9949.

</details>


### [183] [SoK: The Constant Time Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13000)
*Billy Bob Brumley*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper systematizes constant-time programming models across academia and industry, identifies a recurring gap between model protections and specification assumptions, and presents an offensive methodology for finding timing vulnerabilities outside cryptographic primitives. The authors apply this methodology to discover a specification-level vulnerability in private key loading, confirmed in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL, with the surprising result that BoringSSL leaks far more per observation despite its stricter threat model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no consensus on what "constant time" means across academia and industry, creating a dangerous gap between what timing models protect against and what security specifications assume. This ambiguity leaves cryptographic implementations vulnerable to timing attacks that originate outside the traditional cryptographic primitive boundary.

Method: The authors systematize existing constant-time models and their historical evolution, identify where models and specifications diverge, and develop an offensive methodology to detect timing leaks beyond the cryptographic primitive boundary. They then apply this methodology to real-world libraries (OpenSSL and BoringSSL), focusing on private key loading operations.

Result: A specification-level timing vulnerability was discovered in private key loading. Both OpenSSL and BoringSSL were confirmed to leak timing information. Counterintuitively, BoringSSL — which adopts an explicitly stricter threat model — exhibits per-observation leakage that is several orders of magnitude stronger than OpenSSL's.

Conclusion: The gap between constant-time models and specification-level assumptions represents a systemic vulnerability class. Libraries with stricter threat models may paradoxically introduce stronger side-channel signals, suggesting that constant-time guarantees must be verified end-to-end, including at the specification and API boundaries, not just within cryptographic primitives.

Abstract: Constant time programming patterns is the primary defense against timing attacks on cryptographic implementations, yet what "constant time" means varies across academia and industry. This work systematizes constant time models and their evolution, identifies a recurring gap between what models protect and what specifications assume, and distills an offensive methodology for discovering timing vulnerabilities that originate outside the cryptographic primitive boundary. Applying this methodology, we locate a specification-level vulnerability related to private key loading, and confirm the leak in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL. Counterintuitively, BoringSSL's per-observation signal is several orders of magnitude stronger than OpenSSL's, despite an explicitly stricter threat model.

</details>


### [184] [The Emergence of Autonomous Penetration Capabilities in Large Language Model-Powered AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13079)
*Jiaqi Luo,Jiarun Dai,Zhile Chen,Jia Xu,Weibing Wang,Yawen Duan,Brian Tse,Geng Hong,Xudong Pan,Yuan Zhang,Min Yang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new evaluation framework for autonomous penetration testing by LLM-powered AI agents, featuring 300 target servers across two difficulty tiers. Evaluating 19 models reveals success rates from 10.7% to 69.3%, with capability scaling alongside general model capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of autonomous penetration capabilities in AI systems suffer from opaque methodologies, unrealistic scenarios, and excessive prior knowledge or task-specific guidance. These shortcomings prevent accurate assessment of how well frontier AI systems can autonomously perform penetration testing — a core subtask within high-impact cyberattack scenarios that represent a critical red line for AI safety.

Method: The authors construct a two-component evaluation framework: (1) target servers with two difficulty tiers — Tier 1 (one secure service alongside a vulnerable service) and Tier 2 (three secure services alongside a vulnerable service), totaling 300 target servers; and (2) a general-purpose agent scaffolding equipped with standard cybersecurity tools, deliberately withholding any target-specific prior knowledge. Nineteen open-weight and proprietary LLMs are evaluated under this framework.

Result: Current LLMs achieve autonomous penetration success rates ranging from 10.7% to 69.3% across the evaluated models. The results also reveal that autonomous penetration capability improves in tandem with advances in overall model capability.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a more realistic, standardized, and transparent means of evaluating autonomous penetration capabilities. The findings indicate that as frontier models continue to improve, their capacity for autonomous cyberattacks may correspondingly increase, underscoring the importance of monitoring this red-line capability.

Abstract: Nowadays, the autonomous execution of cyberattacks capable of causing substantial real-world harm is widely regarded as one of the critical red lines that frontier AI systems must not cross. Within this broader red-line scenario, autonomous penetration represents a core enabling capability and subtask: the ability of LLM-powered AI systems to independently conduct adversarial operations against a target server without human intervention, identify and exploit vulnerabilities, and obtain unauthorized access or control. A growing body of work has sought to assess the autonomous penetration capabilities of AI systems. However, existing evaluations often employ opaque methodologies, rely on unrealistic or overly simplified penetration-testing scenarios, or provide LLMs with excessive prior knowledge and task-specific guidance, and cannot accurately capture the extent to which modern AI systems can autonomously perform this core capability within broader high-impact cyberattack scenarios.
  To address these limitations, we construct a new autonomous penetration evaluation framework consisting of two components: target servers and agent scaffolding. Specifically, on the target-server side, we design two levels of target environments based on the number of secure services without known vulnerabilities deployed alongside a vulnerable service: Tier~1 (one secure service) and Tier~2 (three secure services), resulting in a total of 300 target servers. Meanwhile, the agent scaffolding adopts a general-purpose agent architecture equipped with a set of general-purpose cybersecurity tools, without any target-specific prior knowledge. We evaluate 19 open-weight and proprietary LLMs, and find that current models achieve penetration success rates ranging from 10.7% to 69.3%. Moreover, we observe that autonomous penetration capability continues to improve alongside advances in overall model capability.

</details>


### [185] [The Invisible Ink of the Android Malware World: A Longitudinal Study on the Usage of Covert Communication Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13107)
*Zeya Umayya,Manan Aggarwal,Manan Chugh,Mann Nariya,Yogesh Kaushik,Sambuddho Chakravarty*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: First longitudinal study of covert channel (CC) usage in Android malware, analyzing 3.5M samples (2009–2025). Found CC usage growing from 0.30% to 50%, identified 288K APKs across 511 families using CCs, and validated 59 CC-associated IPs across 17 countries. Reveals evolutionary patterns including multi-CC adoption and periodic switching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While proxies, VPNs, and Tor serve legitimate privacy/anti-censorship purposes, malware and botnets exploit these same tools as covert channels to hide C2 communications. Despite growing malware threats, no longitudinal study had systematically examined how Android malware uses covert channels to evade detection.

Method: A multistage pipeline combining static and dynamic analysis on a corpus of 3.5M Android malware samples spanning 2009 to July 2025. Static validation rules identified CC-using APKs and classified malware families. Dynamic analysis captured runtime network behavior, identifying contacted IP addresses and explicitly validating CC presence through network-level features.

Result: Static analysis uncovered 288K APKs using CCs across 511 malware families, with CC usage rising exponentially from 0.30% (2012) to 50% (2025). Dynamic analysis identified 19,308 unique IPs in 85 countries; 59 IPs across 17 countries were explicitly validated as CCs. Longitudinal analysis revealed evolutionary trends: some malware adopted multiple CCs simultaneously, others switched between CCs periodically — one family switched 40 times from 2019–2025.

Conclusion: Covert channel usage in Android malware has undergone significant evolution over 16 years, becoming increasingly prevalent and sophisticated. The findings highlight a critical blind spot in malware detection and call for enhanced monitoring of CC-based evasion techniques in the Android ecosystem.

Abstract: Proxies, VPNs and Tor have long helped the privacy community and users in censored regions to fight censorship. However, the same tools can be maliciously exploited by malware and botnets to conceal their communication to external command and control servers. Despite being a critical concern fueled by the proliferation of malware based attacks, no longitudinal studies have analyzed how malware applications use covert channels (CC) to evade detection.
  We fill this gap by performing the first study of the usage of covert channels in the Android malware ecosystem. To that end, we develop a multistage pipeline that combines static and dynamic analysis to investigate both system and network-level features. We applied this pipeline on a corpus of 3.5M Android malware spanning 2009 to July 2025. Our carefully crafted static validation rules uncovered 288K APKs that used CCs spanning 511 malware families and CC usage growing exponentially from 0.30\% (2012) to 50\% (2025). Overall, in dynamic analysis, we identified 19,308 unique IP addresses being contacted in 85 countries, out of which we were able to explicitly validate the presence of CCs for 59 IP addresses across 17 countries. Further, we performed a longitudinal dataset study spanning over 16 years for CC based malware and found that CC usage has evolved, \textit{e.g.,} some malware adopted by using more than one CCs; others switched between them periodically (one family switched CC usage 40 times from 2019 to 2025).

</details>


### [186] [An Assessment Framework for Application-Level Cryptographic Agility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13425)
*Navaneeth Rameshan,Gregoire Messmer*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Proposes a seven-dimensional component-based framework for assessing cryptographic agility in APIs, evaluates six major APIs against it, and identifies three critical, pervasive gaps — intent-based key creation, policy-driven algorithm selection, and algorithm transformation operations — that individually prevent agile post-quantum migration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The post-quantum cryptographic transition requires wholesale algorithm replacement across all software, but current cryptographic APIs lack agility by design. The term "cryptographic agility" conflates multiple independent capabilities (algorithm replacement, policy-based selection, implementation substitution), and no structured decomposition exists to systematically evaluate or build agile APIs.

Method: The authors introduce a component-based assessment framework decomposing cryptographic agility into seven orthogonal dimensions: three coupling dimensions (capturing what application code knows about algorithms and providers), one cross-cutting decoupling mechanism, a governance authority dimension, and two agility enablers measuring actual migration capability. The framework is non-linear and captures non-hierarchical profiles. Six representative APIs (PKCS#11, OpenSSL 3.0, JCA, Google Tink, AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault Transit) are evaluated against this framework.

Result: Evaluation reveals three pervasive, independent gaps across all six APIs: (1) no system supports intent-based key creation, (2) none provides policy-driven algorithm selection distinct from access control, and (3) none offers dedicated operations for algorithm transformation of existing keys. Each gap is individually sufficient to prevent agile cryptographic migration.

Conclusion: The post-quantum transition remains fundamentally a software engineering problem despite decades of API evolution, because existing cryptographic APIs exhibit structural deficiencies in agility that cannot be addressed without rethinking API design along the seven proposed dimensions.

Abstract: The impending post-quantum transition to new cryptography will require complete replacement of algorithms within all software. The cryptographic APIs used today make this transition challenging because they were not designed with agility as a concern. There is no method for systematically assessing cryptographic agility as an overall ability. In addition to this, the term itself refers to multiple independent capabilities. Specifically, it includes replacing algorithms, selecting by policy, and substituting implementations. This lack of structured decomposition limits both the evaluation of systems and the development of cryptographically agile APIs.
  We introduce a component-based assessment framework that characterizes application-level cryptographic agility along seven orthogonal dimensions: three coupling dimensions that measure what the application code knows about algorithms and providers, a cross-cutting decoupling mechanism, a governance authority dimension, and two agility enablers that measure actual migration capability. The framework is non-linear and captures non-hierarchical profiles: a system may achieve high operation decoupling yet low creation decoupling, or strong versioning without externalized configuration.
  We evaluate six representative APIs (PKCS#11, OpenSSL~3.0, JCA, Google Tink, AWS KMS, and HashiCorp Vault Transit) against the framework, revealing three pervasive and independent gaps: no system supports intent-based key creation, none provides policy-driven algorithm selection (as distinct from access control), and none offers dedicated/first-class operations for algorithm transformation of existing keys. These gaps are individually sufficient to prevent agile migration, explaining why the post-quantum transition remains a software engineering problem despite decades of API progress.

</details>


### [187] [Intent-Based Cryptographic API Design for Cryptographic Agility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13445)
*Navaneeth Rameshan,Gregoire Messmer*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Current cryptographic APIs are algorithm-specific and lack mechanisms for policy-driven selection or key migration, making post-quantum transitions difficult. This paper proposes design principles for cryptographically agile APIs — abstraction, stability, temporal flexibility, separation, and extensibility — and demonstrates their implementation via Protocol Buffers patterns, including an intent vocabulary based on scopes, abstract policy APIs, and stable key identifiers with evolution tracking, enabling cryptographic updates as operational processes without application code changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organizations transitioning to post-quantum cryptography face the challenge of updating algorithms across large software portfolios. Existing cryptographic APIs are tightly coupled to specific algorithms, lack policy-based algorithm selection, and provide no straightforward key migration path. Algorithm transition is fundamentally a software engineering problem, not merely a cryptographic one.

Method: The authors derive design principles from five architectural characteristics: Abstraction, Stability, Temporal Flexibility, Separation, and Extensibility. These principles are instantiated using Protocol Buffers API design patterns. Key technical contributions include: (1) an intent vocabulary based on scopes that decouples key creation from algorithm identities and enables transparent algorithm substitution; (2) an abstract policy API for cryptographic governance that is agnostic to policy format; and (3) stable key identifiers supporting evolution operations (rotation, transformation, migration) while preserving identity and history.

Result: The proposed API design enables cryptographic updates to become an operational process that does not require rewriting application code. Algorithm substitutions within a scope are transparent, keys can migrate between algorithms and providers, and both original key identity and evolution history are tracked.

Conclusion: By adopting these design principles and patterns, organizations can achieve cryptographic agility — turning algorithm migration from a disruptive code-rewrite effort into a routine operational task, thereby addressing the software engineering barriers that currently impede post-quantum cryptography adoption.

Abstract: As organizations move toward post-quantum cryptography, they face the major challenge of updating cryptographic algorithms across large, complex software portfolios. However, most cryptographic APIs in use today were designed around specific algorithms. These APIs expect explicit use of specific algorithms, provide little or no support for policy-based algorithm selection, and offer no straightforward way to migrate existing keys to newer algorithms. This makes the transition to post-quantum cryptography challenging. The companion assessment framework identifies the barriers to cryptographic agility and explains why algorithm transition is largely a software engineering problem.
  To address the limitations of current cryptographic APIs, we identify the principles necessary to design a cryptographically agile API. The design principles are derived from five fundamental architectural characteristics (Abstraction, Stability, Temporal Flexibility, Separation, and Extensibility). We also show how the design principles can be implemented using several examples of Protocol Buffers API design patterns. In particular, we present an intent vocabulary that is based on scopes which allows for decoupling key creation from algorithm identities. It also supports transparent substitutions of algorithms in the applicable scope. Cryptographic governance is enabled by an abstract policy API that does not prescribe the policy format. Keys are represented by stable identifiers and support key evolution operations (rotation, transformation, migration), facilitating migration between algorithms and providers while tracking both the original key identity and its evolution history. With this approach, updating cryptography becomes an operational process without the need to rewrite application code.

</details>


### [188] [Differentially Private Hierarchical Heavy Hitters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13563)
*Ari Biswas,Graham Cormode,Yaron Kanza,Divesh Srivastava,Zhengyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper investigates differentially private release of Hierarchical Heavy Hitters (HHH) in both streaming and non-streaming settings. Key findings: in the non-streaming case, relative error for residual count estimation is independent of hierarchy height and number of heavy hitters; in the streaming case, absolute error for frequency estimation is independent of available space, despite high global sensitivity of streaming approximation functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While HHH detection in data streams is well-studied, the problem of releasing HHH under differential privacy—when the underlying data is private—has not been explored. This gap motivates the paper.

Method: The authors analyze differential privacy mechanisms for HHH in two settings: (1) non-streaming, using residual count estimation with global sensitivity analysis, and (2) streaming, where they address the challenge that streaming approximation functions have high global sensitivity (linear in available space), contrasting with the low sensitivity of exact counting queries.

Result: Two main results: (1) In the non-streaming setting, the relative error in estimating residual count for any prefix is independent of both the hierarchy height and the number of heavy hitters. (2) In the streaming setting, the absolute error for frequency estimation is independent of the available space, despite streaming approximation functions having global sensitivity linear in space.

Conclusion: The paper establishes the first differentially private mechanisms for HHH release with provable error bounds that exhibit counterintuitive independence properties, opening the study of private hierarchical heavy hitter problems in both batch and streaming contexts.

Abstract: The task of finding _Hierarchical_ Heavy Hitters (HHH) was introduced by Cormode et al. [VLDB 2003] as a generalisation of the heavy hitter problem. While finding HHH in data streams has been studied extensively, the question of releasing HHH when the underlying data is private remains unexplored. In this paper, we study differentially private HHH release in both the streaming and non-streaming setting. In the non-streaming setting, we show the surprising result that the relative error in estimating the residual count for any prefix is independent of the height of the hierarchy and the number of heavy hitters in the stream. Meanwhile, in the streaming setting, although the exact version of HHH has low global sensitivity (as counting queries are 1-sensitive), the approximation functions due to streaming have high global sensitivity, linear in the available space. Despite this obstacle, we show that the absolute error for estimating frequencies in the steaming setting is independent of the available space.

</details>


### [189] [Beyond the IT Checklist: Engineering a Reasonable Standard of Care for Cyber Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13612)
*Matthew E. Jablonski,Linton Wells,Kathryn B. Laskey,F. Brett Berlin*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: U.S. critical infrastructure cyber policy over-emphasizes administrative compliance and IT-focused security controls, creating dangerous gaps for cyber-physical systems where digital failures cause physical harm. The paper maps 292 policy documents (2000-2025) against the NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2 resilience lifecycle, identifies three systemic disconnects, and proposes a modernized standard of care based on hazard-specific traceability, assurance cases, and cyber resiliency engineering paired with economic incentives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current U.S. cyber policy treats documentation of controls and incident reports as proxies for safety, an approach that may suffice for pure IT systems but is dangerously inadequate for cyber-physical systems (CPS) where digital failures can produce kinetic harm — injury, death, or physical destruction. The authors seek to empirically diagnose the structural misalignment between existing policy obligations and the engineering demands of cyber-resilient critical infrastructure.

Method: The authors constructed a corpus of 292 critical infrastructure policy documents spanning 2000–2025. They coded these documents against the four phases of the NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2 resilience lifecycle (Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt) to map how "reasonable care" obligations are distributed across the lifecycle. This mapping enabled the identification of gaps and over-concentrations in policy coverage.

Result: Three major disconnects emerged: (1) miscalibrated delegated standards — IT-focused control catalogs (e.g., NIST SP 800-53) are referenced for Withstand and Recover phases but poorly align with physics-based hazards; (2) recovery is defined as notification/incident reporting rather than engineered navigation back to safe operating states; (3) uneven adaptation requirements across sectors, with some critical infrastructure sectors lacking meaningful post-incident learning obligations. Obligations are heavily concentrated in the Anticipate phase, emphasizing administrative compliance over operational resilience engineering.

Conclusion: A modernized standard of care must be anchored in hazard-specific traceability, structured assurance cases, and cyber resiliency engineering. Federal policy should pair these engineering obligations with targeted economic incentives so that resilient architectures become a viable business decision rather than an unfunded mandate.

Abstract: Current U.S. cyber policy, centered on security, often treats documentation of controls and incident reports as a proxy for safety in the built environment. This paper argues that such an approach is inadequate for cyber-physical systems, where digital failures can produce kinetic harm. We construct and code a corpus of critical infrastructure policy documents (N=292, 2000-2025) to examine how "reasonable care" is operationalized across the NIST SP 800-160 Vol.~2 resilience lifecycle. The resulting maps show that obligations are concentrated in the Anticipate phase and emphasize administrative compliance, while Withstand and Recover phases rely heavily on delegated references to IT-focused control catalogs that are poorly aligned with physics-based hazards. We identify three major disconnects: miscalibrated delegated standards, recovery defined as notification rather than engineered navigation, and uneven adaptation requirements across sectors. We then propose a modernized standard of care anchored in hazard-specific traceability, structured assurance cases, and cyber resiliency engineering. Finally, we recommend that federal policy pair these engineering obligations with targeted incentives so that resilient architectures for critical infrastructure become a viable business decision rather than an unfunded expectation.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [190] [SAIGuard: Communication-State Simulation for Proactive Defense of LLM Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12474)
*Ruxue Shi,Yili Wang,Mengnan Du,Qinggang Zhang,Rui Miao,Yixin Liu,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: SAIGuard is a proactive defense framework for LLM-based multi-agent systems that intercepts and sanitizes malicious messages before they propagate, using communication-state simulation and reconstruction-deviation detection, outperforming reactive defenses in reducing attack success rates while preserving collaborative utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems are vulnerable to cascading security failures because attacks can spread through inter-agent communication. Existing defenses are reactive (post-execution detection and isolation), which risks irreversible damage and degrades collaborative performance. A proactive, pre-propagation defense is needed.

Method: SAIGuard performs communication-state simulation over the MAS interaction graph to estimate the impact of incoming messages on both local agent states and the global MAS state. It detects risky messages by measuring reconstruction deviations from benign communication patterns. Instead of isolating compromised agents, it sanitizes or regenerates suspicious messages before they enter the system.

Result: Experiments across diverse topologies and attack scenarios show that SAIGuard reduces attack success rates while maintaining MAS utility, outperforming reactive defense baselines.

Conclusion: Proactive, simulation-based interception with message sanitization (rather than agent isolation) is a more effective security paradigm for LLM-based multi-agent systems, offering robust protection without sacrificing collaborative utility.

Abstract: LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) solve complex tasks through inter-agent collaboration, but their communication-driven nature also allows security risks to spread across agents and trigger system-wide failures. Existing MAS defenses mainly follow a reactive paradigm after execution by detecting and isolating harmful agents, which may cause irreversible damage and degrade collaborative utility. To address this, we propose a proactive defense framework for MAS security, namely a Simulation-aware Interception Guard (SAIGuard). SAIGuard performs communication-state simulation over the MAS interaction graph, estimates the impact of incoming messages on local agent states and the global MAS state, and detects risky messages via reconstruction deviations from benign communication patterns. Instead of isolating agents, SAIGuard sanitizes or regenerates suspicious messages before it propagation into system. Experiments across diverse topologies and attack scenarios show that SAIGuard reduces attack success rates while maintaining MAS utility, outperforming reactive defenses.

</details>


### [191] [Smarter Saboteurs, Better Fixers: Scaling & Security in Linear Multi-Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12709)
*Timothy McAllister,Sina Abdidizaji,Ivan Garibay,Ozlem Ozmen Garibay*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Larger LLMs are more vulnerable to malicious instructions in linear multi-agent workflows, but a lightweight terminal "Fixer" agent nearly eliminates the performance degradation, restoring control-level results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM-based multi-agent systems are deployed, understanding how adversarial compromise interacts with model scale is a critical safety concern. Prior work attributed brittleness to linear topologies, but this may conflate topology with lack of correction.

Method: Experiments across two open-weight model families at multiple scales on the HumanEval benchmark, testing linear multi-agent workflows with and without a terminal Fixer stage under adversarial prompt injection.

Result: A compliance-correction symmetry emerges: larger models (up to 27B) show a 53.7pp drop in performance under malicious instructions in uncorrected pipelines, but a Fixer stage reduces this to only 0.6pp, restoring statistical parity with control.

Conclusion: Strictly linear collaboration structures can be viable and resilient to adversaries when correction is included; previously observed brittleness in linear topologies may stem from lack of correction rather than topology itself.

Abstract: As LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) are deployed in the wild, the resilience of their collaboration structures against adversarial compromise becomes a critical safety concern. Attackers may leverage prompt-injection or jailbreaking to sabotage individual agents within MAS workflows, but the interaction between model scaling and system-level resilience remains poorly understood. This paper investigates how model scale affects the security of linear multi-agent workflows. Our experiments across scales of two open-weight model families on the HumanEval benchmark reveal a compliance-correction symmetry: larger models are far more likely to faithfully execute malicious instructions, with the control-to-malicious performance drop reaching 53.7pp at 27B in uncorrected pipelines. However, appending a lightweight terminal Fixer stage collapses this to 0.6pp and restores statistical parity with control-level performance, demonstrating that strictly linear collaboration structures can be viable and resilient to adversaries at this scale, and suggesting that the brittleness previously attributed to linear topology may stem from a lack of correction.

</details>


### [192] [The Internet of Agentic AI: Communication, Coordination, and Collective Intelligence at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12835)
*Quanyan Zhu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper proposes the Internet of Agentic AI (IoAI), an open ecosystem enabling heterogeneous autonomous agents to discover, communicate, and coordinate across diverse environments. It synthesizes foundations from multi-agent systems, networking, game theory, and security to define the architectures, protocols, and trust mechanisms needed for scalable agent ecosystems, with illustrative case studies in manufacturing and operational coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The shift from isolated AI model inference to distributed, interactive agent systems demands a unifying framework. Existing work in agentic AI, multi-agent systems, and networking remains fragmented. The authors aim to provide a cohesive vision and architectural blueprint for large-scale, interoperable agent ecosystems spanning cloud, edge, devices, and cyber-physical domains.

Method: The paper takes a synthesis and conceptual design approach, integrating foundations from single-agent agentic AI, multi-agent systems, distributed computing, communication networks, game theory, and security engineering. From this synthesis, it characterizes deployment models, workflow lifecycles, communication protocols, interoperability layers, resource management, and trust architectures, validated through case studies in adaptive manufacturing and distributed operational coordination.

Result: The resulting IoAI framework identifies key architectural components and mechanisms for scalable agent ecosystems. It highlights central research challenges including controlled emergence, semantic interoperability, secure identity, incentive-compatible coordination, resource-aware orchestration, and governance for large-scale autonomous agent networks.

Conclusion: Realizing the IoAI vision requires addressing foundational challenges across semantic interoperability, trust, incentives, and governance. The framework provides a roadmap for building open, scalable, and secure ecosystems where heterogeneous autonomous agents can collaborate effectively across diverse deployment contexts.

Abstract: The rapid emergence of autonomous AI agents is transforming artificial intelligence from isolated model inference into distributed systems of reasoning, communication, and action. This paper develops the vision of the Internet of Agentic AI (IoAI): an open ecosystem in which heterogeneous agents discover one another, negotiate responsibilities, exchange context, invoke tools, and execute workflows across cloud, edge, device, organizational, and cyber-physical environments. We synthesize foundations from single-agent agentic AI, multi-agent systems, distributed computing, communication networks, game theory, and security engineering to characterize the architectures and mechanisms required for scalable agent ecosystems. The paper examines agent deployment models, workflow lifecycles, communication protocols, interoperability layers, resource-management challenges, and trust architectures, with case studies in adaptive manufacturing and distributed operational coordination. The resulting framework highlights the central research challenges of controlled emergence, semantic interoperability, secure identity, incentive-compatible coordination, resource-aware orchestration, and governance for large-scale networks of autonomous agents.

</details>


### [193] [Effects of Social Interactions in Self-Organising Railway Traffic Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13068)
*Fabio Oddi,Federico Naldini,Leo D'Amato,Grégory Marlière,Paola Pellegrini,Vito Trianni*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper investigates the predictive neighbourhood horizon — a key structural parameter in self-organised railway traffic management — showing empirically that short horizons suffice for effective decentralized coordination, while longer horizons degrade computational responsiveness without improving global schedule optimality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling traffic management to complex real-world railway networks using self-organised, decentralized approaches raises a critical design question: how far ahead should each train predict and negotiate? The horizon parameter defines the local interaction topology and directly governs the trade-off between local computational tractability and global schedule coherence.

Method: A closed-loop simulation framework is employed, where trains predict their neighbourhood over a variable horizon, detect future conflicts, generate traffic plan hypotheses, and reach consensus via distributed negotiation. The horizon is systematically varied to evaluate its impact on the full decentralized coordination pipeline — from conflict detection through to distributed schedule consensus.

Result: Empirical results run counter to intuition: short time horizons are sufficient for maintaining schedule feasibility and coherence in safety-critical settings, while longer horizons degrade local tractability and computational responsiveness without delivering any improvement in global schedule optimality.

Conclusion: The predictive neighbourhood horizon exhibits a clear asymmetric trade-off: increasing it beyond a modest threshold harms responsiveness with no corresponding benefit, indicating that decentralized railway traffic management systems can operate effectively with limited look-ahead, simplifying both the interaction graph and the consensus problem.

Abstract: Recent research is exploring self-organised traffic management as a solution for scaling to complex real-world networks. In such a system, trains predict their neighbourhood, produce traffic plan hypotheses, and agree via consensus with neighbours on a future traffic plan to be implemented. This paper investigates a structural parameter within this pipeline: the predictive neighbourhood horizon. The horizon is used by trains to identify future potential conflicts with neighbours, and to establish the local interaction topology, that is, the subset of trains to negotiate with. As the primary design variable, the horizon directly determines the size and density of the social interaction graph, whereas its impact on the complexity of local sub-problems and the distributed consensus dynamics represents a trade-off to be explored. Through a closed-loop simulation framework the study evaluates how variations of the horizon impact the overall decentralised coordination process, from initial conflict detection to distributed schedule consensus. The analysis focuses on investigating the potential trade-off introduced by the horizon choice: balancing local tractability and computational responsiveness with the need for global schedule coherence and feasibility in safety-critical environments. Contrary to intuition, our empirical results indicate that the short time horizons suffice, while long values compromise local tractability and computational responsiveness with no gain in global schedule optimality.

</details>


### [194] [$α$-fair heterogeneous agent reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13076)
*Yao-hua Franck Xu,Tayeb Lemlouma,Jean-Marie Bonnin,Arnaud Braud*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework integrating α-fairness with Heterogeneous-Agent Trust Region Learning (HATRL) to address inequitable reward distribution in cooperative multi-agent systems. By introducing a fair advantage function that dynamically weights agent utilities, the global objective smoothly transitions between utilitarian efficiency and α-fairness welfare. Two practical algorithms, α-fair HATRPO and α-fair HAPPO, are developed and shown to outperform standard HATRL methods in sequential social dilemmas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cooperative multi-agent systems typically optimize for utilitarian efficiency, ignoring reward distribution and producing inequitable leader-follower dynamics. Existing fairness-based methods either break the stationarity of Markov Games or lack rigorous theoretical guarantees regarding monotonic improvement and Nash Equilibrium convergence. This creates a gap between fair objective design and theoretically sound learning frameworks.

Method: The authors propose bridging α-fairness with Heterogeneous-Agent Trust Region Learning (HATRL). The core innovation is a fair advantage function that dynamically weights each agent's utility based on their expected returns. This allows the global objective to interpolate from purely utilitarian efficiency to α-fairness welfare controlled by the α parameter. Two practical algorithms are introduced: α-fair HATRPO and α-fair HAPPO, both preserving HATRL's theoretical guarantees of monotonic improvement and convergence to Nash Equilibria.

Result: Experiments in sequential social dilemmas (CleanUp and CommonHarvest) demonstrate that α-fair HATRPO and α-fair HAPPO outperform standard HATRL algorithms from a utilitarian perspective while simultaneously achieving more socially equitable outcomes.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully unifies fairness objectives with theoretically grounded multi-agent trust-region learning. The α-fairness parameter provides a principled mechanism to balance efficiency and equity, and the resulting algorithms deliver both higher utilitarian performance and better social welfare in cooperative multi-agent settings.

Abstract: Cooperation in multi-agent systems is typically optimized through utilitarian objectives that maximize overall efficiency but fail to account for reward distribution, often resulting in inequitable "leader-follower" dynamics. While fairness-based approaches encourage pro-social behaviors where every agent benefits from cooperation, many current algorithms - including those utilizing reward shaping - break the stationarity of Markov Games or lack rigorous theoretical guarantees. This creates a critical gap between fair objective methods and theoretically safe learning frameworks. We propose a novel framework that bridges $α$-fairness with Heterogeneous-Agent Trust Region Learning (HATRL), ensuring monotonic improvement and convergence toward Nash Equilibria. Our approach leverages a fair advantage function that dynamically weights agent utilities based on their expected returns, allowing the global objective to transition from purely utilitarian efficiency to $α$-fairness welfare based on the parameter $α$. We introduce two practical algorithms, $α$-fair HATRPO and $α$-fair HAPPO, and demonstrate through experiments in sequential social dilemmas like CleanUp and CommonHarvest that they perform better than HATRL's algorithms from a utilitarian point of view while achieving socially higher outcomes.

</details>


### [195] [See What I See, Know What I Think: Dense Latent Communication Across Heterogeneous Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13594)
*Siyi Chen,Xiaoyan Zhang,Meng Wu,Jonathan Tremblay,Valts Blukis,Stan Birchfield,Rene Vidal,Alvaro Velasquez,Sijia Liu,Qing Qu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Proposes a dense alignment method for heterogeneous KV-cache communication between different LLMs, using a lightweight cross-model cache transformation with two-phase training (reconstruction then generation). Achieves strong performance in both context-aware and context-unaware transfer, outperforming text communication at 2–3× lower compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems rely on lossy, expensive text-based communication (decode/re-encode). While KV-cache communication is promising, existing work is mostly homogeneous (same model) or restrictive in heterogeneous settings (shared input, steering-only). The core unsolved challenge is cross-model latent alignment for genuine "mind reading" — transferring both what an agent sees and how it thinks.

Method: Information-structure analysis revealing a duality: context-aware transfer relies on sparse reasoning signals, while context-unaware transfer demands dense contextual knowledge preservation. The proposed solution uses (1) a lightweight cross-model cache transformation module mapping KV-caches between heterogeneous models, and (2) a two-phase training protocol: reconstruction training followed by generation training.

Result: Evaluated across all six transfer directions among Qwen3-4B, 8B, and 14B on six in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks. Outperforms prior heterogeneous baselines, matches or exceeds text communication in context-aware settings at ~2–3× lower compute, and uniquely remains effective in context-unaware transfer where prior methods collapse.

Conclusion: Dense alignment via lightweight cache transformation and two-phase training enables effective heterogeneous KV-cache communication across diverse model pairs, supporting both context-aware and context-unaware transfer. This advances multi-agent efficiency by substantially reducing communication costs compared to text-based methods.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems communicate mostly through text, paying a lossy and expensive decode and re-encode cost. KV-cache communication is a promising alternative, yet most prior work is homogeneous, using duplicate copies of the same model, and avoids the central challenge of cross-model latent alignment; existing heterogeneous methods are also restrictive, typically assuming shared input and using transferred caches mainly for steering. We study a more fundamental question: can heterogeneous agents be aligned well enough to perform real "mind reading" and transfer both what one agent sees and how it thinks? Our information-structure analysis reveals a duality: context-aware transfer is driven by sparse reasoning signals, while context-unaware transfer, where the receiver sees no input, requires dense contextual knowledge preservation. Motivated by this, we propose dense alignment for heterogeneous KV-cache communication via a lightweight cross-model cache transformation and two-phase training: reconstruction followed by generation. Across all six directions of {Qwen3-4B, 8B, 14B} and six in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, our method outperforms prior heterogeneous baselines, matches or exceeds text communication in context-aware settings at roughly 2 to 3 times lower compute, and remains effective in context-unaware transfer where prior methods collapse.

</details>


### [196] [Tuning Agent-Based Predator-Prey Models Toward Lotka-Volterra Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13639)
*Corinna Mandl,Siddharth Chaturvedi,Marcel van Gerven*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of tuning parameters in large-scale agent-based models (ABMs) to avoid pathological behaviors like population collapse, using a predator-prey system with neural network-controlled agents. The authors employ a feature-based loss function to optimize environmental and demographic parameters, aiming to reproduce classical Lotka-Volterra dynamics, and implement the model in ABMax, a JAX-based framework for efficient batched simulation on hardware accelerators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale agent-based models contain many local rules and parameters, making them highly sensitive to small changes that can trigger runaway behavior, population collapse, or artificial saturation. The central motivation is to develop a principled method for tuning these parameters so that emergent population dynamics match desired ecological patterns — specifically, classical Lotka-Volterra cycles — rather than devolving into unrealistic or pathological regimes.

Method: The authors construct a continuous predator-prey ABM where sheep and wolves are active agents equipped with local sensing, internal energy reserves, and recurrent neural network (RNN) controllers. They define a feature-based loss function that rewards sustained oscillations, appropriate phase lag, bounded populations, and long-term persistence. This loss is used to optimize environmental and demographic parameters, first for agents with random (fixed) controllers, and subsequently for agents with evolved neural network controllers in a more naturalistic setting. The simulation framework, ABMax, is built on JAX to leverage batched computation on hardware accelerators (GPUs/TPUs).

Result: The paper demonstrates that feature-based optimization can successfully tune ABM parameters to yield stable Lotka-Volterra-like population cycles. The ABMax framework enables efficient large-scale simulation, and the two-stage approach — optimizing first with random controllers, then with evolved controllers — progressively yields more naturalistic and robust population dynamics while avoiding collapse, runaway growth, or boundary saturation.

Conclusion: Parameter tuning via feature-based loss functions is an effective strategy for steering complex agent-based models toward desired emergent dynamics. The combination of ABMax (JAX-based batched simulation) with evolutionary optimization of both environmental parameters and neural network controllers offers a scalable methodology for calibrating large-scale ABMs to exhibit realistic, sustained ecological cycles.

Abstract: Recent growth in compute power has made it increasingly feasible to use large-scale agent-based models to simulate complex adaptive systems. A central difficulty is that such models contain many local rules and parameters, where small changes can lead to runaway behaviour, population collapse, or saturation at artificial bounds. We study this problem in a continuous predator-prey system where sheep and wolves are active agents with local sensing, internal energy, and recurrent neural network-based controllers. We ask whether environmental and demographic parameters can be tuned so that the resulting population dynamics resemble classical Lotka-Volterra cycles. We optimise these parameters with a feature-based loss that rewards sustained oscillations, phase lag, bounded populations, and long-term persistence, first for random controllers and then for evolved controllers in a more naturalistic setting. The model is implemented in ABMax, a JAX-based agent-based modelling framework that enables efficient batched simulation on hardware accelerators.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [197] [Quickest Detection of Hallucination Onset: Delay Bounds and Learned CUSUM Statistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12476)
*Igor Itkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hallucination detection is reframed from static token classification to streaming quickest change detection. A Markov model validates the setup; a CUSUM-like recurrent labeler detects onsets in 11–13 tokens vs. 31 for a linear baseline. An information-rate theorem shows the learned score captures only ~1/4.5 of available divergence, explaining the residual gap from the theoretical lower bound (~1.3 tokens).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard evaluation of token-level hallucination detectors via aggregate AUC conceals temporal detection delay, which is the operationally relevant metric for streaming monitors. The authors aim to make delay measurable and to understand the gap between practical detectors and theoretical optimality.

Method: Hallucination onset detection is cast as a quickest change detection (QCD) problem. A first-order Markov model is fitted to RAGTruth to validate that hallucinated/f faithful states follow a change-point structure, yielding Lorden's lower bound on detection delay. A causal recurrent labeler is then interpreted as a CUSUM procedure with a learned increment. Detection delay is compared against a per-token linear baseline. A controlled decomposition separates contributions of per-token scoring quality from temporal accumulation. Finally, a Donsker–Varadhan-type information-rate theorem quantifies how much of the feature-level divergence the learned score captures.

Result: Lorden's lower bound gives ~1.3 tokens delay at a 0.01 false-alarm rate. The causal recurrent labeler detects in 11–13 tokens versus 31 for the linear baseline. Most improvement stems from better per-token scoring, not temporal smoothing. The learned score realizes only ~1/4.5 of the divergence carried by the features; the remaining gap is split between an irreducible recalibration-resistant deficit and a finite-horizon effect.

Conclusion: Classification metrics (AUC) hide the delay structure critical for streaming hallucination monitors. Sequential analysis exposes it and reveals a large optimality gap attributable to imperfect score learning. Future work may focus on improving score-level divergence capture or incorporating stronger temporal models to approach the theoretical bound.

Abstract: Token-level hallucination detectors are evaluated as classifiers, by AUC over all tokens, yet a streaming monitor is judged by its reaction time: the number of tokens that pass between the onset of a hallucination and the alarm. We formulate hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection problem. A first-order Markov model of the latent faithful/hallucinated state, validated on RAGTruth, places the task inside classical change-point theory and yields Lorden's lower bound on detection delay: about 1.3 tokens at a false-alarm rate of 0.01. We then show that a causal recurrent labeler acts as a CUSUM with a learned increment; at a matched false-alarm rate it detects in 11-13 tokens, against 31 for a linear per-token baseline, and a controlled decomposition attributes most of this advantage to a better per-token score rather than to temporal accumulation. An information-rate optimality theorem of Donsker-Varadhan type explains the remaining order-of-magnitude gap: the learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of the divergence the features carry, a deficit that recalibration cannot remove, with the remainder a finite-horizon effect. Classification metrics conceal this delay structure; sequential analysis makes it measurable

</details>


### [198] [Boltzmann Attention: Learnable Ising Couplings for Cooperative Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12478)
*Gilhan Kim,Daniel K. Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Boltzmann attention introduces learnable pairwise couplings into attention mechanisms via an Ising model formulation, enabling explicit modeling of inter-position interactions beyond standard softmax attention, with consistent improvements on language modeling and bracket matching tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard attention computes relevance through individual query-key similarities with softmax normalization; while softmax introduces competition, it lacks explicit parameterization of learnable interactions between attention decisions — i.e., cooperative or antagonistic co-attention structure. This limits the model's capacity to represent dependencies among attended positions.

Method: Boltzmann attention formulates attention as an energy-based model governed by an interacting Ising model. Beyond the usual data-dependent local fields (standard attention logits), the method augments the energy function with learnable pairwise couplings that capture inter-position correlations. Attention patterns are sampled from the resulting Boltzmann distribution. Diabatic quantum annealing is also explored as a practical sampling alternative to exact Boltzmann computation.

Result: Experiments on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching show Boltzmann attention consistently outperforms standard softmax attention within a standard Transformer architecture, with gains increasing at longer sequence lengths. A four-way ablation confirms improvements stem specifically from the learnable pairwise couplings. Diabatic quantum annealing maintains competitive performance while offering a practical training approach.

Conclusion: Explicit inter-position interactions via pairwise couplings provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling. The Ising formulation also opens a pathway toward quantum-computing-based sampling strategies for attention computation.

Abstract: Attention mechanisms are central to modern sequence models, yet standard attention computes relevance primarily through individual query--key similarities. Although softmax normalization introduces competition among positions, a standard attention layer does not explicitly parameterize learnable interactions between attention decisions. This limits its ability to directly model cooperative or antagonistic co-attention structure within the attention mechanism itself. We propose Boltzmann attention, an energy-based generalization in which attention patterns are governed by an interacting Ising model. The method augments the usual data-dependent local fields with learnable pairwise couplings, allowing the model to represent inter-position correlations beyond those captured by softmax or sigmoid attention. Experiments on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching show that Boltzmann attention consistently improves over standard softmax attention within a standard Transformer architecture, with the advantage becoming more pronounced as sequence length increases. A four-way ablation confirms that the improvement arises from the learnable pairwise couplings. These results suggest that explicit inter-position interactions provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling. Moreover, the Ising formulation opens a natural path toward quantum-computing-based sampling strategies: we demonstrate that diabatic quantum annealing provides a practical training method while maintaining competitive performance with exact Boltzmann computation.

</details>


### [199] [ReCal: Reward Calibration for RL-based LLM Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12479)
*Qihang Yu,Hanwen Tong,Zhengqi Zhang,Bo Zheng,Feng Wei,Shengyu Zhang,Zemin Liu,Fei Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReCal is a reward calibration framework for RL-based LLM routing that introduces hierarchical reward decomposition with component-wise advantage estimation and distribution-aware optimization to address ambiguous credit assignment and optimization bias from heterogeneous task rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL-based LLM routing methods aggregate multiple objectives (correctness, format) into a single scalar reward, causing ambiguous credit assignment and conflicting optimization signals. Additionally, reward variability across instances biases optimization toward trivial samples over informative ones.

Method: ReCal proposes (1) hierarchical reward decomposition with component-wise advantage estimation to separate and properly attribute credit for different reward components, and (2) a distribution-aware optimization strategy using variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization to calibrate optimization variability across heterogeneous instances.

Result: Experiments on seven datasets show that ReCal consistently improves both routing performance and training stability compared to baselines.

Conclusion: ReCal effectively addresses the core challenges of reward ambiguity and optimization bias in RL-based LLM routing, demonstrating that proper reward calibration is critical for robust multi-objective routing policy learning.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) routing has emerged as an effective paradigm for leveraging the complementary strengths of multiple LLMs through dynamic model and reasoning-strategy selection. Recent reinforcement learning (RL)-based routing methods further improve routing quality by optimizing routing policies from interaction feedback. However, they still struggle to provide informative and comparable learning signals under heterogeneous tasks with varying difficulty. In practice, multiple objectives (e.g., correctness, format behavior) are aggregated into a single scalar reward, leading to ambiguous credit assignment and conflicting optimization signals. Moreover, reward signals exhibit significant variability across instances, where some instances produce higher or more variable rewards, introducing optimization bias that favors trivial samples over informative ones. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{ReCal}, a \textbf{\underline{Re}}ward \textbf{\underline{Cal}}ibration framework for RL-based LLM routing. We first introduce a hierarchical reward decomposition mechanism with component-wise advantage estimation. We further propose a distribution-aware optimization strategy that calibrates optimization variability through variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization. Experiments on seven datasets demonstrate that ReCal consistently improves routing performance, and training stability over baselines. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ReCal.

</details>


### [200] [Representing Time Series as Structured Programs for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12481)
*Jaeho Kim,Changhun Oh,Seokhyun Lee,Irina Rish,Changhee Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: T2SP converts time series into structured symbolic programs (trends, periods, events) that align with LLMs' textual/code modalities, enabling off-the-shelf LLMs to reason about time series without fine-tuning, yielding consistent improvements on editing, captioning, and QA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are powerful reasoners but natively operate on text. Representing time series as raw numerical sequences creates a modality mismatch that degrades performance on long sequences and incurs heavy computation. The authors seek a representation that shifts temporal structure extraction away from the LLM and into the representation itself.

Method: T2SP is a deterministic, training-free method that decomposes a time series into trends, periods, and salient events, then expresses them in a structured symbolic program format. This format is inherently aligned with the textual and code-like modalities LLMs are trained on, allowing off-the-shelf models to apply existing reasoning capabilities without fine-tuning.

Result: Evaluated across three reasoning tasks — editing, captioning, and question answering — T2SP consistently improved performance, reduced reasoning time, and lowered failure rates compared to raw-string serialization baselines.

Conclusion: T2SP provides an effective interface between time series and LLMs by embedding temporal structure into the representation itself, enabling better reasoning with less computational overhead.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong reasoning and instruction-following capabilities, making them potentially powerful tools for time-series analysis. However, time series lie outside their native textual modality, raising a fundamental question: how should time series be represented so that LLMs can reason about them effectively? Existing work typically serializes raw numerical sequences or fine-tunes pre-trained LLMs on time-series data.
