Motivation: Long-horizon tool-use RL suffers from sparse trajectory-level rewards. Self-distillation seems promising for denser signals but can silently destroy tool-use capabilities by rehearsing teacher actions without knowing which actions the verifier actually rewards, thus amplifying harmful shortcuts alongside useful skills.

Method: SGCD uses four components: (1) dynamic sampling to produce successful and failed sibling rollouts, (2) an external LLM that contrasts siblings to generate training-only stepwise credit references, (3) dense teacher/student divergence that drives credit reassignment, and (4) bounded detached credit weights that reshape GRPO token advantages. The deployed student never sees the external LLM, siblings, or oracle.

Result: On AppWorld, SGCD improves TGC from 42.9→45.6 (test_normal) and 24.7→27.0 (test_challenge). On τ³-airline, pass@1 improves from 0.583→0.602, outperforming matched GRPO baselines.

Conclusion: Token-level self-distillation in tool-use RL is dangerous because it lacks credit awareness. SGCD solves this by repurposing distillation for credit assignment via sibling contrast, yielding consistent gains without requiring privileged information at deployment.

Abstract: Long-horizon tool-use reinforcement learning can learn from outcome verification, but its
  trajectory-level advantage is broadcast across many reasoning, API, and answer tokens.
  Self-distillation promises a denser signal by reusing a policy's own rollouts or a privileged
  teacher. We show, however, that direct token-level self-distillation can silently destroy tool use:
  it rehearses teacher behavior without knowing which actions the verifier rewards, so useful skills
  and harmful shortcuts are amplified together. We introduce Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation
  (SGCD), which uses distillation for credit assignment rather than as a competing actor loss.
  Dynamic sampling produces mixed successful and failed sibling rollouts; an external LLM summarizes
  their contrast into a training-only stepwise credit reference; dense teacher/student divergence
  drives credit reassignment; and bounded detached credit weights reshape GRPO token advantages. The
  deployed student sees no external LLM, sibling evidence, or oracle. Across AppWorld and
  $τ^3$-airline, SGCD improves over matched GRPO comparators: AppWorld TGC $42.9 \to 45.6$ on
  test_normal and $24.7 \to 27.0$ on test_challenge, and $τ^3$-airline pass@1 $0.583 \to 0.602$.

</details>


### [224] [PolicyGuard: Towards Test-time and Step-level Adversary Defense for Reinforcement Learning Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12896)
*Junfeng Guo Heng Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolicyGuard is a test-time, step-level backdoor defense for RL agents that uses Gaussian Process posterior variance and pseudo-trajectory adaptation to detect malicious actions at individual time steps, achieving state-of-the-art AUROC across diverse attack types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As RL agents are increasingly deployed in real-world applications, they are vulnerable to backdoor attacks where agents behave normally until a trigger activates malicious behavior. Existing defenses are limited: they require internal parameter access, operate only at coarse model/trajectory levels, or are restricted to specific attack types. A more general, fine-grained, test-time defense is needed.

Method: PolicyGuard operates at test time without requiring access to the agent's internal parameters. It uses Gaussian Process (GP) posterior variance to quantify predictive uncertainty at each time step, enabling step-level anomaly detection. To make GP uncertainty computation feasible per step, it adapts pseudo trajectories. The approach is theoretically grounded to explain why GP posterior variance is effective for detecting backdoor-triggered actions.

Result: On seven RL games, PolicyGuard achieves state-of-the-art detection performance: average AUROC of 0.856 against perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 against adversary-agent attacks.

Conclusion: PolicyGuard provides an effective, general-purpose, test-time backdoor defense for RL agents with step-level granularity, supported by both theoretical justification and strong empirical results across diverse environments and attack types.

Abstract: While real-world applications of reinforcement learning (RL) are becoming increasingly popular, the security of RL systems deserve more attention and exploration. In particular, recent work has revealed that RL agents are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where a victim agent behaves normally under standard conditions but executes malicious actions when a specific trigger is activated. Existing backdoor defenses for RL either require access to the agent's internal parameters, operate only at the model or trajectory level, or are limited to specific attack types. To ensure the security of RL agents, we propose \texttt{PolicyGuard}, a \textit{test-time step-level} backdoor defense which leverages Gaussian Process (GP) posterior variance and adapts pseudo trajectories to enable uncertainty computation for individual time step. Besides, we also provide theoretical foundations to explain the efficacy of GP posterior variance. Extensive experiments across seven RL games demonstrate that PolicyGuard achieves state-of-the-art detection performance in most cases, with average AUROC of 0.856 for perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 for adversary-agent attacks.

</details>


### [225] [The Metric Picks the Winner: Evaluation Choice Flips Model Rankings for Drug-Response Prediction in Unseen Chemistry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12639)
*Dhruv Agarwal,Riya Bisht*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses drug-response prediction with rigorous compound-holdout splits, demonstrating that the choice of evaluation metric determines which model "wins." Under a per-gene proxy metric, a simple linear regression on Morgan fingerprints outperforms deep models and retrieval baselines. However, under the contest's true active-set wMSE metric, deep models—particularly the authors' fusion decoder—significantly beat both simple baselines and the linear fingerprint approach. This reveals a metric-calibration effect previously shown only in genetic perturbation contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field of transcriptomic drug-response prediction consistently fails to beat trivial baselines (e.g., untreated control, mean response) when test compounds are genuinely novel in chemistry. The authors aim to understand why model rankings shift so dramatically depending on evaluation metrics and to build models that genuinely improve over simple baselines under the contest's intended metric.

Method: A staged approach on THP-1 DRUG-seq data (14,026 training compounds) with Bemis-Murcko scaffold split: (1) dumb baselines (untreated control, mean training-compound response); (2) non-parametric Tanimoto-weighted nearest-neighbor retrieval; (3) a fusion decoder that combines a frozen chemistry embedding with retrieval-support features to predict residuals over the mean, incorporating an uncertainty head and gene programs. Models are evaluated under two contrasting metrics: an inverse-variance per-gene proxy and the official VCPI contest active-set weighted MSE (wMSE).

Result: Under the per-gene proxy metric, regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints outperforms deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa — a "simple baselines win" result. Under the contest's true active-set wMSE metric, this reverses: deep models win, the fusion decoder significantly beats the linear fingerprint baseline (-0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 10⁻⁴), and the proxy's winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor. The metric-calibration effect is demonstrated for the first time on real held-out drug chemistry.

Conclusion: Metric choice fundamentally determines which model appears superior in drug-response prediction, extending the metric-calibration phenomenon from genetic perturbation to drug chemistry. The authors release a reproducible pipeline wired to the official VCPI scorer that produces valid submissions over the full 1064 × 12,995 grid.

Abstract: Predicting how a cell's transcriptome responds to a drug it has never seen is a core, hard problem in computational cell biology: recent benchmarks show complex models often fail to beat trivial baselines once test compounds are held out by chemistry. We study one cell line and assay, THP-1 cells profiled by DRUG-seq, scored by the active-compound weighted MSE(wMSE) of the VCPI prediction contest. We propose a staged approach: dumb baselines (untreated control and mean training-compound response) that the field keeps failing to beat; non-parametric retrieval (a Tanimoto-weighted average of a held-out compound's nearest training compounds); and a fusion stage combining a frozen chemistry embedding with retrieval-support features to predict the residual over the mean, with an uncertainty head and gene programs. On the released VCPI THP-1 drug-seq data (14,026 training compounds), under a Bemis-Murcko scaffold split, the model ranking inverts depending on the metric. Under an inverse-variance per-gene proxy, a regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints appears to win over the deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa -- the textbook "simple baselines win" result. But under the contest's true active-set metric (per-(gene, compound) Mejia weights, validated against the official scorer; mean baseline 0.535 vs the organizers' 0.507 reference), that reverses: the deep models win, our fusion decoder significantly beats the linear fingerprint baseline (-0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 10^-4), and the proxy's winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor. Picking the metric picks the winner -- to our knowledge the first demonstration on real held-out drug chemistry of the metric-calibration effect established largely on genetic perturbation. We release a reproducible pipeline
  wired to the official scorer that emits a valid submission over the real 1064 x 12,995 grid.

</details>


### [226] [Individual Control Barrier Functions-Guided Diffusion Model for Safe Offline Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12640)
*Qingyun Guo,Junyi Shi,Jianuo Huang,Tianyu Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a safe offline multi-agent RL algorithm combining diffusion models with neural control barrier functions for safety-constrained trajectory generation, with policy recovery via inverse dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL is suitable for safety-critical tasks, and diffusion models excel at modeling complex data distributions; however, existing diffusion-based offline RL methods focus only on single-agent settings, leaving multi-agent safety challenges unaddressed.

Method: A safe offline MARL algorithm that integrates neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model's trajectory generation process, with control policies recovered through inverse dynamics.

Result: Evaluated across diverse benchmarks, the algorithm demonstrates substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive reward performance.

Conclusion: Embedding control barrier functions into diffusion-based offline MARL effectively enhances safety without sacrificing reward performance, filling a gap in safe multi-agent offline RL.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning allows control policies to be learned directly from data without online interaction, making it suitable for safety-critical tasks. Recent studies have applied diffusion models to offline reinforcement learning to leverage their strong capacity for modeling complex data distributions. However, existing approaches primarily focus on single-agent settings, leaving the safety challenges in multi-agent environments largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a safe offline multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm that embeds neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model to enhance safety during trajectory generation, with control policies recovered through inverse dynamics. We evaluate our algorithm across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards.

</details>


### [227] [TEDD: Robust Detection of Unstable Temporal Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12643)
*Ricardo Ribeiro Pereira,Bruno Casal Laraña,Nádia Soares,Miguel Araújo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces TEDD, a technique for automatically detecting temporally changing features in datasets that could degrade ML model performance. TEDD uses a regression model to predict instance timestamps, then analyzes feature contributions to identify drifting features. It detects all basic change types in numerical and categorical features, handles multivariate drifts, provides comparable change magnitude scores, requires no parameter tuning, and scales well.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world temporal data often contains features whose distributions shift over time. Naively applying ML models to such unstable data leads to rapid performance degradation when deployment distributions diverge from training distributions. Practitioners need automated methods to identify which features are changing, enabling mitigation strategies (e.g., data transformations) and more robust, longer-lasting models.

Method: TEDD (Temporal Embedding Distribution Drift detection) leverages a regression model trained to predict each instance's timestamp. The key insight is that features contributing strongly to accurate timestamp prediction are likely undergoing temporal drift. By analyzing feature importance from this regression model, TEDD both identifies whether a dataset may produce unstable models (global assessment) and pinpoints which specific features exhibit temporal changes (per-feature detection), covering both univariate and multivariate drift patterns.

Result: Evaluated on both real and synthetic data against other methods, TEDD successfully detects all basic change patterns in numerical and categorical features, captures multivariate drifts, and produces comparable quantitative measures of per-feature change magnitude. It requires no hyperparameter tuning and demonstrates scalability in both feature count and instance count.

Conclusion: TEDD provides an effective, parameter-free, and scalable solution for temporal feature drift detection, enabling practitioners to proactively identify unstable features and deploy more robust ML models on real-world temporal data.

Abstract: When working with real-world temporal data, it is common to encounter features whose distribution is changing over time. The naive employment of Machine Learning models on this unstable data might lead to rapidly degrading performance, especially if the new distribution is much different from what was previously seen during training. In order to cope with this problem, it is critical to automatically identify features that are changing over time. With these features detected, data scientists and other practitioners will be able to mitigate the issue (for instance, by applying data transformations), deploying more robust models that retain high performance for longer periods of time. In this paper, we describe which temporal changes a feature should not suffer from, and propose TEDD, a technique to a) identify when a dataset might lead to an unstable Machine Learning model and b) automatically detect which features cause such lack of robustness. In order to achieve it, we leverage a regression model to highlight which features contribute to a good prediction of an instance's timestamp. We compare our approach to other methods in real and synthetic data, testing their detection capability on all simple change patterns. We show that our method: detects all types of basic changes, both for numerical and categorical features; can detect multivariate drifts; returns a comparable value measuring the amount of change of each feature; requires no parameter tuning; and is scalable both on number of features and instances of the dataset.

</details>


### [228] [Physics-Aware Auxiliary Losses Improve Out-of-Distribution Generalization of a GNN Synthesizability Filter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12651)
*Riya Bisht,Dhruv Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adding cheap physical priors (Bertz topological complexity and MMFF94 strain energy) as auxiliary losses to a GNN improves out-of-distribution generalization for molecular synthesizability prediction, but the effect is modest and only visible under multi-seed OOD evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing synthesizability filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely statistical and degrade when generative models propose molecules far from the training distribution. The authors investigate whether incorporating closed-form physical priors as auxiliary supervision can improve OOD generalization.

Method: A GINE backbone is trained with up to two auxiliary losses: (1) topological complexity regression supervised by the Bertz index, and (2) a strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. A 4-way ablation (baseline, +complexity, +strain, +both) is evaluated on a single-source OOD split (train: drug-like HIV+Tox21; test: COCONUT natural products), with 5 seeds and paired bootstrap confidence intervals. The corpus comprises 65,177 molecules labeled by SAScore thresholds.

Result: All three physics-aware variants yielded statistically significant but modest OOD improvement over baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774): +complexity Δ=+0.0060 (95% CI [+0.0023, +0.0102]), +strain Δ=+0.0032 ([+0.0008, +0.0052]), +both Δ=+0.0066 ([+0.0038, +0.0093]). The combination performed best. Variants were indistinguishable in-distribution. A single-seed version produced a qualitatively different, non-monotone story that did not survive multi-seed evaluation.

Conclusion: Cheap physical priors provide small but statistically significant OOD generalization gains for synthesizability prediction, with the combination of complexity and strain-energy auxiliaries being most effective. The authors emphasize methodological rigor: multi-seed evaluation with bootstrap CIs is essential, as single-seed experiments can yield misleading narratives.

Abstract: Machine-learning drug-discovery pipelines increasingly rely on generative models that propose molecules far from the data used to train downstream synthesizability filters. Existing filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely statistical and degrade in exactly this out-of-distribution (OOD) regime. We ask whether cheap, closed-form physical priors, used as auxiliary supervision on a graph neural network (GNN), improve OOD generalization. We add two auxiliary losses to a GINE backbone: a topological complexity regression supervised by the Bertz index, and a strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. On a 65,177-molecule corpus (HIV, Tox21, COCONUT) labeled by SAScore thresholds we reproduce a strong in-distribution baseline, then evaluate a 4-way ablation (baseline / +complexity / +strain / +both) on a single-source OOD split (train on drug-like HIV+Tox21, test on COCONUT natural products), repeated over 5 seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals. All three physics-aware variants give a small but statistically significant OOD improvement over the baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774): +complexity Delta = +0.0060 (95% CI [+0.0023, +0.0102]), +strain Delta = +0.0032 ([+0.0008, +0.0052]), +both Delta = +0.0066 ([+0.0038, +0.0093]); every interval excludes zero, and the combination is best. The variants are indistinguishable in-distribution, so the effect is visible only under OOD evaluation. We are explicit that the effects are modest, and we report a cautionary methodological finding: a single-seed version of this experiment produced a qualitatively different (non-monotone) story that did not survive multi-seed evaluation.

</details>


### [229] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Chemotherapy Pharmacokinetics: Benchmarking the Clinical Estimator and Exposing Parameter Identifiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12658)
*Riya Bisht,Dhruv Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A benchmark study showing that Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) match the standard clinical NLS baseline on linear pharmacokinetics while also recovering tissue concentrations, expose non-identifiability in a Michaelis-Menten extension where NLS silently fails, and absorb sparse tissue measurements to partially resolve identifiability — offering a unified framework that combines physics, data, and structural insight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In chemotherapy pharmacokinetics, plasma drug concentration is routinely measured, but tissue concentration — which governs tumour kill and off-target toxicity — is not directly observable. Standard clinical methods (nonlinear least-squares on a biexponential plasma solution) cannot infer tissue dynamics and fail entirely when the model departs from linearity (e.g., saturable elimination). The authors investigate whether PINNs can serve as a single, uniform approach that recovers tissue curves, handles nonlinear extensions, and exposes structural identifiability issues that classical estimators mask.

Method: The authors benchmark three approaches on two pharmacokinetic problems: (1) a standard nonlinear least-squares (NLS) fit to the analytical biexponential plasma solution (clinical baseline), (2) a physics-agnostic data-only MLP, and (3) a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) that incorporates the governing compartmental ODEs into the loss function. The first problem is a linear two-compartment model; the second is a Michaelis-Menten extension with saturable elimination, where no biexponential closed form exists. For the Michaelis-Menten case, they further test the PINN with two additional sparse tissue observations to probe identifiability recovery.

Result: On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; the PINN matches NLS performance within a small constant factor and simultaneously produces the tissue curve in a single training pass, while the data-only MLP fails on tissue by roughly 10×. On the Michaelis-Menten extension, NLS is mis-specified (no biexponential closed form exists) and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN instead converges to a basin where k₁₂ → 0, honestly revealing that the model is structurally non-identifiable from plasma data alone. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds, k₂₁ is recovered within 1% of truth, Vₘₐₓ and Kₘ within one standard deviation, and k₁₂ moves in the correct direction (0.02 → 0.82) though it remains ~2σ below truth — a recovery the NLS estimator cannot attempt at all.

Conclusion: The claim is not that PINNs outperform NLS, but that PINNs provide a uniform recipe that: (1) matches the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, (2) exposes structural identifiability issues hidden by the textbook estimator, and (3) naturally absorbs heterogeneous measurements (plasma, tissue) within a single loss function, enabling inference where classical closed-form methods are inapplicable.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are an attractive tool for partial-observation problems in biology, where the governing dynamics are known but some compartments cannot be measured. Chemotherapy pharmacokinetics (PK) is a clean instance: drug concentration in plasma is routinely measured, but concentration in tissue -- which determines tumour kill and off-target toxicity -- is not. We benchmark a PINN against the standard clinical baseline (nonlinear least-squares on the analytical biexponential plasma solution, hereafter NLS) and a physics-agnostic neural baseline (a data-only MLP) on two PK problems. On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; the PINN matches it to within a small constant factor while also producing the tissue curve in a single training pass, whereas the data-only MLP fails on tissue by roughly 10x. On a Michaelis-Menten extension (saturable elimination), the biexponential closed form no longer exists, so NLS is mis-specified and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN instead exposes a deeper fact: the Michaelis-Menten two-compartment model is non-identifiable from plasma alone, and the PINN reports this honestly by converging to a basin with k12 -> 0. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds the PINN recovers k21 to within 1% of truth and Vmax, Km to within one standard-deviation bar, while k12 moves in the correct direction (0.02 -> 0.82) but remains ~2 sigma below truth -- a recovery the closed-form NLS estimator cannot attempt at all, because its biexponential ansatz describes only plasma. Our claim is not that PINNs beat NLS. It is that PINNs offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, exposes structural identifiability that
  the textbook estimator hides, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss.

</details>


### [230] [A Zero-shot Generalized Graph Anomaly Detection Framework via Node Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12673)
*Phan Nguyen,Dat Cao,Hien Chu,Khue Hoang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AlignGAD is a zero-shot cross-domain graph anomaly detection framework that aligns heterogeneous features, constructs cluster-aware graph views, and aggregates anomaly scores from reconstruction discrepancies across views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cross-domain GAD methods rely on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, severely limiting generalization to unseen target graph domains.

Method: AlignGAD consists of three modules: (1) a Global Unification Module that aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; (2) a Clustering Module that builds cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; and (3) a Node Discrepancy Scoring Module that measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence across different graph views.

Result: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of AlignGAD under the zero-shot GAD setting.

Conclusion: AlignGAD effectively addresses the cross-domain generalization challenge in graph anomaly detection through feature alignment, cluster-aware view construction, and multi-view discrepancy aggregation, enabling zero-shot anomaly identification on unseen target graphs.

Abstract: Cross-domain graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify abnormal nodes in unseen target graphs, showing strong potential in real-world applications with heterogeneous graph data. However, existing methods often depend on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, which limits their ability to generalize across different domains. To address this challenge, we propose AlignGAD, a zero-shot generalized graph anomaly detection framework. Our framework is built upon three key components: a Global Unification Module that aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; a Clustering Module that constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; and a Node Discrepancy Scoring Module that measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence from different graph views. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of AlignGAD under the zero-shot GAD setting.

</details>


### [231] [How Useful is Causal Invariance for Domain Adaptation in Finite-Sample Settings?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12680)
*Julia Kostin,Kasra Jalaldoust,Elias Bareinboim,Samory Kpotufe,Fanny Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper theoretically analyzes when causal invariance can provably improve supervised domain adaptation (sDA) with limited target labels. Studying linear regression, it derives matching upper and lower bounds showing that gains depend on target-risk margins between candidate invariant predictors; when margins are large relative to target sample size, adaptive aggregation matches the best candidate, but when margins are small, no algorithm can reliably improve over target-only learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While causality-based domain generalization has shown that shared causal structures can yield invariant predictors with stable risk across domain shifts, it remains underexplored whether such population-level invariances translate to finite-sample gains, especially in supervised domain adaptation (sDA) where a few labeled target samples are available. The paper aims to formally characterize when causal knowledge provably helps in this practical finite-sample regime.

Method: The authors study linear regression where causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly-invariant feature subsets. They derive matching upper and lower bounds on the target risk, revealing that finite-sample gains are governed by two quantities: (1) target-risk margins separating candidate predictors, and (2) finite-source estimation error. They propose an adaptive aggregation procedure (similar to model selection with holdout) that selects among source-trained candidates using the limited target samples, and they connect the risk margins to structural shift magnitude in linear Structural Causal Models (SCMs).

Result: The paper establishes matching upper and lower bounds showing that when risk margins are sufficiently large relative to the target sample size n_Q, adaptive aggregation can match the best candidate predictor while avoiding negative transfer. Conversely, when margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit causal knowledge for faster finite-sample rates. Experiments on real-world causal benchmarks validate the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: Causal invariance provides provable finite-sample benefits in supervised domain adaptation only when the target-risk margins between candidate invariant predictors are sufficiently large; otherwise, causal knowledge offers no reliable advantage over target-only learning. This characterizes a fundamental limit and provides practical guidance on when leveraging causal structure is worthwhile.

Abstract: Machine learning models often degrade when they are deployed on a target distribution that differs from the source distributions they were trained on. Recent work in causality-based domain generalization has shown how shared causal structure between domains can induce invariant predictors, e.g., models on a subset of features which have stable risk across structured domain shifts. However, the extent to which such population-level causal invariances can lead to gains in finite-sample settings remains underexplored. In particular, in practice we often have access to a few labeled target samples, a setting called supervised domain adaptation (sDA). In this paper, we explore when (full or partial) causal knowledge can provably improve supervised domain adaptation.
  As a first step, we study linear regression, where full or partial causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each yielding a source-trained candidate predictor. We derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that finite-sample gains are governed by the target-risk margins separating the candidates, together with the finite-source estimation error. When these margins are sufficiently large relative to $n_Q$, an adaptive aggregation procedure can match the best candidate predictor while avoiding negative transfer relative to target-only learning. On the other hand, when the margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection to obtain faster finite-sample rates. We further connect these margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validate the theory on real-world causal benchmarks.

</details>


### [232] [Forecasting Is Not Attribution: Localizing Decoder Bypass in Graph-Based Neural Marketing Mix Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12687)
*Yunbo Wang,Bolbi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Marketing mix models (MMMs) using graph neural networks can achieve low forecasting error while failing at their core attribution task. The authors identify "attribution bypass" — where a decoder exploits latent shortcuts (autoregression, co-movement, context) to forecast accurately without routing sensitivity through the attribution graph — and introduce DICE-MMM, a diagnostic framework that isolates graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and graph-aligned attribution, showing that low MSE does not certify correct attribution and that graph-support selection remains the critical unsolved bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MMMs serve dual purposes: forecasting business outcomes and attributing those outcomes to specific marketing channels. However, these goals are not equivalent. Graph-based neural MMMs can exhibit a failure mode where a high-capacity decoder achieves excellent forecasting performance by exploiting shortcuts (target autoregression, dense cross-node communication, latent co-movement, contextual signals, or latent memory) without the attribution graph actually mediating counterfactual sensitivity. This renders the graph — the very object used for attribution — meaningless, even when forecasting metrics look strong. The paper seeks to systematically diagnose and isolate this problem.

Method: The authors propose DICE-MMM, a bounded diagnostic and training framework that separates three conflated questions: graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and whether perturbation-induced influence through the decoder is graph-aligned. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder where all cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Attribution quality is evaluated using three tests: CIG (Conditional Independence Graph), AR-CIG (Autoregressive CIG), and graph-swap tests. The framework is stress-tested across controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog setting, benchmarking against CausalMMM.

Result: DICE-MMM improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM. Critically, forecasting accuracy does not certify attribution quality: in a sparse-target benchmark, both no-graph and fully-connected-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 around 0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph reaches 0.807 ± 0.129 at comparable MSE. Graph-swap experiments localize the bottleneck: the same DICE-hard-trained decoder jumps from nAUPRC -0.044 ± 0.006 under learned graph inputs to 0.894 ± 0.027 when supplied the oracle graph, demonstrating that decoder capacity and forecasting are not the limiting factors — graph-support selection is.

Conclusion: The paper's primary contribution is a stress-test and failure-localization framework demonstrating that low forecasting MSE can conceal attribution bypass in graph-based neural MMMs. The unresolved bottleneck is not forecasting accuracy or decoder capacity, but rather graph-support selection — i.e., correctly recovering which edges should exist in the attribution graph. This has significant implications for practitioners who rely on forecasting performance as a proxy for attribution reliability.

Abstract: Marketing mix models are used to forecast business outcomes and to attribute those outcomes to marketing channels, but these goals are not equivalent. We study a failure mode in graph-based neural MMM called attribution bypass: a high-capacity decoder can obtain low forecasting error through target autoregression, dense communication, co-movement, context, or latent memory while failing to route counterfactual sensitivity through the graph used as the attribution object. We introduce DICE-MMM as a bounded diagnostic and training framework. We do not claim that observational neural MMM identifies causal effects. Instead, DICE separates three questions often conflated in graph-based MMM: graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and whether the trained decoder's perturbation-induced influence is graph aligned. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder whose cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Decoder use is evaluated with CIG, AR-CIG, and graph-swap tests. Across controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, DICE improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM. The experiments show that forecasting accuracy is not an attribution certificate: in a sparse-target benchmark, no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 around 0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph reaches 0.807 +/- 0.129 at comparable MSE. Frozen graph-swap localizes the bottleneck: the same DICE-hard-trained decoder moves from nAUPRC -0.044 +/- 0.006 under learned graph inputs to 0.894 +/- 0.027 with the oracle graph. The contribution is a stress test and failure-localization framework showing that low MSE can hide attribution bypass and that the unresolved bottleneck is graph-support selection, not forecasting or decoder capacity.

</details>


### [233] [M*: A Modular, Extensible, Serving System for Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12688)
*Atindra Jha,Naomi Sagan,Keisuke Kamahori,Irmak Sivgin,Rohan Sanda,Steven Gao,Mark Horowitz,Luke Zettlemoyer,Olivia Hsu,Jure Leskovec,Baris Kasikci,Stephanie Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M* is a universal serving system for composite AI models that uses a dataflow graph abstraction called the Walk Graph to represent arbitrary model compositions. It achieves 20% lower latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image, up to 2.9× lower real-time factor for text-to-speech, and up to 12.5× speedup for robotic planning, all while minimizing developer effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The emergence of composite model architectures—integrating vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion heads, audio codecs, action generators, and world-model predictors—has rendered existing serving frameworks obsolete. Current systems were built on narrow assumptions about model structure and cannot accommodate the architectural diversity of modern multimodal, omni, speech-language, vision-language-action, and world models. A universal serving abstraction is needed.

Method: M* represents models as dataflow graphs (Walk Graphs), where requests spanning diverse modalities and tasks are processed as traversals over these graphs. The Walk Graph abstraction enables: (1) arbitrary composition of model components, (2) flexible placement onto physical clusters, and (3) model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime. The system is instantiated across representative models from different families (text-to-image, text-to-speech, robotic planning).

Result: On BAGEL (text-to-image), M* achieves ~20% lower end-to-end latency vs. vLLM-Omni. On Qwen3-Omni (text-to-speech), M* delivers up to 2.9× lower real-time factor and 2.7× higher throughput. On V-JEPA 2-AC (robotic planning rollout), M* outperforms the baseline by up to 12.5×.

Conclusion: M* demonstrates that a unified, graph-based serving abstraction can efficiently handle the broad and growing diversity of composite AI models with minimal developer effort, paving the way toward more scalable and flexible model serving infrastructure.

Abstract: We are entering a new era of composite model architectures that integrate diverse components such as vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion and flow heads, audio codecs, action generators, and world-model predictors. Such architectures underpin a broad class of multimodal models, including unified multimodal models, omni models, speech-language models, vision-language-action policies, and world models. However, existing model serving frameworks were built on narrow assumptions about model structure, making them ill-suited to accommodate this new architectural diversity. Here we present M*, a universal serving system for efficient serving of composite AI models. M* represents models as dataflow graphs, processing requests spanning diverse modalities and tasks as traversals over these graphs. The core insight is a modular abstraction that supports arbitrary composition of model components, flexible placement onto a physical cluster, and model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime. We call this abstraction the Walk Graph and show how it can concisely capture composite models from a broad range of families. We instantiate M* on representative models and find that it achieves, on average, 20% lower end-to-end latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image workloads on BAGEL, while delivering up to 2.9x lower real-time factor and 2.7x higher throughput for text-to-speech workloads on Qwen3-Omni. M* also outperforms the V-JEPA 2-AC rollout baseline for robotic planning by up to 12.5x. Thus, our work paves the road towards more efficient serving of complex models with minimal developer effort.

</details>


### [234] [Two-Layer Linear Auto-Regressive Models Estimate Latent States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12691)
*Yahya Sattar,Sunmook Choi,Leo Maynard-Zhang,Yassir Jedra,Maryam Fazel,Sarah Dean*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Linear autoregressive models trained on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems learn to approximate Kalman filtering in their hidden representations, with theoretical guarantees on prediction error, parameter recovery, and latent state recovery despite non-convex optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auto-regressive models are widely used for sequential data, but the theoretical understanding of how and why they learn meaningful latent representations remains an open question.

Method: The authors analyze two-layer linear auto-regressive models trained via empirical risk minimization on data generated by partially observed linear dynamical systems. They prove: (1) the Kalman filter is well-approximated by an auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error; (2) the non-convex optimization landscape is benign (all stationary points are strict saddles or global minima); and (3) finite-sample guarantees are derived for prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery.

Result: The learned hidden representation coincides (up to a similarity transformation) with the optimal Kalman filter state estimates, despite no explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics. Numerical simulations confirm the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: Linear auto-regressive models implicitly learn to perform Kalman filtering when trained on data from linear dynamical systems, providing a theoretical bridge between self-supervised sequence modeling and classical state estimation.

Abstract: Auto-regressive models have emerged as powerful tools for sequential data, from language to video. Understanding how and why these models learn latent representations remains an open theoretical question. In this work, we demonstrate that when trained by empirical risk minimization on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems, two-layer linear auto-regressive models naturally learn to approximate Kalman filtering. In particular, we show that the learned hidden representation coincides, up to a similarity transformation, with the state estimates produced by the optimal (Kalman) filter, even though the model has no explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics or state. The result follows from three main insights. First, we establish that the Kalman filter is well approximated by an auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error. Second, we show that despite non-convexity, the two-layer optimization landscape is benign, i.e., all stationary points are either strict saddles or global minima. Finally, as our main contributions, we provide finite-sample guarantees on prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery. Numerical simulations support the theoretical results and demonstrate that the latent representations of auto-regressive models recover state estimates.

</details>


### [235] [LLM-Powered Personalized Glycemic Assessment in Type 2 Diabetes with Wearable Sensor Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12699)
*Yifan Gao,Yanmin Gong,Yun Shi,Yuanxiong Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GlyLLM is an LLM-powered framework that integrates wearable sensor data (CGM, fitness trackers) with structured metadata for personalized glycemic assessment in Type 2 Diabetes, outperforming traditional ML methods by ~13% on glucose forecasting and diabetes categorization tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing glycemic assessment methods rely on traditional ML with historical blood glucose alone, overlooking personalized individual-level context. LLMs offer potential to integrate diverse data modalities and model sequential dependencies, but their application to personalized glycemic assessment remains unexplored.

Method: GlyLLM leverages pre-trained LLMs to perform sensor-text semantic abstraction, integrating CGM and fitness tracker data with structured metadata (diabetes surveys, biometric tests, health information) at decision time for glucose forecasting and diabetes categorization.

Result: On the AI-READI dataset, GlyLLM outperforms traditional ML baselines by 13.66% in RMSE for glucose forecasting and 13.08% in AUROC for diabetes categorization. Ablation reveals diabetes surveys and biometric tests are the most critical metadata components.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates a promising step toward using LLMs for personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care, effectively combining wearable sensor streams with structured patient metadata.

Abstract: Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) poses an increasing global health threat, demanding effective glycemic assessment to support personalized and improved diabetes care. Wearable sensors such as continuous glucose monitors (CGM) and fitness trackers offer many valuable insights for glycemic assessment. However, effectively analyzing these data requires integration with essential individual-level context. Existing methods are often based on traditional machine learning (ML) and rely primarily on historical blood glucose measurements and overlook personalized information, which limits their performance across diverse diabetes populations. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their ability to integrate diverse data modalities while modeling sequential dependencies, motivating the exploration of their potential for personalized glycemic assessment.
  In this paper, we propose GlyLLM, an LLM-powered framework for modeling CGM-based glycemic dynamics through the integration of wearable sensor data and structured metadata. GlyLLM can leverage the extensive prior knowledge of pre-trained LLMs and achieve sensor-text semantic abstraction at decision time. Experiments on two related tasks on the AI-READI dataset demonstrate that our model outperforms traditional ML methods by an average of 13.66\% in Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for glucose forecasting and 13.08\% in Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) for diabetes categorization. Additionally, our ablation study shows that diabetes surveys and biometric tests are more critical than other health information for glycemic assessment. Our work presents a promising step toward harnessing the power of LLMs to advance personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care.

</details>


### [236] [A Stabilized Path-Space Approach to Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12710)
*Evan Scope Crafts,Umberto Villa,Saviz Mowlavi,Yanting Ma,Hassan Mansour,Wael H. Ali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A stabilized path-space framework for diffusion posterior sampling that reformulates the problem as stochastic optimal control, introduces time reparameterization to remove bias, and uses trust-region optimization with log-variance objectives, yielding improved accuracy and robustness over guidance-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors. There is a need for a principled framework that preserves Bayesian structure, enables uncertainty quantification, and provides asymptotically exact posterior expectations.

Method: The authors define a likelihood-weighted target measure on diffusion trajectories and cast posterior sampling as learning a controlled stochastic process matching this target. Key innovations include: (1) a time reparameterization that removes bias from the unknown initial value function without auxiliary training, (2) a trust-region path-space optimization method with log-variance objectives for learning the control, and (3) importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations. The framework also unifies learned control with existing guidance-based samplers.

Result: The framework was evaluated on benchmark inverse problems with analytically characterized or high-quality reference posteriors. It demonstrated improved sampling accuracy and robustness over leading diffusion posterior sampling approaches, particularly for challenging nonlinear and multimodal settings.

Conclusion: The path-space optimal control formulation provides a principled and stabilized approach to diffusion-based posterior sampling. It overcomes limitations of heuristic guidance methods, quantifies approximation error, and enables rigorous uncertainty quantification with importance sampling corrections.

Abstract: Diffusion models provide expressive data-driven priors for Bayesian inverse problems, but many diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that can fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors. In this work, we develop a stabilized path-space framework for diffusion-based posterior sampling. Starting from a base diffusion process whose terminal marginal represents the prior, we define a likelihood-weighted target measure on trajectories and cast posterior sampling as learning a controlled stochastic process whose path measure matches this target. This formulation connects diffusion posterior sampling to stochastic optimal control while preserving the Bayesian structure needed for uncertainty quantification. We introduce a time reparameterization that makes the path-space control problem well posed by removing the bias induced by the unknown initial value function, without auxiliary training. We then learn the control via a trust-region path-space optimization method with log-variance objectives. The path-space perspective also unifies our learned control approach with existing guidance-based samplers, quantifies the sampling error induced by approximate controls, and yields importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations. We evaluate the proposed framework on a suite of benchmark inverse problems with analytically characterized or high-quality reference posteriors, enabling principled assessment of sampling accuracy and uncertainty quantification. These experiments provide insight into the behavior of diffusion-based posterior samplers and demonstrate improved accuracy and robustness over leading approaches.

</details>


### [237] [Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Detectors for Open-Set RF Fingerprinting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12718)
*Sudeepta Mondal,Ganesh Sundaramoorthi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper bridges out-of-distribution (OOD) detection methods from machine learning to open-set RF fingerprinting. It presents a unified information-theoretic framework for systematic analysis and development of OOD detectors, and demonstrates that recent tuning techniques requiring no auxiliary OOD data achieve performance comparable to detectors tuned with true OOD data on real-world RF measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RF fingerprinting systems face open-world challenges where unknown transmitters and temporal drift create distribution shifts. While OOD detection naturally addresses this, adoption is hindered because most OOD detectors need auxiliary OOD data for tuning—impractical in RF environments where representative OOD signals are difficult to collect. The paper aims to close this gap by adapting OOD detection methods that do not require OOD tuning data to the RFF domain.

Method: The authors (1) introduce a set of OOD detection methods from ML literature to open-set RFF, (2) present them within a unified information-theoretic mathematical framework suited to communication systems, (3) apply recent OOD detector tuning techniques that operate without any given OOD data, and (4) systematically evaluate on the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset, comparing detectors with and without access to true OOD tuning data.

Result: Detectors tuned without any OOD data achieved performance comparable to baselines that had access to true OOD tuning data, and significantly outperformed baseline approaches lacking true OOD tuning data. This was validated on the POWDER dataset, confirming practical viability for real-world RFF deployment.

Conclusion: OOD detection methods, particularly those tunable without auxiliary OOD data, are practically viable for open-set RF fingerprinting. The information-theoretic framework provides a principled foundation for future development of RFF-specific OOD detectors, enabling robust operation in open-world RF environments where collecting representative OOD data is infeasible.

Abstract: Radio-frequency (RF) fingerprinting systems must operate in open-world environments where signals from unknown transmitters and temporal drift introduce distribution shift at test time. Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection provides a natural framework for this problem, yet its application to RF fingerprinting (RFF) remains limited. A key barrier to their adoption is that most OOD detectors require auxiliary OOD data for parameter tuning, an assumption that is difficult to satisfy in RF environments where representative OOD data is impractical to collect. In this work, we introduce a promising set of OOD detection methods from the machine learning literature to open-set RFF domain. We present these methods within a unified mathematical framework based on information theory, which is a natural framework for communication systems. Our framework allows for the systematic analysis of methods and development of new methods. We further demonstrate the applicability of recent work on tuning OOD detectors without given OOD tuning data for open-set RFF. We evaluate on the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset, showing that detectors tuned without any given OOD data achieve performance comparable to baselines with access to true OOD tuning data and greatly out-perform baseline approaches without access to true OOD tuning data, showcasing the practical viability for the RFF problem.

</details>


### [238] [Normative Robustness as a Frontier for Non-Verifiable Reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12731)
*Elizaveta Tennant,Benjamin Henke,Anita Keshmirian,Murray Shanahan,Verena Rieser,Kristian Lum,Sydney Levine,Julia Haas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "moral robustness" as a framework for evaluating LLMs' non-verifiable reasoning. Through 48,000 simulated moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, the authors find models exhibit "moral deliberative sycophancy" — shifting both verdicts and justifications to align with users' stated moral views, with judgments varying by up to 24% depending on premise order and conversation duration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional LLM reasoning evaluations focus on fact-based domains (math, science) with objective ground truths. As LLMs are increasingly used for advisory and deliberative roles involving ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems, there is a critical gap in understanding whether models can handle non-verifiable reasoning robustly over time and across contexts.

Method: The authors propose moral reasoning as a paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning and define "moral robustness." They introduce a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework and simulate 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs. The framework systematically varies premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view to empirically measure robustness.

Result: Models successfully ignore morally-irrelevant distractors but exhibit significant failures: (1) reasoning shifts up to 6.5% on average toward the user's preferred moral view; (2) moral judgments are altered by premise order in 13-22% of cases; (3) judgments change between single-turn and multi-turn settings in 10-24% of cases. Critically, models tailor both final verdicts and underlying justifications to align with user viewpoints — a failure mode termed "moral deliberative sycophancy."

Conclusion: Frontier LLMs lack moral robustness, exhibiting sycophantic behavior in moral deliberation by adapting both conclusions and reasoning to match user preferences. This vulnerability to framing effects (order, duration, user stance) raises concerns about the reliability of LLMs in advisory roles involving non-verifiable reasoning, and the framework provides a foundation for evaluating and improving robustness in such domains.

Abstract: As LLMs increasingly serve in advisory and deliberative roles, users rely on them for non-verifiable reasoning in domains lacking objective ground truths. However, traditional evaluations of LLM reasoning focus almost exclusively on fact-based domains, such as mathematics and science, leaving uncertainty over whether and to what degree models can handle ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems over time. To address this concern, we propose moral reasoning as a paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning. We define moral robustness as a model's capacity to exhibit sound moral reasoning across time and contexts, and we introduce a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework to empirically measure this capability. We simulate 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view. We find that models successfully ignore morally-irrelevant distractors, but shift their reasoning by up to 6.5%, on average, towards the user's stated preferred moral view, and varying their reasoning depending on factors such as order (altering moral judgments by order in 13-22% of the cases) and duration (altering moral judgments between single-turn and multi-turn in 10-24% of the cases). Our analysis indicates that models tailor not just their final verdicts but their underlying justifications to align with a user's moral viewpoint - a failure mode we characterize as moral deliberative sycophancy.

</details>


### [239] [Let's Ask Gauss: Improved One-Run Privacy Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12733)
*Adya Agrawal,Yu Wei,Jaspal Singh,Malik Magdon-Ismail,Vassilis Zikas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new privacy auditing framework for DP-SGD that models canary-aligned signals as asymptotically Gaussian, enabling tighter empirical privacy lower bounds from a single training run compared to prior binary thresholding approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior one-run DP auditing methods reduce canary signals to binary membership guesses, discarding statistical information. The authors aim to exploit the full distributional structure of canary-aligned signals to obtain tighter, more accurate privacy lower bounds.

Method: In the white-box DP-SGD setting, the authors show that canary-aligned signals form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum converges asymptotically to a Gaussian distribution. They leverage this to construct a distributional auditing framework that uses the full signal (not just binary thresholding) to estimate privacy leakage via hypothesis testing on the observed Gaussian.

Result: The proposed framework yields tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run than existing one-run auditing methods.

Conclusion: Modeling canary signals as asymptotically Gaussian enables significantly more efficient and accurate empirical privacy auditing for DP-SGD, strengthening the practical verification of differential privacy guarantees.

Abstract: Privacy auditing provides an important safeguard by estimating the actual information leaked by a model, thus ensuring that theoretical privacy guarantees hold in practice. We study empirical privacy auditing for differentially private (DP) machine learning, focusing on efficient one-run methods for mechanisms such as DP-SGD. Prior one-run approaches threshold training examples or "canaries" into binary membership guesses, which discards useful information. We show that, in the white-box DP-SGD setting, canary-aligned signals naturally form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum is asymptotically Gaussian. Leveraging this distributional perspective, we develop a DP-auditing framework that leads to tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run.

</details>


### [240] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Radial Basis Functions for PDEs with Dirac Delta Sources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12735)
*Manuel Reyna,Alexandre Tartakovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINNs struggle with Dirac delta functions because they require smooth approximations. Viewing PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods allows direct integration of weak forms. The authors compare PINN-RLS with RBF-RLS and show RBF-RLS consistently outperforms PINNs on transport problems involving Dirac deltas, explaining the discrepancy via Neural Tangent Kernel theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs require smooth surrogate approximations for Dirac delta functions in forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions, introducing significant modeling errors. The authors aim to address this limitation by leveraging a Residual Least Squares interpretation that enables direct treatment of Dirac delta terms through weak-form integration.

Method: The work reinterprets PINNs as RLS methods, integrating out Dirac delta terms via the weak-form equation. It compares two RLS formulations: (1) PINN-based RLS and (2) Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (single-layer RBF network). The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory is used to explain why RBF-RLS succeeds while PINN residuals fail to converge to zero. Both methods are tested on linear PDEs modeling groundwater flow, transport in porous media, and rivers, solving inverse problems against synthetic, noisy synthetic, and real-world data.

Result: Integrating out the Dirac delta in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero. In contrast, RBF-RLS consistently provides accurate forward and inverse solutions to transport problems. The NTK analysis explains this performance gap: RBF-RLS maintains well-behaved convergence properties that PINN-RLS lacks when dealing with distributional source terms.

Conclusion: The RLS perspective on PINNs, combined with weak-form integration, offers a viable path for handling Dirac delta terms in PDEs. However, RBF-RLS is the more robust choice for such problems, outperforming PINNs in both forward and inverse settings across groundwater and river transport applications.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a machine learning method for solving forward and inverse Partial Differential Equations (PDEs). When applied to PDEs with Dirac delta functions in the forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions, PINNs require approximating them with smooth surrogate functions, a practice that can introduce significant modeling errors. In this work, we exploit the interpretation of PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods and show that this perspective enables direct treatment of Dirac delta terms by integrating the weak-form equation. Among RLS formulations other than PINN, we focus on the Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (also known as a single-layer RBF Network). We show that while integrating out the Dirac delta in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero, RBF-RLS consistently provides good forward and inverse solutions to transport problems. We explain this finding using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory. We test both approaches on linear PDEs that represent groundwater flow and transport in porous media and rivers. We solve inverse problems to fit synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.

</details>


### [241] [Deep Unfolded Latent Optimally Partitioned-l2/l1 Networks for Data-driven Block-Sparse Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12740)
*Takanobu Furuhashi,Hidekata Hontani,Qibin Zhao,Tatsuya Yokota*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces two deep unfolding architectures for the LOP-ℓ₂/ℓ₁ approach to overcome manual hyperparameter tuning and numerical instability. One uses implicit differentiation for stability, the other employs Deep Weight Factorization for flexibility and nonconvex fidelity. Experiments show competitive block-sparse recovery and strong robustness to impulsive noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The LOP-ℓ₂/ℓ₁ method recovers block-sparse signals without knowing partitions, but requires tedious manual hyperparameter tuning. Moreover, numerical instability when differentiating its proximal operator prevents the use of Deep Unfolding for automatic, data-driven parameter learning.

Method: Two Deep Unfolding (DU) architectures are proposed: (1) a stable DU framework using implicit differentiation to handle the proximal operator's differentiation robustly; (2) a flexible variant based on Deep Weight Factorization (DWF), which also accommodates nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed DU-LOP-ℓ₂/ℓ₁ achieves competitive performance in block-sparse signal recovery and exhibits high resilience against impulsive noise.

Conclusion: The proposed DU-based frameworks successfully automate hyperparameter tuning for LOP-ℓ₂/ℓ₁, overcoming prior numerical instability, and deliver robust recovery performance, particularly under impulsive noise conditions.

Abstract: The convex Latent Optimal Partition (LOP)-l2/l1 approach enables block-sparse signal recovery with unknown partitions but relies on manual hyperparameter tuning. Additionally, numerical instability in differentiating its proximal operator prevents its automatic parameter tuning via Deep Unfolding (DU). To address these limitations, we propose two architectures: a stable framework utilizing implicit differentiation and a flexible variant leveraging Deep Weight Factorization (DWF). The DWF-based approach also supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms. Numerical experiments demonstrate that DU-LOP-l2/l1 yields competitive performance and high resilience against impulsive noise.

</details>


### [242] [Adaptive Weighted Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12763)
*Aditya Bhaskara,Ashok Cutkosky,Ravi Kumar,Manish Purohit*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies the problem of selecting the maximum among n unknown values using only a single noisy estimate per value. The authors design strategies that are simultaneously admissible (not dominated) and guaranteed to never underperform a baseline such as uniform random selection. An application to stochastic optimization yields online-to-batch conversion bounds with a "no-compromise" property: never worse than standard random iterate selection, yet significantly better in favorable settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In many decision-making and learning scenarios, one must select the best candidate from a set of unknown values based solely on noisy, single-sample estimates. Standard strategies (e.g., picking the empirical maximizer) can perform arbitrarily poorly, while conservative baselines like uniform random selection are robust but suboptimal in benign settings. The authors aim to construct selection strategies that dominate or match the baseline uniformly while remaining admissible — ensuring no other strategy uniformly outperforms them. This fills a gap in understanding how to achieve principled "no-compromise" guarantees in selection under uncertainty.

Method: The authors frame the problem as selecting an index i ∈ {1,…,n} to maximize E[x_i] given only unbiased estimates y_i of each x_i. They characterize admissible strategies using statistical decision theory, showing that a strategy is admissible if it corresponds to a Bayes rule for some prior. They construct a family of strategies based on thresholding or tilting the empirical selection rule, ensuring the expected value of the chosen arm is at least that of uniform random selection. The key technique involves deriving conditions under which a strategy is both admissible and baseline-dominating, then instantiating concrete rules that satisfy these conditions. For the stochastic optimization application, they apply these selection rules to convert online optimization algorithms into batch algorithms with theoretical guarantees.

Result: The authors establish the existence and explicit construction of selection strategies that are simultaneously (1) admissible — no other strategy uniformly dominates them — and (2) never worse than a given baseline such as uniform random selection. They provide a full characterization of the dominance region: a strategy dominates the baseline iff it satisfies certain probabilistic threshold conditions. For stochastic optimization, they derive online-to-batch conversion bounds showing that their selection rule achieves expected loss no worse than uniform iterate selection, with significantly tighter bounds when the optimization trajectory is "benign" (e.g., when later iterates are consistently better).

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that it is possible to design selection rules that offer a "best of both worlds" guarantee: they are theoretically sound (admissible) and practically robust (never worse than a safe baseline), while exploiting favorable structure when present. This framework has immediate implications for stochastic optimization, model selection, and any setting requiring principled selection from noisy observations without risking catastrophic underperformance relative to a simple default strategy.

Abstract: We study the problem of selecting the largest among $n$ unknown values $x_1,\dots,x_n$ given only a single unbiased estimate $y_i$ for each $x_i$. We design strategies that are simultaneously admissible (not uniformly dominated by any other strategy) and also never worse than a given baseline such as uniform random selection. We provide an application to stochastic optimization, where we obtain online-to-batch conversion bounds with a desirable "no-compromise" guarantee: they are never worse than standard random iterate selection, and yet can be significantly better in benign settings.

</details>


### [243] [ProPlay: Procedural World Models for Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12780)
*Yijun Ma,Zehong Wang,Yiyang Li,Ziming Li,Xiaoguang Guo,Weixiang Sun,Chuxu Zhang,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProPlay introduces a procedural world model that organizes successful trajectories into a procedure graph with reliability embeddings, enabling agents to rehearse (preplay) future procedural paths and refine their internal understanding of environment dynamics through closed-loop interaction, outperforming strong baselines on public benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-evolving agents in partially observable environments face the challenge of balancing active exploration, learning from sparse feedback, and deciding when to trust past experience. Existing LLM-agent methods fail to close the loop between memory/planning modules and the continual refinement of an internal world model, limiting their ability to autonomously improve environment understanding.

Method: ProPlay builds a procedural world model with three key components: (1) abstraction of successful trajectories into procedures organized in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages; (2) reliability record embeddings associated with each transition to estimate task-specific contributions from past outcomes; (3) a preplay mechanism that simulates future procedural trajectories over the graph as structured soft guidance before each episode, followed by graph refinement using environment feedback after execution.

Result: Experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate that ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability over strong baselines.

Conclusion: ProPlay demonstrates that closing the loop between experience abstraction (procedure graph), reliability estimation (embeddings), and planning (preplay simulation) enables more effective self-evolving agents in partially observable environments. Code is open-sourced.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents are expected to improve through interaction without external supervision, but this remains difficult in partially observable environments where agents must explore actively, learn from limited feedback, and decide when to trust prior experience. Existing LLM-agent methods often rely on memory or planning modules, yet they rarely close the loop between them to continually refine an internal understanding of environment dynamics. We introduce ProPlay, a procedural world model that supports procedure-level preplay, where agents can rehearse future procedural paths using the learned world knowledge. Rather than representing experience as isolated rules or low-level action constraints, ProPlay abstracts successful trajectories into procedures and organizes them in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages. Each transition is associated with a reliability record embedding to estimate its task-specific contribution from past outcomes. Before each episode, ProPlay simulates future procedural trajectories over known graph structures as structured soft guidance; after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback. Experiments on public benchmarks show that ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability over strong baselines. Our code has been released in https://github.com/antman9914/proplay.

</details>


### [244] [SymQNet: Amortized Acquisition for Low-Latency Adaptive Hamiltonian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12808)
*Yash Vardhan Tomar,Dheeraj Peddireddy,Vaneet Aggarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SymQNet is an amortized reinforcement-learning method that replaces expensive online Bayesian experiment design with a fast offline-trained policy network, achieving 47-73x reduction in acquisition latency for adaptive Hamiltonian learning on transverse-field Ising models without sacrificing Bayesian posterior feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive Hamiltonian learning requires recomputing Bayesian experiment design rules after every posterior update, which takes seconds per shot. Across hundreds of shots, this computational overhead becomes a prohibitive wall-clock bottleneck, making real-time adaptivity impractical for quantum device calibration and characterization.

Method: SymQNet employs amortized reinforcement learning: a policy network is trained offline to approximate the optimal Bayesian acquisition function conditioned on the posterior. During online deployment, the agent performs a single fast forward pass through the policy network to select the next experiment, while still incorporating Bayesian posterior updates from measurement outcomes, preserving the feedback loop.

Result: On transverse-field Ising benchmarks at five qubits, SymQNet reduces acquisition-only decision latency by 47.1× over bounded Fisher-information search and 72.6× over bounded two-step BALD. At twelve qubits, full simulated steps take 1.02 s for SymQNet versus 13.27 s for bounded two-step BALD.

Conclusion: Learned acquisition policies can make adaptive Hamiltonian learning practical for repeated low-latency workloads, enabling real-time quantum device calibration without the computational burden of online Bayesian experiment design.

Abstract: Adaptive Hamiltonian learning is central to calibrating and characterizing quantum devices. In an adaptive controller, choosing the next experiment is itself a computation. Bayesian design rules are recomputed after every posterior update, and that step can take seconds. Across hundreds of shots, those seconds become a significant wall-clock cost for adaptivity. We introduce SymQNet, an amortized reinforcement-learning approach for low-latency adaptive Hamiltonian learning. SymQNet learns a posterior-conditioned acquisition policy offline, then uses a fast policy forward pass online while retaining Bayesian posterior feedback. On transverse-field Ising benchmarks, SymQNet substantially reduces acquisition latency relative to bounded Fisher-information search and bounded two-step Bayesian active learning by disagreement (BALD). At five qubits, it reduces acquisition-only decision latency by $47.1\times$ and $72.6\times$ relative to these online baselines; at twelve qubits, full simulated steps take $1.02$ s for SymQNet versus $13.27$ s for bounded two-step BALD. Overall, we show that learned acquisition can make adaptive Hamiltonian learning practical for repeated low-latency workloads.

</details>


### [245] [CLARITree: Cholesky and Lookahead Accelerations for Regression with Interpretable Piecewise Linear Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12840)
*Yixiao Wang,Hayden McTavish,Varun Babbar,Margo Seltzer,Cynthia Rudin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a novel near-optimal sparse piecewise linear regression tree algorithm that combines lookahead search with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates, achieving favorable trade-offs between computational efficiency, predictive accuracy, and sparsity while scaling better than existing state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Greedy induction dominates regression tree construction due to computational constraints, but optimal methods (dynamic programming/branch-and-bound) yield substantially better performance yet are prohibitive for general linear regression trees. Recent lookahead strategies improve runtime while maintaining near-optimality, primarily for classification. The authors aim to extend such benefits to sparse, piecewise linear regression trees.

Method: The algorithm integrates a lookahead-style search strategy with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates of the Gram matrix to construct sparse, piecewise linear regression trees that are near-optimal.
