Result: Both theoretical and empirical demonstrations show the method achieves a favorable trade-off among computational efficiency, predictive accuracy, and sparsity, and scales significantly better than the current state of the art.

Conclusion: The proposed algorithm successfully bridges the gap between greedy efficiency and optimal accuracy for linear regression trees, offering a scalable, near-optimal solution that advances the state of the art.

Abstract: Regression trees are among the most interpretable yet expressive model classes in machine learning. Historically, greedy induction has been the dominant approach for constructing well-performing regression trees. While optimal methods based on dynamic programming and branch-and-bound exist, they are computationally prohibitive for general linear regression trees, despite often achieving substantially better performance than greedy approaches. Recent work has shown that specialized lookahead strategies can dramatically improve runtime while maintaining near-optimal performance, primarily in classification settings. In this work, we develop a novel algorithm for near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees that combines a lookahead-style search strategy with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates of the Gram matrix. We demonstrate, both theoretically and empirically, that our method achieves a favorable trade-off between computational efficiency, predictive accuracy, and sparsity, and scales significantly better than the current state of the art.

</details>


### [246] [TimeROME-DLM: Temporal Causal Tracing and Low-Rank Inference-Time Knowledge Editing for Masked Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12841)
*Zhengtao Yao,Liuyang Song,Hongbo Zhang,Chenhao Wei,Haoyan Xu,Guang Yang,Siheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TimeROME-DLM introduces the first training-free, gradient-free, inference-time knowledge-editing framework for masked diffusion language models (MDLMs). It pairs a temporal causal-tracing method (TIE) with a closed-form low-rank residual edit memory, achieving effective forgetting with minimal utility loss across multiple MDLM architectures at greatly reduced computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge-editing and unlearning methods (ROME, MEMIT, etc.) are designed for autoregressive transformers and fail when applied to masked diffusion language models (MDLMs). They either rely on assumptions invalid under iterative denoising or require expensive gradient updates that consume excessive VRAM and cause model collapse at standard learning rates. There is a clear need for a dedicated knowledge-editing framework tailored specifically to MDLMs.

Method: The authors propose TimeROME-DLM, comprising two components: (1) Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal-tracing, which identifies the coordinate (layer, token position) whose intervention most strongly influences object prediction at later denoising steps for each fact; (2) a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject keys and target deltas across all forget facts, then applies a single ridge-regularised update at that coordinate during every diffusion forward pass, with sparsification to limit utility spillover. All backbone weights remain frozen, with only three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q) tuned on a small validation set.

Result: On TOFU forget01 with TOFU-finetuned LLaDA-8B-Base, TimeROME-DLM reduces forget-set log-probability by ~83 nats. Retain-set log-probability stays nearly flat (within ~1 nat) across 50 sequentially inserted facts. The method transfers successfully to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B. It achieves a 4–14× wall-clock speedup with zero additional VRAM over the strongest training-time baseline and scales sub-linearly to 400 facts.

Conclusion: TimeROME-DLM closes the locate-then-edit gap between AR LLMs and MDLMs, delivering effective and efficient knowledge editing at a fraction of the computational cost previously required.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) such as LLaDA now rival autoregressive (AR) LLMs, but every existing knowledge-editing and unlearning method (ROME, MEMIT, etc.) targets AR transformers and either makes assumptions that fail under iterative denoising, or requires gradient updates whose backward-pass activations cost tens of GB of extra VRAM and which collapse MDLMs at standard learning rates. We introduce TimeROME-DLM, the first training-free, gradient-free, inference-time knowledge-editing framework for MDLMs. It couples two components: a Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal-tracing protocol that identifies, for each fact, the coordinate whose intervention most strongly drives the object prediction at later denoising steps; and a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject keys and target deltas across all forget facts and applies a single ridge-regularised update at that coordinate at every diffusion forward, with sparsification to limit utility spillover. Backbone weights stay frozen; only three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q) are tuned on a small validation split. On TOFU forget01 with TOFU-finetuned LLaDA-8B-Base, TimeROME-DLM cuts forget-set log-probability by roughly 83 nats. The same configuration transfers to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B. It keeps retain-set log-probability nearly flat (within ~1 nat at the utility-safe operating point) across 50 sequentially inserted facts, delivers a four- to fourteen-fold wall-clock speedup with zero additional VRAM over the strongest converged training-time baseline, and scales sub-linearly to 400 facts. TimeROME-DLM closes the locate-then-edit gap between AR LLMs and MDLMs at a fraction of the computational cost.

</details>


### [247] [Interpretable Factor Decomposition for Decision Intelligence in Large-Scale Financial Markets: Evidence from China's A-Share Market](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12843)
*Xiao Han,Yao Xiao,Zhen Zhang,Moxuan Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An interpretable XGBoost pipeline with TreeSHAP attribution reveals that behavioral signals (turnover, momentum) dominate valuation ratios in predicting Chinese A-share cross-sectional returns, with SHAP and ablation analyses jointly uncovering feature substitutability patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To decompose cross-sectional equity return predictability into auditable, interpretable factor contributions, addressing the "black box" problem in machine learning applications to asset pricing and enabling rigorous attribution of predictive power to specific signal categories.

Method: XGBoost model trained on 60-month rolling windows with 55-month out-of-sample periods, applied to 3,632 Chinese A-share stocks (2009–2019). TreeSHAP is used for global and local feature attribution, decomposed across 55 industry groups. Ablation analysis cross-validates SHAP rankings and reveals feature substitutability. Long-short quintile portfolios are evaluated with AUC, spreads, and Carhart four-factor alphas.

Result: XGBoost achieves mean AUC of 0.547, long-short spread of +2.38%/month (Newey-West t = 5.94; annualized Sharpe 2.23), and Carhart-adjusted alpha of +2.31%/month (t = 7.48). SHAP decomposition shows behavioral signals contribute 58.2% of predictive attribution vs. 10.7% for valuation ratios. Ablation confirms the ranking but diverges from SHAP in ways that reveal feature substitutability.

Conclusion: Behavioral signals are the dominant drivers of cross-sectional return predictability in Chinese A-shares. Combining SHAP with ablation analysis yields richer insights into predictor structure than either method alone, enhancing interpretability and auditability in machine learning–driven factor research.

Abstract: We present an interpretable machine learning pipeline to decompose Cross-Sectional Equity Return Predictability into auditable factor contribution. We apply an XGBoost model with TreeSHAP attribution and conduct stress testing on 3632 Chinese A-share stocks from 2009 until 2019. Using 60-month, rolling windows over 55 months of out-of-sample data, XGBoost obtains a mean AUC of 0.547 and +2.38%/month (Newey-West t = 5.94; Annualized Sharpe 2.23) long-short spread for the top vs bottom quintiles. This alpha is persistent after adjusting for the Carhart four-factor model (+2.31%/month; t = 7.48). SHAP Decomposition indicates that behavioral signals (turnover and momentum) account for 58.2% of predictive attribution compared to 10.7% for valuation ratios, on average, across 55 industry groups. Ablation analysis serves to cross-validate this ranking and provides evidence that SHAP and ablation diverge in a manner that highlights feature substitutability structure that is largely invisible to either method used in isolation.

</details>


### [248] [Multimodal Graph Negative Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12863)
*Zhengyu Wu,Xu Wang,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Guang Zeng,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphMNL introduces graph-aware multimodal negative learning to address node-level branch semantic imbalance in Multimodal Attributed Graphs (MAGs). Instead of forcing inferior branches to imitate dominant predictions, it teaches them which classes a node is unlikely to belong to, achieving state-of-the-art results on Grocery (72.47% accuracy) and Reddit M (76.60 F1) datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for MAGs handle multimodal heterogeneity through cross-branch agreement/alignment, implicitly treating the dominant branch prediction as reliable supervision. However, when the dominant branch is biased, forced imitation propagates its bias to other branches and suppresses useful original semantics. The paper identifies this as "node-level branch semantic imbalance" — where branches differ across nodes in semantic informativeness and reliability.

Method: GraphMNL uses Negative Learning (NL) as cross-branch guidance. It builds a branch library, identifies dominant and inferior branches via graph-aware reliability arbitration, gates unstable transfer, and applies target-preserving negative learning over non-target classes. This decouples target supervision (learning the correct class via supervised loss) from branch guidance (suppressing unlikely alternatives via NL when branch agreement is unreliable).

Result: GraphMNL achieves 72.47% accuracy on Grocery datasets and 76.60 F1 score on Reddit M datasets, outperforming existing methods in comprehensive experimental evaluations.

Conclusion: The target-preserving negative learning framework effectively resolves branch semantic imbalance by decoupling supervision from guidance, preventing bias propagation while preserving useful semantics from all branches.

Abstract: Multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs) integrate graph topology with heterogeneous modality attributes, such as text and images, thereby enabling richer modeling of complex relational systems. However, such expressiveness also makes learning on MAGs depend on multiple semantic sources, including structural topology, textual and visual attributes, each of which can be regarded as a branch for node representation. Node-level branch semantic imbalance arises when these branches differ across nodes in semantic informativeness and reliability: a branch that provides discriminative semantics for one node may mislead another due to bias in modality quality or structural context. Existing methods often mitigate such heterogeneity through cross-branch agreement or alignment, implicitly treating the dominant prediction as reliable supervision. When the dominant branch is biased, forced imitation may propagate its bias to other branches and suppress original semantics that are useful for classification. We propose GraphMNL, a graph-aware multimodal negative learning framework that addresses this issue by using Negative Learning as cross-branch guidance. Instead of forcing inferior branches to imitate a teacher prediction, the model teaches them which classes a node is unlikely to belong to. GraphMNL builds a branch library, identifies dominant and inferior branches via graph-aware reliability arbitration, gates unstable transfer, and applies target-preserving negative learning over non-target classes. This design decouples target supervision from branch guidance so that supervised losses learn the correct class, while Negative Learning suppresses unlikely alternatives when branch agreement is unreliable. Through the comprehensive experimental evaluation, GraphMNL achieves the best performance on Grocery datasets with 72.47% accuracy and 76.60 F1 score on Reddit M datasets.

</details>


### [249] [SMGFM: Spectral Multimodal Graph Pretraining for Multimodal-Attributed Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12867)
*Zhengyu Wu,Xu Wang,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Guang Zeng,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMGFM proposes a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes node signals into graph-frequency bands, assigns band-level semantic roles via topology-conditioned routing, and performs frequency-aware cross-modal fusion, achieving SOTA on MAG benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs), structure-induced semantics (topology-consistent, low-frequency) and modality-intrinsic semantics (local, fine-grained, high-frequency) contribute differently to tasks, but traditional graph learning couples them indiscriminately, causing harmful smoothing or misalignment. A method to disentangle these semantic roles before fusion is needed.

Method: SMGFM uses graph-frequency decomposition via scalable Chebyshev filters to split each modality's node signals into frequency bands. It then constructs frequency-resolved modality tokens, estimates coupling reliability through topology-conditioned routing, performs band-modality interaction, and applies frequency-routed objectives that align smooth consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes.

Result: Extensive experiments on MAG datasets show SMGFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across both graph-level and modality-level downstream tasks.

Conclusion: Leveraging graph-frequency variation as a prior to assign semantic roles before cross-modal fusion effectively addresses the entanglement problem in MAGs, and the spectral routing framework provides a principled way to balance structural consistency with modality-specific expressiveness.

Abstract: Multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs) couple graph topology with node semantics from text, images, and other modalities. Traditional graph learning contextualizes node semantics by coupling topology with node features. However, this coupling design becomes troublesome in MAGs, where structure-induced and modality-intrinsic semantics may contribute differently to downstream tasks. Structure-induced semantics promote relational consistency through smooth topological variation, whereas modality-intrinsic semantics often encode local, fine-grained distinctions that should not be uniformly smoothed or aligned. Therefore, the key challenge is to identify semantic roles before cross-modal fusion. To this end, we leverage graph-frequency variation as a prior, where low-frequency components capture topology-consistent semantics and high-frequency components preserve modality-specific semantics. Based on this intuition, we propose SMGFM, a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes each modality-specific node signal into graph-frequency bands and assigns band-level semantic roles before cross-modal interaction. Concretely, SMGFM constructs frequency-resolved modality tokens with scalable Chebyshev filters, estimates their coupling reliability through topology-conditioned routing, and performs band-modality interaction before fusion. Its frequency-routed objectives align smooth consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes, mitigating spatial-domain entanglement and uniform cross-modal alignment. Extensive experiments conducted on the MAG datasets demonstrate that SMGFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across graph-level and modality-level tasks.

</details>


### [250] [Multi-Bitwidth Quantization for LLMs Using Additive Codebooks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12876)
*Liza Babaoglu,Shuangyi Chen,Ashish Khisti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Drop-by-Drop is a post-training quantization framework that enables inference-time precision control over LLM weights from a single checkpoint. Grounded in successive refinement theory, it uses Matryoshka-style supervision with additive codebooks so that ordered subsets yield accurate partial reconstructions at multiple bitwidths, reducing storage overhead while maintaining competitive performance across Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are deployed on heterogeneous hardware with varying resource constraints, yet existing quantization methods require separate models for each bitwidth. The key problem is achieving adaptive performance-efficiency trade-offs at inference time without retraining, using a single stored model.

Method: Drop-by-Drop leverages information-theoretic successive refinement: under a weighted MSE distortion aligned with LLM loss, Gaussian-distributed LLM weights can be optimally reconstructed with increasing fidelity as additional bits are added. The framework uses additive codebooks with Matryoshka-style supervision in the loss function, where ordered codebook subsets produce accurate partial reconstructions at each precision level — all from a single post-training quantization pass.

Result: A single Drop-by-Drop checkpoint serves multiple bitwidths, significantly reducing storage and memory overhead compared to maintaining separate quantized models, while maintaining competitive perplexity and accuracy across major architectures (Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, Mistral).

Conclusion: Drop-by-Drop demonstrates that successive refinement principles can be effectively applied to LLM weight quantization, enabling practical multi-precision deployment from a single model without sacrificing performance, thereby addressing a critical need in heterogeneous LLM serving environments.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed across heterogeneous hardware with varying resource constraints, the ability to adaptively manage the trade-off between performance and efficiency without retraining is critical. We propose Drop-by-Drop, a novel multi-bitwidth post-training quantization framework that enables inference-time precision control over LLM weights from a single trained model. Our method is theoretically grounded in information theory and successive refinement. We establish that LLM weights, which commonly follow a Gaussian distribution, can be optimally reconstructed with increasing fidelity as additional bits are incorporated, under a weighted mean squared error distortion motivated by LLM loss functions. To realize this in practice, Drop-by-Drop incorporates Matryoshka-style supervision into the loss function, exploiting the structure of additive codebooks. Drop-by-Drop produces a single model where ordered subsets of codebooks yield accurate partial reconstructions at each precision level. This approach significantly reduces storage and memory overhead by allowing a single checkpoint to serve multiple bitwidths, while maintaining competitive perplexity and accuracy across major architectures, such as Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral.

</details>


### [251] [LongSpike: Fractional Order Spiking State Space Models for Efficient Long Sequence Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12895)
*Xinrui He,Qiyu Kang,Xuhao Li,Zheng-Jun Zha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongSpike introduces fractional-order State-Space Modeling (f-SSM) into Spiking Neural Networks to overcome the "memoryless" bottleneck of first-order ODE-based neurons, enabling superior capture of long-range dependencies. The framework achieves state-of-the-art SNN accuracy on LRA, WikiText-103, and Speech Commands while maintaining energy efficiency through sparse computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional SNNs rely on first-order ODEs governing neuronal dynamics, which imposes a "memoryless" bottleneck that severely limits the model's ability to capture complex, long-range dependencies in long-sequence tasks. The authors aim to break this constraint by incorporating richer temporal memory into spiking neurons.

Method: The authors propose LongSpike, which integrates fractional-order State-Space Modeling (f-SSM) from control theory into SNNs. By extending integer-order SSMs to the fractional-calculus regime, the model enables hierarchical integration of neuronal dynamics with long-memory kernels. A key technical contribution is a state-space formulation that supports efficient parallel training, mitigating the typical computational overhead and parallelization difficulties of fractional operators.

Result: LongSpike achieves state-of-the-art SNN performance on the Long Range Arena (LRA) benchmark, large-scale WikiText-103 language modeling, and the Speech Commands dataset, outperforming existing SNNs in accuracy while preserving sparse synaptic computation.

Conclusion: Fractional-order SSMs offer a principled way to equip SNNs with long-range memory without sacrificing the biological plausibility and energy efficiency that make SNNs attractive. LongSpike demonstrates that this approach is both effective and computationally feasible, setting a new benchmark for SNNs on long-sequence tasks.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are well-regarded for their biological plausibility and energy efficiency in processing sequential data. However, dominant SNN architectures typically rely on first-order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) to govern neuronal state transitions. This first-order assumption imposes a "memoryless" bottleneck, limiting the model's capacity to capture the complex, long-range dependencies inherent in long-sequence tasks. In this work, we propose LongSpike, a novel SNN framework that integrates fractional-order State-Space Modeling, or f-SSM, from control theory into the spiking domain. By extending traditional integer-order SSMs to the fractional-calculus regime, LongSpike enables the hierarchical integration of neuronal dynamics with long-memory kernels. To mitigate the computational overhead and parallelization challenges typically associated with fractional operators, we leverage a state-space formulation that supports efficient, parallel training. Empirical evaluations on challenging benchmarks, including Long Range Arena (LRA), large-scale WikiText-103, and Speech Commands, demonstrate that LongSpike outperforms state-of-the-art SNNs in accuracy while preserving sparse synaptic computation. The code is available at https://github.com/xinruihe389-commits/LongSpike.

</details>


### [252] [Selecting Samples on Graphs: A Unified Dataset Pruning Framework for Lossless Training Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12913)
*Dongyue Wu,Zilin Guo,Xiaoyu Li,Jiajia Liu,Jingdong Chen,Nong Sang,Changxin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a unified graph-based dataset pruning framework that models datasets as weighted graphs, casting pruning as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem. A greedy algorithm with formal approximation guarantees outperforms existing methods, reducing training time by over 40% on ImageNet-1k without accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dataset pruning methods rely on either intrinsic signals (sample-wise importance) or extrinsic signals (pairwise diversity), each capturing only one aspect of sample utility. This leads to brittleness across different pruning ratios and data distributions. A unified framework integrating both signal types is needed for robust and effective pruning.

Method: The dataset is modeled as a weighted graph where node weights encode intrinsic value (sample importance) and edge weights encode extrinsic value (pairwise diversity/redundancy). Dataset pruning is formulated as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP). Since MWCP is NP-hard, a principled greedy algorithm based on sample-wise marginal gains is used. Under mild conditions, the authors prove a formal approximation guarantee that holds across a broad family of importance metrics.

Result: The method outperforms existing dataset pruning approaches across various pruning ratios and distributions. On ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50, it reduces training time by over 40% without sacrificing accuracy.

Conclusion: A unified graph-based framework that jointly captures intrinsic and extrinsic sample utility provides a principled, robust, and practically effective solution to dataset pruning, with strong theoretical backing and substantial empirical gains.

Abstract: The rapid growth of modern training datasets has significantly increased computational cost, motivating dataset pruning~(DP) methods which retain only a subset of informative samples to reduce training cost.
  Existing pruning criteria typically rely on either intrinsic signals that assess samples independently or extrinsic signals that promote diversity via pairwise relations.
  While effective in their own specific regimes, each captures only one aspect of sample utility and lacks robustness across different pruning ratios or data distribution.
  In this work, we present a unified graph-based DP framework.
  By modeling the dataset as a weighted graph, where node weights encode intrinsic value and edge weights encode extrinsic value, DP can be cast as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP).
  Although MWCP is NP-hard, its structure admits a principled greedy solution based on sample-wise marginal gains.
  Under a few mild conditions, we further prove that this unified objective enjoys a formal approximation guarantee, which applies to a broad family of importance metrics and provides practical design guidelines.
  Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing DP methods while substantially reducing training cost, reducing training time by over 40\% without sacrificing accuracy on ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50.

</details>


### [253] [Where Computation Lives Inside TabPFN: Causal Localisation of Attention Head Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12917)
*Atharva Gupta,Dhruv Kumar,Murari Mandal,Saurabh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First causal mechanistic analysis of TabPFN 2.5 reveals temporal specialization in feature-wise attention heads: one dominant head exhibits 2–5× greater causal necessity at its peak layer, with the peak layer shifting by task complexity, while remaining heads show symmetric late-layer profiles. Contrastive activation steering fails to transfer across samples due to TabPFN's context-dependent attention mechanism rather than stable parametric directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the growing adoption of tabular foundation models like TabPFN, their internal computational mechanisms remain opaque. Understanding how these models distribute computation across attention heads and layers is essential for interpretability, reliability, and potential controllability — analogous to mechanistic interpretability efforts in language models.

Method: The authors employ causal mechanistic analysis tools — activation patching, ablation, and attention entropy measurement — applied to TabPFN 2.5's feature-wise attention heads across two synthetic regression datasets of varying complexity. They also test inference-time steerability using contrastive activation steering to assess whether the model encodes task structure in stable parametric directions.

Result: A single attention head dominates causal necessity by 2–5× over others at its peak layer. The dominant head's peak layer shifts depending on task complexity, while non-dominant heads exhibit symmetric late-layer activity profiles. Attention entropy and patching results converge in identifying the computationally active layers. Contrastive activation steering fails to transfer across samples, revealing that task structure is encoded through context-dependent attention rather than stable directions.

Conclusion: TabPFN 2.5 exhibits clear temporal specialization among attention heads, with one head carrying disproportionate computational responsibility that adapts to task complexity. The failure of activation steering underscores a fundamental mechanistic difference from language models: TabPFN relies on in-context learning via dynamic, context-dependent attention patterns, making standard steering techniques ineffective and highlighting the need for new interpretability and control paradigms tailored to tabular foundation models.

Abstract: We present the first causal mechanistic analysis of a tabular foundation model, investigating how TabPFN 2.5's feature wise attention heads distribute computation across layers. Using activation patching, ablation, and attention entropy across two synthetic regression datasets, we find clear temporal specialisation: one head's causal necessity dominates that of the others by 2 to 5 times at peak layer, with its dominant layer shifting across tasks of different complexity, while the remaining heads exhibit symmetric late layer profiles. Attention entropy and patching provide convergent evidence for the computationally active layers of the dominant head. We additionally investigate inference time steerability via contrastive activation steering, which fails to transfer across samples. We attribute this result to TabPFN's in context learning mechanism, which encodes task structure through context dependent attention rather than the stable parametric directions that make steering tractable in language models.

</details>


### [254] [LoRA-Muon: Spectral Steepest Descent on the Low-Rank Manifold](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12921)
*Franz Louis Cesista,Katherine Crowson,Cédric Simal,Stella Biderman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRA-Muon adapts the Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule for low-rank fine-tuning. It solves LoRA's sensitivity to initialization, poor learning rate transfer across ranks, and underperformance versus dense baselines. The method serves as a low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon/Shampoo, with optimal learning rates that transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor rescaling. It also obviates QR decomposition and second-moment storage, improving accelerator efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA fine-tuning with standard factor-wise optimizers (e.g., AdamW) suffers from three problems: sensitivity to initialization choices, poor transferability of optimal learning rates across ranks, and frequent failure to match dense training baselines. The authors seek a low-rank optimizer that can serve as a reliable proxy for full-rank spectral optimizers like Muon and Shampoo, while being robust and easy to tune.

Method: The authors derive LoRA-Muon by applying Muon's spectral steepest-descent update rule to the low-rank setting. Key technical components include: (1) porting Muon's Newton-Schulz-based spectral normalization to operate on the low-rank factors, and (2) introducing a split weight-decay rule tailored for the low-rank factorization. The method avoids explicitly forming the full-rank gradient, sidestepping QR decomposition and second-moment storage as required by LoRA-RITE, making it more memory-efficient and accelerator-friendly. The authors also analyze Spectron, showing its update depends on arbitrary factor scaling, making it poorly suited when fine-tuning starts from imbalanced factors.

Result: In compute-matched experiments on TinyShakespeare, a rank-2 LoRA-Muon proxy recovers the dense model's best tested learning rate. A rank-32 LoRA-Muon run achieves lower mean validation loss than the dense baseline in a seed-averaged sweep. Optimal learning rates transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor rescaling. The analysis also shows Spectron's factor-scaling dependence and demonstrates that LoRA-RITE's simplified QR-coordinate core implements the same spectral update as LoRA-Muon, but less efficiently.

Conclusion: LoRA-Muon is an effective low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon and Shampoo-family optimizers, offering robust hyperparameter transfer and improved validation performance over dense baselines. Its design avoids the computational overhead of QR decomposition and second-moment storage, making it a practical choice for memory-efficient low-rank fine-tuning.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) significantly reduces compute and memory costs for finetuning Deep Learning models but is often harder to tune than dense training: when using factor-wise optimizers such as AdamW, it is sensitive to initialization choices, its optimal learning rates transfer poorly across ranks, and it often fails to beat dense baselines. We derive LoRA-Muon by applying the Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to the low-rank setting. Along with our split weight-decay rule, our main claim is that LoRA-Muon is a good low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon and Shampoo-family optimizers. Its optimal learning rates transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor-rescaling. In our compute-matched TinyShakespeare study, a rank-$2$ proxy recovers the dense best tested learning rate, and a rank-$32$ LoRA-Muon run attains lower mean validation loss than the dense baseline in the seed-averaged sweep. We further show that the Spectron optimizer depends on arbitrary factor scaling, so it would likely be a poor fit when finetuning starts from badly imbalanced factors, and that LoRA-RITE's simplified QR-coordinate core implements the same spectral update. LoRA-Muon computes that update without QR-decomposition and avoids storing second moments, making it more accelerator-friendly and memory-efficient.

</details>


### [255] [Order Is Not Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12923)
*Gareth Seneque,Lap-Hang Ho,Nafise Erfanian Saeedi,Jeffrey Molendijk,Tim Elson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that control in AI/biological systems requires a receiver-gated response law—a denominator-indexed operator mapping inputs to response displacement—rather than mere order identification. It provides empirical evidence across LLM, biological, and stochastic-operator panels showing local admitted control with predictable response vectors, while explicitly bounding the scope to exclude coordinate identity claims and pre-generation control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI alignment, interpretability, and neural perturbation studies conflate identifying order-inducing objects with achieving genuine control. The authors argue this is a category error: order is not control. True control requires a formal, receiver-gated response law grounded in driven-dissipative systems theory, and the paper aims to establish this distinction empirically across biological and artificial systems.

Method: The paper formulates a receiver-gated response law as a denominator-indexed operator mapping material state, action/drive, bath, and receiver state to response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. This framework is tested across multiple panels: (1) biological panels (mouse ALM, C. elegans, zebrafish) providing physical response-operator evidence; (2) LLM panels measuring generated-output response laws across four material conditions; (3) constitution-conditioned adapter panels examining susceptibility reshaping; and (4) stochastic-operator panels separating measured opportunity from deployable action policies.

Result: LLM panels show response vectors predictable at 72.8–73.7% component-sign accuracy, rising to 84.3–84.8% on nonzero components. Held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families at 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy respectively. Biological panels demonstrate physical response-operator evidence while excluding coordinate identity and controller conclusions. The framework identifies that interventions can be admitted, saturated, sign-changing, leaky, or overdriven depending on medium, bath, receiver state, action port, and comparator.

Conclusion: Control requires a local, receiver-gated response law; order-identification alone is insufficient. The evidence supports local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators across biological and LLM systems. However, the paper explicitly excludes deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities from its scope, establishing clear boundaries for the claims.

Abstract: AI alignment, interpretability, steering, and neural perturbation studies identify order-inducing objects. We argue that order is not control. Control requires a receiver-gated response law: a denominator-indexed operator mapping material state, action/drive, bath, and receiver state to response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. We identify it across biological, LLM, adapter, and stochastic-operator panels. The laws are local: an intervention can be admitted, saturated, sign-changing, leaky, or overdriven depending on medium, bath, receiver state, action port, and comparator. Control is assigned when finite effort moves a target or outcome-readout class under the same denominator while damage, null/evasive, invalid format, overdrive, and unnecessary effort stay bounded. Mouse ALM, C. elegans, and zebrafish panels provide physical response-operator evidence while excluding coordinate identity and controller conclusions. LLM panels show generated-output response laws: across four material conditions, response vectors are predictable at 72.8-73.7% component-sign accuracy, rising to 84.3-84.8% on nonzero components; held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families at 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy. Constitution-conditioned adapters reshape susceptibility as prepared media, and stochastic-operator panels separate measured opportunity from deployable action policies. This gives a driven-dissipative response-system account at the mesoscopic control level: drives act through prepared media, baths, and receivers, producing admitted movement, impedance, sinks, or overdrive. The evidence supports local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators, while leaving deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities outside scope.

</details>


### [256] [Is Spurious Correlation Removal Always Learnable?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12930)
*Yibo Zhou,Bo Li,Hai-Miao Hu,Hanzi Wang,Xiaokang Zhang,Ruifan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Invariant learning faces a computational barrier: even when the invariant subspace is identifiable, polynomial-time algorithms fail under a sparse-recovery primitive, while exhaustive search succeeds. The paper quantifies environment diversity via a separation parameter γ, derives minimax risk bounds, identifies a phase transition under label shifts, and validates findings on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To theoretically characterize when and why invariant learning algorithms fail despite statistical identifiability, and to understand how environment diversity quantitatively affects learnability, estimation error, and computational tractability.

Method: The authors construct a conditional computational hardness result by reducing from a black-box samplable supervised sparse recovery primitive. They introduce a separation parameter γ to measure environment diversity and analyze the curvature of invariance objectives. Under local Gaussian regularity and sufficient diversity, they derive minimax risk bounds and characterize a phase transition at sample size n* ∝ k(d−k)/(|E|γ²) under label-induced distribution shifts.

Result: A conditional computational barrier separates polynomial-sample exhaustive search from any polynomial-time constant-accuracy algorithm. The minimax risk for invariant subspace recovery is Θ(k(d−k)/(n|E|)). A phase transition occurs at n* with error scaling as 1/γ² beyond the threshold. Synthetic and real datasets confirm the predicted gaps, transitions, and motivate diversity diagnostics.

Conclusion: Invariant learning exhibits a fundamental computational-statistical gap driven by insufficient environment diversity. The separation parameter γ serves as a practical diagnostic for diversity, and the phase transition provides guidance on sample requirements for reliable invariant prediction under distribution shifts.

Abstract: Invariant learning can fail even when the invariant structure is statistically identifiable. We show a conditional computational barrier: under a black-box samplable supervised sparse recovery primitive motivated by average-case sparse-recovery reductions, there exist \emph{samplable} multi-environment instances with a one-dimensional predictive invariant subspace ($k=1$) that are learnable with polynomial samples by exhaustive search, while any polynomial-time constant-accuracy recovery algorithm would contradict the primitive. We further quantify environment diversity by a separation parameter $γ$, which controls identifiability and the curvature of invariance objectives. Under sufficient diversity and local Gaussian regularity, the minimax risk is $\mathbb{E}[\dist(\hat{V},V_{\mathrm{inv}})^2]=Θ(k(d-k)/(n|\mathcal{E}|))$, and under label-induced shifts a phase transition occurs at $n^*\propto k(d-k)/(|\mathcal{E}|γ^2)$ with refined estimation error scaling proportional to $1/γ^2$. Synthetic and real datasets illustrate the predicted gaps and transitions and motivate simple diversity diagnostics.

</details>


### [257] [Circuit Synchronization Precedes Generalization: Causal Evidence from Fourier Structure in Grokking Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12966)
*Achyuthan Sivasankar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a metric that predicts grokking in transformers ~1,700 steps before it occurs. The authors show that the "inter-phase gap" (delay between circuit formation and grokking) is controlled by weight decay, with timing proportional to 1/λ, and confirm that the Fourier circuit precursor requires multiple attention layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grokking in transformers—a sudden jump from chance to perfect accuracy—is attributed to Fourier circuits, but the timing, causal structure, and controllability of this phenomenon are poorly understood. Existing metrics like excluded loss detect grokking only near the transition, leaving a need for earlier predictors and causal understanding.

Method: The authors propose FSD, a normalized, permutation-tested metric that measures how synchronized a model's internal representations are with Fourier components, without requiring prior circuit knowledge. They test across nine configurations (five primes, three seeds), compare FSD timing against existing baselines, and perform causal interventions: forking training at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay λ to measure the effect on grokking timing. Architecture ablations (attention-only, MLP-only, single-layer) isolate which components are necessary.

Result: FSD synchronizes 500–3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps), outperforming excluded loss in all nine cases. Varying λ produces a strictly monotone relationship: Δt ∝ 1/λ (R²=1.00 and 0.99 in clean cases), consistent with theory Δt ~ (1/λ)·log(||W_mem||/τ). Attention-only models grok with strong FSD precursors; MLP-only models never grok; single-layer models show lagged FSD, confirming the precursor is a multi-block circuit property.

Conclusion: FSD is the earliest known predictor of grokking, and the inter-phase gap is a regularisation phenomenon controlled by weight decay. The Fourier circuit precursor requires multi-layer attention; MLPs alone are insufficient. These findings clarify the causal structure and controllability of grokking.

Abstract: Grokking -- where a transformer on modular arithmetic suddenly transitions from near-chance to near-perfect validation accuracy -- is attributed to a Fourier circuit, but its timing, causal structure, and controllability remain poorly understood. We introduce the Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalised, permutation-tested metric for Fourier circuit synchronisation requiring no prior circuit knowledge. Across nine modular addition configurations (primes p in {53, 71, 97, 113, 131}, three seeds), FSD synchronises 500-3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps; all nine positive, sign-test p~0.004), and precedes a restricted-logit loss baseline (Nanda et al.'s excluded loss) in all nine cases, making it the earliest available predictor. We provide direct causal evidence that the inter-phase gap is a regularisation phenomenon: forking training at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay lambda produces strictly monotone earlier grokking, with Delta_t proportional to 1/lambda. This law replicates across three primes (p in {53,97,131}; R^2=1.00 and R^2=0.99 for two clean cases), captured as Delta_t ~ C/lambda, consistent with (1/lambda)*log(||W_mem||/tau). Architecture ablations show an attention-only model groks with a strong FSD precursor; an MLP-only model never groks; a single-layer model's FSD lags, confirming the precursor is a multi-block circuit property.

</details>


### [258] [Predicting Cognitive Load from Speech and Interaction Dynamics in Dyadic Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12971)
*Tahiya Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Investigates whether speech and interaction dynamics from natural dyadic conversations can predict perceived cognitive load, finding that turn-taking features (overlap, speaker switch) predict temporal demand while imbalanced participation predicts mental demand.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on estimating cognitive load from speech has been confined to controlled lab settings; little is known about the reliability of such predictions in natural, collaborative conversational contexts.

Method: Audio recordings from 53 dyads performing nine collaborative tasks were analyzed. Static acoustic features, dynamic features, and interaction features were extracted and used to train a two-head Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) encoder for predicting cognitive load scores.

Result: Conversational interaction features provide useful signals for predicting cognitive load. Temporal demand correlates with turn-taking dynamics (overlap, speaker switch), while mental demand is linked to imbalanced participation between speakers.

Conclusion: Task structure and conversational interaction dynamics are critical for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings, extending beyond traditional lab-based speech-only approaches.

Abstract: Estimating cognitive load from speech has largely been studied in controlled laboratory settings, with limited understanding of its reliability in natural collaborative conversations. We investigate whether speech and interaction dynamics predict perceived cognitive load during dyadic conversations. We analyze audio from 53 dyads performing nine collaborative tasks and extract static acoustic, dynamic, and interaction features to train a two-head Gated Recurrent Unit encoder to predict cognitive load scores. Results show conversational interaction provides useful signals for predicting cognitive load related to time pressure, mental work, effort, and task performance. Temporal demand is associated with turn-taking dynamics such as overlap and speaker switch, while mental demand is linked to imbalanced participation between speakers. These findings highlight the importance of task structure and conversational interaction for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings.

</details>


### [259] [EPM-JEPA: Operator-Side Experience Modulation in JEPA-Family World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12979)
*Vedant Pandya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two mechanisms for injecting accumulated experience into JEPA world models under distribution shift are compared: operand-side residual injection (EI-JEPA) vs. operator-side LoRA weight modulation (EPM-JEPA). EPM-JEPA yields a null pre-registered result against EI-JEPA but consistently outperforms a no-memory baseline. The D_shift trajectory is decomposed into three independent dynamical processes rather than equilibrium convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: JEPA-family world models employ static predictors whose weights are fixed after training, making them brittle under test-time distribution shifts where dynamics diverge from the training distribution. The authors aim to investigate how accumulated test-time experience can be incorporated into the predictor to improve adaptation, comparing two fundamentally different injection mechanisms.

Method: Two mechanisms are compared: (1) EI-JEPA (operand-side injection), where a compressed experience representation is added as a residual to the predictor's hidden state; (2) EPM-JEPA (operator-side modulation), where the same compressed representation generates low-rank weight deltas via LoRA applied to the predictor's weights. Evaluation uses a pre-registered protocol on Moving MNIST with a gravity shift distribution. The D_shift^{n=50} metric quantifies distribution shift performance over 50-step rollouts, with three random seeds.

Result: Pre-registered comparison: EPM-JEPA (D_shift^{n=50} = 0.7848 ± 0.0078) vs. EI-JEPA (0.8238) yields Δ = 4.74%, classified as Outcome C (null result). Secondary non-pre-registered: EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over no-memory baseline (0.8000) consistently across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms baseline. Mechanistic analysis decomposes the D_shift trajectory into three independent processes: buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and an intrinsic LoRA settling transient of +0.021, indicating the system never reaches equilibrium but exhibits a dynamical peak.

Conclusion: Weight-level modulation (EPM-JEPA) provides a specific benefit over hidden-state injection for experience incorporation, though the pre-registered comparison against EI-JEPA was null. The trajectory analysis reveals the performance reflects transient dynamics rather than equilibrium convergence, motivating PEM-JEPA as a physics-grounded successor to address this dynamical-peak limitation.

Abstract: JEPA-family world models use a static predictor whose weights do not adapt when test-time dynamics diverge from training. We compare two mechanisms for incorporating accumulated experience into a JEPA predictor under distribution shift: operand-side injection, where a compressed experience representation is added as a residual to the predictor's hidden state (EI-JEPA), and operator-side modulation, where the same representation generates low-rank weight deltas via LoRA applied to the predictor's weights (EPM-JEPA). On a pre-registered comparison (Moving MNIST, gravity shift), EPM-JEPA (D_shift^{n=50} = 0.7848 +/- 0.0078, three seeds) differs from EI-JEPA (0.8238) by delta = 4.74% - Outcome C: a null result - by our stated criterion, a valid outcome. As a secondary, non-pre-registered observation, EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over a no-memory baseline (0.8000), consistently across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline, indicating the benefit is specific to weight-level modulation. Our primary contribution is a mechanism analysis: the D_shift^{n=50} trajectory reflects three independent dynamical processes - buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and an intrinsic LoRA settling transient of +0.021 - rather than convergence to equilibrium. These findings motivate PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor addressing this dynamical-peak limitation.

</details>


### [260] [Exposure Bias as Epistemic Underidentification in Recursive Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12990)
*Riku Green,Zahraa S. Abdallah,Telmo M Silva Filho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recursive forecasting is reformulated as an epistemic underidentification problem: one-step Bayes supervision fails to identify the correct recursive predictor because self-generated induced states create ambiguity that numeric state alone cannot resolve. The authors introduce induced states and provenance variables, decompose rollout error, and show empirically that provenance-aware correction can improve closed-loop performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard framing of recursive multi-step forecasting as a distribution shift problem (training on observed histories vs. deploying on self-generated predictions) is incomplete. The authors argue that under partial observability or state truncation, there is a deeper epistemic issue: one-step supervision does not uniquely determine the correct behavior during recursive rollout, even with deterministic latent dynamics.

Method: The authors formalize the problem using induced states Z (the information available to the model at each rollout step) and provenance variables P (encoding the causal origin of each state — whether it came from the data distribution or from prior model predictions). They derive a decomposition of induced-state error into three components: (1) teacher-forcing vs. rollout mismatch, (2) representation–class approximation error, and (3) provenance information gaps. They also propose a simple binary provenance encoding and a provenance-aware correction method to mitigate these gaps.

Result: Empirically, the authors demonstrate that: (1) recursive rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime different from teacher-forcing; (2) fixed induced states define a consistent local corrective task; (3) closed-loop improvements come from both local adaptation and changes in which induced states are visited during rollout; and (4) provenance-aware correction yields additional performance gains, though these gains are conditional rather than uniform across settings.

Conclusion: Recursive multi-step forecasting should be understood as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty, not merely as distribution shift. The induced-state/provenance framework provides a more complete account of exposure bias and suggests that explicitly modeling the provenance of states can improve closed-loop forecasting, albeit conditionally.

Abstract: Recursive multi-step forecasting is usually framed as distribution shift: models are trained on observed histories but deployed on their own predictions. We show this framing is incomplete by proving that, under partial observability or state truncation, recursive rollout is also an epistemic underidentification problem. Even with deterministic latent dynamics, one-step Bayes supervision identifies behavior only on observed contexts and need not identify the deployed recursive predictor once rollout queries self-generated induced states whose correct local targets are not determined by numeric state alone. We formalize this with induced states $Z$ and provenance variables $P$, and derive a decomposition of induced-state error into teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation--class approximation, and provenance information gaps. Empirically, we show that rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime, that fixed induced states define a distinct local corrective task, and that closed-loop gains arise not only from local adaptation but also from changing the induced states visited during rollout. Using a simple binary provenance encoding, provenance-aware correction can further improve performance, though gains are conditional rather than uniform. These results recast exposure bias as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty.

</details>


### [261] [DeepJEB++: Foundation Model-Driven Large-Scale 3D Engineering Dataset via 2D Latent Space Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12994)
*Soyoung Yoo,Leekyo Jeong,Jinsu Ra,Dongeon Lee,Sunwoong Yang,Hyogu Jeong,Namwoo Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeepJEB++ is a three-stage foundation-model-driven framework that augments a small set of jet engine bracket designs into a 40× larger, simulation-labeled 3D dataset by operating in the 2D latent space, lifting to 3D via a generative foundation model, and automatically assigning finite-element labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven engineering design suffers from a scarcity of large-scale 3D datasets that pair geometry with physics-based performance labels. Existing 3D augmentation methods fail to preserve subtle geometric variations, and automating simulation labeling is challenging because boundary conditions depend on the generated geometry.

Method: Stage 1: Fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders of seed brackets; synthesize novel views via latent interpolation with a VLM-based quality filter to ensure manufacturability. Stage 2: Lift validated 2D images to 3D meshes using a domain-adapted generative foundation model. Stage 3: Automatically detect load/bolt interfaces on each mesh and assign finite-element labels (mass, stress, displacement) via an automated pipeline.

Result: Starting from fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ generates 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets — a 40× expansion — using only a single GPU per stage. Quality is validated along three axes: manufacturability, label fidelity against SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency.

Conclusion: Foundation-model-driven augmentation in 2D latent space, followed by lifting to 3D and automated labeling, is a viable and resource-efficient strategy for scaling engineering datasets. The dataset will be publicly released to support reproducible engineering-AI research.

Abstract: Data-driven engineering design is constrained by the lack of large-scale 3D datasets that pair geometry with physics-based performance labels. In particular, existing 3D data augmentation techniques have limitations in preserving subtle and diverse geometric variations, and it remains difficult to automate the subsequent simulation-labeling process, where boundary conditions vary depending on the generated geometry. We present DeepJEB++, a foundation-model-driven data-augmentation framework that expands a small seed set of jet engine brackets into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset under constrained resources. Our key idea is to augment in the data-rich 2D latent space, then transfer to 3D. In Stage 1, we fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders and synthesize novel views by latent interpolation, retaining manufacturable designs through a vision-language-model (VLM) quality filter. In Stage 2, the validated images are lifted to 3D meshes by a domain-adapted generative foundation model. In Stage 3, an automated pipeline recognizes the load and bolt interfaces on each mesh and assigns finite-element labels -- mass, stress, and displacement -- without manual intervention. We assess augmentation quality along three intrinsic axes: manufacturability, label fidelity against the SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency. Starting from fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ yields 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets -- a 40x expansion -- using a single GPU per stage. The dataset will be made publicly available to support reproducible engineering-AI research.

</details>


### [262] [Reliability of Probabilistic Emulation of Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12997)
*Sam F. Greenbury,Radka Jersakova,Paolo Conti,Marjan Famili,Christopher Iliffe Sprague,Edwin Brown,Jason D. McEwen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compares generative models (diffusion/flow matching) vs. CRPS-trained ensembles for probabilistic forecasting. Finds CRPS ensembles offer more reliable uncertainty calibration and faster inference, especially in latent space. Generative models only match coverage when trained in ambient space, which is computationally prohibitive for high dimensions. Releases AutoCast and AutoSim frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles have shown strong predictive accuracy for physical system forecasting, no systematic assessment of their uncertainty reliability exists. The authors aim to fill this gap.

Method: Evaluates both approaches on diverse 2D spatiotemporal physical systems under matched model size and computational budget. Assesses reliability via empirical coverage of predictive intervals, plus accuracy and computational efficiency metrics. Compares ambient-space vs. latent-space training for both paradigms. Introduces AutoCast (modular framework) and AutoSim (dataset generation).

Result: CRPS-trained ensembles achieve more reliable uncertainties (better coverage) on both single-step and autoregressive rollouts compared to latent-space generative models. CRPS inference is significantly faster. Generative models in ambient space attain comparable coverage but with much higher latency. CRPS ensembles in latent space show no marked degradation vs. ambient space. Both approaches maintain good predictive accuracy.

Conclusion: CRPS-trained ensembles are the more practically favorable approach: they deliver well-calibrated uncertainties, fast inference, and robustness to latent-space training. Generative models require ambient-space training to match uncertainty quality, which is often infeasible for high-dimensional problems. Released frameworks facilitate further research.

Abstract: Two dominant approaches have emerged for generating probabilistic forecasts of physical systems: generative models, such as diffusion or flow matching; and ensembles of deterministic models with stochasticity injected, trained using the continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) loss. While both approaches have demonstrated strong predictive accuracy, the reliability of their uncertainties has not been systematically assessed. We address this gap by developing a framework to evaluate both approaches across diverse 2D spatiotemporal physical systems, under matched model size and computational budget. We assess the reliability of probabilistic emulation by inspecting the empirical coverage of predictive intervals, while also considering accuracy and computational efficiency metrics. CRPS-trained ensembles typically achieve more reliable uncertainties on both single-step prediction and autoregressive rollouts, demonstrating better coverage than the standard alternative of training generative models in a latent space. Moreover, the CRPS approach offers significantly faster inference. When generative models are trained in ambient rather than a compressed latent space, which is often infeasible for high-dimensional problems, they exhibit comparable coverage to CRPS-trained ensembles, though with substantially larger inference latency. In contrast, when CRPS-trained ensembles are trained in latent space they do not show a marked degradation in coverage with respect to ambient space. Both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles demonstrate good predictive accuracy. To facilitate future research and application, we release AutoCast, a modular framework implementing both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles, alongside AutoSim, a flexible dataset generation package for rapid prototyping.

</details>


### [263] [scLLM-DSC: LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13007)
*Ping Xu,Pengjiang Li,Tian Du,Zaitian Wang,Jiawei Gu,Ziyue Qiao,Pengfei Wang,Yuanchun Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: scLLM-DSC integrates LLM-derived gene semantics with transcriptomic topological structure via cross-modal contrastive alignment, achieving state-of-the-art scRNA-seq cell clustering by bridging the gap between generative LLMs and discriminative clustering tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing scRNA-seq clustering methods are semantically agnostic — they rely solely on numerical statistical patterns and neglect the biological functions encoded by genes. While LLMs capture rich biological semantics, their generative pre-training objective is structurally mismatched with discriminative downstream tasks like cell clustering. A framework is needed to effectively inject LLM-based gene knowledge into a clustering-compatible architecture.

Method: scLLM-DSC proposes a dual-view framework: (1) a Knowledge-Driven Semantic View that leverages NCBI gene priors and Cell2Sentence embeddings contextualized by LLMs to capture biological semantics; (2) a Structure-Aware Topological View extracted through a graph-guided encoder to preserve transcriptomic neighborhood relationships. These two views are integrated via a cross-modal contrastive alignment mechanism that enforces consistency between semantic and transcriptomic representations in a shared latent space, followed by deep structural clustering.

Result: Extensive benchmarking shows scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy, demonstrating the effectiveness of incorporating LLM-based gene semantics into scRNA-seq cell clustering.

Conclusion: Augmenting scRNA-seq clustering with LLM-derived biological semantics, when properly aligned with transcriptomic structure through cross-modal contrastive learning, yields substantial improvements over purely data-driven approaches. scLLM-DSC validates that bridging generative LLM knowledge and discriminative clustering is both feasible and beneficial.

Abstract: Clustering is fundamental to scRNA-seq analysis, serving as a cornerstone for identifying cell populations and resolving tissue heterogeneity. However, existing methods focus on mining numerical statistical patterns, suffering from semantic agnosticism by neglecting the intrinsic biological functions encoded by genes. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer promising semantic capabilities, their direct adaptation to cell clustering is hindered by the structural mismatch between generative pre-training objectives and discriminative downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose scLLM-DSC, a novel LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering framework. Diverging from data-driven paradigms, scLLM-DSC establishes a semantically-grounded representation by synergizing two views: a Knowledge-Driven Semantic View derived from NCBI gene priors and contextualized Cell2Sentence embeddings, and a Structure-Aware Topological View extracted via a graph-guided encoder. Crucially, we introduce a cross-modal contrastive alignment mechanism to enforce consistency between biological semantics and transcriptomic features within a unified latent space. Extensive benchmarks demonstrate that scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.

</details>


### [264] [CausalMoE: A Billion-Scale Multimodal Foundation Model for Granger Causal Discovery with Pattern-Routed Heterogeneous Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13024)
*Bo Liu,Di Dai,Jingwei Liu,Jiarui Jin,Xiaocheng Fang,Guangkun Nie,Hongyan Li,Shenda Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausalMoE is a billion-scale multimodal foundation model for Granger causal discovery that uses a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts to handle distribution shifts and dynamic regimes, combined with Causality-Aware Self-Attention for sparse graph recovery, and integrates LLMs/VLMs for multimodal priors, achieving SOTA in both fully supervised and few-shot settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural GCD methods use a "one-size-fits-all" approach that fails to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes in real-world time series, resulting in entangled representations and spurious causal graphs. There is a need for a model that explicitly addresses patch-level heterogeneity and regime-specific dynamics.

Method: CausalMoE introduces three key components: (1) A Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts that dynamically identifies latent temporal patterns and routes patches to specialized domain experts, decoupling regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics; (2) A Causality-Aware Self-Attention mechanism operating across variables with proximal optimization to yield sparse Granger causal graphs; (3) First integration of LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors for regularizing causal estimation.

Result: CausalMoE achieves new state-of-the-art performance on fully supervised benchmarks and demonstrates effective generalization in few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling patch-level heterogeneity via a mixture-of-experts architecture, combined with causality-aware attention and multimodal priors from LLMs/VLMs, significantly advances Granger causal discovery, particularly under distribution shifts and data-scarce regimes.

Abstract: Granger Causal Discovery (GCD) is fundamental for analyzing temporal dependencies in complex systems. However, existing neural GCD methods predominantly rely on a "one-size-fits-all" paradigm, struggling to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes inherent in real-world time series. This often leads to entangled representations and spurious causal graphs. In this paper, we propose CausalMoE, a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that explicitly models patch-level heterogeneity. CausalMoE introduces a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts, which dynamically identifies latent temporal patterns and routes patches to specialized domain experts, effectively decoupling regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics. To ensure interpretable graph recovery, we design a Causality-Aware Self-Attention mechanism operating across variables, yielding sparse Granger causal graphs via proximal optimization. Furthermore, CausalMoE is the first to integrate LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors, regularizing causal estimation in complex scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CausalMoE establishes a new state-of-the-art on fully supervised benchmarks, while effectively generalizing to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.

</details>


### [265] [TWLA: Achieving Ternary Weights and Low-Bit Activations for LLMs via Post-Training Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13054)
*Zhixiong Zhao,Zukang Xu,Zhixuan Chen,Xing Hu,Zhe Jiang,Dawei Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TWLA is a post-training quantization framework that achieves 1.58-bit weight ternarization and 4-bit activation quantization for LLMs. It combines an asymmetric ternary quantizer with manifold optimization, Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation for outlier suppression, and inter-layer-aware mixed-precision activation quantization to overcome heavy-tailed activation distributions, enabling end-to-end low-bit inference with high accuracy retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ternarization methods keep activations in high precision due to heavy-tailed activation distributions, which fundamentally limits end-to-end inference acceleration. The authors aim to simultaneously achieve low-bit weights (1.58-bit) and low-bit activations (4-bit) without significant accuracy degradation.

Method: TWLA consists of three components: (1) E2M-ATQ — a two-stage asymmetric ternary quantizer that first optimizes in Euclidean space then relocates onto the Stiefel manifold to minimize layer-output error; (2) KOTMS — applies a Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weight distributions into ternary-friendly tri-modal shapes while using the shared rotation to statistically suppress activation outliers; (3) ILA-AMP — an inter-layer-aware mixed-precision scheme that incorporates adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs into bit allocation and jointly optimizes for per-layer activation quantization gain disparities to prevent cascading errors from weak layers.

Result: Extensive experiments show that TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4 (1.58-bit weights, 4-bit activations) while delivering significant inference speedup compared to methods that retain high-precision activations.

Conclusion: TWLA demonstrates that simultaneous low-bit weight ternarization and activation quantization is feasible for LLMs by addressing both the weight ternarization optimization problem and the activation outlier challenge through orthogonal transformations and inter-layer-aware mixed precision, enabling practical end-to-end accelerated inference.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit exceptional general language processing capabilities, but their memory and compute costs hinder deployment. Ternarization has emerged as a promising compression technique, offering significant reductions in model size and inference complexity. However, existing methods struggle with heavy-tailed activation distributions and therefore keep activations in high precision, fundamentally limiting end-to-end inference acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose TWLA, a post-training quantization (PTQ) framework that achieves 1.58-bit weight compression and 4-bit activation quantization while maintaining high accuracy. TWLA comprises three components: (1) Euclidean-to-Manifold Asymmetric Ternary Quantizer (E2M-ATQ) minimizes layer-output error under weight ternarization via a two-stage optimization from Euclidean initialization to manifold relocation; (2) Kronecker Orthogonal Tri-Modal Shaping (KOTMS) applies a Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weights into ternary-friendly tri-modal distributions, while the shared rotation statistically suppresses activation outliers; and (3) Inter-Layer Aware Activation Mixed Precision (ILA-AMP) explicitly introduces adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs in bit allocation and jointly optimizes for the layer-wise disparity of activation quantization gains induced by the shared orthogonal transform, preventing cascades triggered by a few weak layers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4, while delivering significant inference acceleration. The code is available at <https://github.com/Kishon-zzx/TWLA>.

</details>


### [266] [A green solvent screening tool for emerging materials via uncertainty aware, transformer enhanced transfer learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13060)
*Ioannis Kouroudis,Simon Ternes,Zhaosu Gu,Gohar Ali Siddiqui,Marina Ustinova,Angelo Lembo,Alessio Gagliardi,Aldo Di Carlo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses the data scarcity problem in predicting solubility parameters for green solvent discovery by transferring a pre-trained foundational model (trained on QM9) to predict multiple solubility descriptors (Hansen parameters, Dielectric Constant, Gutmann Donor/Acceptor numbers) with minimal data, incorporating uncertainty quantification, and deploying a practical screening tool that successfully rediscovers known green solvents and proposes new candidates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solvent usage is projected to increase substantially due to emerging technologies (organic/hybrid photovoltaics, batteries, catalysis), making green solvent substitution critical. However, machine learning approaches are severely constrained by limited data on key solubility parameters, necessitating methods that can achieve high predictive performance with minimal training data.

Method: A transfer learning approach where a foundational model pre-trained on QM9 quantum chemical targets is fine-tuned for solubility descriptor prediction with minimal data requirements. The pipeline integrates uncertainty quantification for confidence assessment. Targets include Hansen solubility parameters, Dielectric Constant (extensive data available as baseline), and Gutmann Donor/Acceptor numbers (extremely limited data). Results are deployed as an easy-to-use, customizable screening and ranking tool for solvent substitution.

Result: The model successfully predicts Hansen solubility parameters and Dielectric Constant (baseline targets with extensive databases). Critically, high model performance is achieved on Gutmann Donor and Acceptor numbers despite extremely limited available data. The approach augments solubility descriptor data by orders of magnitude with high-quality predictions. The deployed tool rediscovers known green solvent alternatives and proposes new eco-friendly candidates.

Conclusion: Transfer learning from a QM9 pre-trained foundational model effectively overcomes data scarcity in solubility prediction, enabling high-quality predictions across multiple descriptors. The integration of uncertainty quantification and deployment as a practical screening tool demonstrates real-world applicability for accelerating green solvent discovery, validated by the rediscovery of known alternatives and identification of novel candidates.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of solubility remains a central challenge across materials science and sustainable chemistry. In particular due to emerging technologies like organic and hybrid photovoltaics, batteries, and catalysis, solvent usage is expected to increase significantly within the coming years. Therefore, substituting solvents with greener alternatives is vital. This is where machine learning can have substantial impact. However, the limited data on critical parameters of solubility significantly constraints machine learning efficacy. In this work, we transfer a pre-trained foundational model on QM9 targets to our application with minimal data requirements. Additionally, the pipeline integrates uncertainty quantification, allowing the user to gauge the confidence of the predictions. As baseline, we succeed in predicting the Hansen solubility parameters and Dielectric Constant for which extensive databases exist. Importantly, we achieve high model performance on additional targets, such as Gutmann Donor and Acceptor numbers, where the available data is extremely limited. Overall, we augment data on solubility descriptors by orders of magnitude with high quality predictions. For effective dissemination, we deploy easy-to-use, easily integrateable with high throughput labs, customizable tool for ranking and screening possible solvent substitutes. Finally, we rediscovered known green solvent alternatives and proposed new candidates proving its relevance for finding eco-friendly solvents.

</details>


### [267] [Limits of spectral learning under noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13067)
*Sabin Roman,Ljupco Todorovski,Saso Dzeroski,Marta Sales-Pardo,Roger Guimera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral methods for regression suffer from predictable coefficient drift under additive label noise. The authors derive a closed-form overlap expression between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors, governed by a single intrinsic noise scale, and identify a fundamental noise threshold beyond which coefficient recovery becomes unstable. Predictions are confirmed across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spectral methods approximate functions via basis expansions, but the stability of estimated coefficients under measurement noise is poorly understood.
