Motivation: Standard diffusion model guidance is limited to simple cases like conditional sampling; the target distribution is often implicitly defined through heuristics. There is a need for a principled, more expressive framework that can handle complex distributional targets beyond what standard guidance can express.

Method: The authors propose Jeffrey guidance, which applies Jeffrey's rule of conditioning — a general Bayesian updating principle — to diffusion models. It updates the marginal distribution of the model toward a prescribed target distribution while preserving the conditional structure and minimally perturbing the joint distribution in a KL-divergence sense.

Result: Targeting a prescribed Inception embedding distribution led to substantial FID reductions on CIFAR-10 and FFHQ. On CelebA-HQ, Jeffrey guidance successfully updated an unconditional diffusion model to enforce statistical independence between attributes for fairness.

Conclusion: Jeffrey guidance provides a principled, flexible framework for diffusion model control that subsumes and extends standard guidance, enabling applications like distribution-matching and fairness-constrained generation.

Abstract: A key strength of diffusion models lies in their flexibility, since their outputs can be controlled at sampling time through guidance. However, beyond simple cases such as conditional sampling, the target distribution is often left implicit, defined only through a sampling rule or a heuristic energy function. To address this, we propose Jeffrey guidance, a principled framework that extends diffusion-model control to applications beyond what standard guidance can express. It leverages Jeffrey's rule of conditioning to update marginal distributions towards a prescribed target, preserving the conditional structure and minimally perturbing the joint distribution. We first demonstrate Jeffrey guidance by targeting a prescribed embedding distribution. With Inception embeddings as the target, this leads to substantial reductions in FID on both CIFAR-10 and FFHQ. We further apply Jeffrey guidance to fairness on CelebA-HQ, updating an unconditional diffusion model to enforce independence between attributes.

</details>


### [291] [To GAN or Not To GAN: Segmentation Analysis on Mars DEM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13252)
*Douglas Dziedzorm Agbeve,Aditya V. Handrale,Salim Fares,Seif E. Idani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes automatic detection of Martian mounds using neural network-based semantic segmentation, comparing supervised segmentation with a generative adversarial approach, and finding that synthetic data augmentation did not improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual mapping of mounds on Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) is labor-intensive. Automating mound detection on Mars is essential for rover navigation and for identifying water-related or life-conducive environments, aiding the search for extraterrestrial life.

Method: The paper employs two neural network-based semantic segmentation methodologies: (1) a supervised semantic segmentation model, and (2) a generative adversarial network (GAN) approach. The GAN is used to generate artificial training data, and results from both approaches are compared to evaluate the impact of synthetic data augmentation.

Result: The comparison reveals that incorporating artificially generated data via the GAN-based approach did not improve detection performance over the purely supervised semantic segmentation model alone.

Conclusion: GAN-based synthetic data augmentation offers no benefit for Martian mound detection in this context; a standard supervised semantic segmentation approach suffices for automating mound identification on Mars.

Abstract: To better understand Martian Surface, which is needed to enable Rovers navigate Mars with ease, it is necessary to be able to determine the location of mounds. Detecting and studying these morphologies can also help us find evidence of extraterrestrial life, in this case, more specifically, water or signs of life conducive environments. Detection of mounds was done by manually mapping morphological parameters onto Digital Elevation Models. This paper solves the problem by automatically detecting and or predicting mounds on Mars using Neural Network based Semantic Segmentation methodologies. This is done by using supervised semantic segmentation model and generative adversarial approach. A comparison of the approaches shows that adding extra artificially generated data did not improve the result.

</details>


### [292] [Extracting Governing Equations from Latent Dynamics via Multi-View Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13260)
*Paolo Muratore,Mackenzie Weygandt Mathis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DYSCO is a multi-view temporal contrastive learning algorithm that recovers latent dynamical systems from noisy, high-dimensional measurements. It disentangles signal from noise by leveraging multiple independent views of the same underlying process, parameterizes dynamics in a structured functional basis for symbolic equation recovery, and provides theoretical identifiability guarantees up to an affine indeterminacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for identifying latent dynamical systems from high-dimensional observations struggle under realistic noise conditions and lack theoretical guarantees for nonlinear observations. The authors aim to bridge this gap by exploiting multi-view data—multiple independent noisy observations of the same latent process—to achieve provable identification and symbolic recovery of governing equations.

Method: DYSCO employs multi-view temporal contrastive learning: it processes multiple independent noisy views of the same latent trajectory through an encoder that extracts shared latent states. The dynamics are parameterized in a structured functional basis (enabling symbolic recovery within an affine gauge). Contrastive objectives align representations across views and time, separating signal from noise. Theoretical analysis extends identifiability results to the nonlinear noisy observation setting.

Result: DYSCO achieves accurate recovery of latent trajectories and flow fields across chaotic, oscillatory, and metastable dynamical regimes. It performs robustly under both Gaussian and Poisson observation noise, with the latter demonstrated as relevant for neural recordings. Theoretical guarantees establish strong identification up to an affine indeterminacy.

Conclusion: DYSCO demonstrates that multi-view contrastive learning can provably recover latent dynamical systems from noisy nonlinear observations, enabling both trajectory reconstruction and symbolic discovery of governing equations, with practical relevance for scientific domains including neuroscience.

Abstract: Identifying latent dynamical systems from noisy, high-dimensional measurements is a central problem at the intersection of representation learning, system identification, and scientific discovery. We present DYSCO, a multi-view temporal contrastive learning algorithm that jointly recovers latent trajectories and the governing dynamics from such observations, by leveraging multiple independent noisy views of the same underlying process to disentangle signal from noise. By parameterizing the dynamics in a structured functional basis, our framework further enables symbolic recovery of the governing equations within an affine gauge. We offer theoretical guarantees for strong identification up to an affine indeterminacy, extending prior identifiability results to the realistic setting of noisy nonlinear observations. Empirically, we demonstrate accurate recovery of both latent trajectories and flow fields across a diverse set of dynamical regimes (e.g., chaotic, oscillatory, and metastable) under both Gaussian and Poisson observation noise, the latter being particularly relevant for neural recordings.

</details>


### [293] [Different Layers, Different Manifolds: Module-Wise Weight-Space Geometry in Transformer Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13276)
*Kirato Yoshihara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether different transformer modules (attention vs. MLP) benefit from different manifold constraints during optimization. Results show attention layers perform best under Stiefel geometry while MLP layers thrive under DGram geometry; reversing or uniformizing these assignments causes instability due to singular value growth and softmax saturation in DGram-constrained attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manifold constraints in neural network optimization (e.g., Stiefel, DGram) are typically applied uniformly across all weight matrices, but different transformer modules may have distinct geometric preferences due to their different functional roles. The authors aim to uncover potential asymmetries.

Method: The authors study Manifold Muon for GPT-2 pretraining, systematically comparing layer-wise assignments of Stiefel and DGram constraints across attention and MLP blocks. They evaluate all four combinations of {Stiefel, DGram} × {Attention, MLP} under a shared hyperparameter regime.

Result: A clear asymmetry emerges: assigning Stiefel to attention layers and DGram to MLP layers yields the best performance. The inverted assignment (DGram-attention, Stiefel-MLP) and the all-DGram configuration become unstable. The instability is traced to singular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights, which amplifies attention logits and induces softmax saturation.

Conclusion: Symmetry-aware and geometry-aware optimization for transformers should be module-specific rather than uniform. The functional differences between attention and MLP modules dictate distinct preferred manifold geometries.

Abstract: Weight-space geometry plays a central role in neural network optimization, yet manifold constraints are often applied uniformly across all weight matrices. In this work, we ask whether different transformer modules prefer different manifold geometries. We study Manifold Muon for GPT-2 pretraining and compare layer-wise assignments of Stiefel and DGram constraints across attention and MLP blocks. Our results show a clear asymmetry: constraining attention layers with Stiefel geometry while assigning DGram geometry to MLP layers gives the best performance among the tested configurations, whereas the inverted assignment and all-DGram configuration become unstable under the shared hyperparameter setting. We trace this failure to singular value growth in DGram-constrained attention weights, which can amplify attention logits and induce softmax saturation. These findings suggest that symmetry-aware and geometry-aware optimization for transformers should be module-specific rather than uniform.

</details>


### [294] [Once-for-All: Scalable Simultaneous Forecasting via Equilibrium State Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13285)
*Beinan Xu,Andy Song,Jiti Gao,Feng Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ESE introduces a paradigm for simultaneous multi-system forecasting by estimating an equilibrium state, then predicting all systems at once based on deviation from equilibrium. It matches SOTA accuracy while achieving 10-70x speedup with linear-time complexity and strong robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world domains like economics and healthcare involve multiple interacting systems that need separate but coordinated forecasts. Existing methods predict one system at a time, which is computationally inefficient and fails to exploit inter-system relationships. There is a need for a fast, scalable approach that jointly forecasts all systems while maintaining accuracy.

Method: ESE operates in a single pass: it first estimates a global equilibrium state across all interacting systems, then generates forecasts by measuring the difference (deviation) between each system's current state and the estimated equilibrium. This deviation-from-equilibrium formulation enables holistic, coordinated predictions with linear-time complexity.

Result: On synthetic and real-world datasets (currency exchange, COVID-19 spread), ESE matches or exceeds SOTA accuracy. It achieves 10-70x inference speedup when integrated with conventional predictors. Its linear-time complexity ensures superior scalability as the number of systems grows, and it remains accurate under diverse perturbations.

Conclusion: ESE is a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction method. It matches SOTA accuracy while dramatically improving speed and scalability, and integrates seamlessly with existing predictors to combine their strengths.

Abstract: We introduce Equilibrium State Estimation (ESE), a novel paradigm for simultaneous prediction, where multiple interacting systems require separate yet coordinated forecasts. Such scenarios often arise in real-world settings such as economics and healthcare modeling. Unlike existing approaches that predict one system at a time, ESE forecasts all systems in a single pass. It first estimates the equilibrium state across systems, then generates holistic forecasts based on the difference between the current state and the estimated equilibrium. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets, including currency exchange and COVID-19 spread modeling, demonstrate that ESE is at least as accurate as state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods while being significantly faster. In addition, ESE integrates seamlessly with conventional predictors, combining their accuracy with its exceptional efficiency and delivering a 10-70x speedup. With linear-time complexity, ESE scales far better than SOTA methods as the number of systems increases. Moreover, it remains accurate under diverse perturbations, establishing ESE as a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction method.

</details>


### [295] [Clipping Makes Distributed and Federated Asynchronous SGD Robust to Stragglers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13287)
*Samuel Erickson,Mikael Johansson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides theoretical justification for why gradient clipping stabilizes asynchronous SGD (ASGD). Using a sub-Weibull noise model that captures heavy-tailed gradient noise in deep learning, the authors prove that clipping eliminates dependence on the maximum delay in oracle complexity, and establish both convergence in expectation and high-probability convergence — the latter being a first for asynchronous optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ASGD scales training by avoiding idle time waiting for slow workers, but constant-step-size ASGD suffers from large delays caused by stragglers, negatively affecting convergence. Empirically, gradient clipping is known to stabilize asynchronous deep learning training, yet a rigorous theoretical explanation for this phenomenon was lacking.

Method: The authors adopt a sub-Weibull model for gradient noise, which generalizes sub-Gaussian and sub-exponential distributions to heavy-tailed settings observed in deep learning. Under this framework, they analyze ASGD with gradient clipping and derive bounds showing that clipping removes the dependence on the maximum delay in oracle complexity. They prove convergence in expectation and, for the first time in asynchronous optimization, convergence with high probability.

Result: Gradient clipping provably removes the dependence on the maximum delay in the oracle complexity of ASGD. The authors establish convergence guarantees both in expectation and with high probability under the sub-Weibull noise model, providing the first high-probability convergence result for asynchronous optimization.

Conclusion: Gradient clipping serves as an effective mechanism to mitigate the adverse effects of delayed updates from slow workers in ASGD. The theoretical results formally justify the empirical practice of clipping for stabilizing asynchronous training of deep neural networks.

Abstract: In modern machine learning, parallelization of training is an important strategy for increasing scale. Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent (ASGD), which maximizes the utilization of available hardware by avoiding waiting for slow workers. However, with constant step sizes, the convergence of ASGD is nonetheless affected negatively by slow workers due to large delays in updates. At the same time, it has been empirically observed in asynchronous training of deep learning models that gradient clipping "stabilizes" training. In this work, we provide a theoretical justification for this behavior, as we show that clipping removes the dependence of the maximum delay in the oracle complexity. We employ a sub-Weibull model of gradient noise which generalizes sub-Gaussian and sub-exponential distributions to more heavy-tailed distributions, motivated by empirical observations in deep learning. We show convergence in expectation, and the first time in asynchronous optimization, convergence with high probability.

</details>


### [296] [Quantizing Time-Series Models As Dynamical Systems: Trajectory-Based Quantization Sensitivity Score](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13300)
*Mariya Pavlova,Harrison Bo Hua Zhu,Elizsveta Semenova,Yingzhen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TQS introduces a dynamical-systems stability lens for post-training quantization sensitivity, decoupling sensitivity analysis from quantizer selection. This enables a priori, calibration-free mixed-precision planning (TQS-PTQ) suitable for black-box or fused-operator settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional PTQ methods tightly couple sensitivity analysis to specific quantization procedures, bit-widths, and calibration data, limiting flexibility and applicability to black-box or compiled networks with fused operators. A decoupled, a priori sensitivity metric is needed for efficient quantization budget planning without costly second-order approximations or calibration data.

Method: The authors model a network's forward pass as a discrete-time dynamical system and define the Trajectory-based Quantization Sensitivity Score (TQS), which quantifies how quantization-induced errors propagate and amplify over the rollout horizon. TQS is computed independently of quantizer choice and bit-width, enabling a priori sensitivity estimation. This score is then integrated into TQS-PTQ, a flexible mixed-precision quantization framework.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that the dynamical-systems perspective yields a robust, high-performing mixed-precision PTQ framework, effective for low-precision deployment in resource-constrained environments, without requiring calibration data or second-order approximations.

Conclusion: A dynamical-systems stability approach to quantization sensitivity (TQS) successfully decouples sensitivity estimation from the quantization process, enabling flexible, calibration-free, and black-box-compatible mixed-precision deployment.

Abstract: We introduce the Trajectory-based Quantization Sensitivity Score (TQS), a metric that reframes post-training quantization (PTQ) through the lens of dynamical-systems stability. By modeling the network's rollout as a discrete-time dynamical system, TQS characterizes how quantization-induced errors propagate and amplify over the rollout horizon. Unlike conventional PTQ methods, where sensitivity analysis is often coupled to the quantization procedure, TQS enables a priori sensitivity estimation decoupled from quantizer selection and bit-width assignment. This separation allows for quantization budget planning even for black-box or compiled networks with fused operators. Building on this, we present TQS-PTQ, a flexible mixed-precision framework that requires no calibration data or costly second-order approximations. Our experiments show that a dynamical-systems perspective provides a robust, high-performing pathway for low-precision deployment in resource-constrained settings.

</details>


### [297] [Rarity-Gated Context Conditioning for Offline Imitation Learning-Based Maritime Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13311)
*Yongmin Kim,ByeongHoon Jeon,Sungil Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RGFiLM introduces a rarity-aware conditioning module that uses a data-driven rarity score to gate feature-wise linear modulation, reducing false alarms in rare contexts for imbalanced contextual anomaly detection tasks. Tested on maritime trajectory anomaly detection, it achieves the best F1-FPR trade-off against context-agnostic and context-conditioned baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contextual anomaly detection suffers from frequency bias: rare context regimes — often the most critical — cause context-conditioned models to produce unstable decisions and excessive false alarms. Existing conditioning methods do not explicitly account for context rarity.

Method: RGFiLM (Rarity-Gated Feature-wise Linear Modulation) augments standard FiLM (scaling and shifting of hidden features conditioned on context) with a gating mechanism controlled by a rarity score estimated from the empirical distribution of context variables. Under rare contexts, the gate becomes more decisive; under frequent contexts, modulation remains conservative.

Result: Evaluated on maritime trajectory anomaly detection using AIS motion sequences with ERA5 environmental context in an environment-sensitive detour scenario, RGFiLM achieved the best mean F1–FPR trade-off among all compared context-agnostic and context-conditioned methods.

Conclusion: Explicitly accounting for context rarity via gated feature-wise modulation is an effective strategy for mitigating false alarms in context-sensitive anomaly detection under imbalanced context distributions.

Abstract: Contextual anomaly detection aims to identify abnormal behavior conditional on context variables, but practical deployments often face highly imbalanced context distributions where rare regimes can be critical information. Under such frequency bias, context-conditioned models can produce unstable decisions and excessive false alarms in rare contexts. We propose Rarity-Gated Feature-wise Linear Modulation (RGFiLM), a rarity-aware conditioning module that combines feature-wise modulation (i.e., context-conditioned scaling and shifting of hidden features) with a gate controlled by a data-driven rarity score. The rarity score is estimated from the empirical distribution of context variables and regulates how strongly context modulates intermediate representations: the gate becomes more decisive under rare contexts while remaining conservative under frequent contexts. We evaluate RGFiLM on maritime trajectory anomaly detection using AIS motion sequences with ERA5 environmental context in an environment-sensitive detour scenario. When instantiated in a sequential anomaly scoring pipeline, RGFiLM achieves the best mean F1--False Positive Rate (FPR) trade-off among the compared context-agnostic and context-conditioned methods. These results suggest that explicitly accounting for context rarity is an effective approach for reducing false alarms in context-sensitive anomaly detection.

</details>


### [298] [Navigating the Safety-Fidelity Trade-off: Massive-Variate Time Series Forecasting for Power Systems via Probabilistic Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13338)
*Kaijie Xu,Anqi Wang,Xilin Dai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PowerPhase is a large-scale probabilistic forecasting benchmark with up to 36,964 channels from AC power-flow grids, featuring constraint-aware safety metrics. PowerForge, a causal quantile forecaster with type-specific heads, achieves best average rank, revealing a safety-fidelity trade-off between distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multivariate probabilistic forecasting benchmarks are limited to ~2,000 channels and lack both channel-specific physics and operational constraints. Power-system benchmarks either omit temporal structure or probabilistic evaluation. There is a need for a large-scale benchmark that jointly evaluates distributional forecast accuracy and physical constraint adherence in realistic grid settings.

Method: The authors introduce PowerPhase, a benchmark spanning six transmission grids (2,000–36,964 channels). Each target trajectory is produced by an AC power-flow solve, grounding forecasts in physical grid physics. Novel constraint-aware metrics — Safety_mBrier, NECV (Normalized Expected Constraint Violation), and CVaR-alpha — are proposed alongside standard CRPS and Distortion. Eight baselines are evaluated across three seeds. Additionally, PowerForge is proposed: a scenario-based quantile forecaster employing type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups to exploit physical dependencies.

Result: Distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction metrics rank models differently, exposing a "safety-fidelity" trade-off. PowerForge achieves the best average rank across all six grids, demonstrating that modeling type-specific structure and causal relationships improves both forecast quality and constraint compliance.

Conclusion: Probabilistic forecasting at grid scale requires joint evaluation of statistical fidelity and physical constraint satisfaction. PowerPhase fills this gap as the largest such benchmark, and PowerForge shows that architecture designs respecting physical variable structure can improve the safety-fidelity Pareto frontier.

Abstract: Probabilistic forecasting models are increasingly deployed on multivariate systems with distinct channel physics and operational constraints, but existing benchmarks evaluate neither property at scale. Public canonical multivariate benchmarks cap out at 2,000 channels, while power-system benchmarks either lack temporal structure or probabilistic evaluation. We introduce PowerPhase, a probabilistic forecasting benchmark built on six transmission grids ranging from 2,000 to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels, more than an order of magnitude beyond popular canonical multivariate benchmarks. Each target trajectory is the output of an AC power-flow solve, and PowerPhase ships with constraint-aware metrics, including Safety_mBrier, NECV, and CVaR-alpha, that complement CRPS and Distortion. Across eight baselines and three seeds, distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction rank models differently, a trade-off we term safety-fidelity. We further propose PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster with type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups, which achieves the best average rank on every grid.

</details>


### [299] [Enhanced Low-Density Region Exploration in Classifier-Guided Diffusion Models Through Modified Reverse Diffusion Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13347)
*Jagriti Singh,Shekhar Verma,Muneendra Ojha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-free, sampling-time method that extends classifier-guided diffusion models to improve long-tail sample coverage by steering trajectories toward low-confidence regions and toward predicted real images, improving recall while maintaining FID.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard classifier-guided diffusion concentrates probability mass around high-density class means, yielding poor coverage of rare/tail samples. Existing tail-sampling methods require additional networks and training (e.g., low-density-seeking classifiers with synthetic-vs-real discriminators), while other sampler improvements do not explicitly address long-tail coverage.

Method: A purely sampling-time, density-aware extension using two forms of guidance applied at noisy images (not on predicted noise): (1) steering trajectories toward low-confidence regions via modified classifier gradients to explore low-probability samples, and (2) guiding toward the predicted real image at each timestep to keep samples near the real data manifold. Both build on a pretrained conditional diffusion model and classifier on ImageNet, requiring no additional training.

Result: The proposed sampler consistently improves ADM model recall at 64×64 resolution while maintaining comparable FID. At 256×256, visual results demonstrate the effect of different combinations of both guidance types. Standard ADM classifier guidance combined with predicted real image guidance also yields high perceptual quality samples on ImageNet at 256×256.

Conclusion: Low-density-seeking guidance can be achieved at sampling time without extra training, effectively improving tail coverage. The dual-guidance approach (low-confidence steering + real-image guidance) balances exploration of rare samples with fidelity to the data manifold.

Abstract: Diffusion models have emerged as state-of-the-art generative models for high-fidelity image synthesis, particularly in their classifier-free guided and classifier-guided forms. However, standard classifier guidance concentrates probability mass around high-density class mean, leading to poor coverage of rare samples in the tails of the class-conditional distributions. Recent work on diffusion-based tail sampling mitigates this by training an additional low-density-seeking classifier with a synthetic-vs-real discriminator, at the cost of additional networks and training. In parallel, a number of samplers and distillation techniques accelerate or refine diffusion sampling, but do not explicitly address long-tail coverage. We propose a purely sampling-time, density-aware extension of classifier-guided conditional diffusion model that targets low-density regions without any additional training. We have applied guidance at noisy images not on predicted noise like most diffusion models. Starting from a pretrained conditional diffusion model and classifier on ImageNet, we modify the guided reverse dynamics by steering trajectories toward low-confidence regions via the modified classifier gradient, and at each time step, we also guide the sampling process toward the predicted real image. 1st guidance helps explore low-probability samples, and 2nd guidance helps to generate samples to be close to the real data manifold. The proposed sampler consistently improves ADM model recall at 64x64 resolution while maintaining a comparable FID, and with a 256x256 ADM model, we showed the results visually with different combinations of both guidance. We also showed that standard ADM classifier guidance, combined with predicted real image guidance, helps generate high perceptual quality samples with a 256x256 ADM model on ImageNet.

</details>


### [300] [VideoMDM: Towards 3D Human Motion Generation From 2D Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13364)
*Amir Mann,Gal Michael Harari,Merav Keidar,Or Litany*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VideoMDM trains a diffusion-based 3D human motion prior using only 2D pose supervision from monocular videos, bypassing the need for 3D ground truth. A noisy 2D-to-3D lifter provides approximate 3D poses, which are diffused, denoised in 3D, and supervised via a depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss that is equivalent in expectation to 3D supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Acquiring high-quality 3D human motion ground truth is expensive and requires specialized capture setups, limiting scalability. Monocular video is abundant, but existing methods typically lift 2D to 3D only at inference rather than learning a true 3D motion prior during training. The authors aim to bridge the gap between 2D-supervised and fully 3D-supervised motion generation.

Method: The framework consists of: (1) a pretrained 2D-to-3D lifter that produces noisy approximate 3D poses from accurate 2D keypoints; (2) a diffusion model that learns to denoise these 3D pose sequences; (3) a novel depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss that, under mild assumptions, is equivalent in expectation to direct 3D supervision; and (4) adapted 3D motion regularizers—velocity consistency and over-parameterized representation alignment—reformulated for the 2D setting.

Result: On HumanML3D, VideoMDM achieves an FID of 0.88 compared to 0.54 for fully 3D-supervised MDM, nearly closing the gap. On real-world video datasets (Fit3D, NBA), the method generates motions consistently preferred by human evaluators and yields strong quantitative metrics.

Conclusion: It is possible to learn a coherent 3D human motion manifold during training using only 2D supervision, effectively matching the performance of fully 3D-supervised diffusion models and enabling scalable motion generation from abundant monocular video data.

Abstract: We introduce VideoMDM, a diffusion-based framework that trains 3D human motion priors directly from accurate 2D poses extracted from monocular videos, without any 3D ground truth. A pretrained 2D-to-3D lifter provides approximate 3D pose sequences that serve as a noisy teacher: these are diffused, denoised by the model in 3D, and supervised in 2D by reprojecting the prediction and comparing against accurate keypoints. We show that, under mild assumptions, a depth-weighted 2D reprojection loss is equivalent in expectation to direct 3D supervision, and we adapt standard 3D motion regularizers - velocity consistency and over-parameterized representation alignment - to this 2D setting. Unlike methods that lift 2D to 3D only at inference, VideoMDM learns a coherent 3D motion manifold during training. On HumanML3D it nearly closes the gap to fully 3D-supervised MDM (FID 0.88 vs 0.54); On real video datasets Fit3D and NBA the method learns to generate motions consistently preferred by humans, with strong quantitative results.

</details>


### [301] [Positional Encoding in the Context of Memristor-Based Analog Computation for Automatic Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13379)
*Benedikt Hilmes,Nick Rossenbach,Ralf Schlüter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Large output values from positional encodings cause ADC degradation in memristor-based Transformer inference. Adjusting ADC weight/precision bits per layer reduces degradation by ~50% with stable energy; removing encoding-related linear transformations yields ~30% reduction when ADC is fixed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memristor-based analog computing enables efficient vector-matrix multiplication for NLP, but suffers from distortion in weight programming and execution. The authors identify that large output values from transformed positional encodings specifically cause major degradation during analog-to-digital conversion (ADC), a critical bottleneck in memristor-based Transformer inference.

Method: The authors analyze how positional encoding transformations produce large-magnitude outputs that exceed ADC dynamic range. They propose two mitigation strategies: (1) adjusting the proportion of weight bits and precision bits of the ADC on a per-layer basis for memristor layers, and (2) removing encoding-related linear transformations entirely when ADC modification is not feasible.

Result: With ADC configurability, execution degradation is reduced by approximately 50% relative while maintaining stable estimated energy consumption. Without ADC modification, removing encoding-related linear transformations reduces degradation by approximately 30% relative.

Conclusion: Positional encoding transformations are a key source of ADC-related degradation in memristor-based Transformer execution. Layer-specific ADC bit allocation is an effective mitigation, and architectural simplifications (removing linear transforms tied to encodings) offer a viable fallback when hardware is fixed.

Abstract: Memristors provide a new chance for resource-efficient computation of neural models for natural language processing by enabling analog execution of vector-matrix-multiplication. Yet, computations on these devices are currently subject to larger distortion, both in weight programming and execution. In this work, we identify large output values of transformed positional encodings to cause major degradation within analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) as part of memristor-based computation. By adjusting the proportion of weight and precision bits of the ADC of specific memristor layers, we reduce the degradation of the execution by ~50% relative, while keeping the estimated energy consumption stable. Additionally, we investigate scenarios where the ADC cannot be modified. In that case the degradation can be reduced by ~30% relative after removing encoding-related linear transformations.

</details>


### [302] [Hölder++: Improving the Quality-Coherence Trade-off in Multimodal VAEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13381)
*Huyen Vo,María Martínez-García,Isabel Valera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hölder++ is a novel multimodal VAE that improves the trade-off between generative quality and cross-modal coherence by implementing exact Hölder pooling, modeling both shared and private representations, and using hierarchical inference for better disentanglement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal VAEs struggle with a trade-off: improving generative quality/diversity often comes at the cost of cross-modal semantic coherence, and vice versa. Recent work using approximate Hölder pooling improved coherence but slightly reduced diversity, motivating a more refined approach.

Method: The authors propose three innovations: (1) the first exact (non-approximate) implementation of Hölder pooling for multimodal VAEs; (2) Hölder+, which extends the architecture to separately model a shared latent representation and modality-specific private representations; and (3) Hölder++, which adds hierarchical inference to further disentangle shared and private latent variables.

Result: Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality–coherence trade-off over prior methods, produces more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are informative for downstream tasks.

Conclusion: The combination of exact Hölder pooling, shared-private representation modeling, and hierarchical inference effectively addresses the longstanding quality–coherence trade-off in multimodal VAEs, yielding better generation and more useful learned representations.

Abstract: Existing approaches for multimodal variational autoencoders (VAEs) face a trade-off between generative quality and coherence-i.e., they struggle to generate realistic and diverse samples that, at the same time, are semantically consistent across modalities. A recent work shows that using a simple approximation to Hölder pooling as an aggregation method improves coherence over the SOTA MMVAE+, despite assuming a single shared representation across all modalities. Yet, it slightly compromises sample diversity. Inspired by this insight, we propose Hölder++, a novel multimodal VAE that improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off through: (i) the first implementation of Hölder pooling without any approximation for multimodal VAEs; (ii) an extended architecture that models distinct shared and private (i.e., modality-specific) representations (Hölder+); and (iii) hierarchical inference that further enhances the disentanglement between the shared and private representations (Hölder++). Our experiments corroborate that Hölder++ consistently improves the generative quality-coherence trade-off, yields more structured latent spaces, and learns shared representations that are informative for downstream tasks.

</details>


### [303] [PolyFlow: Safe and Efficient Polytope-Constrained Flow Matching with Constraint Embedding and Projection-free Update](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13400)
*Jianming Ma,Qiyue Yang,Yang Zhang,Liyun Yan,Zhanxiang Cao,Yazhou Zhang,Yue Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolyFlow is a polytope-constrained flow matching framework that embeds polyhedral constraints directly into flow-based generative models, achieving zero constraint violation without post-hoc corrections or iterative solvers, while maintaining high distributional fidelity and significantly lower inference latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow-based generative models struggle in safety-critical physical systems due to strict constraint requirements. Existing methods enforce constraints via post-hoc corrections, which are computationally expensive and distort the learned distribution. A method is needed that natively respects constraints without sacrificing efficiency or generative quality.

Method: PolyFlow introduces a discrete-time flow formulation combined with a projection-free architecture. Constraints (arbitrary polyhedral sets) are embedded directly into both the model parameterization and the flow dynamics. This eliminates discretization errors and guarantees strict constraint satisfaction by design, avoiding expensive iterative projection solvers.

Result: PolyFlow achieves zero constraint violation across planning and control tasks while maintaining high distributional fidelity. Compared to state-of-the-art constrained generation baselines, it significantly reduces inference latency and offers a favorable trade-off between safety, efficiency, and generative quality.

Conclusion: Directly embedding polyhedral constraints into flow matching models is both feasible and advantageous, enabling safe, efficient, and high-fidelity generative modeling for safety-critical applications without the drawbacks of post-hoc correction methods.

Abstract: While flow-based generative models have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of domains, deploying them in safety-critical physical systems remains challenging due to strict constraint requirements. Existing approaches typically enforce safety through post-hoc corrections, which incur substantial computational overhead and may distort the learned distribution. We propose PolyFlow, a polytope-constrained flow matching framework that embeds constraints directly into the model and flow dynamics. PolyFlow introduces a discrete-time flow formulation and a projection-free architecture, which eliminate the discretization error and guarantee strict satisfaction of arbitrary polyhedral constraints, without the need for expensive iterative solvers. Experimental results show that PolyFlow achieves zero constraint violation while maintaining high distributional fidelity across a range of planning and control tasks. Compared to state-of-the-art constrained generation baselines, PolyFlow significantly reduces inference latency and demonstrates a favorable trade-off between safety, efficiency, and generative quality. Code is available on https://github.com/MJianM/PolyFlow.

</details>


### [304] [Accelerating Speculative Diffusions via Block Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13426)
*Alexander Soen,Hisham Husain,Valentin De Bortoli,Arnaud Doucet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an efficient speculative sampling scheme for continuous diffusion models, adapting the original acceptance-rejection mechanism from LLMs. It enables block verification for diffusions, provably improving draft acceptance rates, and analyzes a training-free self-speculative "Free Drafter" achieving up to 6.3% speedup over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speculative decoding has been successfully applied to LLMs but adapting it to continuous diffusion models is challenging because the core acceptance-rejection scheme requires sampling from a residual distribution, which is non-trivial in continuous space. Existing adaptations either use inefficient sampling or resort to alternative, less faithful schemes. The authors aim to implement the original speculative sampling mechanism faithfully and efficiently for diffusions.

Method: The authors propose a novel scheme that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism (acceptance-rejection with residual distribution sampling) for continuous diffusion models. Key innovations include: (1) adapting block verification — a technique from LLMs that verifies multiple draft tokens at once — to the diffusion setting, which provably improves draft acceptance rates; and (2) formalizing and analyzing the "Free Drafter," a heuristic self-speculative drafter requiring no additional training, which leverages the target model itself for drafting with negligible overhead.

Result: The proposed Free Drafter with block verification yields up to a 6.3% wall-clock speedup over existing speculative diffusion methods, with no additional model training and negligible computational overhead beyond the existing parallel verification pass.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that faithful and efficient speculative sampling is achievable for continuous diffusion models. By adapting block verification, the method provably improves draft acceptance rates and enables practical speedups with a completely training-free self-speculative drafter, advancing the applicability of speculative decoding to diffusion-based generation.

Abstract: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference by using a draft model to generate tokens, with an acceptance-rejection scheme that ensures that the output matches the target distribution. Adapting this to continuous diffusions is difficult because speculative sampling requires drawing from a residual distribution. While straightforward in discrete spaces, efficiently sampling this residual in continuous space is non-trivial. Consequently, existing diffusion adaptations either use computationally inefficient sampling techniques or rely on an alternative scheme. In this work, we introduce a novel scheme that efficiently implements the original speculative sampling mechanism for diffusion models. Our approach offers a critical advantage over current methods: it enables us to adapt block verification from LLMs to diffusions -- which provably improves the acceptance rate of drafts. Furthermore, we formalize and analyze the Free Drafter, a heuristic self-speculative drafter for diffusions that requires no training. By enabling block verification, our Free Drafter yields up to a 6.3% speedup over existing speculative methods with no additional training and negligible overhead beyond the existing parallel verification pass.

</details>


### [305] [How Much Memory Do We Need? Adaptive Memory Gate for Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13443)
*Jihyeon Hur,Yongseok Kwon,Min-Gi Jo,Jeongwhan Choi,Noseong Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AMGFNO introduces a learnable gating mechanism that dynamically adjusts memory weight in neural operators based on observation conditions (resolution, viscosity), achieving 55-79% nRMSE reduction over fixed-weight approaches on KS and Burgers' equations, with gate values automatically decaying as resolution increases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory-augmented neural operators use a fixed memory weight regardless of observation conditions (resolution, physical parameters like viscosity). Preliminary experiments show that optimal memory weight varies significantly across these conditions, meaning a fixed weight cannot simultaneously optimize performance across diverse settings. This calls for an adaptive mechanism.

Method: The authors propose AMGFNO (Adaptive Memory Gated Fourier Neural Operator), which incorporates a learnable gate that dynamically modulates the memory weight based on input conditions. The gating mechanism allows the model to automatically adjust how much past state information is incorporated, enabling adaptation to varying resolutions and physical parameters without manual tuning.

Result: On the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations, AMGFNO achieves 55-79% normalized RMSE reduction compared to fixed-weight memory-augmented neural operators at low resolution. The learned gate value automatically decreases from approximately 0.7 to near-zero as resolution increases, validating the hypothesis that memory contribution should diminish with richer observations.

Conclusion: Dynamic memory modulation via learnable gating substantially outperforms fixed memory weighting across varying observation conditions. The emergent behavior — gate values decreasing with resolution — aligns with intuition: higher-resolution observations require less reliance on past states. AMGFNO provides a unified, adaptive framework for memory-augmented neural operators.

Abstract: Neural operators have emerged as a powerful data-driven approach for solving time-dependent PDEs. Among recent advances, memory-augmented neural operators explicitly incorporate past states and have achieved remarkable performance under low-resolution observation settings. However, existing approaches apply a fixed memory weight regardless of observation conditions, such as resolution or physical parameters, limiting their adaptability. Our preliminary experiments reveal that optimal memory weight varies with resolution and viscosity, implying that a fixed memory weight cannot simultaneously optimize performance across diverse settings. We propose AMGFNO, which dynamically modulates memory weight through a learnable gate. On the Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Burgers' equations, AMGFNO achieves 55-79% nRMSE reduction over at low resolution, with the learned gate value automatically decreasing from $\bar{g} \approx 0.7$ to near-zero as resolution increases.

</details>


### [306] [Clustering Node Attributed Networks with Graph Neural Networks and Self Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13444)
*Rodrigo de Sapienza Luna,Daniel Ratton Figueiredo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-learning GNN framework for attributed graph clustering that iteratively refines node representations and clustering assignments, using a context graph in each round. It outperforms single-round GNN clustering and single-modality methods, and is competitive with state-of-the-art on real data when clusters are balanced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional graph clustering uses only edge information, but real-world graphs often have informative node attributes. Existing methods that leverage both sources exist, but the authors aim to improve unsupervised clustering by iteratively refining both the graph structure used for representation learning and the clustering itself, addressing limitations of single-round GNN approaches.

Method: A multi-round self-learning framework where each round: (1) a GNN generates node representations, (2) these representations are used to cluster nodes, (3) the clustering output modifies the graph for the next round's GNN, and (4) a context graph (built from the original graph) guides representation learning. The process is fully unsupervised.

Result: On synthetic data, the method extracts information from both edges and attributes, outperforming single-modality algorithms when either source is weak. Multi-round learning consistently beats a single long training round. On real datasets, it is competitive with state-of-the-art methods when cluster sizes are balanced.

Conclusion: The iterative self-learning GNN framework effectively fuses network topology and node attributes for unsupervised graph clustering. Multi-round refinement yields superior performance over single-round GNN clustering, and the approach holds its own against leading methods on real-world data, particularly under balanced cluster conditions.

Abstract: Graph clustering - partitioning the node set of a graph into disjoint subsets that reflect some latent information - is a fundamental problem as it finds applications in a myriad of different scenarios. While this classic problem has been tackled for decades by different communities, a recent variation of the problem driven by real data considers the scenario where nodes have attributes that are also informative. This has triggered novel methods that simultaneously leverage network information (edges) and node information (attributed) in the design of novel clustering algorithms. This work proposes a novel framework that builds on prior works that have applied graph neural networks (GNN) to graph clustering. The proposed framework operates in rounds of self learning in a fully unsupervised setting. In each round, a GNN generates representations for nodes that are used to cluster the nodes. This clustering influences the graph used to generate the node representation in the next round. Moreover, a context graph built in each round using the original graph is used to generate the node representations. Empirical results show that the proposed methodology extracts information from both network edges and node attributes in synthetic data, outperforming algorithms focused solely on the network or attributes when neither are very informative. Multiple rounds of learning also improve the performance and always outperforms a long single round of training (i.e., classic GNN graph clustering). When considering real datasets, empirical results indicate that the proposed methodology is competitive to state-of-the-art methods when cluster sizes are balanced.

</details>


### [307] [Uncertainty Estimation for Molecular Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13451)
*Paul Seij,Christian A. Naesseth,Stephan Mandt,Metod Jazbec*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A post-hoc uncertainty estimation method for pretrained 3D molecular diffusion models is proposed, using Laplace approximation to measure noise prediction variability across the generation trajectory; the resulting uncertainty score negatively correlates with sample quality and enables test-time filtering to improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models for 3D molecular generation lack a principled mechanism to signal when a generated molecule is likely low-quality, limiting reliable deployment and sample selection.

Method: The method applies a Laplace approximation to the denoising network's parameters, then tracks the variability (variance) of the noise prediction over the course of the reverse diffusion trajectory. This yields a per-sample uncertainty score without modifying the pretrained model.

Result: The uncertainty score shows a consistent negative correlation with established sample-level quality metrics, and filtering generated samples based on this score improves overall model performance via test-time scaling.

Conclusion: Post-hoc Laplace-based uncertainty estimation provides an effective, principled signal for identifying low-quality molecular samples from diffusion models, enabling practical test-time quality control without retraining.

Abstract: Diffusion models have seen wide adoption for 3D molecular generation, yet they offer no principled signal of when a generated molecule is likely to be of low quality. We propose a post-hoc method for estimating per-sample uncertainty in pretrained molecular diffusion models. Building on a Laplace approximation of the denoising network, we measure the variability of the noise prediction across the generation trajectory. Empirically, we show that the resulting uncertainty score is informative of sample quality, exhibiting a negative correlation with established sample-level quality metrics. We further study how the proposed uncertainty score can be used to filter generated samples, improving model performance via test-time scaling.

</details>


### [308] [Reinforcement Learning for Neural Model Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13461)
*Shaivi Malik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper frames neural model editing as a reinforcement learning problem, using RL agents to learn weight modification policies (multiplicative in MaskWorld, additive in ShiftWorld) guided by a composite reward balancing utility preservation and task-specific editing objectives. Evaluated on bias mitigation and machine unlearning, the learned policies achieve strong results (near-0% forget accuracy with >90% retain accuracy on unlearning; >5% bias improvement while maintaining utility).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing specialized editing algorithms for pretrained neural networks is time-consuming and requires significant manual effort for each new objective. The authors aim to replace hand-crafted editing algorithms with a general-purpose framework where editing policies are learned automatically via reinforcement learning.

Method: The framework formulates neural model editing as an RL problem. Two environments are introduced: MaskWorld (multiplicative weight scaling) and ShiftWorld (additive weight updates). An RL agent modifies model weights and receives a reward that combines a utility-preservation term with a task-specific editing objective. The agent is evaluated on two tasks: bias mitigation in text classification and machine unlearning in image classification.

Result: On machine unlearning, learned policies reduce forget set accuracy to nearly 0% while preserving over 90% retain set accuracy. On bias mitigation, policies improve bias-related performance by more than 5% while maintaining general classification utility.

Conclusion: Neural model editing can be successfully cast as an RL problem, enabling editing policies to be learned from reward feedback rather than manually engineered for each specific editing task.

Abstract: Editing pretrained neural networks requires specialized algorithms tailored to specific objectives. Designing such algorithms is often time-consuming and demands significant effort. We present an exploratory framework that formulates neural model editing as a reinforcement learning problem, where agents modify models using reward feedback. We introduce two environments: MaskWorld, where agents scale weights multiplicatively, and ShiftWorld, where agents apply additive weight updates. The reward function combines a utility-preservation objective with a task-specific editing objective, enabling agents to learn targeted modifications while maintaining overall model performance. We evaluate the framework on bias mitigation in text classification and machine unlearning in image classification, both of which traditionally rely on specialized algorithms. Our results show that the learned policies reduce forget set accuracy to nearly 0% while preserving over 90% retain set accuracy on the unlearning task. In the bias mitigation setting, the learned policies improve bias-related performance by more than 5% while maintaining general classification utility. Our findings show that neural model editing can be cast as a reinforcement learning problem, allowing editing policies to be learned from reward feedback rather than manually engineered for each task.

</details>


### [309] [CRAFTIIF: Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13486)
*William Smits*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRAFTIIF is an unsupervised anomaly detection framework for multivariate time series that targets all four anomaly types (point, distributional, temporal, collective) using type-specific wavelet features and isolation forests, achieving state-of-the-art results on the mTSBench benchmark without dataset-specific tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unsupervised multivariate time series anomaly detection methods are limited to addressing only one or two of the four structurally distinct anomaly types (point, distributional, temporal, collective), and offer poor interpretability. A unified framework capable of detecting all four types without per-dataset tuning is needed.

Method: CRAFTIIF generates K=500 random analytic wavelet features across four families (Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet), each targeting a specific anomaly type. Five structured Isolation Forests are trained: one per anomaly type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies. Detection thresholding uses an adaptive combination of Otsu's method and MAD, automatically calibrating across anomaly rates from 0.1% to 69.2%. Type attribution is inherent via branch firing in type-specific IFs, requiring no post-hoc explanation.

Result: On all 19 mTSBench datasets, CRAFTIIF achieves mean F1=0.228 (all datasets) and F1=0.322 (13 detectable datasets). It ranks first among 25 methods on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329, +40.7%). A diagnostic framework identifies 6 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method. Ablation confirms adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), four-branch structure (+20%), and meta-IF (+23%) are each critical.

Conclusion: CRAFTIIF demonstrates that targeting all four anomaly types with type-specific wavelet features and structured isolation forests yields substantial improvements in unsupervised anomaly detection, while providing built-in interpretability through branch-firing attribution. The diagnostic framework also reveals fundamental detectability limits in certain benchmark datasets.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in multivariate time series is challenged by four structurally distinct anomaly types -- point (isolated spikes), distributional (level shifts), temporal (rhythm changes), and collective (inter-sensor correlation breakdowns) -- each requiring different feature representations. Most unsupervised methods target only one or two types and provide limited interpretability. We present CRAFTIIF (Cross-Resolution Analytic Four-Type Interpretable Isolation Forest), a fully unsupervised framework targeting all four types without dataset-specific tuning. CRAFTIIF generates K=500 random analytic wavelet feature draws across four families (Morlet, DOG, Haar, Coiflet), each targeting a specific anomaly type, feeding five structured Isolation Forests -- one per type plus a meta-IF for compound anomalies. An adaptive Otsu/MAD threshold calibrates detection automatically across anomaly rates from 0.1% to 69.2%. Because each IF is trained exclusively on type-specific features, branch firing provides direct anomaly-type attribution by construction, without post-hoc explanation. Evaluated on all 19 datasets of the mTSBench benchmark (Zhou et al., TMLR 2026), CRAFTIIF achieves mean F1=0.228 (all 19 datasets) and F1=0.322 (13 detectable datasets), ranking first among all 25 evaluated methods on VUS-PR (0.463 vs. previous best 0.329, +40.7%). A diagnostic framework -- oracle F1, detectability limits, and branch separation ratios -- identifies 6 of 19 datasets as fundamentally undetectable by any unsupervised method. Ablation over 11 conditions confirms adaptive thresholding (+38% F1), four-branch structure (+20%), and meta-IF (+23%) are each essential. Code: https://github.com/smitswil/craftiif

</details>


### [310] [A2D2: Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13565)
*Sophia Tang,Yuchen Zhu,Molei Tao,Pranam Chatterjee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A2D2 introduces a unified reward-guided fine-tuning framework for any-length discrete diffusion models, jointly optimizing insertion and unmasking policies with a quality-based inference schedule, backed by theoretical guarantees and outperforming prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While discrete diffusion models have been extended to any-length generation via token insertion, principled reward-guided fine-tuning for such models remains unexplored. Existing approaches are limited to fixed-length settings or inference-time guidance without theoretical guarantees.

Method: The authors derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for joint insertion-unmasking path measures, enabling convergence to the reward-tilted distribution without target samples. They establish unmasking and insertion quality as tractable proxies for minimizing decoding error and propose the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss, which provably yields the optimal path measure. A quality-based adaptive inference schedule is also introduced.

Result: A2D2 empirically improves reward optimization while enhancing generation flexibility and accuracy compared to prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.

Conclusion: A2D2 provides the first principled framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models, with theoretical guarantees and empirical gains in both reward alignment and generation quality.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models offer a simple and stable likelihood-based framework for sequence generation, recently extended to any-length settings via token insertion. Principled reward-guided fine-tuning for any-length discrete diffusion, however, remains largely unexplored. We introduce Fine-Tuning Any-Length Discrete Diffusion for Adaptive Decoding (A2D2), a unified framework for reward-guided fine-tuning of any-length discrete diffusion models via joint optimization of the insertion and unmasking policies together with a quality-based inference schedule. We derive the Radon-Nikodym derivative for the joint insertion-unmasking path measures, enabling theoretically guaranteed convergence to the intractable reward-tilted sequence distribution without requiring target samples. Building on this, we establish unmasking and insertion quality as tractable approaches for minimizing decoding error and introduce the Adaptive Joint Decoding (AJD) loss, which provably yields the optimal path measure that generates the reward-tilted distribution. Empirically, A2D2 improves reward optimization while enhancing generation flexibility and accuracy over prior fixed-length fine-tuning and inference-time guidance methods.

</details>


### [311] [Adjusted Cup-Product Neural Layer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13568)
*Snigdha Chandan Khilar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an adjusted cup product neural layer that hardwires the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory, producing a gauge-invariant readout. The key theoretical result shows that on a closed cycle, the output depends entirely on the adjustment coefficient — setting it to zero eliminates the output regardless of other parameters. The adjustment is thus the sole source of gauge-invariant signal, and this observable is a nonzero quadratic form invariant under both 1-form and 2-form gauge transformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains, but standard cup products on cochains are not gauge invariant. The authors seek to design a neural network primitive that produces gauge-invariant outputs by incorporating higher gauge theory structures, enabling machine learning models to respect the gauge symmetries inherent in physical and geometric data.

Method: The authors propose the adjusted cup product neural layer, a neural primitive that hardwires the algebraic structure of the cup product together with an adjustment term derived from higher gauge theory. They mathematically analyze the gauge invariance properties of this construction under both 1-form and 2-form gauge transformations, and characterize the output on closed cycles.

Result: The main theoretical result demonstrates that on a closed cycle, the output of the adjusted cup product layer depends entirely on the adjustment coefficient. Setting this coefficient to zero removes the output completely, independent of all other parameters. Thus, the adjustment term is the only source of gauge-invariant signal. The authors further prove this observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under both 1-form and 2-form gauge transformations.

Conclusion: The adjusted cup product provides a mathematically principled neural primitive for constructing gauge-invariant readouts, with the adjustment coefficient being the sole carrier of gauge-invariant signal. This has implications for designing neural networks that operate on cohomological data while respecting higher gauge symmetries.

Abstract: Many important observables in physics and geometry are cup products of cochains. The adjusted cup product neural layer has been introduced in this paper. It is a neural primitive that hard wires the cup product with an adjustment term from higher gauge theory. This creates a readout that is gauge invariant by design. Their main theoretical result shows that on a closed cycle the output relies entirely on the adjustment coefficient. Setting this coefficient to zero removes the output completely regardless of other parameters. Thus the adjustment is the only source of gauge invariant signal. They prove this observable is a nonzero quadratic form and is exactly invariant under one and two gauge transformations.

</details>


### [312] [Existence Precedes Value: Joint Modeling of Observational Existence and Evolving States in Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13571)
*Yifan Hu,Hongzhou Chen,Peiyuan Liu,Yiding Liu,Zewei Dong,Jiang-Ming Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Timeflies reformulates time series forecasting with missing data as a joint problem of predicting both whether future observations will occur (observability) and their values. It uses a two-stream architecture (observation + value) with three coupling modules, and introduces the Shadow benchmark with an OVJE metric for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for irregular/missing time series (Neural ODEs, continuous-time graph networks) implicitly assume future observation timestamps are known at inference — an "oracle assumption" that is unrealistic in practice. In real systems, knowing whether a valid observation will occur at all is often as important as knowing its value.

Method: Timeflies employs a dual-stream architecture: (1) an observation stream and (2) a value stream, coupled via three modules: reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction. This jointly models observation dynamics and state evolution. The authors also construct Shadow, a benchmark combining public datasets with industrial data, and propose the Observation-Value Joint Entropy (OVJE) metric for comprehensive evaluation of coupled predictability.

Result: Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods on the Shadow benchmark, demonstrating the benefit of explicitly modeling future observability alongside value prediction in time series with missing data.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling future observability is crucial for reliable time series forecasting under missing data. Timeflies provides a unified solution by jointly inferring observation occurrence and value estimation, validated on both public and real-world industrial data.

Abstract: Real-world time series are often highly incomplete and irregular due to sensor dormancy, transmission delays, and event-driven sampling, making reliable forecasting fundamentally challenging. Existing methods have evolved from impute-then-forecast pipelines to continuous-time models such as Neural ODEs and continuous-time graph networks. While these approaches improve the modeling of historical irregularity, they still rely on an implicit oracle assumption at inference time: the timestamps of future valid observations are presumed to be known in advance. This assumption limits practical relevance, since in many real systems the more fundamental question is not only what the future value will be, but also whether a valid observation will occur at all. In this paper, we propose Timeflies, a unified framework that reformulates forecasting as a joint problem of future observability inference and value estimation. To explicitly model the interaction between observation dynamics and state evolution, Timeflies adopts an observation stream and a value stream, coupled through three dedicated modules for reliability-aware embedding, observation-guided dependency modeling, and joint prediction. We further construct Shadow, a benchmark that combines natural missingness from public datasets with real-world industrial data, and introduce the Observation-Value Joint Entropy (OVJE) metric to comprehensively evaluate this coupled predictability. Extensive experiments show that Timeflies consistently outperforms existing methods, highlighting the importance of explicitly modeling future observability in time series forecasting with missing values. Code and dataset are available in https://github.com/ant-intl/Timeflies.

</details>


### [313] [Learning with Simulators: No Regret in a Computationally Bounded World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13576)
*Sasha Voitovych,Abhishek Shetty,Noah Golowich,Alexander Rakhlin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces simulatable processes, a framework where a learner with access to an approximate simulator of an arbitrarily dependent data-generating process can recover PAC-style generalization guarantees (VC-dimension bounds). Shows statistical/computational advantages of conditional sampling and presents a single algorithm that learns any VC class under polynomial-time samplable processes with regret bounded by time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical learning theory heavily relies on independence assumptions (or weak dependence), while results for strongly dependent data are scarce. The authors aim to bridge this gap by asking: what minimal assumptions allow generalization when data can be arbitrarily complex and dependent?

Method: Introduces the simulatable process framework: the learner has access to a simulator that approximates the true (potentially dependent) data-generating distribution. Under this access model, they analyze PAC learning with conditional sampling oracles and propose a universal algorithm leveraging time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity to compete against any polynomial-time samplable process.

Result: With simulator access, one recovers classical VC-dimension error bounds despite arbitrary dependence. Conditional sampling yields strict statistical and computational gains. A single universal algorithm can learn any VC class under all polynomial-time samplable processes, with regret controlled by the process's time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity.

Conclusion: Simulatable processes provide a significant conceptual broadening of the PAC model, showing that independence can be replaced by simulator access while retaining strong generalization guarantees. This unifies and extends classical learning theory to richly dependent data settings.

Abstract: Understanding the minimal assumptions necessary for generalization is the fundamental question in learning theory. Unfortunately, most results rely heavily on independence (or some proxy thereof) of the data-generating process, while results for strongly dependent data are far more limited. Towards addressing this gap, we introduce the framework of simulatable processes, where the learner has access to a simulator that approximates the distribution generating the data (which may be an arbitrarily complex and dependent process). Surprisingly, given access to such a simulator, we show that we can recover the same learning guarantees as in the classical setting with independent data, namely, error bounds that depend on the VC dimension. Further, we use this framework to study the power of conditional sampling and show strict statistical and computational advantages in this setting. As a highlight of our framework, we exhibit a single algorithm that simultaneously learns any given VC class under all processes samplable in bounded polynomial time, with regret controlled by the time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity of the process. This provides a significant conceptual broadening of the classical PAC model.

</details>


### [314] [Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging: Transitioning from Uniform Priors to Sparse Posteriors in Ensemble Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13589)
*Meher Sai Preetam,Meher Bhaskar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCSB is a post-training framework that jointly optimizes ensemble pruning and probability calibration by solving a simplex-constrained optimization with a concave quadratic penalty to induce genuine sparsity, achieving up to 96% compression with improved calibration and preserved accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard bagging ensembles assign uniform weights to all base estimators, ignoring their varying local competence. This causes model overconfidence and poor probability calibration. Existing attempts to prune via L1 regularization fail on the simplex because the L1 norm is constant there (the "L1-simplex paradox"), necessitating a novel sparsity-inducing approach.

Method: The authors formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization problem over the probability simplex, minimizing OOB loss. To overcome the L1-simplex paradox, they introduce a concave quadratic penalty that genuinely induces sparsity on the simplex, yielding a sparse weight vector. The method is model-agnostic and operates as post-training compression.

Result: SCSB achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, linear inference speedups, and superior probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error) while preserving or improving generalization accuracy.

Conclusion: SCSB provides a mathematically rigorous, model-agnostic solution for simultaneous ensemble pruning and calibration, demonstrating that concave quadratic penalization effectively resolves the L1-simplex paradox and yields compact, well-calibrated bagging ensembles.

Abstract: We present Simplex-Constrained Sparse Bagging (SCSB), a mathematically rigorous framework for post-training compression and probability calibration of bootstrap-based bagging ensembles. Standard bagging ensembles (such as Random Forests, Bagged SVMs, and Bagged Neural Networks) assign uniform voting power to all constituent estimators. However, this naive uniform prior ignores the varying local competence of base estimators and contributes to model overconfidence. We formulate ensemble pruning and calibration as a joint optimization problem over the probability simplex by minimizing the Out-Of-Bag (OOB) loss. To induce sparsity, we address the theoretical "L1-simplex paradox" -- the mathematical reality that the L1 norm is constant on the simplex and fails to prune -- by introducing a concave quadratic penalty. SCSB is model-agnostic and achieves up to 96% ensemble compression, yielding linear inference speedups and superior probability calibration (lowered Expected Calibration Error) while preserving or enhancing generalization accuracy.

</details>


### [315] [The Stable Recovery Manifold: Geometric Principles Governing Recoverability in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13637)
*Ayushman Trivedi,Bhavika Melwani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the geometric structure of knowledge retention in continual learning, finding that forgotten task information remains recoverable in a low-dimensional subspace (~8 dimensions) despite representational drift. Catastrophic forgetting is reframed as an accessibility/manifold-alignment problem rather than irreversible information destruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors seek to move beyond the conventional view that catastrophic forgetting constitutes destruction of previously learned knowledge. Building on the Accessibility Collapse framework, they aim to characterize the geometric structure of recoverability — specifically, whether forgotten information persists in a compact, decodable subspace despite representational reorganization during sequential training.

Method: The study employs Split CIFAR-100 with a ResNet-18 trained sequentially across 10 tasks.
