The authors introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), defined as the minimum number of singular directions needed to preserve 90% of full probe performance. They analyze recoverability, representational drift (via principal angles), and recovery complexity, then construct a simple geometric model to explain recoverability variance.

Result: Recovery subspace dimensionality remains stable throughout training (mean k_t = 8.0), contradicting the Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862). The geometric model explains 82.2% of recoverability variance. These results support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis.

Conclusion: Catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem, not information destruction. Forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable in a stable low-dimensional subspace, even as representations undergo substantial drift. This reframing has implications for designing continual learning methods focused on preserving access pathways rather than preventing weight change.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting is often viewed as the destruction of previously learned knowledge during sequential learning. Building on the Accessibility Collapse framework, we investigate the geometric structure of recoverability in continual learning. Using Split CIFAR-100 and a sequentially trained ResNet-18, we analyze recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity across ten tasks. We introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), a measure of the minimum number of singular directions required to preserve 90 percent of full probe performance. Contrary to our Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis, recovery dimensionality remains stable throughout training (mean k_t = 8.0) despite substantial representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862), and a simple geometric model explains 82.2 percent of recoverability variance. These findings support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis, suggesting that forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization. The results indicate that catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem rather than information destruction.

</details>


### [316] [Dense Supervision, Sparse Updates: On the Sparsity and Geometry of On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13657)
*Guo Yu,Wenlin Liu,Yulan Hu,Hao-Xuan Ma,Jun-Peng Jiang,Han-Jia Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPD produces small, coordinate-sparse, FFN-heavy updates that are spectrally concentrated and biased toward near-zero coordinates of source weights, retaining geometric signatures of on-policy post-training despite dense supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) combines on-policy student trajectories with dense teacher supervision, but how this hybrid actually changes model parameters is poorly understood.

Method: Analyzed parameter updates across multiple language and vision-language model pairs under OPD, examining sparsity patterns (layer-wise distribution, FFN vs. attention), optimizer ablations (SGD vs. AdamW), and geometric properties (rank, spectral concentration, alignment with source weight singular subspaces, coordinate-wise update distribution relative to source weight magnitudes).

Result: OPD updates are small and coordinate-sparse, distributed across layers with FFN dominance; training only the sparse subnetwork recovers near-full OPD performance. SGD underperforms AdamW, suggesting heterogeneous gradient scales persist. Updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated, lying away from principal singular subspaces and disproportionately on near-zero source weight coordinates.

Conclusion: Dense teacher supervision does not make OPD equivalent to ordinary dense parameter rewriting; OPD retains key geometric signatures characteristic of on-policy post-training.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (\textsc{OPD}) has recently become a prominent post-training recipe as it combines two desirable ingredients: on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision, yet how this hybrid changes a model's parameters remains unclear. Across several language and vision-language model pairs and use cases, our analysis yields two main findings. On sparsity, \textsc{OPD}-style updates are small and coordinate-sparse. They are distributed across layers and are usually FFN-heavy. This sparse structure is operationally useful: training only the discovered subnetwork recovers nearly the same performance as full \textsc{OPD}. However, the sparsity-inducing SGD optimizer underperforms AdamW in our optimizer ablation, likely because dense teacher supervision preserves heterogeneous coordinate-wise gradient scales where AdamW's adaptive scaling remains useful. On geometry, the updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated; they lie mostly away from the principal singular subspaces of the source weights and fall disproportionately on coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. These findings suggest that dense teacher supervision does not turn \textsc{OPD} into ordinary dense parameter rewriting; instead, \textsc{OPD} retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

</details>


### [317] [Understanding Truncated Positional Encodings for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13671)
*James Flora,Mitchell Black,Weng-Keen Wong,Amir Nayyeri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the theoretical properties of truncated positional encodings (PEs) in GNNs, revealing that unlike their complete counterparts, truncated spectral and walk-based PEs have fundamentally different expressive powers, with truncated spectral PEs being no stronger than 1-WL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Complete positional encodings (spectral and walk-based) are theoretically equivalent in expressive power (between 1-WL and 3-WL), but their O(n³) complexity forces practitioners to use truncated variants (e.g., first k eigenvectors or adjacency powers). The theoretical properties of these truncated PEs remain unknown, creating a gap between theory and practice.

Method: The authors conduct a theoretical analysis of truncated PEs, separating them into spectral (Laplacian eigenspaces, effective resistance) and walk-based (adjacency matrix polynomials) families. They compare expressive power under truncation, introduce k-harmonic distances as a case study to differentiate closely related spectral PEs, and validate findings experimentally on real-world datasets with mixed truncated PEs.

Result: Under truncation, spectral and walk-based PE families become fundamentally different in expressive power — a sharp departure from their complete-form equivalence. Truncated spectral PEs are provably no stronger than the 1-WL test. The k-harmonic distances further illustrate that even closely related truncated spectral PEs differ expressively. Experiments show that mixing truncated PEs outperforms any single family on real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Truncation fundamentally alters the theoretical landscape of positional encodings, breaking the equivalence between spectral and walk-based families. Practitioners should prefer mixtures of truncated PEs rather than relying on a single family, as this yields better empirical performance. The work opens a new direction for theoretically characterizing truncated PEs in GNNs.

Abstract: Positional encodings (PEs) enhance the power of graph neural networks (GNNs), both theoretically and empirically. Two of the most popular families of PEs - spectral (e.g., Laplacian eigenspaces, effective resistance) and walk-based (polynomials of the adjacency matrix) - are theoretically equivalent in expressive power, with expressivity between the 1-WL and 3-WL tests. However, this equivalence assumes the GNN uses the "complete" version of these PEs, which requires $O(n^3)$ time and space complexity. Instead, practitioners commonly use truncated variants of these encodings, such as the first $k$ eigenspaces or powers of the adjacency matrix. However, the theoretical properties of these truncated PEs are unknown. In this work, we initiate the study of these truncated PEs. Theoretically, we show that, under truncation, several families of PEs are fundamentally different in expressive power. As a corollary, we show that truncated spectral PEs are no longer stronger than the 1-WL test. We also study a family of spectral PEs, the $k$-harmonic distances, to highlight the differences in expressive power of even closely related truncated PEs. Finally, we experimentally show that a mix of truncated PEs is preferable to any single family on real-world datasets.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [318] [Trait, Not State: The Durability of Reading Identity in Social Highlighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12904)
*Kazuki Nakayashiki,Keisuke Watanabe*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether a reader's document highlighting pattern is a stable personal trait or a transient state. By tracking users' highlighting behavior over 24+ months, the study finds that individual selection signatures remain remarkably stable—even profiles built from early readings predict future highlights ~3x better than non-personal baselines, with negligible within-person drift. The trait-like stability persists even when controlling for domain repetition and interest neighborhoods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on social highlighting established that document selection is individually distinctive, but only cross-sectionally. The authors seek to determine whether this "selection signature" is a stable trait (persistent over time) or a transient state (drifting with context), addressing a fundamental temporal dimension absent from earlier research.

Method: The authors freeze each reader's first six months of highlighting activity as a personal profile and measure its predictive advantage (own-vs-other) on later selections at increasing time gaps (up to 24+ months). They employ two granularity levels: a coarse global layer and a fine layer where negatives/controls are drawn from the reader's own interest neighborhood. The anchor cell replicates the prior cross-sectional result to validate the experimental harness. Negative examples are matched by calendar era to prevent supply drift from being mistaken for personal drift. Analyses include retention ratios across time bins, domain-repeat ablation, and within-person profile recency comparisons.

Result: Four key findings: (1) At the fine layer, no statistically detectable paired decline in self-advantage appears at any horizon (6–12 month retention R = 1.00 [0.85, 1.18], n = 212). (2) At the coarse layer, only the 12–24 month bin shows a significant decline (~13%). (3) The signal is not reducible to repeated domains: ~90% survives after excluding all profile sources. (4) Within-person drift is slow: a recent-half profile beats the old half by only +0.042. Prospectively, personal profiles rank future reads at roughly 3× the average precision of every simple non-personal prior tested.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that readers' selection signatures operate as a trait—a stable signature that persists under continued engagement—rather than a transient state. The scope is limited to heavy, long-tenured readers on a single platform, and the design cannot separate exposure effects from genuine choice-driven selection.

Abstract: Prior work on a social web highlighter located individuality in selection -- which documents a person chooses to highlight -- but measured it cross-sectionally. We ask the temporal question: is a reader's selection signature a trait or a state? We freeze each reader's first six months of highlighting as a profile and track its own-vs-other advantage on their later selections at growing gaps (to 24+ months), with negatives drawn from the same calendar era -- so supply drift cannot masquerade as personal drift -- at a coarse global level and at a fine level whose negatives and controls come from the reader's own interest neighborhood; the anchor cell reproduces the prior cross-sectional level (+0.188 vs +0.169), validating the harness. Four results. Within the same users, the fine-layer advantage shows no statistically detectable paired decline at any horizon (6-12 month retention R = 1.00 [0.85, 1.18], n = 212; the farthest bin is compatible with a modest decline; the only contrast whose interval excludes zero is the coarse layer at 12-24 months, about 13%). The signal is not reducible to repeated domains (~90% survives excluding all profile sources). Within-person drift is slow (a recent-half profile beats the old half by +0.042). Prospectively, personal profiles -- even one built from a reader's earliest documents, median 20 months before evaluation -- rank their next reads at roughly 3x the AP of every simple non-personal prior tested. We use "trait" operationally (a stable signature under continued engagement); the scope is heavy, long-tenured readers of one platform, and exposure is not separable from choice.

</details>


### [319] [Charge as a Construct-Validity Factor in Chinese Legal Case Retrieval: A Cross-Benchmark Audit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12993)
*Yao Liu,Tien-Ping Tan,Zhilan Liu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: LeCaRDv2's high NDCG@10 scores are largely explained by a construct-validity confound: relevance is defined by shared legal charges, so a simple charge-matching baseline (BM25-tiebroken) recovers 99.2% of the gap between BM25 and the best-trained system. The authors propose a reusable Charge-Controlled Evaluation (CCE) protocol to screen benchmarks before interpreting NDCG@10 as evidence of legal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper investigates whether strong NDCG@10 scores on Chinese Legal Case Retrieval benchmarks genuinely reflect retrieval model sophistication or are largely driven by a benchmark design artifact — specifically, that relevance is defined by shared legal charges, making same-charge ranking a powerful but non-substantive heuristic.

Method: The authors conduct a series of ablation and confound analyses across three LCR benchmarks (LeCaRDv2, LeCaRDv1, CAIL2022): (1) measuring the charge-to-relevance association via relevance lift and macro-AUC; (2) testing a charge-only ranking baseline (shared primary charge, BM25 tiebreak) against trained systems; (3) decomposing trained reranker advantage into between-charge and within-charge components; (4) evaluating a predicted-charge cascade; and (5) proposing a reusable Charge-Controlled Evaluation (CCE) protocol with partial-input checks.

Result: On LeCaRDv2, the charge-only baseline recovers 99.2% of the BM25-to-best-trained NDCG@10 gap, with no statistically detectable difference from the best system. The trained reranker's advantage shrinks to +0.026 NDCG@10 within-charge. The charge-to-relevance signal weakens across benchmarks (macro-AUC: 0.871 → 0.759 → 0.728), and charge-based recovery drops accordingly (84.3% on LeCaRDv1, out-of-spec on CAIL2022). An exploratory charge-pool first-stage improves R@100 by +0.025. The CCE protocol triggers return null or descriptive across all three benchmarks.

Conclusion: Charge is a high-leverage construct-validity factor at the benchmark level, not a uniform explanation of NDCG@10 nor evidence that systems rely on charge. The CCE protocol provides a systematic screening tool so future LCR benchmarks can be validated before their scores are interpreted as indicators of legal reasoning capability.

Abstract: Chinese Legal Case Retrieval (LCR) benchmarks grade a reference judgment relevant when its legal characterization matches the query, and strong systems now reach NDCG@10 of 0.85-0.88. Most of the BM25-to-best-trained gap is recoverable with no retrieval model: ranking candidates only by shared primary charge, broken by BM25, closes 99.2% of it on LeCaRDv2 -- with no detectable difference from the best-trained system. This reflects benchmark design: LeCaRDv2 defines top relevance via the crime's key constitutive elements, which encode the charge, so same-charge cases are relevant by construction (relevance lift 4.49; charge-to-relevance macro-AUC 0.871). Holding charge fixed, the trained reranker's advantage over BM25 collapses to a small within-charge residual (+0.026 NDCG@10, cluster-bootstrap CI excluding zero, about a quarter), the only non-definitional positive. The effect is not uniform: the same rule recovers 84.3% on LeCaRDv1 and is out of spec on CAIL2022, with the charge-to-relevance signal weakening in step (macro-AUC 0.871/0.759/0.728); a predicted-charge cascade reproduces 76.6% on LeCaRDv2 but does not transfer. The construct is also cashable at first stage: an exploratory zero-training charge-pool channel lifts LeCaRDv2 recall (R@100 +0.025, wrong-charge controls hurt), reported as a positive control for the confound, not a retrieval method or novelty claim. Charge is thus a high-leverage construct-validity factor at the benchmark level -- not auniform explanation of NDCG@10, and not evidence that any system relies on charge. We package established construct-validity and partial-input checks as a reusable charge-controlled protocol (CCE); on all three benchmarks its triggers come back null or descriptive, behaving as designed. We release the scripts, schema, and protocol so future benchmarks can be screened before their NDCG@10 is read as legal-reasoning ability.

</details>


### [320] [CFALR: Collaborative Filtering-Augmented Large Language Model for Personalized Fashion Outfit Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13001)
*Yujuan Ding,Junrong Liao,Yunshan Ma,Yi Bin,Wenqi Fan,Tat-Seng Chua,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CFALR combines collaborative filtering with large language models for personalized outfit recommendation, using CF-enhanced embeddings to bridge semantic and collaborative interaction spaces, outperforming both CF-based and LLM-based methods on Polyvore and IQON benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing collaborative filtering methods struggle with data sparsity and complex user-item-outfit relationships, while template-based approaches are constrained by rigid pre-designed structures. There is a need for a framework that balances user preferences with aesthetic compatibility without these limitations.

Method: CFALR describes user-outfit interactions in natural language and leverages LLMs to capture fashion semantics. It employs CF-enhanced embeddings to bridge semantic and collaborative interaction spaces, incorporating trainable projection layers for integrating relational and content features, and a CF-augmented generative mechanism to efficiently navigate the large outfit combination space.

Result: Experiments on Polyvore and IQON benchmarks demonstrate CFALR's superior performance over both traditional CF-based and LLM-based methods in personalized fill-in-the-blank and personalized outfit generation tasks.

Conclusion: CFALR represents the first LLM-based architecture specifically designed for personalized outfit recommendation, successfully synergizing collaborative filtering signals with LLM-based fashion semantics to achieve state-of-the-art results.

Abstract: Personalized outfit recommendation poses a significant challenge in e-commerce and social media platforms, requiring systems that balance user preferences with aesthetic compatibility. Collaborative filtering (CF) provides a traditional solution for this, but it struggles with data-sparse scenarios and complex user-item-outfit relationships. Meanwhile, existing template-based approaches are constrained by rigid pre-designed structures. To bridge these research gaps, we introduce CFALR (Collaborative Filtering-Augmented Large Language Model for Recommendation), a novel framework that synergizes collaborative filtering with large language models for personalized outfit recommendation. Specifically, CFALR describes user-outfit interactions in natural language and leverages LLMs to capture fashion semantics while employing CF-enhanced embeddings to bridge the semantic space and the collaborative interaction spaces. Our technical contributions include: (1) the first LLM-based architecture specifically designed for personalized outfit recommendation, (2) a CF-augmented generative mechanism that efficiently navigates the extensive combination space of outfit items, and (3) trainable projection layers that optimally integrate relational and content features. Experiments on Polyvore and IQON benchmarks demonstrate CFALR's superior performance over both traditional CF-based and LLM-based methods in personalized fill-in-the-blank and personalized outfit generation tasks.

</details>


### [321] [The Clustering Strikes Back: Building Cost-Effective and High-Performance ANNS at Scale with Helmsman](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13145)
*Yuchen Huang,Baiteng Ma,Yiping Sun,Yang Shi,Xiao Chen,Xiaocheng Zhong,Zhiyong Wang,Yao Hu,Erci Xu,Chuliang Weng*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: HELMSMAN is a clustering-based ANNS system deploying on all-flash servers for RedNote, combining a userspace storage stack, learned pruning, and GPU-accelerated construction to slash hardware costs by over 90% while handling billion-scale indexes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RedNote's in-memory graph-based ANNS (HNSW) incurs unsustainable memory footprint growth due to expanding users and content, leading to massive CapEx and OpEx. Existing alternatives fail due to kernel I/O overhead, rigid pruning strategies, and slow index construction on all-flash infrastructure.

Method: HELMSMAN integrates three innovations: (1) an ANNS-oriented userspace storage stack that bypasses kernel I/O bottlenecks; (2) a leveling-learned pruning module that dynamically adapts pruning rather than using a fixed strategy; (3) GPU-accelerated pipelines for fast index construction. The system is built on clustering-based ANNS atop all-flash servers.

Result: HELMSMAN reduces hardware costs by over 90%. It enables billion-scale index builds and rebuilds within hours. In production, 40 machines running HELMSMAN replace approximately 35,000 CPU cores and 0.35 PB of DRAM, operating stably for several months.

Conclusion: Clustering-based ANNS on all-flash servers is a viable, cost-effective alternative to in-memory graph-based ANNS at scale. The combination of userspace I/O, learned pruning, and GPU acceleration overcomes the key limitations of prior disk-based approaches, enabling production-grade billion-scale vector search with dramatically lower hardware requirements.

Abstract: RedNote (a.k.a., Xiaohongshu, a global-scale social network platform) widely adopts approximate nearest neighbor search (ANNS) to power its search, recommendation, and advertising services. Due to the demanding Service Level Agreements (SLAs), we have to rely on in-memory graph-based ANNS (i.e., HNSW) to provide high throughput and low latency.
  However, the ever-growing user base and content volume have led to an explosive increase in memory footprint and consequently huge CapEx and OpEx. After exploring various alternatives, we find that building a clustering-based ANNS on top of all-flash servers can be promising. Yet, we still experience severe overheads from the kernel I/O stack, a fixed pruning strategy, and slow index construction.
  We present HELMSMAN, a high-performance and cost-effective clustering-based ANNS system, which combines an ANNS-oriented userspace storage stack, a leveling-learned pruning module, and GPU-accelerated pipelines of construction. HELMSMAN saves over 90% of hardware costs and enables billion-scale index (re)builds within hours. In the current production deployment, operating stably for several months, 40 machines now host ANNS workloads that previously required about 35,000 cores and 0.35 PB DRAM.

</details>


### [322] [CoDeR: Local Constraint-Compatible Retrieval Beyond Semantic Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13204)
*Xingkun Yin,Xuebin Tang,Hongyang Du*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CoDeR is a dense retrieval method that decouples topical relevance from constraint compatibility to address failure cases where semantically similar documents violate query constraints (e.g., negation, exclusion). It uses a compatibility scorer trained with lexical-polarity contrastive supervision, reducing violating documents in top ranks without LLM calls at inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional IR systems treat semantic similarity as a proxy for relevance, but for constraint-sensitive queries (e.g., involving negation, antonymy, exclusion), topically similar documents may violate the constraint direction. This "constraint-violating evidence exposure" degrades retrieval quality, motivating a method that explicitly separates topical matching from constraint satisfaction.

Method: CoDeR combines a standard topical dense encoder (for candidate recall) with a constraint-compatibility scorer implemented as a bi-encoder. The compatibility scorer is trained using lexical-polarity supervision: contrastive pairs of constraint-satisfying vs. constraint-violating evidence. At inference, the compatibility signal either rescore topically retrieved candidates or retrieves an auxiliary compatibility-oriented candidate set — all without external LLM calls.

Result: On controlled diagnostic benchmarks targeting antonymy, negation, and exclusion, CoDeR reduces Violation@2 by 20.59, 23.53, and 5.77 points respectively over the strongest non-CoDeR baselines. It also improves First Violating Rank (FVR), pushing violating documents deeper in the ranked list.

Conclusion: Decoupling topical relevance from constraint compatibility is effective for constraint-sensitive retrieval. CoDeR demonstrates that lightweight, inference-efficient compatibility scoring can substantially reduce constraint violations without relying on external LLMs.

Abstract: Information retrieval systems have long treated semantic similarity as a proxy for relevance. For constraint-sensitive queries, this proxy can fail when a document is topically close to the query but supports the opposite constraint direction, such as satisfying an attribute that should be excluded or affirming a relation that should be negated. We study this failure as constraint-violating evidence exposure and propose CoDeR, a local constraint-compatible dense retrieval method that separates topical relevance from constraint compatibility. CoDeR keeps a standard topical encoder for candidate coverage and adds a compatibility scorer, implemented as a bi-encoder, trained with lexical-polarity supervision over contrastive satisfying and violating evidences. The compatibility signal can be used to rescore topical candidates or to retrieve an auxiliary compatibility-oriented candidate set, producing a ranked document list without external Large Language Model~(LLM) calls at inference time. We evaluate CoDeR on controlled diagnostics and public negative-constraint retrieval benchmarks. Across three controlled diagnostic sets targeting antonymy, negation, and exclusion, CoDeR reduces V@2 by 20.59, 23.53, and 5.77 points relative to the strongest non-CoDeR baselines, and improves FVR by pushing the first violating document deeper in the ranking.

</details>


### [323] [CQC-RAG: Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Cross-Query Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13438)
*Yanjia Sun,Sifan Liu,Jie Shao*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CQC-RAG introduces a Cross-Query Consistency Hypothesis — correct answers maintain high confidence across syntactically varied but semantically equivalent queries, while hallucinations do not — and builds a framework that rewrites queries, constructs query-conditioned contexts, and selects answers based on confidence stability, outperforming prior multi-query baselines on TriviaQA (+4.76 EM) and MuSiQue (+9.12 EM).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems are brittle: semantically equivalent queries with different syntax yield different retrieval results, and irrelevant documents induce hallucinations. Existing multi-path methods inject diversity through uncontrolled decoding randomness and evaluate answers within a single evidence view, limiting robustness.

Method: CQC-RAG has three stages: (1) Query rewriting — the original question is paraphrased into multiple syntactically diverse but meaning-preserving queries. (2) Query-conditioned context construction — a shared document pool is reranked per query to build distinct reasoning contexts. (3) Cross-query consistency evaluation — an evidence-grounded protocol extracts answer-evidence pairs, and final answers are selected based on confidence stability (high and consistent confidence across query variants), enabling self-evaluation without external supervision.

Result: On four open-domain QA benchmarks, CQC-RAG achieves +4.76 percentage points EM over the strongest multi-query baseline on TriviaQA and +9.12 pp EM on MuSiQue, demonstrating effective filtering of noise-induced hallucinations.

Conclusion: Cross-query consistency provides a reliable self-evaluation signal for RAG: correct answers exhibit stable confidence under syntactic perturbation, while hallucinated answers do not. This enables robust answer selection without external supervision or expanded retrieval coverage.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a common approach for improving the factuality of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet its reliability remains highly sensitive to how external evidence is retrieved and used. Semantically equivalent queries with different syntactic forms may lead to different retrieval results, while irrelevant or misleading documents can further induce hallucinated answers. Existing multi-path reasoning methods improve robustness by sampling multiple candidate answers and applying voting- or confidence-based selection, but they still face two limitations: diversity is often injected through uncontrollable decoding randomness, and answer evaluation is usually confined to a single query-induced evidence view. To address these limitations, we propose a Cross-Query Consistency Hypothesis: correct answers tend to maintain high confidence across semantically equivalent but syntactically diverse queries, whereas noise-induced hallucinations exhibit unstable confidence under such query variations. Based on this hypothesis, we introduce CQC-RAG, a framework that co-designs query-level diversity injection with cross-query consistency evaluation. CQC-RAG rewrites the original question into diverse but meaning-preserving queries, reranks a shared document pool to construct query-conditioned reasoning contexts, applies an evidence-grounded protocol to extract answer-evidence pairs and selects answers according to their confidence stability across these contexts. This design enables self-evaluation without external supervision and does not rely on expanded retrieval coverage. Experiments on four open-domain question answering benchmarks show that CQC-RAG outperforms the strongest previous multi-query baseline by +4.76 pp EM on TriviaQA and +9.12 pp EM on MuSiQue, validating the effectiveness of cross-query consistency for filtering noise-induced hallucinations.

</details>


### [324] [OneRetrieval: Unifying Multi-Branch E-commerce Retrieval with an Editable Generative Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13533)
*Xuxin Zhang,Ben Chen,Yue Lv,Siyuan Wang,Yupeng Li,Yufei Ma,Zihan Liang,Tong Zhao,Ying Yang,Huangyu Dai,Lingtao Mao,Zhipeng Qian,Xinyu Sun,Chenyi Lei,Wenwu Ou,Kun Gai*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: OneRetrieval proposes the first editable generative retrieval framework for industrial e-commerce search, using Keyword-Aligned Encoding (KAE) to match deep recall quality while preserving the real-time editability of inverted indexes, deployed at Kuaishou serving hundreds of millions of daily PVs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial e-commerce search relies on a multi-branch retrieval stage fused by hand-tuned merging without joint optimization. Generative retrieval (GR) promises unification into a single model, but existing GR methods lack real-time editability — the ability to inject new terms within hours without model retraining, which the inverted-index branch uniquely provides. The goal is a one-model substitute that jointly delivers competitive recall quality AND real-time editability.

Method: OneRetrieval introduces Keyword-Aligned Encoding (KAE), tying each identifier position to an interpretable attribute word. Key components: (1) Information-theoretic merging organizes 18 attribute categories into six codebook groups with non-uniform capacity. (2) Reserved slots in each codebook enable post-deployment binding of new words without retraining. (3) A four-stage fine-tuning pipeline jointly secures retrieval quality and editability. This contrasts with closed-codebook methods (fixed quantized embeddings at training) and open-vocabulary methods (relying on model generalization for new terms).

Result: On five million real-traffic requests, OneRetrieval matches the deep recall of the strongest generative baseline, with an intervention hit rate over an order of magnitude above closed-codebook encodings. Online deployment replacing the inverted-index branch significantly lifts order volume; extending to nearly the entire retrieval stage holds conversion while improving CTR. The system is deployed at Kuaishou serving hundreds of millions of PVs daily.

Conclusion: OneRetrieval demonstrates that generative retrieval can simultaneously achieve competitive recall quality and real-time editability — previously a structural gap in the literature. The KAE framework and reserved-slot codebook design make it the first editable GR method, proving viable as a drop-in replacement for the inverted-index branch in industrial-scale e-commerce search, with measurable business metric gains.

Abstract: Industrial e-commerce search serves hundreds of millions of items through a multi-branch retrieval stage fused by hand-tuned merging without joint optimization. Generative retrieval (GR) raises the prospect of collapsing this stage into a single model, yet unification is gated by more than retrieval quality: the inverted-index branch converts below the platform average yet persists because it is almost the only branch where operations can inject a new term within hours without any model update; a one-model substitute must preserve this real-time editability. Existing GR methods structurally lack it: closed-codebook methods fix each slot to a quantized embedding at training, while open-vocabulary methods leave new-term routing to model generalization. We present OneRetrieval, a one-model GR framework built on Keyword-Aligned Encoding (KAE), which ties each identifier position to an interpretable attribute word, pairing competitive recall quality with the editability of the inverted index -- to our knowledge the first editable generative retrieval method. An information-theoretic merging organizes 18 attribute categories into six codebook groups with non-uniform capacity; reserved slots in each codebook can be bound to new words after deployment without retraining; and a four-stage fine-tuning pipeline secures quality and editability jointly. On five million real-traffic requests, OneRetrieval matches the deep recall of the strongest generative baseline, with an intervention hit rate over an order of magnitude above closed-codebook encodings. Online, replacing the inverted-index branch significantly lifts order volume; extending to nearly the entire stage holds conversion while improving CTR. The system is deployed at Kuaishou, serving hundreds of millions of PVs daily.

</details>
